K.K33: How to maintain high quality passenger services in line with organisational requirements.
K.K34: Own limits, authority, and responsibility with regard to ticket checks.
K.K35: The process for on-board checking of tickets and how to respond.
K.K36: The procedures for the safe arrival and dispatch of transport in line with own organisations requirements.
K.K37: The wider considerations and factors that contribute to a safe dispatch environment, for example platform/station crowd control, disruption to services, potential dispatch irregularities.
K.K38: The methods and reasons for stopping a vehicle within the station confines during an emergency.
K.K39: The procedures for transport service turnaround.

S.S1: Maintain safe and compliant working practices and travel environments, in accordance with regulation and organisational requirements.
S.S2: Prepare own work area to support safe and efficient delivery of transport services.
S.S3: Recognise and manage inappropriate passenger/public activity to maintain the integrity of the transport service.
S.S4: Identify passenger/public behaviour that indicates vulnerability and respond to help keep them safe.
S.S5: Respond to incidents and emergency situations in line with requirements.
S.S6: Identify potential incidents, breaches of security and emergency situations, and take appropriate action to ensure safety.
S.S7: Prepare for your next shift in accordance with operational requirements and own organisations procedures.
S.S8: Assist vulnerable passengers who require help, using specialised equipment or systems where necessary.

S.S10: Diagnose passenger related issues to provide accurate advice and support.
S.S11: Educate passengers to keep safe within the travel environment.
S.S12: Select from a range of communication methods to tailor messages to the audience.
S.S13: Utilise available equipment and systems in accordance with operational requirements.
S.S14: Recommend an impartial travel solution balancing the passengers circumstances with potential routes, network operations, and available tickets.
S.S15: Promote the benefits of products and services to eligible passengers.

S.S17: Handle cash in accordance with regulations.
S.S18: Account for the balance of payments during your shift.

S.S23: Stop a vehicle to reduce the risk to those potentially affected by an emergency situation.

S.S4: Analyse and interpret information to assess whether it meets legal, regulatory, process or policy requirements
S.S5: Analyse information to identify key issues, draw conclusions and make recommendations
S.S6: Demonstrate different communication methods and adapt communication style to their audience, being clear on purpose of communication and outputs required, for example; verbal, written, virtual communication, presentations
S.S7: Demonstrate different communication styles such as, active listening, team communications, business storytelling, negotiation techniques, conflict management, cross-cultural communications, equality and inclusivity considerations
S.S8: Maintain documents in line with current regulation and policy
S.S9: Challenge and influence managers and colleagues on desired course of action
S.S10: Build and maintain working relationships with stakeholders, contracting and managing stakeholder expectations.
S.S11: Build trust with colleagues, collaborating to achieve results
S.S12: Manage conflict, demonstrating empathy and a desire to work towards win-win outcomes
S.S13: Lead others to achieve desired outcomes
S.S14: Apply continuous improvement techniques to deliver improved compliance and risk outcomes for the organisation,  for example seek feedback and respond to improve performance
S.S15: Develop strategies to implement change
S.S16: Network with others in the profession to keep up to date with changes in the industry, best practice and potential opportunities
S.S17: Use digital tools for research, analysis, and to present data using visualisation techniques.
S.S18: Demonstrate technology to others and keeps up to date with developments in IT relative to their role
S.S19: Handle data safely and securely and share information in compliance with data protection legislation and organisation’s policy

B.B1: Displays honesty and integrity - truthful in their actions. Shows integrity by doing the right thing, maintains confidentiality and acts with due care and diligence
B.B2: Takes ownership of continued professional development, acts as a role model and supports others in their development.
B.B3: Takes an adaptable, evidence-based approach to decision making in the context of specific situations or environments.
B.B4: Resilient in challenging, changing environments. Shows emotional intelligence to resolve conflicts when they arise
B.B5: Takes responsibility for decisions and procedures implemented.
B.B6: Displays a growth mindset, learning from failures, spotting opportunities and overcoming challenges
B.B7: Collaborates and promotes teamwork across diverse teams; internal, external and across disciplines

K.K001: Financial Services Market: The structure of the Financial Services market including: Product Providers’ Services, Support Services and Platforms. The role of the Financial Adviser and associated supporting roles (e.g. the role of the Paraplanner). Different customer propositions including independent, restricted and non-advice models.
K.K002: Reg ul a tory & Compliance: The Financial Services regulatory framework, and the ethics and key principles of the advice process. The requirements and practical implications of the Statements of Principles for approved persons (APER) and the Fit and Proper Test for Approved Persons (FIT).
K.K003: Products & Cash Flow Modelling: Financial products & financial planning tools (e.g. Cash flow modelling). How to analyse which features and benefits of the relevant financial products are appropriate to the clients’ specific needs and objectives.
K.K004: Systems and Controls: All systems and controls required to deliver the key objectives for the client, and firm. How these fit within the wider company and the financial advice profession and how they protect the client and the company.
K.K005: Understanding Clients’ Needs & Objectives: The significance and importance of Knowing Your Client (KYC). How to prioritise a client’s needs and objectives. How to assess a client’s Attitude to Investment Risk and Capacity for Loss in relation to their needs and objectives.

S.S001: Know your customer: Make decisions confidently and autonomously in full awareness of the risk/potential external implications attached. Know when to seek further professional input, whilst maintaining high ethical standards and adhering to Treating Customers Fairly requirements.
S.S002: Customer service: Proactively and regularly meet the company’s stated service/quality assurance standards, including complaints procedures. Consistently support colleagues and collaborate to achieve a successful customer outcome.
S.S003: Business development: Develop long term relationships with customers. Create new opportunities for business growth through referrals and networking. Build a new business or sustain and grow an existing business by prioritising workloads and deliver in accordance with the associated business plan.
S.S004: Ma n ag in g Risk: Apply regulatory requirements both in the role of a Financial Adviser and also as applied to the Company. Maintain, the Fit and Proper person standards as laid down by the relevant regulator(s).
S.S005: Using systems and processes: Demonstrate technical proficiency in using the firm’s I.T. systems required to complete the advice process, maintain accurate records, and agree review meetings to sustain the client / adviser relationship. Ensure Data Protection Act is not contravened.
S.S006: Communication skills: Build rapport and communicate with empathy. Present and explain complex information simply and clearly both orally and verbally.

B.B001: Professionalism: Professionalism and an ethical attitude. That technical knowledge is maintained and recorded in line with requirements of the Regulator and Professional Bodies.
B.B002: Ad a p tability: Behaviours to meet and exceed the requirements of client needs and objectives within the requirements of a continually changing regulatory environment.
B.B003: Business orientated: Pro-activeness in considering the commercial demands in a financial advice business and in looking for ways to develop and improve the business model and its service to customers.
B.B004: Positive Mental A ttitude: A proactive “can-do” attitude in all aspects of work including overcoming challenges and responding to set backs. Self-motivation in achieving the objectives in the role.
B.B005: Re l a tionship management & collaboration: A proactive approach to building and maintaining relationships with both internal and external customers. Consistent support for colleagues and collaborates to achieve team and individual results.

K.K1: Presentation skills and methods.

K.K3: Legislation and organisational policies relating to equity, diversity and inclusion in the workplace and their impact on the organisation and stakeholders.
K.K4: Approaches to people management, for example recruitment, performance management, reward, and talent management and resource planning.

K.K6: Methods for researching, analysing, interpreting and evaluating data to inform judgements and enable decision making.
K.K7: Financial management techniques and implications of decisions for budgets.
K.K8: How to identify and manage organisational improvement opportunities.
K.K9: Project management tools and techniques.
K.K10: Methods used to identify, manage and prioritise stakeholder relationships.
K.K11: The current and future needs of the sector and the impact on their organisation.
K.K12: Problem solving and decision-making techniques.
K.K13: Influencing and negotiation models and techniques.
K.K14: Conflict resolution and mediation processes.
K.K15: Communication techniques and approaches.
K.K16: Ethics and values-based leadership theories and principles, for example employee wellbeing.
K.K17: Change management concepts and methods for implementing change within the organisation.
K.K18: Leadership and management tools and techniques.
K.K19: The sector in which the organisation operates and its impact on their role.
K.K20: The continuous development requirements and learning needs of their team.
K.K21: Business continuity principles, including risk assessment, contingency planning and disaster recovery.
K.K22: Organisational policies and procedures, for example health and safety.
K.K23: Responsible organisation policies and practices covering social, environmental, and economic factors, including sustainability.
K.K24: Coaching and mentoring techniques.
K.K25: The strategic direction of the organisation and the impact on operational plans.

S.S1: Communicate and present information to stakeholders using different types of media.
S.S2: Identify problems and provide solutions.
S.S3: Manage and set goals and accountabilities for individuals and teams.
S.S4: Analyse performance data for individuals and teams to identify areas for improvement.
S.S5: Manage and influence project activity to deliver within budget and resource requirements.
S.S6: Lead and influence the team and individuals to support an inclusive culture of equity, diversity, and the promotion of well-being.
S.S7: Motivate team members and individuals through collaborative activities, for example one-to-one coaching and team meetings, to achieve organisational goals.
S.S8: Use digital tools to analyse information and monitor performance and budgets to drive the implementation and delivery of plans and projects.
S.S9: Research, interpret and analyse information to inform the implementation of business plans or projects.
S.S10: Evaluate the impact of outcomes from organisational plans or projects to drive the decision-making process.
S.S11: Interpret and implement the practical application of regulation, legislation and organisational policies for stakeholders.
S.S12: Manage continuous improvement and change for their team and organisation.
S.S13: Analyse and prioritise organisation activities in response to the operating environment.
S.S14: Implement business continuity plans, including risk assessment, contingency planning and disaster recovery, to ensure the uninterrupted operation of critical functions.
S.S15: Identify and respond to external factors that may influence the future landscape and evaluate their impact on the organisation.
S.S16: Influence and negotiate with stakeholders to shape and agree goals and outcomes.
S.S17: Manage relationships across multiple and diverse stakeholders.
S.S18: Deliver sustainable services and solutions which allow the organisation to respond to changes in social, economic and environmental factors.
S.S19: Manage and facilitate learning and continuous professional development for their team.
S.S20: Coach and mentor individuals within their team.
S.S21: Develop and implement operational plans that align with the strategic direction of the organisation.

B.B1: Acts professionally, ethically and with integrity.
B.B2: Supports an inclusive culture, treating colleagues and stakeholders fairly and with respect.
B.B3: Takes accountability and ownership of their own and the team’s tasks and workload.
B.B4: Seeks learning opportunities and continuous professional development for self and the wider team.
B.B5: Works flexibly and adapts to circumstances.
B.B6: Works collaboratively with others across the organisation and stakeholders.

K.K1: The dairy industry structure. Financial considerations. Ethical business practices.
K.K2: Different teams and functions involved in dairy production. Dairy technologist role. Limits of scope of practice: when to seek input from others and when to escalate.
K.K3: Good Manufacturing Practice (GMP). Production and operational planning concepts.
K.K4: Health and safety. Health and Safety at Work Act – responsibilities. Health and safety culture. Control of Substances Hazardous to Health (CoSHH). The Reporting of Injuries, Diseases and Dangerous Occurrences Regulations (RIDDOR). Manual handling. Personal Protective Equipment (PPE). Types of hazards. Risk assessments, mitigation methods, and method statements (safe systems of work).
K.K5: Principles of food and dairy safety. Allergenic control. Good Hygienic Practice (GHP). Microbiology and food borne illnesses. Biological, physical, allergenic (cross-contamination), and chemical contamination of dairy and related products. Food storage, temperature control and preservation of dairy products. Hygienic design, construction and maintenance of food premises and equipment, and their cleaning and disinfection. Pest control. Personal hygiene and training requirements. Food safety culture. Traceability.
K.K6: Food safety legislation principles: Food Safety Act, Regulation (EC) 852/2004, The General Food Law Regulation (EC) 178/2002, The Food Safety and Hygiene (England) Regulations, The Food Hygiene (Scotland) Regulations, The Food Hygiene (Wales) Regulations, The Food Information to Consumers Regulations (EC)1169/2011, Food Information Regulations, Regulation (EC) No 2073/2005 on Microbiological criteria for foodstuffs, The Weights and Measures (Packaged Goods) Regulations. Sources of information for legislation changes.
K.K7: Food safety management control of dairy and related food systems: Hazard Analysis and Critical Control Points (HACCP), Threat Analysis of Critical Control Points (TACCP), and Vulnerability Assessment of Critical Control Points (VACCP).
K.K8: Principles of dairy chemistry. Chemical properties. Compositional milk and dairy product analysis. Quality testing of milk and dairy products.
K.K9: Principles of dairy microbiology. Types of micro-organisms and their structure. Cultivation conditions and procedures. Aseptic conditions and Good Laboratory Practice (GLP): organisation and discipline within the laboratory. Hygiene monitoring and auditing. Laboratory analysis techniques. Indicator organisms. Interpretation of microbiological data as an indicator of risk.
K.K10: Milk and dairy derivatives as raw materials. Primary production. Dairy farming practice. Composition of milk and dairy produce. Additives. Dairy products as an ingredient in other foods and associated allergenic issues. Dairy alternatives (non-milk based ingredients).
K.K11: Function of quality assurance within the dairy industry. Quality assurance schemes and factors affecting the Quality Management Plan (QMP).
K.K12: Cleaning, disinfection, and sterilisation. Different techniques: chemical, heat, steam technology and ultraviolet - when they should be used. Components of Clean In Place (CIP). New developments in cleaning technology. Environmental impact of cleaning. Cleaning validation, verification, and optimisation techniques.
K.K13: Basic physical and hygienic design principles of dairy process engineering: mass and energy balances, modes of thermal transfer, principles of fluid flow, and rheology (deformation and flow of materials - solids and liquids).
K.K14: Principles of food industry unit operations. Preservation operations. Dairy industry unit operations. Factory service operations.
K.K15: Product development processes: recipe development, specifications, market gap identification, acceptance testing and process design, packaging requirements, and nutrition and organoleptic characteristics.
K.K16: Milk processing: end-to-end. New concepts in milk processing. By-products.
K.K17: Cheese and fermented product technology. Cheese and cheese related products. Yogurt and related products. Soured creams and crème fraiche.
K.K18: Butter and milk fat technology. Butter manufacture. Yellow fat and dairy spread manufacture. Ice cream manufacture. Dairy dessert manufacture.
K.K19: The chemistry of whey protein. Whey processing techniques and technology.
K.K20: Leadership and management techniques: influencing, negotiation, and conflict management.
K.K21: Change management principles and techniques.
K.K22: Project management roles and techniques: planning, prioritising, organising, stakeholder management, and risk management.
K.K23: Problem solving and fault finding: 5 whys, root cause analysis, Failure Mode Effects Analysis (FMEA).
K.K24: Continuous improvement principles and techniques: Plan-do-check-act (PDCA), Lean, 6 Sigma, and Statistical Process Control (SPC). Lean manufacturing tools. Process mapping.
K.K25: Data analysis techniques. Data analysis and reporting systems.
K.K26: Dairy supply chain: mapping and risk management. World dairy economic drivers. Milk and dairy product supply and demand factors. Food security.
K.K27: Environmental Protection Act and environmental management systems.
K.K28: Measuring environment impact and environmental audit requirements.
K.K29: Dairy automation and process control. Control systems. Instrumentation. Control theory principles. Machine communications and networking. Integrated control and automation systems. Digital manufacturing. Dairy industry automated processes.
K.K30: Information technology: Management Information Systems (MIS), spreadsheets, presentation, word processing, email, virtual communication and learning platforms. General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR). Cyber security.
K.K31: Communication techniques.
K.K32: Report writing techniques.
K.K33: Workplace training and development techniques: coaching and transfer of knowledge.
K.K34: Equality, diversity, and inclusion. Unconscious bias.

S.S3: Comply with food safety regulations and procedures.
S.S4: Comply with health and safety regulations, guidelines, and procedures.
S.S5: Comply with environmental regulations, guidelines, and procedures.

S.S14: Apply problem solving techniques, identifying issues. Propose solutions to problems.
S.S15: Use continuous improvement techniques and make recommendations.
S.S16: Apply project management techniques: planning and prioritising tasks, organising resources, managing stakeholders and risk management.

S.S18: Develop technical written content. For example, operating procedures, working instructions, and reports.

S.S23: Identify training needs. Coach individuals.

B.B1: Take personal responsibility for and promote food safety and health and safety.
B.B2: Take personal responsibility for and promote sustainable working practices.
B.B3: Act in a professional manner.
B.B4: Take responsibility for the quality of work and enable others to work to high standards. For example, decisive, self-reliant, and motivated.
B.B5: Respond and adapt to work demands and situations.
B.B6: Recognise limitations, seek input from others and escalate issues when required.
B.B7: Collaborate with others for example, within teams, across disciplines, and external stakeholders, promoting inclusion.

K.K001: MCA: MCA’s Organisation, mandate and position in the context of government.
K.K002: Emergency Planning: The emergency planning process, including Major Incident Plans, the National Contingency Plan for Marine Pollution and relevant business continuity plans.
K.K003: Nautical: Nautical knowledge in relation to collision regulations, buoyage, pilotage, navigation, stability and communications in order to assess the context and likely impact on operations.
K.K004: Counter pollution, salvage, wreck and coastal protection: MCA’s responsibilities with regard to Counter Pollution, Salvage, Wreck and Coastal Protection.
K.K005: Vessel traffic monitoring: MCA’s obligations (and a MOO’s responsibilities) for monitoring vessel traffic.
K.K006: Skills: The Maritime Operations Officer will be able to:
K.K007: Chart and map work: Apply navigational and plotting skills in the use of maps and charts.
K.K008: Response, Co- ordination, Mission Conduct and Incident Command: Categorise, respond to and co-ordinate Coastguard operations. Gather and analyse information to inform operational plans, execute missions and brief senior officers. Send rescue resources (such as lifeboats, air assets and Coastguard Rescue Teams) to incidents and manage their on-scene activity. Work with other declared resources and partner organisations (both during and outside of incidents) to give advice and updates. Make (and record) effective decisions during incident working, using Standard Operating Procedures.
K.K009: Search: Predict the likely movement of various types of objects drifting at sea by employing established methods and techniques for the calculation of search areas. Plan searches on the shore and land for any target using established procedures. Effectively allocate suitable resources and prioritise search effort. Plan and coordinate an effective maritime search, using available tools, resources and analysis of the conditions for search activity.
K.K010: Environmental Factors: Access and interpret weather, tide and other current environmental factors in order to assess the likely impact on operations.
K.K011: Operational Systems: Ensure the effective and efficient use of operational communications including the full range of Information and Communications Technology (ICT), radio and other related equipment and systems found within a Coastguard Operations Centre and maintenance of this equipment.
K.K012: Values / Behaviour: The Maritime Operations Officer must always:
K.K013: Safety: Lead by example in promoting safe working practices that protect our people, our assets and all those at work or leisure in the maritime environment. Embed safety awareness in their own culture and through partnership working, education and regulation.
K.K014: Leading and Communicating: Put forward their own views in a clear and constructive manner, choosing the most appropriate communication method Act in a fair and respectful way in dealing with others Be able to learn from mistakes
K.K015: Delivering at Pace: Work in an organised manner using own knowledge and expertise to deliver on time and to standard, working with energy and pace to get the job done. Take responsibility for quality of own work, remain focused on delivery and maintain consistent performance.
K.K016: Making Effective Decisions: Investigate and respond to gaps, errors and irregularities in information. Speak up to clarify decisions and query these constructively.
K.K017: Trust: Behave in an honest, considered and consultative manner and can be relied on to honour and deliver MCA’s commitments.
K.K018: Respect: Treat everyone fairly and with dignity, encouraging and valuing everyone’s contribution. Not tolerate bullying and harassment.
K.K020: Qualifications: Apprentices without level 2 English and maths will need to achieve this level prior to taking the end-point assessment.
K.K022: Review date: The Standard will be reviewed in 3 years from approval, unless there is evidence of significant change which warrants earlier amendment.

K.K1: Operating procedures for the equipment that is used on vessels, for example main engines, pumps and winches.
K.K2: Procedures when providing support to diving operations.
K.K3: The principles of salvage work.
K.K4: Methods for laying and recovering moorings.
K.K5: Anchor handling methods.
K.K6: The different ways in which vessels contribute to construction work at sea.
K.K7: Transfer of personnel and equipment: ship to ship, ship to shore, use of vessels, crew responsibilities.
K.K8: Ship manoeuvring: steering systems and their functions, avoiding and navigating locks, docks and bridges.
K.K9: Guard and survey duty procedures.
K.K10: How vessels support aquaculture.
K.K11: Radio communication methods and their application.
K.K12: Setting up and recovery of a towline, including all components (wire, shackles, swivel, delta plate, stretcher, bridles).
K.K13: The Minimum Breaking Load that applies to a towing set.
K.K14: Emergency procedures, applicable to vessel environments and locations.
K.K15: Survival techniques in water, including the use of LSA and PPE, for example, lifejacket usage, boarding survival craft, operate location devices.
K.K16: Operation of survival equipment carried on vessels, for example, retrieval equipment.
K.K17: Techniques for safe working practices across small commercial vessel operations. For example, crane operations, international signalling system.
K.K18: Hygiene procedures for the safe preparation of meals on board.
K.K19: Statutory and industry regulations for safe working on board a vessel, for example,  The Workboat Code, International Regulations for the Prevention of Collisions at Sea.
K.K20: The impact of external forces on a vessel such as sea state, swell and wind.
K.K21: The basic stability of a vessel and the factors which may affect it, for example, distribution of cargo and fuel.
K.K22: Maintenance techniques for vessel equipment.
K.K23: The security regulations and processes of challenge and identification checks for any person boarding a  vessel.
K.K24: Information technology and digital systems for example computer based recording systems and navigation equipment. GDPR and cyber security regulations.
K.K25: Paper chart work and tidal navigation techniques.
K.K26: Radar use for position monitoring and collision avoidance techniques.
K.K27: Electronic chart use for position monitoring.
K.K28: Principles and use of weather forecast and general terms.
K.K29: Code vessel paperwork to ensure regulatory compliance.
K.K30: Own responsibilities under the organisational safety management system (SMS).
K.K31: Maintenance of survival equipment carried on vessels, for example, retrieval equipment.
K.K32: Principles of equity, diversity, and inclusion in the workplace. Unconscious bias.
K.K33: Emergency first aid techniques and procedures.
K.K34: The signs of fatigue in themselves and others and how to recognise them.
K.K35: Documentation: methods and requirements - electronic and paper.
K.K36: Environmental and sustainability regulations and guidance. Environmental hazards that can arise from small commercial vessel operations.
K.K37: Loading and unloading cargo and equipment using a deck crane.
K.K38: Verbal communication techniques and maritime terminology.
K.K39: Principles of team working.
K.K40: Fire fighting equipment (FFE) carried on vessels, for example, manual and mechanical fire pumps, the use of fixed fire systems, and fire alarms.
K.K41: Capabilities techniques for keeping a safe navigational watch.

S.S1: Operate vessel equipment including main engines, pumps, emergency equipment and winches.
S.S2: Support the safety of those on board, for example, passengers, technicians and other crew.
S.S3: Maintain a vessel's weather and watertight integrity and take necessary action if breached.
S.S4: Operate life saving appliances (LSA) and firefighting equipment (FFE).
S.S5: Administer emergency first aid.
S.S6: Coordinate a response to an emergency situation.
S.S7: Steer a vessel in different marine environments, for example, maintain and alter course when required to do so, taking into account local environment and conditions.
S.S8: Manoeuvre a vessel to enable recovery of "man overboard" and the safe boarding of emergency services and authorities.
S.S9: Load and unload cargo and equipment using deck cranes, for example, the correct slinging of loads, use of international signalling methods and correct stowage of dangerous goods.
S.S10: Anchor a vessel.
S.S11: Communicate with others verbally using maritime terminology for example, colleagues and stakeholders.
S.S12: Perform deck operations as appropriate to the vessel type, and on board equipment, for example, plough dredging, survey activities, setting up a tow, recovery of towline in adverse weather conditions, transfer of personnel and equipment.
S.S13: Contribute to maintaining vessel equipment including main engines, pumps, emergency equipment and winches
S.S14: Respond to emergency situations, for example, deploying life rafts, deploying man overboard equipment, using emergency towlines.
S.S15: Undertake running and emergency repairs.
S.S16: Create a safe means to board and disembark from a vessel.
S.S17: Recognise and respond to the signs of fatigue in self and others.
S.S18: Prepare food on board.
S.S19: Use information and digital technology. Comply with GDPR and cyber security regulations.
S.S20: Keep a safe navigational watch
S.S21: Use vessel radio equipment.
S.S22: Maintain lifesaving appliances (LSA) and firefighting equipment (FFE)
S.S23: Follow safe working practices.
S.S24: Apply equity, diversity and inclusion policies.
S.S25: Complete security checks for persons boarding a vessel
S.S26: Moor a vessel.
S.S27: Unmoor a vessel.
S.S28: Follow safe working practices under the organisational safety management system (SMS)
S.S29: Record or enter information - electronic or paper based, for example, risk assessments, equipment service records, handover documents and manufacturers' documentation,  work sheets, checklists.
S.S30: Follow procedures in line with environmental and sustainability regulations, standards, and guidance. Segregate resources for reuse, recycling and disposal.
S.S31: Use electronic navigational charts to fix the vessels position.
S.S32: Apply team working principles

B.B1: Prioritise health and safety.
B.B2: Flexible in dealing with new and changing situations.
B.B3: Team-focus to meet work goals.
B.B4: Consideration and respectful of others, including, those who need to sleep whilst others are awake.
B.B5: Actively protects the marine environment from pollution
B.B6: Act ethically

K.K1: Legislation, standards of proficiency, codes of conduct and ethics, underpinned by the principals of Good Scientific Practice, that apply to the role of healthcare science practitioner.
K.K2: Equality and diversity legislation, local and national policies and local ways of working.
K.K3: Principles of duty of candour, probity, honesty and integrity in professional practice.
K.K4: Importance of critical reflection and evaluation of the evidence base that underpins clinical technical practice.
K.K5: Factors impacting on mental health and the importance of promoting mental health and wellbeing.
K.K6: Scientific principles of investigations and therapeutics offered by healthcare science services.
K.K7: Principles and practice of equipment management, maintenance, repair and safety.
K.K8: Technical skills teaching frameworks, assessment methods, including the assessment of technical skills.
K.K9: Standard operating procedures that apply to the role and the procedures for maintaining and reviewing them.
K.K10: Principles that underpin the delivery of person-centred care.
K.K11: Principles of a duty of care, safeguarding and protection as they apply to adults and children.
K.K12: The rights of individuals that apply when giving informed and meaningful consent.
K.K13: Clinical technical procedures that apply to clinical conditions encountered within own role.
K.K14: How to determine the correct level of language to use when sharing oral or written information with others or when giving feedback, including for those with complex needs.
K.K15: Support available in difficult situations or when a complaint is made.
K.K16: Policies and protocols that relate to the right of individuals to make informed decisions.
K.K17: Importance of accurate, up to date and signed record keeping.
K.K18: Principles relating to confidentiality, including its limitations, and how it applies in relation to own work.
K.K19: Value and impact of healthcare science in the delivery of healthcare services and on the wider healthcare team.
K.K20: Advanced concepts of leadership and their application to own practice.
K.K21: Delegation, reporting and escalation protocols that apply in the workplace and how these support team working.
K.K22: Conditions that support the delivery of safe and effective work practices carried out by the team.
K.K23: Health, safety and security legislation, local and national policies, including infection prevention and control that apply in the workplace.
K.K24: Risk assessment methodologies, including strategies for dissemination of the findings, and approaches to implementing the changes required.
K.K25: Quality assurance, accreditation and service improvement or innovation processes.
K.K26: Tools and techniques used to undertake  audit, including analysis, interpretation and communication of audit findings to promote quality.
K.K27: Governance and ethical frameworks applied to service improvement programmes.
K.K28: Continuous improvement principles for the delivery of quality outcomes.
K.K29: Importance of identifying opportunities for research, innovation and implementing change.
K.K30: Process and tools used for contributing to research, innovation, service improvement and grant proposals.
K.K31: Models of critical reflection and self-reflection to enhance the quality of patient care.
K.K32: Underpinning theories and benefits to staff of the ongoing appraisal processes.
K.K33: How to participate in an appraisal or performance review and support the development of an action plan.

S.S1: Practice in line with the legislation, standards of proficiency, codes of conduct and ethics that apply to healthcare science practitioners.
S.S2: Promote non-discriminatory, equitable and inclusive practices and ensure that each person is treated with dignity and respect.
S.S3: Promote the principles of a duty of candour, acting with integrity and in the best interest of individuals.
S.S4: Recognise and resolve problems and support others to do the same.
S.S5: Identify ways of promoting own good mental health and wellbeing and that of others.
S.S6: Independently analyse or interpret accurately clinical technical data.
S.S7: Be responsible and accountable for the safety and functioning of equipment evaluating and resolving issues.
S.S8: Analyse, present or explain technical results to others.
S.S9: Develop and review standard operating procedures (SOPs) and support others to participate in the process.
S.S10: Practice or support the provision of person-centred care.
S.S11: Promote and maintain the principles of a duty of care, safeguarding and protection, working with others to ensure individuals do not come to harm.
S.S12: Practice and support others to apply policy and protocols that relate to informed consent.
S.S13: Deliver or support the delivery of high quality clinical technical procedures as applicable to own role.

K.K1: The role of regulation as a tool of Government through statutory and non-statutory measures.

K.K15: The purpose of auditing and inspection and associated techniques, processes and documentation.
K.K16: Types of internal controls that businesses can have in place to support compliance.

K.K22: Principles of customer service and their application to interacting with key stakeholders when carrying out duties.

K.K25: Ethical practices that seek to preserve the trust of stakeholders including the need for fairness, accountability and transparency.

K.K1: The full scope of the discipline of UX, including definitions, principles and ontologies, as well as the different perspectives, approaches or schools of thought and the theories that underpin them. Advanced methods and techniques to review, consolidate, extend and synthesise their knowledge and understanding, and to initiate and carry out projects.
K.K2: Key schools of thought and specialist areas of practice, including Human Computer Interaction (HCI) and sociological, psychological and design approaches to UX, including User Centred Design (UCD), data-led design and experimental testing.
K.K3: The essential concepts of digital product design, service design and User Interface (UI) design, and how these fundamental concepts can be applied to new and emerging forms of user interaction.
K.K4: The broad and evolving digital landscape, including the interaction between online and offline, and the various channels that direct users to products and services (search engines, direct traffic, referrals etc.), including how to critically analyse and interpret analytics data.
K.K5: The non-functional aspects of digital product/service development and improvement and the relationship they have with user experience (e.g. performance, cyber security, interoperability).
K.K6: The changing role of digital in human experiences and the impact of technology in social, commercial, environmental and cultural contexts and how to operate within ambiguous and uncertain situations.
K.K7: How to achieve an ethical balance when applying psychological and persuasive techniques (e.g. scarcity, reciprocity and social proofing) to encourage users to carry out desired actions.
K.K8: How UX principles adapt to accommodate different forms of interaction across multiple touchpoints (physical and/or digital), and to formulate and apply these principles in complex contexts.
K.K9: How to initiate and deploy accurately established techniques of UX practices to new and emerging technologies and interfaces (e.g. conversational UI, wearable UI, multimodal UI, and augmented, virtual and mixed reality (AR, VR and MR) interfaces).
K.K10: How UX practices and design recommendations can be effectively applied throughout development, improvement and continuous delivery life cycles using a range of methodologies, including iterative, agile and lean approaches.
K.K11: How to solve problems through testing and evaluating solutions via analysis of test data and results from feasibility, acceptance and usability testing.
K.K12: How to interpret organisational policies, standards and guidelines in relation to their impact on UX, and anticipate any potential conflicts between organisational and user needs.
K.K13: The legal, ethical, professional and regulatory frameworks which affect digital products and services.
K.K14: The benefits and constraints of creating inclusive user experiences, including how to critically analyse and evaluate designs against accessibility guidelines, policies and regulatory requirements.
K.K15: Awareness and understanding of the core tools and technologies involved in digital product and service design and development, including a basic level of knowledge of the advantages of certain tools and technologies for specific applications and purposes.

S.S1: Apply creative, analytical and critical thinking skills to the design, development and improvement of UX solutions and systematically analyse and apply structured problem-solving techniques to complex UX challenges.
S.S2: Use design thinking and/or service design methods to determine the design and implementation of new value propositions, products and services, and improve existing ones.
S.S3: Select, formulate and apply from a range of user research methods including those from the fields of Human Computer Interaction (HCI), sociology, psychology and ethnography, including qualitative and quantitative approaches.
S.S4: Compose, construct and use multiple user research approaches to form an understanding of user populations, including surveys, field based research, contextual inquiry, user interviews, focus groups, stakeholder interviews/workshops, formative lab-based and direct user testing sessions (e.g. acceptance and usability testing).
S.S5: Critically analyse and evaluate assumptions and findings to understand user and stakeholder needs (including behaviours, emotions, beliefs and preferences), and define the solutions’ functional, non-functional, structural and content requirements.
S.S6: Critically evaluate arguments, assumptions, abstract concepts and data (that may be incomplete), to make judgements, and to frame appropriate questions to achieve a solution - or identify a range of solutions - to a problem.
S.S7: Analyse, interpret, synthesise and apply insights, to inform the development of personas, user journeys and system workflows, to ensure user and organisational needs are met.
S.S8: Design, facilitate and evaluate experimental tests using tools such as A/B and multivariate testing to enable a data-led approach to the development and continual improvement of UX solutions.
S.S9: Design, facilitate and evaluate requirements gathering, ideation and co-design activities, involving stakeholders and/or users.
S.S10: Creatively explore and devise a range of design solutions, including the production of system and user flows, static wireframes and prototypes of varying degree of fidelity, from paper prototypes to interactive prototypes.
S.S11: Adapt and evaluate design solutions according to the context of intended use, including responsive, mobile, online, offline, personal, public and enterprise, working with multidisciplinary product teams to assess the impact of implementing specific design recommendations.
S.S12: Design and refine clear, logical information architectures for content and data.
S.S13: Independently analyse test data, interpret results and evaluate the suitability of proposed solutions, considering current and future contexts of use, including in consultation with team members from other disciplines to ascertain a holistic view on the applicability of design recommendations.
S.S14: Articulate and communicate complex information, concepts and ideas effectively and concisely, through written, visual and verbal means.
S.S15: Communicate concepts in a manner appropriate to the audience, adapting communication techniques accordingly between user research participants, stakeholders or varying degrees of seniority and team members from a broad spectrum of specialist fields.
S.S16: Manage expectations and present user research insight, proposed solutions and/or test findings to clients and stakeholders.
S.S17: Use advanced cognitive skills to deal with competing interests within and outside the organisation, through well-reasoned arguments and excellent negotiation skills.
S.S18: Work autonomously and interact effectively within wide, multidisciplinary teams, including designers, developers, engineers, analysts, project managers etc.
S.S19: Identify the preferences, motivations, strengths and limitations of other people and apply these insights in order to work more effectively with and to motivate others.
S.S20: Demonstrate competence in customer service, in active listening and in leading, influencing and persuading others.
S.S21: Balance and trade-off competing quality, time and budget criteria, demonstrating understanding of business need, managing time effectively and being able to plan and complete UX activities to schedule.

B.B7: Undertakes independent decision-making in complex, unpredictable and changing circumstances.
B.B8: Is comfortable and confident interacting with people from different backgrounds and demographics and in delivering excellent customer service.

K.K001: Industry and organisation understanding: Deep understanding of the structure of the Financial Services industry and in particular the role their part of the sector and organisation plays; the purpose of the function in which they work; how their function relates to the wider business.  Good understanding of the impact the external environment has on Financial Services as appropriate to their role, together with relevant best practice. Sound understanding of the organisation’s ‘Values’ and professional standards and how these are evidenced through their role. Clearly understands the purpose of the different areas of the organisation they need to work with in their role, and how they support them.
K.K002: Financial Services legal/regulatory/ compliance framework: Deep understanding of the relevant Financial Services legal and regulatory framework and ethics, and the purpose behind them. Sound understanding of the risk and governance frameworks of their organisation / sector, and how these manifest themselves in the role.  Sound understanding of relevant rules and principles for their own role / team. Understands and can clearly articulate the implications of non-compliance for their organisation.
K.K003: Products and Services: Deep understanding of the relevant Financial Service products and services offered to clients/customers by their organisation, together with sound knowledge of the products and services, and their features and benefits, relevant to their role.
K.K004: Client /Customer Segments: Sound understanding of the client/customer segments that the organisation delivers to and the channels that they use. Understands the approach to delivering fair client/customer outcomes across the business in a financial services setting, including best practice. Deep understanding of their client’s/customer’s relevant financial service requirements relevant to their role (internal and external as appropriate)
K.K005: Systems and Processes: Sound understanding of the organisation’s policies and procedures. Sound understanding of the systems, tools and processes used in the role, together with the standards to be met.  Proficient in the relevant IT skills, this may include the generic (e.g. excel), and organisation and Financial Service specific software/systems, as required to deliver the role outcomes

S.S001: Client/Customer relationships: Builds ethically sound and trusted relationships with internal and/or external clients/customers to form the basis of a long-term partnership. Works with client/customers to identify their relevant financial services requirements.  Provides flexible and innovative solutions, integrating products and excellent service as appropriate to their role, in order to support client/customers and meet their best interests
S.S002: Delivering Services: Carries out all required activities, using agreed systems and processes, to ensure that the relevant policies of the organisation for their area of specialisation are met.  Takes the initiative to meet challenging individual and team performance measures in line with organisation policy, Values, standards and sector relevant regulatory requirements. Can identify and communicate risks to the relevant management or compliance personnel within the organisation, understanding and following the appropriate company process to address. Delivers projects as required.
S.S003: Strategy and Planning: Contributes to the development of strategic and operational plans for their area.  Manages and reports on progress of specific elements of this in their area of responsibility.  Proactively plans and organises their work and time, clearly identifying priorities to meet commitments / KPIs/ deadlines.
S.S004: Problem solving and decision making: Evaluates information quickly and draws accurate conclusions. Assesses a problem from multiple angles to ensure all relevant issues are considered. Gathers the appropriate facts and evidence in order to make decisions effectively, using a structured approach. Responds appropriately to unexpected events.
S.S005: Communicating and networking: Deals effectively with Client/customers/colleagues at every level of the organisation required by the role. Using strong interpersonal skills and communicating well through a range of media, using language that is meaningful to the recipient e.g. written reports, presentations, phone, face to face, email.  Communicates complex information clearly.  Listens actively to understand needs and adapts their style to the recipient.  Engages and networks proactively with Client/customers and colleagues, including senior management, as required, to deliver business outcomes.
S.S006: Teamwork and collaboration: Builds/maintains strong positive working relationships with Clients/colleagues/suppliers as appropriate. Consistently supports colleagues and collaborates to achieve results. Aware of their role within team, how their team fits within the business, and how the team’s decisions impact on others.  Takes a leadership role in the team when appropriate.
S.S007: Continuous improvement: Identifies opportunities to drive higher performance and improve service/processes delivered, including sharing, and looking outside of their team for best practice. Leads and/or contributes to development of specific changes through to implementation – this may include projects wider than their role.
S.S008: Developing self and others: Keeps up to date with sector relevant legal/regulatory changes, and alerts others to the implications where necessary. Seeks feedback and acts on it to improve their performance.  Builds their capability through ownership of their own development.  Acts as a role model, and supports others in their development as appropriate.

B.B001: Honesty and Integrity: Truthful, sincere and trustworthy in their actions. Shows integrity by doing the right thing, demonstrating the organisation’s Values.  Maintains appropriate confidentiality at all times.  Visible role model, and coaches others where appropriate. Follows and promotes appropriate organisational procedures and policies.
B.B002: Adaptability: Adapts positively to changing work priorities and patterns, and is flexible to the needs of the organisation. Demonstrates willingness to adapt approach to assist organisation and individuals to manage priorities as circumstances change, new tasks need to be done, or when business requirements change. Responds well to change.
B.B003: Resilience and Enthusiasm: Displays energy, determination and enthusiasm in the way they go about their role, dealing positively and with determination when setbacks occur, or when managing difficult situations. Stays positive under pressure, and shows tenacity to follow jobs through to completion.
B.B004: Innovation and Creativity: Demonstrates a curious and questioning approach in their work. Is creative, innovative and enterprising when seeking solutions to business needs, whilst understanding relevant boundaries e.g. financial services regulations.
B.B005: Attention to Detail: Handles tasks with thoroughness and accuracy. Takes ownership for the quality of work, and recognises and resolves errors.

K.K001: Funding Product Benefits: The detail of relevant finance products, how to structure them and create solutions that meet customer needs for private and/or business users. The legislative / compliance requirements and how to explain the customer’s contractual obligations.
K.K002: Business Understanding: The requirements to maintain controls over cash-flow, performance and achievements of targets in order to ensure the Motor Finance department is a critical income / cost centre.
K.K003: Buying / Selling Behaviours: Why customers buy, what motivates them to buy and typical types of customers.

Follow and maintain processes to ensure compliance whilst enabling customers to make informed decisions. Demonstrate they can follow a defined sales process.

S.S001: Customer Service: Deliver excellent service, meeting customer needs and delivering fair customer outcomes e.g. producing quotations or reports. Handle a range of situations including dealing with complaints appropriately.
S.S002: Delivering results: Deliver work that meets required quality, standards and targets, using the required systems and processes. Take the initiative to meet challenging individual and team performance measures in line with organisation policy, values, standards and legal/regulatory requirements. Plan and manage own workload effectively, responding to changes when necessary.
S.S003: Communication: Demonstrate good interpersonal skills, communicating effectively and appropriately with all stakeholders e.g. customers, Intermediary staff / management and Finance Company teams. Use a range of media to communicate well e.g. phone, email, letter, face to face, social media.
S.S004: Team working and collaboration: Consistently support colleagues and collaborate to achieve results. Build and maintain strong working relationships with colleagues and clients e.g. sales teams, customer service teams and all levels of management. Is aware of own role within team and impact on others.
S.S005: Personal Development: Proactively plans and records their own development in both current role and career through a range of methods e.g. workplace learning, professional courses. Seek feedback and act on it to improve their performance. Build their own capability through ownership of their own development, in consultation with their manager. Keep up to date with relevant changes.

B.B001: Integrity: Truthful, sincere and trustworthy in their actions. Shows integrity by doing the right thing, in line with employer policies/legislation/compliance etc. Maintains appropriate confidentiality at all times.

B.B004: Resilience: Displays energy and enthusiasm in the way they go about their role, dealing positively with setbacks when they occur. Stays positive under pressure.

K.K17: Your employer’s organisational context and systems and the impact on your practice.
K.K18: The concept of leadership and its application to practice.
K.K19: The requirements of the relevant professional body.
K.K20: Applicable safeguarding/health and safety legislation, policies and procedures.
K.K21: How to maintain your own personal safety and that of others in complex situations.
K.K22: Signs of harm, abuse and neglect and the importance of professional curiosity when these are observed and the appropriate risk assessment tools and processes to use.
K.K23: The role of the Social Worker and roles of others within safeguarding, appropriate to levels of skills and experience.
K.K24: The range of communication methods available to meet specific needs (both  verbal and non-verbal)
K.K25: The importance of the impact of verbal and non-verbal communication
K.K26: How communication skills affect the assessment of, and engagement with, individuals and their families/carers
K.K27: The range of factors that affect effective communication e.g., age, capacity, learning ability and physical ability.
K.K28: The full range of interpersonal skills required to work with other professionals and agencies.
K.K29: The different social and organisational contexts within which social work operates.
K.K30: The applicable legislation, policies, and procedures
K.K31: The principles of good recording and record keeping.
K.K32: The types, and benefits, of assistive technology
K.K33: How to use relevant software applications.
K.K34: IT data sharing protocols.
K.K35: The potential misuses of technology e.g., social media.

S.S1: Ensure professional ethical standards are developed, maintained and promoted.
S.S2: Take responsibility for your decisions and recommendations.
S.S3: Be aware of the impact of your own values on practice.
S.S4: Exercise authority as a Social Worker within the appropriate legal and ethical frameworks.
S.S5: Ensure the highest standard of person centred approach, so that people are treated with dignity and their rights, values and autonomy are respected.
S.S6: Practise in a non-discriminatory manner.
S.S7: Hear the views of people who use services, carers, their families and communities, recognise their expertise, and enable their views to have validity and influence.
S.S8: Promote the best interests of people who use services, carers, their families and communities.
S.S9: Work with people to enable them to make informed decisions and exercise their rights.
S.S10: Work to promote individual growth, development and independence.
S.S11: Undertake assessments of need and/or capacity.
S.S12: Initiate resolution of issues and use initiative.
S.S13: Gather, analyse, critically evaluate, and use research information and knowledge in your practice to develop an understanding of the individual’s situation.
S.S14: Make and receive referrals appropriately.
S.S15: Use social work methods, theories, and models to enable individuals to identify actions to achieve change and improve life opportunities.
S.S16: Manage and weigh up competing/conflicting values or interests to make reasoned professional judgement.
S.S17: Work within scope of practice as an autonomous professional.
S.S18: Maintain high standards of personal and professional conduct.
S.S19: Manage the physical and emotional impact of your practice.
S.S20: Identify and apply strategies to build professional and emotional resilience.
S.S21: Use supervision to support and enhance the quality of your practice.
S.S22: Maintain your own health and well-being.
S.S23: Recognise the need to manage workloads and resources effectively.
S.S24: Keep your skills, knowledge and ongoing professional development up to date.
S.S25: Show an awareness of current and relevant legislation.
S.S26: Use a range of research methodologies to inform your practice.
S.S27: Work in partnership with others
S.S28: Balance appropriate levels of autonomy within a complex system of accountability
S.S29: Respond appropriately to unexpected situations, identify and challenge practices which present a risk to, or from, people you are working with, their carers or others in order to uphold professional requirements.
S.S30: Respond appropriately to signs of harm, abuse and neglect.
S.S31: Establish and maintain personal and professional boundaries.
S.S32: Follow health and safety policies and procedures.
S.S33: Communicate in English at the level required by Social Work England.
S.S34: Communicate your role and purpose sensitively and clearly, using appropriate language and methods.
S.S35: Communicate in a way which is engaging, respectful, motivating, and effective, even when dealing with conflict or resistance to change.
S.S36: Exercise professional curiosity.
S.S37: Have difficult conversations with empathy.
S.S38: Demonstrate effective interpersonal skills.
S.S39: Engage with individuals and their families/carers and sustain effective relationships in order to effect change
S.S40: Engage effectively in inter-professional and inter-agency working to achieve positive outcomes.
S.S41: Support networks, groups, and communities to meet needs and outcomes.
S.S42: Maintain accurate and complete records in accordance with applicable legislation, protocols, and guidelines.
S.S43: Prepare formal reports in line with legislation, policies, and procedures.
S.S44: Critically reflect on/review practice and record the outcomes of reflection appropriately.
S.S45: Present reports in formal settings.
S.S46: Use technology to manage your work.
S.S47: Use technology to communicate appropriately.
S.S48: Maintain individuals’ information security and protect data.
S.S49: Advise people on how to use assistive technology.
S.S50: Promote the use of technology to achieve better outcomes.

B.B1: Communicate openly, honestly and accurately. Listen to people and apply professional curiosity to evaluate and assess the information needed to provide quality advice, support, or care.
B.B2: Treat people with compassion, dignity and respect and work together to empower positive change.
B.B3: Adapt approach according to the situation and context.
B.B4: Commit to continuous learning within social work, with curiosity and critical reflection.
B.B5: Adhere to the Social Work England Standards of Conduct.

K.K1: The importance of continuing professional development throughout own career.
K.K2: The importance of safeguarding, signs of abuse and relevant safeguarding processes.
K.K3: What is required of them by the Health and Care Professions Council, including but not limited to the Standards of conduct, performance and ethics.
K.K4: That relationships with service users, carers and others should be based on mutual respect and trust.
K.K5: The importance of valid consent.
K.K6: The importance of capacity in the context of delivering care and treatment.
K.K7: The scope of a professional duty of care.
K.K8: Legislation, policies and guidance relevant to own profession and scope of practice.
K.K9: The effect of legislation on the delivery of care.
K.K10: The importance of own mental and physical health and wellbeing strategies in maintaining fitness to practise.
K.K11: How to take appropriate action if own health may affect own ability to practise safely and effectively, including seeking help and support when necessary..
K.K12: The need for active participation in training, supervision and mentoring in supporting high standards of practice, and personal and professional conduct, and the importance of demonstrating this in practice.
K.K13: Equality legislation and how to apply it to own practice.
K.K14: The duty to make reasonable adjustments in practice.
K.K15: The characteristics and consequences of barriers to inclusion, including for socially isolated groups.
K.K16: That regard to equality, diversity and inclusion needs to be embedded in the application of all HCPC standards and across all areas of practice.
K.K17: When disclosure of confidential information may be required.
K.K18: The principles of information and data governance and the safe and effective use of health, social care and other relevant information.
K.K19: The need to ensure confidentiality is maintained in all situations in which service users rely on additional communication support, such as interpreters or translators.
K.K20: The concepts of confidentiality and informed consent extend to all mediums, including illustrative clinical records such as photography, video and audio recordings and digital platforms.
K.K21: The characteristics and consequences of verbal and non-verbal communication and recognise how these can be affected by difference of any kind including, but not limited to, protected characteristics, intersectional experiences and cultural differences.
K.K22: The need to support the communication needs of service users and carers, such as through the use of an appropriate interpreter.
K.K23: The need to provide service users or people acting on own behalf with the information necessary in accessible formats to enable them to make informed decisions.
K.K24: The values, beliefs, culture, behaviours and interests of service users and carers, through interview and personal discussion.
K.K25: The principles and practices of other health and care professionals and systems and how they interact with own profession.
K.K26: The need to build and sustain professional relationships as both an autonomous practitioner and collaboratively as a member of a team.
K.K27: The qualities, behaviours and benefits of leadership.
K.K28: That leadership is a skill all professionals can demonstrate.
K.K29: The need to engage service users and carers in planning and evaluating diagnostics, and therapeutic interventions to meet own needs and goals.
K.K30: The need to work with those who provide services in and across different sectors, in order to best meet service user’s needs.
K.K31: The need to adopt an approach which centres on the service user and establish appropriate professional relationships in order to motivate and involve the service user in meaningful occupation.
K.K32: The value of enabling and empowering service users, with the aim of enhancing their access to all services and opportunities available to them.
K.K33: Group dynamics and roles.
K.K34: The need to capitalise, where appropriate, on the dynamics within groups and communities in order to harness the motivation and active involvement of participants.
K.K35: The value of reflective practice and the need to record the outcome of such reflection to support continuous improvement.
K.K36: The value of multi-disciplinary reviews, case conferences and other methods of review.
K.K37: The value of gathering and using data for quality assurance and improvement programmes.
K.K38: The structure and function of the human body, together with knowledge of physical and mental health, disease, disorder and dysfunction relevant to their profession.
K.K39: The role(s) of other professions in health and social care and how they may relate to the role of occupational therapist.
K.K40: The structure and function of health and social care systems and services in the UK.
K.K41: The occupational nature of human beings and how they function in everyday activities such as self-care, productivity and leisure and their changing needs during the lifecycle.
K.K42: The inter-relationship between the person, their environment and their chosen occupation, barriers and enablers in this system and how to change each component as part of rehabilitation.
K.K43: The effect of occupational alienation, dysfunction deprivation and injustice, and the importance of restoring and facilitating opportunities to achieve occupational wellness.
K.K44: Activity and occupation and their relation to and effect on, health, wellbeing and function as part of occupational formulation, diagnosis and therapeutic use of occupation.
K.K45: The theoretical basis of, and the variety of approaches to, assessment, planning, intervention and evaluation that focus on occupational outcomes.
K.K46: The need to identify and assess diverse occupational, physical, psychological, cognitive, cultural and environmental needs and problems of service users and carers.
K.K47: The use of the current philosophical models for occupational therapy that focuses on service users and holistic person-centred care, and a person’s ability to participate in occupations, taking into account physical, biological, psychological and social factors and the environmental context.
K.K48: The concept of teaching and learning including teaching self-management strategies to service users and carers.
K.K49: A range of research methodologies relevant to own role.
K.K50: The value of research to the critical evaluation of practice.
K.K51: The need to consider the assessment of the health, social care, employment and learning needs of service users including the need for risk assessment and positive risk taking.
K.K52: The need to agree the goals and priorities of intervention in relation to occupational needs in partnership with service users, basing such decisions on assessment results.
K.K53: The scientific theories, concepts, principles and professional frameworks underpinning occupational therapy practice.
K.K54: The need to meet the emotional, social, psychological, cognitive and physical health-based occupational needs of service users across a range of practice areas and how this can vary across a range of practice areas.
K.K55: The need to maintain the safety of themselves and others, including service users, carers and colleagues.
K.K56: Relevant health and safety legislation and local operational procedures and policies.
K.K57: Appropriate moving and handling techniques.
K.K58: The role of the profession in health promotion, health education and preventing ill health.
K.K59: How social, economic and environmental factors, wider determinants of health, can influence a person’s health and well-being.

S.S1: Identify the limits of own practice and when to seek advice or refer to another professional or service.
S.S2: Recognise the need to manage own workload and resources safely and effectively, including managing the emotional burden that comes with working in a pressured environment.
S.S3: Keep own skills and knowledge up to date.
S.S4: Maintain high standards of personal and professional conduct.
S.S5: Engage in safeguarding processes where necessary.
S.S6: Promote and protect the service user’s interests at all times.
S.S7: Respect and uphold the rights, dignity, values, and autonomy of service users, including own role in the assessment, diagnostic, treatment and/or therapeutic process.
S.S8: Maintain high standards of care in all circumstances.
S.S9: Exercise a duty of care.
S.S10: Obtain valid consent, which is voluntary and informed, has due regard to capacity, is proportionate to the circumstances and is appropriately documented.
S.S11: Apply legislation, policies and guidance relevant to own profession and scope of practice.
S.S12: Recognise the power imbalance which comes with being a health care professional, and ensure it is not  abused for personal gain.
S.S13: Identify own anxiety and stress and recognise the potential impact on own practice.
S.S14: Develop and adopt clear strategies for physical and mental self-care and self-awareness, to maintain a high standard of professional effectiveness and a safe working environment.
S.S15: Recognise that they are personally responsible for, and must be able to, justify their decisions and actions.
S.S16: Use own skills, knowledge and experience, and the information available, to make informed decisions and / or take action where necessary.
S.S17: Make reasoned decisions to initiate, continue, modify or cease treatment or the use of techniques or procedures, and record the decisions and reasoning appropriately.
S.S18: Make and receive appropriate referrals, where necessary.
S.S19: Exercise personal initiative.
S.S20: Demonstrate a logical and systematic approach to problem solving.
S.S21: Use research, reasoning and problem solving skills when determining appropriate actions.
S.S22: Respond appropriately to the needs of all different groups and individuals in practice, recognising this can be affected by difference of any kind including, but not limited to, protected characteristics, intersectional experiences and cultural differences.
S.S23: Recognise the potential impact of own values, beliefs and personal biases, which may be unconscious, on practice and take personal action to ensure all service users and carers are treated appropriately with respect and dignity.
S.S24: Make and support reasonable adjustments in owns and others’ practice.
S.S25: Actively challenge barriers to inclusion, supporting the implementation of change wherever possible.
S.S26: Adhere to the professional duty of confidentiality.
S.S27: Respond in a timely manner to situations where it is necessary to share information to safeguard service users, carers and/or the wider public and recognise situations where it is necessary to share information to safeguard service users, carers and/or the wider public.
S.S28: Use effective and appropriate verbal and non-verbal skills to communicate with service users, carers, colleagues and others.
S.S29: Communicate in English to the required standard for the profession.
S.S30: Work with service users and/or own carers to facilitate the service user’s preferred role in decision-making, and provide service users and carers with the information they may need where appropriate.
S.S31: Modify own means of communication to address the individual communication needs and preferences of service users and carers, and remove any barriers to communication where possible.
S.S32: Use information, communication and digital technologies appropriate to own practice.
S.S33: Actively listen to a service user’s occupational narrative and analyse the content in order to plan for the future.
S.S34: Keep full, clear and accurate records in accordance with applicable legislation, protocols and guidelines.
S.S35: Manage records and all other information in accordance with applicable legislation, protocols and guidelines.
S.S36: Use digital record keeping tools, where required.
S.S37: Work in partnership with service users, carers, colleagues and others.
S.S38: Contribute effectively to work undertaken as part of a multi-disciplinary team.
S.S39: Identify anxiety and stress in service users, carers and colleagues, adapting own practice and providing support where appropriate.
S.S40: Identify own leadership qualities, behaviours and approaches, taking into account the importance of equality, diversity and inclusion.
S.S41: Demonstrate leadership behaviours appropriate to own practice.
S.S42: Act as a role model for others.
S.S43: Promote and engage in the learning of others.
S.S44: Ensure intervention reviews are informed by changes in service user’s circumstances.
S.S45: Recognise the involvement of public, private and voluntary sector providers in the delivery of health, care and other services which affect occupational performance.
S.S46: Facilitate group work in order to maximise support, learning and change within groups and communities.
S.S47: Work in appropriate partnership with service users in order to evaluate the effectiveness of occupational therapy intervention.
S.S48: Recognise and evaluate the potential of occupational therapy in new and emerging areas of practice.
S.S49: Engage in evidence-based practice.
S.S50: Gather and use feedback and information, including qualitative and quantitative data, to evaluate the responses of service users to own care.
S.S51: Monitor and systematically evaluate the quality of practice, and maintain an effective quality management and quality assurance process working towards continual improvement.
S.S52: Participate in quality management, including quality control, quality assurance, clinical governance and the use of appropriate outcome measures.
S.S53: Evaluate care plans or intervention plans using recognised and appropriate outcome measures, in conjunction with the service user where possible, and revise the plans as necessary.
S.S54: Demonstrate awareness of the principles and applications of scientific enquiry, including the evaluation of treatment efficacy and the research process.
S.S55: Apply the theoretical concepts underpinning occupational therapy, including concepts of:- anatomy- physiology- pathology- human development- ergonomics- biomechanics- psychology- sociology- occupational science.
S.S56: Apply the theoretical concepts underpinning occupational therapy to inform the understanding of physical, emotional and mental health.
S.S57: Analyse activity and occupation and their relation to and effect on, health, wellbeing and function as part of occupational formulation, diagnosis and therapeutic use of occupation.
S.S58: Demonstrate awareness of physical, attitudinal, social, economic, educational, environmental and work-related policies and services and their effect on people within a diverse society.
S.S59: Recognise the value of the diversity and complexity of human behaviour through the exploration of different physical, psychological, cognitive, environmental, social, emotional and spiritual perspectives.
S.S60: Demonstrate awareness of the origins and development of occupational therapy, including the evolution of the profession towards the current emphasis on autonomy and empowerment of individuals, groups and communities.
S.S61: Support others with the facilitation of teaching and learning including teaching self-management strategies to service users and carers.
S.S62: Change own practice as needed to take account of new developments, technologies and changing contexts.
S.S63: Gather appropriate information.
S.S64: Analyse and critically evaluate the information collected.
S.S65: Select and use appropriate assessment techniques and equipment.
S.S66: Undertake and record a thorough, sensitive, and detailed assessment.
S.S67: Undertake or arrange investigations as appropriate.
S.S68: Conduct appropriate assessment or monitoring procedures, treatment, therapy or other actions safely and effectively.
S.S69: Critically evaluate research and other evidence to inform own practice.
S.S70: Engage service users in research as appropriate.
S.S71: Select and use relevant standardised and non-standardised assessment techniques and observation to gather information about the service user’s functional and occupational abilities, occupational performance and participation, taking account of the cultural and environmental context.
S.S72: Formulate specific and appropriate care or case management plans including the setting of timescales.
S.S73: Select as appropriate, the specific occupations and activities for use as therapeutic media, taking into account the particular needs of service users.
S.S74: Use the scientific theories, concepts, principles and professional frameworks underpinning occupational therapy practice.
S.S75: Demonstrate awareness of the broad range of occupations and activities that can be used in intervention and how these should reflect the individual’s occupational needs and preferences.
S.S76: Select or develop therapeutic media and environments, and adapt these as appropriate to meet service user’s needs, build on their abilities and enhance their occupational performance and participation.
S.S77: Demonstrate awareness of relevant health and safety legislation and comply with all local operational procedures and policies.
S.S78: Work safely, including being able to select appropriate hazard control and risk management, reduction or elimination techniques in a safe manner and in accordance with health and safety legislation.
S.S79: Select appropriate personal protective equipment and use it correctly.
S.S80: Establish safe environments for practice, which appropriately manages risk.
S.S81: Apply appropriate moving and handling techniques.
S.S82: Empower and enable individuals, including service users and colleagues, to play a part in managing own health.
S.S83: Engage in occupational health, including being aware of immunisation requirements.

B.B1: Use a personalised approach to care enabling the people you work with to bring about change in order to achieve their chosen occupational goals.
B.B2: Enable occupational justice and be an agent for change through actively anti-discriminatory practice with a diverse range of individuals, groups, and communities.
B.B3: Practice improving the economic, social and environmental sustainability of health and care services recognising the interdependence and ensuring belonging for the people we work with.
B.B4: Be professionally curious and evidence-informed to enable lifelong professional and personal development, including exhibiting leadership behaviours.
