K.K31: The need to engage service users and carers in radiotherapy pre-treatment planning treatment and follow up and where relevant in planning and evaluating their interventional procedures.
K.K32: The value of reflective practice and the need to record the outcome of such reflection to support continuous improvement.
K.K33: The value of multi-disciplinary reviews, case conferences and other methods of review.
K.K34: The value of gathering and using data for quality assurance and improvement programmes.
K.K35: The principles and regulatory requirements for quality control and quality assurance as they apply to their profession.
K.K36: The quality improvement processes in place relevant to their profession.
K.K37: The structure and function of the human body, together with knowledge of physical and mental health, disease, disorder and dysfunction relevant to their profession.
K.K38: The role(s) of other professions and services in health and social care and understand how they may relate to the role of the therapeutic radiographer.
K.K39: The structure and function of health and social care systems and services in the UK.
K.K40: The role of the therapeutic radiographer and other operators in the promotion of health and health education in relation to public health, healthy living and health screening for disease detection.
K.K41: The harms and benefits of population and targeted health screening.
K.K42: The radiobiological principles on which the practice of radiography is based.
K.K43: The concept of risk vs benefit with regards to ionising radiation and non-ionising radiation, acknowledging this will differ depending on modality, and communicate this with service users, taking into consideration service user judgement.
K.K44: The philosophy and principles involved in the practice of their profession.
K.K45: The principles of ionising radiation production, interaction with matter, beam modification, administration of radionuclides and radiation protection.
K.K46: The physical and scientific principles on which image formation using ionising and non-ionising radiation is based.
K.K47: Radiation dosimetry and the principles of dose calculation.
K.K48: The theoretical basis underpinning service user assessment prior to and during their procedure.
K.K49: The capability, applications and range of equipment used in their profession.
K.K50: The concepts and principles involved in the practice of their profession and how these inform and direct clinical judgement and decision making.
K.K51: The pharmacology of drugs used in their profession.
K.K52: The legislation, principles and methods for the safe and effective administration of drugs used in their profession.
K.K53: The mechanisms for the administration of drugs, including intravenous and oral contrast agents.
K.K54: The principles of the safe storage, transportation and disposal of medicinal products used in relation their profession.
K.K55: The different communication needs, anatomy and disease processes and their manifestation in children.
K.K56: The structure and function of the human body in health and disease, including:– regional and cross-sectional anatomy of the head, neck, limbs, thorax, pelvis and abdomen– common pathologies and mechanisms of disease with a concentration on cancer, histology, haematology and the lymphatic and immune systems.
K.K57: Oncology, the pathophysiology of solid and systemic malignancies– epidemiology– aetiology– the management and effect of cancer.
K.K58: The physiological signs and symptoms, clinical investigations and diagnostic procedures that result in referral for radiotherapy.
K.K59: The biochemical science of radiation pathophysiology.
K.K60: The influence of adjuvant and neoadjuvant treatment including surgery and chemotherapy on radiotherapy dose prescription, timing of radiotherapy and post radiotherapy complications.
K.K61: The principles of nuclear medicine and radionuclide procedures in radiotherapy guided planning and radionuclide therapies and theragnostics.
K.K62: A range of research methodologies relevant to own role.
K.K63: The value of research to the critical evaluation of practice.
K.K64: The need to maintain the safety of themselves and others, including service users, carers and colleagues.
K.K65: Relevant health and safety legislation and local operational procedures and policies.
K.K67: The correct principles and applications of disinfectants, methods for sterilisation and decontamination, and for dealing with waste and spillages correctly.
K.K68: The role of the profession in health promotion, health education and preventing ill health.
K.K69: How social, economic and environmental factors, wider determinants of health, can influence a person’s health and well-being.

S.S1: Identify the limits of own practice and when to seek advice or refer to another professional or service.
S.S2: Recognise the need to manage own workload and resources safely and effectively, including managing the emotional burden that comes with working in a pressured environment.
S.S3: Keep own skills and knowledge up to date.
S.S4: Maintain high standards of personal and professional conduct.
S.S5: Engage in safeguarding processes where necessary.
S.S6: Promote and protect the service user’s interests at all times.
S.S7: Respect and uphold the rights, dignity, values, and autonomy of service users, including own role in the assessment, treatment or therapeutic process.
S.S8: Recognise that relationships with service users, carers and others should be based on mutual respect and trust, and maintain high standards of care in all circumstances.
S.S9: Obtain valid consent, which is voluntary and informed, has due regard to capacity, is proportionate to the circumstances and is appropriately documented.
S.S10: Exercise a duty of care.
S.S11: Apply legislation, policies and guidance relevant to own profession and scope of practice.
S.S12: Recognise the power imbalance which comes with being a health care professional, and ensure it is not for personal gain.
S.S13: Practise in accordance with current legislation governing the use of ionising and non-ionising radiation for medical and other purposes.
S.S14: Identify own anxiety and stress and recognise the potential impact on own practice.
S.S15: Develop and adopt clear strategies for physical and mental self-care and self-awareness, to maintain a high standard of professional effectiveness and a safe working environment.
S.S16: Recognise that they are personally responsible for, and must be able to, justify their decisions and actions.
S.S17: Use own skills, knowledge and experience, and the information available, to make informed decisions and/or take action where necessary.
S.S18: Make reasoned decisions to initiate, continue, modify or cease treatment or the use of techniques or procedures, and record the decisions and reasoning appropriately.
S.S19: Make and receive appropriate referrals, where necessary.
S.S20: Exercise personal initiative.
S.S21: Demonstrate a logical and systematic approach to problem solving.
S.S22: Use research, reasoning and problem-solving skills when determining appropriate actions.
S.S23: Respond appropriately to the needs of all different groups and individuals in practice, recognising this can be affected by difference of any kind including, but not limited to, protected characteristics, intersectional experiences and cultural differences.
S.S24: Recognise the potential impact of own values, beliefs and personal biases, which may be unconscious), on practice and take personal action to ensure all service users and carers are treated appropriately with respect and dignity.
S.S25: Make and support reasonable adjustments in own and others’ practice.
S.S26: Actively challenge barriers to inclusion, supporting the implementation of change wherever possible.
S.S27: Adhere to the professional duty of confidentiality.
S.S28: Respond in a timely manner to situations where it is necessary to share information to safeguard service users, carers and/or the wider public and recognise situations where it is necessary to share information to safeguard service users, carers and/or the wider public.
S.S29: Use effective and appropriate verbal and non-verbal skills to communicate with service users, carers, colleagues and others.
S.S30: Communicate in English to the required standard for the profession.
S.S31: Work with service users and / or own carers to facilitate the service user’s preferred role in decision-making, and provide service users and carers with the information they may need where appropriate.
S.S32: Modify own means of communication to address the individual communication needs and preferences of service users and carers, and remove any barriers to communication where possible.
S.S33: Use information, communication and digital technologies appropriate to own practice.
S.S34: Formulate and provide information and support for service users about their treatment and/or imaging process and procedures, with regular reappraisal of their information needs as appropriate.
S.S35: Advise other healthcare professionals about the relevance and application of radiotherapy and, where relevant, imaging modalities to the service user’s needs.
S.S36: Provide appropriate information and support for service users throughout their radiotherapy treatment and care.
S.S37: Keep full, clear and accurate records in accordance with applicable legislation, protocols and guidelines.
S.S38: Manage records and all other information in accordance with applicable legislation, protocols and guidelines.
S.S39: Use digital record keeping tools, where required.
S.S40: Work in partnership with service users, carers, colleagues and others.
S.S41: Contribute effectively to work undertaken as part of a multi-disciplinary team.
S.S42: Identify anxiety and stress in service users, carers and colleagues, adapting own practice and providing support where appropriate.
S.S43: Identify own leadership qualities, behaviours and approaches, taking into account the importance of equality, diversity and inclusion.
S.S44: Demonstrate leadership behaviours appropriate to own practice.
S.S45: Act as a role model for others.
S.S46: Promote and engage in the learning of others.
S.S47: Demonstrate awareness of the need to empower service users to participate in the decision-making processes related to their profession.
S.S48: Demonstrate awareness of the need to encourage, support and mentor staff at all practitioner levels.
S.S49: Demonstrate awareness of roles and responsibilities where work is delegated and how this applies in practice.
S.S50: Interpret and act upon information from other healthcare professionals and service users, in order to maximise health gain whilst minimising risks to the service user, such as from radiation dose.
S.S51: Engage in evidence-based practice.
S.S52: Gather and use feedback and information, including qualitative and quantitative data, to evaluate the responses of service users to own care.
S.S53: Monitor and systematically evaluate the quality of practice, and maintain an effective quality management and quality assurance process working towards continual improvement.
S.S54: Participate in quality management, including quality control, quality assurance, clinical governance and the use of appropriate outcome measures.
S.S55: Evaluate care plans or intervention plans using recognised and appropriate outcome measures, in conjunction with the service user where possible, and revise the plans as necessary.
S.S56: Demonstrate awareness of the principles and applications of scientific enquiry, including the evaluation of treatment efficacy and the research process.
S.S57: Demonstrate awareness of the philosophy and the development of the profession of radiography to inform understanding of current practice.
S.S58: Apply the principles of ionising radiation production, interaction with matter, beam modification, administration of radionuclides and radiation protection.
S.S59: Distinguish between normal and abnormal appearances on images.
S.S60: Demonstrate awareness of the current developments and trends in the science and practice of radiography.
S.S61: Recognise and respond to adverse or abnormal reactions to medications used in relation to their profession.
S.S62: Demonstrate awareness of the principles of Artificial Intelligence (AI) and deep learning technology, and it’s application to practice.
S.S63: Change own practice as needed to take account of new developments, technologies and changing contexts.
S.S64: Gather appropriate information.
S.S65: Analyse and critically evaluate the information collected.
S.S66: Select and use appropriate assessment techniques and equipment.
S.S67: Undertake and record a thorough, sensitive, and detailed assessment.
S.S68: Undertake or arrange investigations as appropriate.
S.S69: Conduct appropriate assessment or monitoring procedures, treatment, therapy or other actions safely and effectively.
S.S70: Critically evaluate research and other evidence to inform own practice.
S.S71: Engage service users in research as appropriate.
S.S72: Formulate specific and appropriate management plans including the setting of timescales.
S.S73: Assess, monitor and care for the service user across the pathway of care relevant to their profession.
S.S74: Undertake and record a thorough, sensitive and detailed clinical assessment, selecting and using appropriate techniques and equipment.
S.S75: Use physical, graphical, verbal and electronic methods to collect and analyse information from a range of relevant sources including service user’s clinical history, diagnostic images and reports, pathological tests and results, dose recording and treatment verification systems.
S.S76: Interrogate and process data and information gathered accurately in order to conduct the procedures most appropriate to the service user’s needs.
S.S77: Appraise image information for clinical manifestations and technical accuracy, and take further action as required.
S.S78: Manage complex and unpredictable situations including the ability to adapt planned procedures.
S.S79: Operate radiotherapy equipment safely and accurately.
S.S80: Check that equipment is functioning accurately and within the specifications, and to take appropriate action in the case of faulty functioning and operation.
S.S81: Select and explain the rationale for radiographic techniques and immobilisation procedures appropriate to the service user’s physical and disease management requirements.
S.S82: Position and immobilise service users correctly for safe and accurate procedures.
S.S84: Assist in the construction of appropriate immobilisation, including beam modifying, devices, individualised to the specific needs of each service user and the treatment regime prescribed.
S.S85: Identify organs at risk (OAR) on images to provide information for radiotherapy treatment planning.
S.S86: Calculate does across a range of radiation modalities, including photons, protons and electrons, utilising a treatment planning system and verify this accordingly with a record and verification system.
S.S87: In relation to radiotherapy planning :-- support service users in understanding radiation exposure, risk and benefit associated with radiation exposure and doses in relation to their imaging examination.- perform multimodality imaging techniques and the image registration process, and where appropriate contrast agent examinations, demonstrating appropriate care to service users and their carers.- manipulate exposure and image recording parameters to optimal effect- perform standard Computed Tomographic (CT) and assist in performing Magnetic Resonance (MR) planning procedures- use to best effect the processing and related technology supporting imaging systems.
S.S88: Generate a treatment plan and verify treatment parameters ensuring optimal radiotherapy prescription delivery.
S.S89: Use to best effect the image processing and related technology, including computer-based imaging systems for radiotherapy purposes.
S.S90: Perform the full range of radiotherapy processes and techniques accurately and safely.
S.S91: Manage and assist with fluoroscopic procedures, including those requiring the use of contrast agents.
S.S92: Interpret and evaluate images obtained during radiotherapy planning and treatment, taking appropriate action to optimise accuracy dose delivery to the target volume.
S.S93: Check that the OAR dose is as planned/prescribed during treatment.
S.S94: Localise the target volume precisely in relation to external surface and anatomical reference markings using a range of techniques including computed tomography and magnetic resonance imaging for the purpose of radiotherapy planning and delivery.
S.S95: Critically evaluate and interpret the radiation prescription in such a way that radiotherapy is delivered accurately and reproducibly.
S.S96: Recognise changing signs, symptoms and progression of disease, and make appropriate decisions not to treat or to review further before proceeding with treatment, including reviewing treatment imaging information.
S.S97: Demonstrate awareness of relevant health and safety legislation and comply with all local operational procedures and policies.
S.S98: Work safely, including being able to select appropriate hazard control and risk management, reduction or elimination techniques in a safe manner and in accordance with health and safety legislation.
S.S99: Select appropriate personal protective equipment and use it correctly.
S.S100: Establish safe environments for practice, which appropriately manages risk.
S.S101: Apply appropriate moving and handling techniques.
S.S102: Ensure the physical safety of all individuals in the therapeutic work environment, especially with regard to radiation safety and high-strength magnetic fields.
S.S103: Use basic life support techniques and be able to deal with clinical emergencies.
S.S104: Empower and enable individuals, including service users and colleagues, to play a part in managing own health.
S.S105: Engage in occupational health, including being aware of immunisation requirements.

B.B1: Act with honesty, integrity, openness, and respect at all times.
B.B2: Act with empathy and compassion by being considerate of others’ feelings, especially when making decisions.
B.B3: Remain calm and resilient when dealing with distressing and emotionally challenging situations and be able to manage own emotional responses.
B.B4: Behave respectfully and be non-judgemental by engaging with people in non-discriminatory ways.
B.B5: Be willing to share knowledge, be self-motivated, proactive, adaptable and reliable to deliver the best possible care.
B.B6: Be decisive and act with confidence when interacting with patients, staff and the public.
B.B7: Practise self-awareness by understanding own emotions, limitations, strengths, weaknesses, and recognising the impact of personal interactions on others.

K.K1: All Health and Safety Law that pertains to the safe management and passage of a commercial vessel, including certification
K.K2: All appropriate terminology, uses and applications of rope work, access, egress, safe movement and lifting gear.
K.K3: How a passage plan is executed with all the leadership skills necessary for that voyage to be undertaken safely, securely, and with consideration to the environment.
K.K4: The processes for safely and securely anchoring a vessel.
K.K5: All International Maritime Organization Conventions e.g. SOLAS, MARPOL, COSWP and IRPCS needed so that a passage plan may be executed appropriately. Company procedures on the bridge. IALA Buoyage ‘A’
K.K6: Find a position and plan a route using chart work, electronic navigational aids, publications and navigational tools. To appraise available information, plot, execute and monitor progress whilst recognising when pro-active actions are most suitable.
K.K7: Understand relevant regulations and company policies to follow in the event of an emergency. The role of the M.A.I.B, HSE and Local Port procedures
K.K8: All aspects of ship manoeuvrability including steering systems and their functions, steering by compass and the responsibilities and implications of locks, docks and bridges
K.K9: How the use of propulsion and steering systems varies in different tidal, fluvial and sea-going conditions including poor weather.
K.K10: How to use maritime publications and charts to plan a voyage. Know how to access the digital resources available to keep such publications up to date.
K.K11: A basic understanding of the principles of IT on board including electronic chart  systems, GNSS, and Radar, including their limitations, performance checks and other supporting navigational aids that are commonplace. Principles of communication between vessels and amongst crews.
K.K12: Legislation, codes and guidance in vessel operations and vessel construction. To know how to keep and execute maintenance plans in regards to deck machinery and to affect ship stability. To understand factors that affect seaworthiness and survivability.
K.K13: General engineering practice and procedures, including pumping and associated control systems and electrical equipment, including digital reporting and recording systems for planned and ongoing maintenance. Shows awareness of UK clean maritime plan as part of the government’s Maritime 2050 long-term strategy. Including awareness of new technologies relating to hybrid vessels and alternative fuels.
K.K14: How to apply legislation and guidance to prevent pollution of the marine environment and has knowledge of policies and procedures for vessel operations.
K.K15: Equity, diversity and inclusion legislation in the workplace.
K.K16: Business objectives considerations: Efficiency, costs, customer satisfaction, Minimising risks to operation.
K.K17: Forecast weather, recognise and interpret deteriorating conditions and review a passage plan accordingly whilst taking action on board to be able to complete the voyage or reach a safe haven.
K.K18: Demonstrates a knowledge of the content and application of the International and National Regulations for Preventing Collisions at Sea as appropriate for vessels in inland waterways, harbours and coastal sea waters (as would be applicable to Boatmasters Licence (BML)
K.K19: Information Technology systems including word processing and email software

S.S1: Take responsibility for the preparation of the vessel prior to going to sea including safe means of access to and egress from the boat and Life saving and fire fighting equipment checks.
S.S2: Respond and manage all emergency situations on board including the operation of emergency equipment coordination with the coastguard and other vessels (Sarco) and the liaison with DP and third party organisations.
S.S3: Handle a vessel in a high traffic density environment in tidal or non-tidal conditions including in locks and in the vicinity of weirs, bridges and other fixed infrastructure.
S.S4: Manoeuvre vessels safely with a variety of propulsion systems including single screw, twin screw, variable pitch, Schottel and other propulsion configurations
S.S5: Ensure vessels are operated and remain in a safe and seaworthy condition in accordance with regulations and company procedures. Maintain machinery including main engines, deck equipment and lifesaving and firefighting equipment in full operational condition.
S.S6: Uses information and digital technology within own organisation. Complies with GDPR and Cyber Security Regulations and policies.
S.S7: Apply team working principles.
S.S8: Comply with International, National and local regulations, and health and safety regulations, standards and guidance
S.S9: Build, manage and lead your team onboard, developing work relationships and performance management skills.
S.S10: Uses verbal communication techniques both at sea and onshore, adapting style and use of terminology to suit the audience.
S.S11: Lead procedures including planning and communications in the event of an emergency situation.
S.S12: Able to manoeuvre a vessel in all conditions to respond to all types of emergency (e.g. man overboard).
S.S13: Use appropriate mooring configurations and equipment in relation to the prevailing tidal, weather conditions and vessel design characteristics.
S.S14: Select a suitable anchorage and executing the procedure as required. To use an anchor(s) to aid in manoeuvring.
S.S15: Prepare for meteorological impacts affecting the safe management and passage of a commercial vessel.
S.S16: Navigate shallow and height restrictive navigations using knowledge of depths, air drafts, vessel dimensions and tide predictions in tidal waters.
S.S17: Offer emergency assistance to help another vessel running into danger. To arrange an emergency tow or offer some powered assistance to reach safety.
S.S18: Adjusts appropriately the passage plan of the vessel under their command, taking account of safety, time and business objectives.
S.S19: Take charge of navigational watch, be able to establish risk of collision and take effective action to avoid collision.
S.S20: Monitors business objective considerations and commercial issues, adjusting actions accordingly.

B.B1: Follows safe working practices and promotes a safety culture.
B.B2: Committed to continued professional development (CPD) to maintain and enhance competence in their own area of practice.
B.B3: Takes ownership of work.
B.B4: Calm and effective under pressure
B.B5: Actively protects the marine environment from pollution.
B.B6: Role-models own organisations equity and diversity requirements
B.B7: Actively protects the marine environment from pollution.

K.K1: The broad range of creative processes involved in creative design, and the importance of being aware of all the principal design disciplines including the different perspectives, approaches or schools of thought and theories that underpin them.
K.K2: The broad range of industry standard creative digital design tools and methods, their strengths and weaknesses and how to apply them.
K.K3: How to produce and maintain a portfolio of creative digital work.
K.K4: The audio, visual and verbal conventions through which sounds, images and words take meaning when creating design concepts for online and non-screen based services and installations.
K.K5: Contemporary audio, video and film technologies, including motion graphics and voice control systems and how to apply them as part of the digital media authoring process.
K.K6: How to present compelling pitches and presentations of creative digital design proposals.
K.K7: How to interpret and work to a design brief to deliver creative digital design solutions.
K.K8: How to critically analyse, interpret and evaluate complex information and concepts for creative digital design to deliver compelling creative digital designs that achieve a determined business outcome.
K.K9: The fundamental concepts of digital design/user interface design using front end frameworks and contemporary digital technologies for different digital media platforms.
K.K10: How to design responsive user centred solutions, including the technical opportunities and restrictions afforded by different platforms, the impact of design on performance and interaction design across a range of media platforms.
K.K11: The fundamentals of user experience and user research, including user personas: needs, wants, pains, prior experiences, user-centric design, customer journeys, calls to action, information architecture and navigation, site flow and information foraging: using wireframes to prototype user journeys.
K.K12: The fundamentals of different digital platform applications including the principles of mobile first design; including when it is, and isn't, relevant, and for multiple responsive breakpoints.
K.K13: The legal, ethical, regulatory, accessibility and governance frameworks which affect the development of creative design artefacts and applications and how to apply them.
K.K14: Contemporary methodologies such as agile and open innovation and how to apply them.
K.K15: The definition and fundamentals of branding; brand strength and positivity, the attributes of effective brands (excellent, different, compelling, authentic), being aware of brand meanings and values, effective brand positioning, visual tone, messaging, editorial voice and brand articulation.
K.K16: How to keep up to date with emerging platforms, environments and technologies.
K.K17: How to work with data and apply the creative digital approaches to represent complex data.
K.K18: The key features of the main UK laws, including Data Protection Act and General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) and the opportunities and constraints offered by data and what is feasible / needed for the current project / task.
K.K19: How to undertake real world 3D digital capture and process 3D data.
K.K20: The principles, controls and workflow inherent in building, texturing, animating and rendering 3D models, including XYZ Space, scaling and transforms, and the range of uses in the real world (e.g. Architecture, Construction, Games, Immersive, Automotive, Aerospace, Medical, Space etc.).
K.K21: The fundamentals, origins, application and potential of contemporary immersive technologies, such as AR (Augmented Reality), VR (Virtual Reality and XR (Mixed Reality), including psychology aspects of 3D environments, and the uses in the real world (including Internet of Things, Architecture, Construction, Games, Retail,Automotive,Aerospace, Medical etc.).

S.S1:
Apply creative design processes to all creative digital development projects, including online services and installations and
non-screen-based media, producing designs compelling to customers / users and to leverage the multitude of digital touch points including, but limited to; mobile, tablet, laptop, desktop,
wearable, consoles and cars.
S.S2: Create designs using industry standard methods; Typography, Digital Art, Video and Special Effects, Rendering, Animation, 3D Graphics and Digital Photography.
S.S3: Create eye-catching creative design concepts and prototypes for presentation to clients using industry standard tools and advanced methods to address design problems set by clients.
S.S4: Create 'production' ready digital media assets including, but not limited to designs, style guides, animations, videos, story boards, images, audio to achieve a determined business outcome.
S.S5: Select, refine, adapt and apply industry standard contemporary creative digital design technologies, and working with data to develop creative design solutions. Apply and leverage data e.g. personalisation,  behavioural, location, weather, in design concepts and solutions.
S.S6: Communicate design concepts and solutions to team members, designers, brand managers, product leads, marketing directors, commercial and sales in a strong and compelling manner, evaluating the outputs and methods used and their implications.
S.S7: Use design concept digital art to deliver creative design sales pitches to clients.
S.S8: Brief and work with third parties such as photographers, model makers, agencies and data professionals.
S.S9: Produce and maintain a portfolio of creative digital work.
S.S10: Keep abreast of industry developments, creative trends and innovations, knowing what’s emerging, and when and when to act to make cost effective use of new and emerging tools and technologies.
S.S11: Input into market/user research and UX briefs and work with their outputs.
S.S12: Collaborate between team members, clients and working with a range of stakeholders including market researchers, user experience professionals, user researchers, developers, photographers, model makers, brand managers, product leads, marketing directors, commercial, sales and communication specialists.
S.S13: Review and build accessibility requirements into all interactive creative digital media solutions.
S.S14: Work effectively both individually and collaboratively as part of a multidisciplinary team, with methodologies such as agile, building and maintaining positive relationships with team members, clients and other stakeholders.
S.S15: Lead and manage end to end creative digital design projects in line with governance policies, meeting deadlines and managing client expectations for different environments, including agile.
S.S16: Deliver well-designed, high quality creative digital solutions including prototyping skills within organisational brand guidelines.
S.S17: Design interactive artefacts using industry standard immersive technology techniques.
S.S18: Be able to plan and undertake own development and life-long learning to keep abreast of emerging technologies and techniques to review how these are best.

B.B1: A passion for creating content for creative and digital media.
B.B2: A strong work ethic and commitment to meet the standards required.
B.B3: Support for others in developing an appropriate balance of leadership and technical skills.
B.B4: Recognition and compliance with equality and diversity in the workplace.
B.B5: Demonstrates resilience, accountability and flexibility especially when managing difficult situations.

K.K1: The underlying scientific techniques, principles, theories, concepts and terminology applied to scientific investigations and experiments relevant to the role application.
K.K2: How scientific techniques and research methodologies can be applied to inform the development of science and technology.
K.K3: Requirements for the development and validation of analytical methodology, including the theoretical basis and limitations, and selection of appropriate equipment used for scientific applications.
K.K4: Sampling method selection appropriate to the application.
K.K5: How statistical techniques can be applied to inform experimental design, test hypotheses, evaluate results, and draw evidence based conclusions.
K.K6: The principles of implementing new processes based on a variety of information and data sources from inside and outside the organisation.
K.K7: Knowing when, how and why to escalate appropriately and recognising limits of scope of practice.
K.K8: Project management principles and stakeholder management.
K.K9: How to identify and recommend appropriate workflow improvements or scientific solutions.
K.K10: The requirements of internal and external stakeholders.
K.K11: The internal and external regulatory environment and codes of conduct pertinent to the occupation, including, quality, health and safety, ethics, sustainability and the environment.
K.K12: The business environment in which the company operates and its impact on the role.
K.K13: Methods available to evaluate own performance and knowledge, and how to set personal development goals aligned to organisational objectives.
K.K14: Tools and Information sources available to keep up to date with advances in technology and scientific working practices.
K.K15: How to select communication methods to influence scientific, non-scientific, specialist and technical audiences.
K.K16: How to determine the scope and success criteria for complex scientific investigations and procedures.
K.K17: Techniques to identify and test the theory of probable root cause of problems, verify functionality and implement solutions.
K.K18: Calibration techniques and strategies to ensure accuracy, limits of detection, precision, standardisation and repeatability in measurements.
K.K19: Methods used to assess and prioritise stakeholders affected by your business or project.
K.K20: Approaches to supporting sustainability by reference to emissions targets, environmental management systems and personal responsibilities.
K.K21: How other roles in the organisation can contribute to achieving sustainability goals.
K.K22: The importance of keeping up to date with recent developments and applications of science relevant to the role, and the benefits that can be realised through their adoption across the wider business.
K.K23: Safe working practices, in terms of managing operational hazards and chemical hazards including reactivity, toxicity, stability and radioactivity.
K.K24: Basic principles of classical and statistical thermodynamics, kinetics, quantum mechanics and spectroscopy and their application to the solution of theoretical and practical problems to wider topics in chemistry.
K.K25: Likely and potential synthetic products, bond-forming reactions, mechanistic pathways, stereochemistry and reactivity.
K.K26: Purification, isolation and characterisation strategies, in the light of physicochemical properties and trends of elements and compounds.
K.K27: Periodic trends in chemistry, the consequences of these trends on reactivity, behaviour and physical properties and how these trends can be applied to the solution of theoretical and practical problems.
K.K28: Atomic and molecular orbitals and their conceptual applications to structure, bonding and reaction pathways.
K.K29: Knowledge of a range of inorganic and organic substances, compounds and materials and their uses.
K.K30: Computational techniques and tools, and how they can be used to aid further understanding and insight of chemical structure, bonding and reactivity.
K.K31: The principles of scaling up chemical processes to meet the demands of their real-world applications in a reproducible, safe and sustainable manner.
K.K32: Mathematics methods and theories applied in physics including complex numbers, vector calculus, matrices with eigenvalues and eigenvectors, probability and statistical techniques.
K.K33: The fundamental areas of physics including electromagnetism, quantum and classical mechanics, statistical physics and thermodynamics, wave phenomena and the properties of matter.
K.K34: The application of the fundamental principles of physics relevant to role. For example atomic physics, environmental physics, fluids, hard and soft condensed matter, materials, medical physics, nuclear and particle physics, optics, and plasmas, as well as the application of physics to other disciplines.
K.K35: Safe operational working practices and physics hazards, including electricity and electrical equipment, electrostatic generators, lasers, ionizing and non-ionising radiation, compressed gases, pressurised and vacuum systems, mechanical and thermal, cryogenic hazard, clean room, magnetic fields.
K.K36: Use of instruments in physics studies relevant to their role (such as measuring, optical, electrical, heat and thermodynamic equipment, semiconductor devices, gravity, wave and sound related apparatus).
K.K37: How numerical analysis and computational physics can be applied to solve problems.
K.K38: How to use computer programming to solve physics based problems.
K.K39: Knowledge of the structure and dynamics of the universe, including composition and behaviour down to subatomic particles.
K.K40: The biochemical processes that occur inside cells, and their regulation as well as intra and interspecific interactions, behaviour and stochastic processes.
K.K41: Concepts and application of the classification of species across all kingdoms of life.
K.K42: Application of biotechnologies.
K.K43: The processes and mechanisms of life, from molecular to cellular, and from organism to ecosystem including molecular and cellular, physiological processes, health and ageing.
K.K44: The structure and function of prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells, including cell division, cell cycle, cell specialisation and cooperation.
K.K45: The structure and function of biologically important molecules and the molecular events that govern their function.
K.K46: Safe working practices, in terms of managing and assessing the risk of operational and biological hazards including handling and disposal of chemicals, lawful handling of living organisms and their entities and safe handling of radioisotopes.
K.K47: Computational techniques and tools, and how they can be used to aid understanding and insight of biological data sets.
K.K48: The use of suitable sampling techniques including sample preparation.

S.S1: Identify and apply scientific approaches to solve problems, support investigations and recommend any follow-up experiments.
S.S2: Evaluate scientific experiments using an appropriate level of qualitative and quantitative numerical analysis.
S.S3: Critically analyse data, relevant literature and input from colleagues to inform investigations.
S.S4: Analyse, interpret and evaluate scientific data and present the results in written and oral form.
S.S5: Manage record keeping to ensure data integrity, traceability, confidentiality and adherence with appropriate regulatory requirements.
S.S6: Promote, enact, document, and monitor the application of quality standards, safe working practices and compliance with risk management systems relevant to the role.
S.S7: Identify opportunities to challenge assumptions and apply influencing skills to make improvements, make new proposals and build on existing ideas.
S.S8: Manage work plan, prioritise tasks and evaluate progress against objectives.
S.S9: Contribute to the development of technical projects across multi-skilled teams and share best practice across the wider business.
S.S10: Conduct complex (chemical, or physical, or biological) scientific investigations in accordance with defined company procedures and legislative requirements.
S.S11: Identify and implement continuous performance improvement using appropriate techniques.
S.S12: Ensure work meets environmental, risk management and sustainability requirements and regulations of the business sector.
S.S13: Evaluate own performance and knowledge, and set personal development goals.
S.S14: Troubleshoot equipment selection and implement sustainable solutions.
S.S15: Identify and collaborate with key stakeholders and provide technical support and advice where required.
S.S16: Identify how other roles in the organisation can contribute to achieving sustainability goals.
S.S17: Select and apply equipment, techniques, and methods to ensure reliable and accurate results.
S.S18: Undertake practical analyses and measurement and communicate the outcomes using appropriate terminology and mathematical and graphical notation.
S.S19: Use appropriate digital solutions for the collection and analysis of data, and the retrieval of appropriate information.
S.S20: Use communication methods suited to objective and audience.
S.S21: Use theoretical models, often involving approximations, to make quantitative predictions.
S.S22: Apply chemical concepts and theories to investigations.
S.S23: Design, develop and carry out chemical science investigations.
S.S24: Recommend synthetic pathways to target-compounds and design experimental activities to test predictions.
S.S25: Design and deploy purification, isolation and characterisation strategies, taking account of physicochemical properties of elements and compounds.
S.S26: Design, develop and carry out qualitative and quantitative sample preparation, chemical measurement, and analysis.
S.S27: Critically compare the results of theoretical or computational modelling with those from experiment and observation.
S.S28: Apply physics theories to solve problems that cross scientific discipline boundaries.
S.S29: Apply computational techniques to analysis and model physical behaviour.
S.S30: Apply physics concepts and theories to investigations.
S.S31: Design, develop and carry out physics based investigations.
S.S32: Use mathematical techniques and theories to solve physics problems.
S.S33: Use computer programming software to solve physics based problems.
S.S34: Critically analyse current and emerging developments in the biosciences and the contribution of Biosciences to the wider industry.
S.S35: Apply Biological concepts and theories to investigations.
S.S36: Design, develop and carry out biological investigations
S.S37: Perform tasks related to sample preparation and culturing techniques.
S.S38: Use of laboratory automation systems.
S.S39: Perform sampling with consideration for sample variation.

B.B1: Takes personal responsibility for sustainable outcomes and encourages others to achieve sustainability goals.
B.B2: Leads by example and advocates for change.
B.B3: Embraces equality, diversity and inclusion
B.B4: Uses initiative to develop and identify new ideas.
B.B5: Committed to and encourages collaborative working.
B.B6: Acts autonomously with self motivation.
B.B7: Takes responsibility for the critical evaluation of own capabilities and development.
B.B8: Upholds company and professional values, and demonstrating integrity, and respect for confidentiality.
B.B9: Committed to the adoption and promotion of safe working practices.

K.K1: different sources of data and intelligence and their strengths and limitations
K.K2: methods used to determine existing and future population health needs and how they are monitored (eg: within a local authority population) and for specific communities (eg: children and young people; people with life-long conditions such as diabetes; people living in prison)
K.K3: the complexities of health inequalities, how they occur, how they are measured, monitored and reported, and the impact on different societies and populations
K.K4: how to analyse and interpret the data generated when tracing patterns of disease and ill-health, and how this data is reported for communities and populations (eg: incidence and prevalence)
K.K5: the challenges of measuring health and wellbeing and health improvement, setting performance indicators for health–related programmes and services, and the importance of evaluation, audit and quality assurance
K.K6: methods used to engage with the public and local communities in line with prevailing evidence of effectiveness (eg: asset based approaches to community development), recognising the role of agency, autonomy, power and control
K.K7: how public health and wellbeing interventions are designed, planned and developed, informed by the best available evidence (about what works, and what doesn’t), and how to evaluate these interventions to track effectiveness and ensure continuing improvement
K.K8: the theories underpinning behavioural science and its relevance to a range of health and wellbeing outcomes, for individuals, communities, and populations, and the appropriate use of behaviour change techniques and tools for different groups, in different settings with different opportunities (e.g.: helping people to make healthy dietary choices; supporting people living with addiction;  informing and minimising risk-taking behaviours;  heeding health promoting messages and advice)
K.K9: the determinants of health, including the wider and social determinants; how these impact on the health and wellbeing of individuals, communities and populations; and the evidence-based approaches to consider when taking action to achieve better health and wellbeing outcomes for all, while ensuring that the needs of the most vulnerable are met
K.K10: infectious disease (incubation, transmission, hygiene, infection control, personal behaviours); how infectious disease can spread in a range of settings; and the prevention and management strategies and protocols used to manage the spread of infectious disease, including the identification, reporting and tracking of notifiable diseases, and current legislation
K.K11: the range of environmental hazards that can pose a risk to the public’s health, including chemical contamination and radiation, and the systems in place to prevent, report, monitor and manage these risks
K.K12: the challenges and successes of disease prevention and management programmes such as immunisation and screening, for whole populations, or specific groups, and the pre-requisites for these programmes to be most effective
K.K13: systems supporting emergency planning and response, the organisations responsible, and the role of public health
K.K14: how to mitigate risks to the public’s health using different approaches such as legislation, licensing, policy, education, fiscal measures
K.K15: how to critically appraise the evidence base, interpret its relevance and application to practice, and how it informs the basis of key public health messages and advice, and national guidance
K.K16: how public health interventions are designed, planned and developed, informed by the best available evidence (about what works, and what doesn’t), and when the evidence base is evolving
K.K17: how to evaluate public health interventions to track effectiveness; ensure continuing improvement; and contribute to the evidence base
K.K18: the ways in which health and care organisations and professionals are held to account for the quality and effectiveness of services and interventions, and how they keep themselves informed of new developments in technologies, treatments and therapies to improve efficacy
K.K19: the complexities of measuring health improvement, setting performance indicators for programmes and services, and the importance of evaluation, audit and quality assurance
K.K20: how health and care services are designed, planned and developed, informed by the best available evidence, and how they are monitored to track effectiveness and ensure continuing improvement
K.K21: how policy and strategy is formed and developed, nationally and locally
K.K22: how policies and strategies are used to implement change, improve services, and secure wide engagement in public health issues and outcomes, the social determinants of health, and the different government and local government departments that influence these
K.K23: the extent to which national and local policies, strategies and service planning impact on health and wellbeing
K.K24: the theories underpinning behavioural science and its relevance to a range of health and wellbeing outcomes, and how it informs the development of policy, strategy, and the planning and implementation of public health interventions and services
K.K25: the principles of partnership working and collaboration, and the skills and approaches necessary to do this successfully
K.K26: ways to determine the organisational relationships and inter-dependencies in the local field of operation; the boundaries of jurisdiction, accountability, and purpose; and where the opportunities for collaboration might lie
K.K27: the different approaches to evaluating the effectiveness of existing partnerships
K.K28: how health and care services are funded, the organisations responsible for the delivery of different services, and the tensions that arise from the availability of finite resources
K.K29: the complexities of measuring health improvement when setting performance indicators for programmes and services in specifications and agreements
K.K30: the legislation and regulations relating to procurement, commissioning models and theories of commissioning for outcomes
K.K31: how progress and deliverables against outcomes and processes agreed through a contract, service level agreement, or memorandum of understanding are managed and monitored
K.K32: the different organisations and agencies, both nationally and locally, that play a key role in the public’s health, their statutory duties and remits, and the leadership and other roles where key accountabilities lie
K.K33: ways to determine and overcome the challenges of making the business case for prevention against competing, and more immediate priorities, for key agencies positioned to promote and protect the public’s health
K.K34: the legislative framework and decision making, administrative and reporting processes that support political and democratic systems (e.g.: unitary and tiered local government structures and service accountabilities)
K.K35: a critical awareness of the political and other tensions that impact on public service provision, and public protection, and ways to encourage a focus on the interests of the public’s health (including helping individuals and communities to have more control over decisions that affect them and promote health equity, equality and justice)
K.K36: the theories and approaches that underpin the leadership and management culture within decision making organisations, including systems leadership approaches
K.K37: healthcare management systems and their applicability to public health systems (locally, regionally)
K.K38: leadership and management approaches that support the influencing role of public health practitioners in situations where they have some statutory authority, (e.g. through the statutory requirement for local authority public health teams to provide public healthcare advice to NHS commissioners), but also in situations when they do not have this authority
K.K39: approaches to transformational change management within health and care systems
K.K40: techniques and methods for communicating sometimes complex messages to different segments of the population to support choices and decisions made at an individual level that impact on health and wellbeing
K.K41: the techniques and methods used to engage with the public and local communities, and to consult with the public in a meaningful way, with an understanding of best practice in the use and application of these methods
K.K42: theories underpinning health education in addressing the wider determinants of health, and the promotion of health for individuals, groups and communities
K.K43: the relevance and application of behavioural science, and the use of social marketing techniques, to deliver accessible messages to different segments of populations and communities, to support behaviours and choices that are made at an individual or community level that impact on health, wellbeing, and healthy life expectancy
K.K44: the principles of programme and project management, and an understanding of the models of project and programme management being used to deliver public health activity
K.K45: the principles of corporate governance and accountability, and a recognition and understanding of the governance frameworks in place within your own organisation and through which public health action is delivered
K.K46: the importance of evaluation, audit and quality assurance
K.K47: how to identify opportunities to build capacity through the specialised and wider public health workforce to strengthen approaches to prevention, understanding  the impact of local system capacity on the delivery of public health services and interventions
K.K48: the principles of corporate governance and accountability, and the importance of engaging with the organisational governance frameworks through which public health action is delivered
K.K49: economic analysis of services and interventions using tools and techniques to determine cost effectiveness, return on investment and value for money to inform decision making
K.K50: the factors that affect the ability of individuals to learn and develop within a community or work environment; and how to provide accessible learning opportunities that enable people to develop both their own learning and the learning of others
K.K51: theories informing the development of public health ethics and law, how these compare to medical ethics, and the different ethical theories that support different public health challenges
K.K52: making the best use of a range of ethical frameworks to support decision making when faced with different ethical dilemmas in practice
K.K53: a developed area of expertise in a particular area of public health (eg: the management of risk behaviours such as smoking, inactivity, poor nutrition; infectious disease prevention and control such as sexually transmitted infections, Tuberculosis (TB), or anti-microbial resistance (AMR); the implementation of immunisation and vaccination programmes; mental health and wellbeing; public health intelligence etc.)
K.K54: a critical insight into the accessibility and availability of health, care and other public services for different groups in the population and the barriers that may exist to prevent the public from successfully receiving the care and support that they need.

S.S1: analyse a range of data sets to draw informed conclusions about local public health needs and use of services, contributing to strategic needs assessments and operational service planning
S.S2: provide and present public health information, analysis, interpretation and insight to support decision making, business planning, policy and strategy development, performance monitoring, and quality assurance
S.S3: manage data and information in compliance with policy and protocol and assess and manage risks associated with using and sharing data and information, data security and intellectual property
S.S4: work with communities to facilitate their engagement and participation in needs assessments, service design and delivery, including action to improve access to, and navigation of, local services
S.S5: recognise where health inequalities prevail; determine how they will influence the planning of public health interventions and services; and to apply the evidence appropriately to make the most impact in alleviating these inequalities
S.S6: apply behavioural insights and information about community needs to support healthy choices that individuals might wish to make, and provide individuals, groups and communities with the capabilities to make changes to their behaviours (eg: increasing levels of physical activity), in the context of a wider set of interventions and actions
S.S7: participate effectively in the assessment and management of outbreaks, incidents, and single cases of contamination and communicable disease, locally and across boundaries
S.S8: apply protocol and standard operating procedures related to the management of infectious disease, and in response to civil emergencies and unscheduled events, within the scope of the role
S.S9: help to identify, analyse and manage the local impact of longer-term hazards and risks to health that may play out at a global, national or local level
S.S10: communicate the risks and benefits of immunisation and screening programmes to a range of audiences eg: health professionals, parents, people from a range of cultures
S.S11: use appropriate methods to access and appraise evidence gained through systematic methods and through engagement with the wider research community
S.S12: critique published and unpublished research, synthesise the evidence and draw appropriate conclusions
S.S13: report and advise on the implications of the evidence base for the most effective practice; to define problems and shape solutions; and to help in the delivery of value for money
S.S14: present an evidence based narrative that is suitably adjusted to inform different types of audiences with different levels of health literacy
S.S15: use and adapt appropriate research techniques and principles to evaluate local services and interventions to contribute to the local evidence-base for effectiveness
S.S16: monitor, evaluate and disseminate (report) the impact of health and care projects, services and interventions, including quality impact
S.S17: engage stakeholders (including service users) in service design and development, to deliver accessible and equitable person-centred services
S.S18: implement standards, protocols and procedures, incorporating national ‘best practice’ guidance into local delivery systems
S.S19: appraise and implement government-led policies and strategies locally (eg: ensuring the equitable and effective implementation of winter fuel schemes to prevent deaths from cold; the implementation of the NHS Health Check programme)
S.S20: assess the impact and benefits of health and other policies and strategies on the public’s health and health inequalities (eg: using health impact assessment approaches or tools)
S.S21: develop or implement actions plans, with, and for, specific groups and communities, to deliver outcomes identified in strategies and policies
S.S22: evaluate one’s own interpersonal skills, and adapt to different situations through a developed proficiency in negotiation, influencing, diplomacy, mediation, facilitation
S.S23: use appropriate methods to establish and sustain effective working relationships with local partners in order to bring about positive outcomes in the health and wellbeing of the local population
S.S24: work collaboratively with colleagues across a broad range of partnership organisations to identify local needs, agree priorities, and deliver on action plans for joint health improvement programmes or services across the area
S.S25: interact with other specialists and colleagues to improve health and reduce health inequalities through the development, monitoring and review of public health programmes, including the commissioning and delivery of these programmes
S.S26: ensure, where relevant, that tendering and procurement processes are managed appropriately and within policy and legislative requirements when commissioning, planning or providing a range of services to bring about improvements in the public’s health (including the drawing up and negotiation of service specifications and performance indicators)
S.S27: facilitate positive contractual relationships understanding how disagreements and changes can be managed within legislative and operational frameworks
S.S28: Operate appropriately within the decision making, administrative and reporting processes that support political and democratic systems
S.S29: present a compelling case for action to improve health and wellbeing and reduce inequalities, using appropriate methods to capture and interpret the evidence
S.S30: use appropriate community engagement techniques to support individuals and communities to have more control over decisions that affect them while promoting health equity, equality and justice
S.S31: respond constructively to political and other tensions while encouraging a focus on the interests of the public’s health
S.S32: to engender trust by acting reliably with integrity, consistency and purpose
S.S33: work effectively within a team that is collectively responsible for leading and managing areas of public health business including the management or supervision of staff, resources or finances
S.S34: use appropriate leadership skills to engage others, build relationships, manage conflict, encourage contribution and sustain commitment to deliver shared objectives
S.S35: adapt to change, manage uncertainty, solve problems, and align clear goals with lines of accountability in complex and unpredictable environments
S.S36: communicate complex information and concepts, including health outcomes, inequalities and life expectancy, to a range of different audiences using different methods (e.g.: exploiting the evolving opportunities of digital capability and dependency, and the associated need for some audiences to be assisted with digital communications)
S.S37: work with communities to facilitate their engagement and participation in action to improve access to, and navigation of, local services and interventions, and to improve health literacy where it is a barrier to access
S.S38: apply a range of tools and technologies to improve health literacy where it is creating a barrier to accessing services and information
S.S39: manage public perception of health risks or solutions, and convey key messages using a range of media processes
S.S40: consult, and listen to individuals, groups and communities likely to be affected by planned intervention or change
S.S41: engage stakeholders and work collaboratively with colleagues across a broad range of partnership organisations to identify local needs, agree priorities and develop action plans for joint public health programmes across boundaries
S.S42: support the development, monitoring and review of public health programmes; identifying risks to delivery and the appropriate risk and issue reporting mechanisms; and re-assessing delivery schedules and methods to respond constructively to change
S.S43: develop projects and plans with key stakeholders and partner agencies to address local public health needs and bring about improvements in identified areas. Lead or contribute to delivery of these projects as appropriate within the scope of your role
S.S44: identify the resources needed to develop and deliver new public health activity, or to sustain public health services and interventions, and how these resources might be sourced
S.S45: help to determine shared priorities and action plans for public health programmes working with colleagues both from within the same organisation and across a range of other agencies
S.S46: build capacity and capability across the field of operation to increase impact and effectiveness of public health programmes and interventions, engaging with professional colleagues, and other groups of workers or volunteers who make up the wider public health workforce
S.S47: use appropriate tools and methods to appraise ethical tensions and to make decisions that promote ethical practice
S.S48: think and write reflectively about one’s own practice, lessons learned, and things that can be done differently for better outcomes eg: to keep a reflective log as part of one’s continuing professional development
S.S49: demonstrate professional characteristics throughout the course of one’s work eg: engendering trust; assuring confidentiality where appropriate; understanding one’s own limitations in terms of capability, accountability and expertise; the addressing of risks and issues in a timely and appropriate manner
S.S50: demonstrate awareness of personal impact on others, both fellow professionals, external partners and members of the public

B.B1: promotes the ability of others to make informed decisions
B.B2: acknowledges the importance of data confidentiality and disclosure and use of data sharing protocols
B.B3: acts in ways that are consistent with legislation, policies, governance frameworks and systems
B.B4: recognises peoples expressed beliefs and preferences
B.B5: promotes equality and diversity
B.B6: recognises the need for, and makes use of, opportunities for personal and others’ development while recognising different approaches and preferences for learning
B.B7: recognises ethical dilemmas or issues and addresses them appropriately e.g.:  through the use of ethical frameworks
B.B8: recognises and acts within the limits of own competence seeking advice when needed
B.B9: contributes to the development and improvement of own and others' practice in public health by the application of evidence in improving own area of work
B.B10: objectively and constructively contributes to reviewing the effectiveness of own area of work
B.B12: continually develops own practice by reflecting on own behaviour and role, identifying where improvements can be made

K.K1: The basic structure and function of the human body and function of the musculoskeletal system relevant to prosthetics and orthotics and required for the role. There will also be an understanding of the main conditions that lead to the use of prosthetic or orthotic devices such as Diabetes, Multiple Sclerosis and Cerebral Palsy. In particular features of conditions such as neuropathy and phantom limb pain which may directly affect the wearing of devices.
K.K2: The structure and properties of materials and their appropriate application to prosthetic or orthotic hardware and clinical practice; a range of modelling techniques; how to measure and adjust a model.
K.K3: How manufacturing machinery and equipment works and how to maintain it to a high standard at all times, when and where to report faults; quality control and how it applies in prosthetic or orthotic design and delivery.
K.K4: Record the relevant manufacturing details in line with department/company’s policies; your responsibilities and duties; the limits of your competence and authority and why it is important to work in ways agreed by your employer.
K.K5: The patient, measurement, material and component information required to manufacture the device that has been requested.
K.K6: Computer aided design technology relevant to the manufacturing of related devices.
K.K7: Equality, diversity and inclusion legislation; how to treat people with dignity and respect; understanding how disability affects and influences prosthetic and orthotic management; the requirement to adapt practice to meet the needs of individuals dealing with emotional needs due to a range of circumstances and experiences.
K.K8: Recognise how communication affects engagement of an individual and to be able to apply a range of communication techniques; taking into account an individual’s emotional state, age, capacity, learning and physical ability, culture, ethnicity and religious beliefs.
K.K9: Ways to assist the communication requirements of individuals; including recognising the need to use interpersonal skills to encourage the active participation of individuals.
K.K10: The limits of own prosthetic or orthotic technical practice and when to seek advice.
K.K11: Informed consent and how to secure it across the age range and in line with cognitive ability; the importance of providing individuals with information that allows them to make informed decisions and safely manage their condition and supplied devices.
K.K12: How duty of care, medical ethics, safeguarding of adults and children apply to own practice.
K.K13: Health and safety legislation, policies and procedures; ways to assess risks that ensures safety and security of the prescribed device.
K.K14: How to maintain confidentiality and apply the principles of information governance.
K.K15: The importance of managing records and data in accordance with legislation, protocols  local procedures and best practice.
K.K16: The need to participate in training, supervision and mentoring.
K.K17: Quality guidelines and device design principles that apply to individual devices; incident reporting and escalation.
K.K18: The need to keep skills and knowledge up to date and the importance of career-long learning; the value of reflection on practice and the need to record the outcome of such reflections
K.K19: Models and theories of support and supervision and how to safely delegate in line with legal and professional guidelines.
K.K20: The consequences of own actions, attitudes and behaviour
K.K21: How to assess and reflect upon own capabilities and limitations

S.S1: Manufacture the prescribed device using manual and computer aided technologies and correct materials to agreed timescales.
S.S2: Ensure the devise is fit for use and purpose and complies with the manufacturer/department/company quality assurance and legal requirements.
S.S3: Test that the device is working correctly and modify if required.
S.S4: Support patient/carer to maintain the device and check for breakages and faults.
S.S5: Check that the completed device meets the prescription provided.
S.S6: Act within the limits of own competence and authority.
S.S7: Use and maintain manufacturing machinery to carry out duties.
S.S8: Identify problems with the manufacturing machinery and report any defects.
S.S9: Provide on-going support to prosthetists and orthotists and in some instances patients\ carers.
S.S10: Apply a range of communication interventions and interpersonal skills to support individuals receiving prosthetic or orthotic care.
S.S11: Utilise IT systems to read and record information, and where appropriate using IT systems as part of the manufacturing process.
S.S12: Work safely and within competency level.
S.S13: Undertake risk assessments using a range of techniques
S.S14: Work collaboratively in partnership with other team members, individuals and carers.
S.S15: Obtain informed consent for prosthetic or orthotic care within your scope of practice.
S.S16: Safeguard individuals, including vulnerable adults and children.
S.S17: Safeguard confidential information relating to individuals at all times.
S.S18: Maintain records that are fit for purpose that comply with employer’s protocols and process them accordingly.
S.S19: Comply with local and national standards regarding reporting of medical device failures and incidents.
S.S20: Work as part of a team, seek help and guidance when you are not sure, escalate concerns in a timely manner to the correct person.
S.S21: Support or supervise colleagues as required, delegating well- defined tasks appropriately.

B.B1: Be respectful of others their beliefs, culture, needs, values and privacy.
B.B2: Takes ownership of work.
B.B3: Puts safety first for themselves and others.

K.K1: The importance of continuing professional development throughout own career.
K.K2: The value of therapy in developing insight and self-awareness through their own personal experience, including personal therapy.
K.K3: The importance of safeguarding, signs of abuse and relevant safeguarding.
K.K4: What is required of them by the Health and Care Professions Council, including but not limited to the Standards of conduct, performance and ethics.
K.K5: The importance of valid consent.
K.K6: The importance of capacity in the context of delivering care and treatment.
K.K7: The scope of a professional duty of care.
K.K8: Legislation, policies and guidance relevant to own profession and scope of practice.
K.K9: The role of the art, music or drama therapist in different settings.
K.K10: That the obligation to maintain fitness to practise includes engagement in own arts-based process.
K.K11: The importance of own mental and physical health and wellbeing strategies including personal therapies, in maintaining fitness to practise.
K.K12: How to take appropriate action if own health may affect own ability to practise safely and effectively, including seeking help and support when necessary.
K.K13: The need for active participation in training, supervision and mentoring in supporting high standards of practice, and personal and professional conduct, and the importance of demonstrating this in practice.
K.K14: Equality legislation and how apply it to own practice.
K.K15: The duty to make reasonable adjustments in practice.
K.K16: The characteristics and consequences of barriers to inclusion, including for socially isolated groups.
K.K17: That regard to equality, diversity and inclusion needs to be embedded in the application of all HCPC standards, across all areas of practice.
K.K18: When disclosure of confidential information may be required.
K.K19: The principles of information and data governance and be aware of the safe and effective use of health, social care and other relevant information.
K.K20: The need to ensure confidentiality is maintained in all situations in which service users rely on additional communication support, such as interpreters or translators.
K.K21: That the concepts of confidentiality and informed consent extend to all mediums, including illustrative clinical records such as photography, video and audio recordings and digital platforms.
K.K22: That the concepts of confidentiality and informed consent extend to illustrative records such as paintings, digital images and other creative practice.
K.K23: The characteristics and consequences of verbal and non-verbal communication and how these can be affected by difference of any kind including, but not limited to, protected characteristics, intersectional experiences and cultural differences.
K.K24: The need to support the communication needs of service users and carers, such as through the use of an appropriate interpreter.
K.K25: The need to provide service users or people acting on own behalf with the information necessary in accessible formats to enable them to make informed decisions.
K.K26: The principles and practices of other health and care professionals and systems and how they interact with own profession.
K.K27: The need to build and sustain professional relationships as both an autonomous practitioner and collaboratively as a member of a team.
K.K28: The qualities, behaviours and benefits of leadership.
K.K29: That leadership is a skill all professionals can demonstrate.
K.K30: The need to engage service users and carers as equal partners in planning, co-design and evaluating diagnostics and assessment outcomes to meet their needs and goals.
K.K31: The role of arts therapists as an integral part of health and social care provision for service users and their integration with health and social care.
K.K32: The need to establish and sustain a therapeutic relationship within a creative and containing environment.
K.K33: The value of reflective practice and the need to record the outcome of such reflection to support continuous improvement.
K.K34: The value of multi-disciplinary reviews, case conferences and other methods of review.
K.K35: The role and value of ongoing clinical supervision in an arts therapy context.
K.K36: The value of gathering and using data for quality assurance and improvement programmes.
K.K37: The need to maintain the safety of themselves and others, including service users, carers and colleagues.
K.K38: Relevant health and safety legislation.
K.K39: The role of own profession in health promotion, health education and preventing ill health.
K.K40: How social, economic and environmental factors (wider determinants of health) can influence a person’s health and well-being.
K.K41: The lived experiences of wellness and illness, as well as the effects of social disablement and exclusion, and consider this alongside diagnostic knowledge relevant to their profession.
K.K42: The roles of other professions in health and social care and how they may relate to the role of arts therapists within the integrated teams that serve communities.
K.K43: The structure and function of health and social care systems and services in the UK.
K.K44: The importance of working in partnership with service users when carrying out research.
K.K45: The theoretical basis of, and the variety of approaches to, assessment and intervention.
K.K46: The psychological and cultural background to health, and be aware of influences on the service user – therapist relationship.
K.K47: The core processes in therapeutic practice that are best suited to service users’ needs and be able to engage these to achieve productive outcomes.
K.K48: The therapeutic relationship, including its limitations.
K.K49: How and why different approaches to the use of the arts in arts therapy and in other settings varies according to context and purpose.
K.K50: Theories of group work and the management of group process.
K.K51: Theories relevant to work with an individual.
K.K52: theories of:- human development- psychology- human communication and language development- the continuum of mental health, psychiatric assessment and treatment and self-help and social resources- disability/impairment and ways in which people experience themselves as having been disabled- impairments of social functioning- the principal psychotherapeutic interventions and their theoretical bases- the nature and application of other relevant interventions.
K.K53: Different methods of understanding the experience of service users, including diagnosis, specifically mental health and learning disability, and be able to critique these systems of knowledge from differed socio-cultural perspectives.
K.K54: That while art therapy has a number of frames of reference, that the arts therapist must adopt a coherent approach to their therapy, including the relationship between theory, research and practice and the relevant aspects of connected disciplines.
K.K55: The practice and process of visual art-making.
K.K56: The role of the physical setting and the art-making process in the physical and psychological containment of emotions.
K.K57: The role and function of the art object within the relationship between service user and art therapist
