S.S9: Maximising the potential for success by identifying, applying and positioning legislation, codes of practice and policies in a variety of situations including union recognition, collective bargaining, individual representation, and other areas.  Implement systems to confidently advise and support members as well as protect own union (for instance from risk of litigation).
S.S10: Delivers informal education and other learning opportunities that meet Trade Union objectives, choosing an appropriate development method tailored to the audience. This includes, but is not limited to, developing members to become representatives in their workplaces, maximising Trade Union presence and autonomy within the workplace setting.
S.S11: Adopts an appropriate leadership style in order to motivate, unify and strengthen Trade Union membership and self-sustaining workplaces within own jurisdiction. Manages setbacks whilst maintaining unity with members. Applies democratic principles in line with own Trade Union rulebook.
S.S12: Design, deliver and evaluate effective and engaging campaigns that win for members and create opportunities for growth. Bring Union policy to life, translating plans into realistic targets, objectives and time frames.
S.S13: Uses tact and diplomacy appropriately to mediate and reconcile different points of view and resolve problems whilst representing and/or advocating on behalf of members. Articulates the case to best effect by selecting persuasive arguments. Manages conflict and handles dissension calmly and confidently, upholding and maximising the reputation of the Trade Union at all times.  Identifies and organises collective responses where appropriate. Explains and defends own point of view; thinks clearly and rapidly under pressure and uncertainty.
S.S14: Adopts and adapts the appropriate negotiating styles and techniques to maximise the potential for successful bargaining (such as effective preparation, building support for the issue, being the legitimate voice of the workforce, adopting a strategic approach, identifying and applying leverage, knowing when to adapt the negotiating strategy, promoting beneficial change and reaching mutually agreeable solutions). Applies sound judgement in progressing and concluding negotiations with the ability to identify routes to a successful outcome.
S.S15: Applies sound judgement related to confidentiality, maintaining it where appropriate and ensures that procedures and legislation are followed.
S.S16: Maximises the organising potential in workplaces within their allocation by applying the principles, practices and methodologies of organising trade union workplaces taking into account own union's approach and strategy to organising. Ability to organise around a wide range of issues including but not limited to industrial issues, health and safety, and workplace learning.
S.S17: Identifies and addresses equality issues and opportunities. Develops and applies strategies that progress the Trade Union equality agenda and own union's equality priorities. Applies campaigning techniques to address inequality in the workplace and beyond.

B.B1: Demonstrates commitment to the TU ethos.  Exercises integrity, impartiality, honesty and diligence. Role models and champions positive behaviours that inspires others. Identifies and brings out and develops leadership skills in self and others.
B.B2: Has a pro-active approach to team working and inclusivity, shares knowledge and ideas and empower others.
B.B3: Acts in ways that are just and fair, promotes access and inclusion, adheres to relevant legal requirements and obligations and addresses and challenges inequality where encountered.
B.B4: Takes responsibility for own development and behaviour by identifying opportunities to improve.
B.B5: Shows resilience to achieve outcomes and remains motivated to enable others and is not deterred by the complexity or size of tasks.
B.B6: Actively prepares ahead of tasks.
B.B7: Challenges employers, stakeholders and members where necessary to achieve outcomes and to embed fairness.

K.K1: Understand British Psychological Society (BPS) Professional Code of Conduct, local and national policies and procedures that define scope of practice.
K.K2: Understand how to assess limits of professional boundaries and capacity and understand when to seek appropriate supervision/advice on practice and whom to refer to so as to ensure best care.
K.K3: Understand the principles of clinical supervision and how this provides a safe and supportive environment to reflect, review and discuss personal and professional responses to work.
K.K4: Understand principles of handling confidential information and knowing how and when to share this information for appropriate professional purposes and only with appropriate individuals, and as necessary with consent.
K.K5: Understand how to maintain knowledge of contemporary evidence-based practice through appropriate continued professional development.
K.K6: :Understand and recognise professional duty to challenge and report discriminatory behaviour.
K.K7: Understand responsibility for fulfilling and maintaining local and national information governance policies.
K.K8: Understand the need to maintain accurate clinical records and why all entries in clinical and practice records are dated, timed and signed.
K.K9: Understand policy and practice with regard to incident reporting within your organisation.
K.K10: Understand how to communicate confidential information.
K.K11: Understand the need for recording of patient consent, including verbal consent where appropriate, and the necessity of ensuring that consent is given for sharing of information for professional purposes.
K.K12: Understand how individual life experiences and life-events may be relevant, when taking an individual history for the purposes of specialist psychological assessment, to enable personalised psychological interventions.
K.K13: Understand cognitive functioning, possessing knowledge of causes and other factors which may determine performance, when conducting an assessment.
K.K14: Understand how to conduct both individualised psychological and cognitive assessments, utilising behavioural observation and measurement, use of self and other observation data, and incorporating data from formal and informal carers.
K.K15: Understand fundamentals of psychometric principles to guide the use of standardised assessment tools with specific populations.
K.K16: Understand how to analyse and appraise the range of cognitive-behavioural and other psychological assessment methods used within applied clinical practice to underpin assessment.
K.K17: Understand how to analyse outputs from specialist psychological assessments across a broad range of patient needs.
K.K18: Understand formulation is derived from and integrates psychological, biological, emotional, interpersonal, social cultural and interpersonal factors.
K.K19: Understand that formulations draw upon psychological theory, providing a clinical framework describing an individual’s problem and/or needs, whilst providing a rationale for how problems have developed and are maintained.
K.K20: Understand a range of psychological hypotheses to explain the development and maintenance of distress in patients.
K.K21: Understand that formulation informs treatment and can inform the work of others in a multidisciplinary team.
K.K22: Understand how to analyse and appraise the range of cognitive-behavioural and other psychological assessment methods used within applied clinical practice to underpin diagnosis and/or formulation.
K.K23: Understand the need to take into account the preferences of the person with complex and chronic conditions, and that of their caregivers when planning a psychological intervention.
K.K24: Understand mental health issues by maintaining awareness of prevalence, incidence and impact of common mental health myths, misconceptions and stereotypes on patients.
K.K25: Understand the importance of therapeutic alliance in embedding positive behaviour change and maintain awareness that mental health stigma and discrimination are major barriers to effective psychological interventions in the management of psychological problems.
K.K26: Understand the appropriateness of the range of evidence-based psychological models and protocols when addressing individualised patient need.
K.K27: Critically appraise a range of psychological models and interventions to inform treatment planning and appropriate choice of treatment protocols.
K.K28: Understand how to analyse and appraise key theoretical concepts of psychological models of treatment both at individual and group level recognised in evidence-based national guidelines.
K.K29: Understand how psychological interventions may impact upon self-management strategies and action plans already in place.
K.K30: Understand that working with people requires setting appropriate intervention goals and agreeing these with patients, their families and their caregivers.
K.K31: Understand that it is necessary for psychological interventions to minimise harm, maximise benefits and result in improvement of overall quality of life indices.
K.K32: Understand how to interpret evidence-based psychological treatment choices with individuals, groups and other healthcare colleagues, when managing complex and chronic needs.
K.K33: Understand how lifespan development affects an individual’s performance and that it is necessary to adjust psychological interventions based on this understanding to enable patients to access and benefit from psychological interventions.
K.K34: Understand how to implement, plan and manage psychological interventions when working with complex and long term needs.
K.K35: Understand and critically appraise best evidence and existing practice to inform clinical decision making where there is no agreed consensus on treatment protocols and evaluate outcome.
K.K36: Understand the range of behavioural change models including health belief models to synthesise best practice in the absence of a strong evidence base, or existence of clinical practice guidelines.
K.K37: Understand, appraise and discriminate the selection of appropriate measurement tools from a range of possible options in the context of individual and service level change.
K.K38: Understand how to conduct an individualised psychological evaluation utilising behavioural observation and measurement.
K.K39: Understand the use of valid and reliable measurement tools for the purposes of self and other observation of outcome and evaluation of treatment, incorporating evaluation from formal and informal carers.
K.K40: Understand fundamentals of psychometric principles to guide the use of standardised evaluation with specific populations to identify appropriate quality improvement strategies.
K.K41: Understand how to communicate to non-psychology colleagues, a range of psychological hypotheses explaining the development and maintenance of distress in patients.
K.K42: Understand, how to support and guide contributions from multidisciplinary team members in order to provide safe, integrated and effective psychological practice. Understand the importance and impact of team and organisational dynamics and culture in service delivery and development.
K.K43: Understand the principles of leadership theory to influence best psychological practice when working in teams.
K.K44: Understand impact of multiple perspectives within the context of multidisciplinary teams.
K.K45: Understand psychological practice requirements and safe practice and how to convey this to the broader clinical workforce in line with the evidence-base.
K.K46: Understand different learning styles and how this can affect the success of training delivery.
K.K47: Understand the range of tools and techniques that can be used to support learning, set goals and evaluate learning.
K.K48: Understand different training approaches using psychological theory and research to bring about changes in the delivery of treatments.
K.K49: Understand the impact of teaching others to enhance reflective practice in the context of a range of service settings.
K.K50: Understand how research is conducted and implemented at an appropriate level to inform effectiveness in clinical practice.
K.K51: Understand the range of legal, ethical, professional, financial and organisational policies and procedures that apply to clinical research activities.
K.K52: Understand the importance and impact of organisational culture in service delivery and development.
K.K53: Understand a range of quantitative and qualitative research methodologies relevant to situation and service context.
K.K54: Understand a range of research approaches drawing on specialist psychological tools to collect data to evaluate own practice as well as to enhance service delivery.
K.K55: Understand knowledge of evidence-based practice through supporting others in planning audit, evaluation and research of their work.
K.K56: Understand models of clinical supervision and requirements for practice in line with the evidence-base and professional codes of conduct.
K.K57: Understand clinical supervision provides opportunities for others to review and modify their practice, maintain high professional standards of competence and to enhance the delivery of individualised care.
K.K58: Understand the appropriate boundaries of professional competency in offering support and supervision to others and recognise the requirement to seek regular supervision for own practice.
K.K59: Understand the evidence base including contemporary approaches to assessing and managing risks in different contexts.
K.K60: Understand how to assess risk in relation to psychological distress and to ensure that risk formulations are integrated with interventions.
K.K61: Understand contingency management and the use of risk indicators in mitigating against crises.
K.K62: Understand the appropriateness of crisis interventions that are safe, effective and compassionate and follow a rights-based approach consistent with service standards.
K.K63: Understand the identification of, reporting and reflection upon critical incidents and serious adverse events influencing and changing clinical practice.

S.S1: Work within the scope of practice of the role and within the bounds of professional competence, in line with employer’s requirements around values, conduct and ethics.
S.S2: In all clinical and professional activities, act in accordance with the BPS Professional Code of Conduct, identifying and challenging discriminatory behaviour.
S.S3: Actively participate in clinical and professional supervision in order to develop individual scope of practice within legal and ethical boundaries to manage risk and enhance clinical practice.
S.S4: Take responsibility for continuous self-reflection, seeking and responding to support and feedback to develop professional knowledge and skills.
S.S5: Communicate effectively, share information and check understanding using clear language and appropriate, written materials, making reasonable adjustments where appropriate in order to optimise people’s understanding.
S.S6: Recognise and accommodate sensory impairments during all communications and the use of personal communication aids.
S.S7: Implement, produce and maintain clear, legible and contemporaneous patient records regarding direct and indirect patient contacts and wider working within teams adhering to professional and ethical standards.
S.S8: Act on the duty to comply with service and national standards of clinical record-keeping.
S.S9: Assess individuals and/or families using a variety of approaches and a range of psychological assessment methods to assess baseline and change post-intervention.
S.S10: Analyse outputs from specialist psychological and cognitive assessments across a broad range of patient needs and disseminate reports to influence own practice and that of others within the multidisciplinary team.
S.S11: Implement best practice by conducting assessments and treatment interventions according to evidence-based practice where there are limited treatment protocols to guide practice.
S.S12: Take account of how conflicting and sometimes contradictory information from carers and other healthcare professionals, in emotive and challenging situations and contexts, may impact on the outcome of assessment.
S.S13: Formulate individual distress to explain how psychological difficulties and presentations are influenced by potentially conflicting sociocultural and attitudinal factors.
S.S14: Create, implement and appraise formulations based upon multiple sources of clinical and other data to inform the management of psychological interventions and where no protocols or treatment guidance exists.
S.S15: Develop collaborative formulations with patients so as to sense-check understandings and influence delivery of evidenced-based individualised psychological interventions.
S.S16: Share formulations with others in a multidisciplinary team to promote patient engagement and to anticipate treatment obstacles and to prevent disengagement.
S.S17: Apply a range of psychological interventions consistent with assessment and diagnosis/formulation.
S.S18: Explain the rationales to individuals, groups and other professional colleagues, for evidence-based psychological treatment models and protocols.
S.S19: Deliver psychological treatments appropriate to the level of patient need and provide treatment at an appropriate level of frequency and duration in the context of distress and complexity.
S.S20: Recognise and respond to individual distress using evidence-based psychological treatment models and protocols.
S.S21: Analyse and appraise the appropriateness of the range of psychological models and protocols when addressing individualised patient need.
S.S22: Analyse and appraise principles of psychological interventions at individual and group level and evaluate episodes of treatment drawing upon evidence-based models and protocols to inform treatment planning and implementation.
S.S23: Plan and implement evidence-based treatment protocols specific to individual or group need for managing complexity and chronicity of presentations.
S.S24: Generate evidence-based psychological interventions taking into account a range of potentially conflicting clinical data.
S.S25: Actively engage patients in treatment regimes to address and resolve emotive contexts and circumstances.
S.S26: Apply psychological interventions that are consistent with self-management strategies and action plans for people with complex and chronic needs.
S.S27: Apply evidence-based psychological interventions addressing complex and/or long-term needs consistent with psychological models of change.
S.S28: Implement evidence-based psychological interventions for people with complex and/or long-term needs with appropriate intervention goals agreed with patients, their families and their caregivers.
S.S29: Plan and implement evidence-based psychological treatment models and protocols while providing an individual patient rationale.
S.S30: Accurately measure and evaluate outcomes in a range of care settings, by selecting the appropriate measurement tools from a range of possible options in the context of individual and service level change.
S.S31: Engage in all stages of audit and evaluation activity, leading to the continuous enhancement and quality improvement of clinical practice.
S.S32: Implement a range of psychological measurement tools with individuals, families, or services to evaluate treatment, individual, service or organisational change.
S.S33: Provide guidance, support and facilitation to multidisciplinary team members in the delivery of psychologically enhanced approaches.
S.S34: Act as a psychological resource within the multidisciplinary team to demonstrate how psychological theories and models can facilitate practice innovations.
S.S35: Apply psychological theory and research to address emotive and challenging situations, taking account of conflicting and contradictory information from carers and other healthcare professionals.
S.S36: Work as part of a multidisciplinary community team or in specialised clinical settings and liaise with relevant external agencies to facilitate and enable psychological interventions.
S.S37: Work collaboratively to identify and meet the learning and development needs of health or care professionals.
S.S38: Communicate new learning approaches and provide constructive feedback to challenge and overcome barriers to implementation of best psychological practice.
S.S39: Communicate to others the core concepts of psychological theory, research and practice in order to enhance their delivery of psychological interventions.
S.S40: Provide training for others to inform and support psychological models of change.
S.S41: Provide training within teams to enhance delivery of clinical and research practice interventions appropriate to the health and psychological needs of patients across a range of service settings.
S.S42: Engage in research activity to identify service gaps and problems so that new approaches and solutions can be implemented to solve clinical and service problems.
S.S43: Communicate clinically relevant research material to a range of practitioners.
S.S44: Apply and analyse a range of research approaches including both qualitative and quantitative methods in clinical practice.
S.S45: Act as a wider resource within teams to inform clinical and research practice, critically appraise, interpret and implement the outcomes of research methodologies such as service evaluation and clinical audit.
S.S46: Evaluate and audit clinical practice through conducting service evaluations to inform change through dissemination of findings ensuring best use of publicly funded resources.
S.S47: Act as a wider psychological resource by offering support and clinical supervision to identify psychological issues in a safe, supportive and professional manner.
S.S48: Provide a supportive, safe space to enable a clinical supervisory process for a broader mental health workforce supporting better psychological treatment outcomes.
S.S49: Act appropriately following employment procedures when serious concerns are raised in clinical supervision about the conduct, competence, or health of a practitioner.
S.S50: Enable support and clinical supervision of team members to promote the implementation of models of psychological change enhancing treatment outcomes.
S.S51: Apply and review risk assessments and formulations when working with complex patients within scope of practice.
S.S52: To effectively communicate decision making processes  which have informed the psychological management of risk. Implement and respond appropriately to risk, using appropriate guidance and support, maintaining compliance with service policy and values.
S.S53: Assess and identify appropriate practice in relation to critical incident and severe adverse events.

B.B1: Treat patients with dignity, respecting individuals’ diversity, beliefs, culture, needs, values, privacy and preferences.
B.B2: Show respect and empathy for those worked with and have the courage to challenge areas of concern and work to evidence-based best practice.
B.B3: Be adaptable, reliable and consistent, show discretion, resilience and self-awareness and demonstrate professional and clinical competence.

K.K1: Continuous Integration - the benefits of frequent merging of code, the creation of build artefacts and ensuring all tests pass, with automation throughout - including common tooling.
K.K2: The principles of distributed Source Control, including how to exploit the features of the tool, such as branching.
K.K3: How to use data ethically and the implications for wider society, with respect to the use of data, automation and artificial intelligence within the context of relevant data protection policy and legislation.
K.K4: The business value of DevOps in terms of Time, Cost, Quality, with an emphasis on building in internal Quality throughout the lifetime of the product.
K.K5: A range of modern security tools and techniques - e.g. threat modelling, vulnerability scanning and dependency checking, with a general awareness of penetration testing - in order to deal with threats and attack vectors within code and across the cyber domain.
K.K6: A range of problem solving techniques appropriate to the task at hand, such as affinity mapping, impact maps, plan-do-check-act/Deming.
K.K7: General purpose programming and infrastructure-as-code.
K.K8: Immutable infrastructure and how it enables continuous refreshing of software, namely the updating of the operating system, container and security patching.
K.K9: Different organisational cultures, the development frameworks utilised and how they can both complement each other and introduce constraints on delivery.
K.K10: How the user experience sits at the heart of modern development practices in terms of strategies to understand diverse user needs, accessibility and how to drive adoption.
K.K11: Monitoring and alerting technologies and an awareness of the insights that can be derived from the infrastructure and applications - collecting logs and metrics, configuring alerting thresholds, firing alerts and visualising data.
K.K12: The persistence/data layer, including which database/storage technologies are appropriate to each platform type and application when considering non-functional and functional needs; e.g. monolith, microservice, read heavy, write heavy, recovery plans.
K.K13: Automation techniques, such as scripting and use of APIs.
K.K14: Test Driven Development and the Test Pyramid.  How the practice is underpinned by unit testing, the importance of automation, appropriate use of test doubles and mocking strategies, reducing a reliance on end-to-end testing.
K.K15: The principles and application of Continuous Integration, Continuous Delivery and Continuous Deployment, including the differences between them.
K.K16: How best to secure data; e.g. encryption in transit, encryption at rest and access control lists (ACL).
K.K17: What an API is, how to find them and interpret the accompanying documentation.
K.K18: Roles within a multidisciplinary team and the interfaces with other areas of an organisation.
K.K19: Different methods of communication and choosing the appropriate one - e.g. face-to-face (synchronous, high bandwidth), instant messaging, email (asynchronous, low bandwidth), visualisations vs. words.
K.K20: Pair/mob programming techniques and when to use each technique.
K.K21: Architecture principles, common patterns and common strategies for translating user needs into both cloud infrastructure and application code.
K.K22: How their occupation fits into the wider digital landscape and any current or future regulatory requirements.
K.K23: The importance of continual improvement within a blameless culture.
K.K24: The difference between Software-as-a-Service (SaaS) v bespoke v enterprise tooling and how to make an informed choice that suits each use case.
K.K25: Maintain an awareness of cloud certification requirements.

S.S1: Communicate credibly with technical and non-technical people at all levels, using a range of methods; e.g. ‘Show and Tell’ and ‘Demonstrations’.
S.S2: Work within different organisational cultures with both internal and external parties
S.S3: Translate user needs into deliverable tasks, writing clear, concise and unambiguous user stories that the whole team can understand.
S.S4: Initiate and facilitate knowledge sharing and technical collaboration
S.S5: Deploy immutable infrastructure
S.S6: Install, manage and troubleshoot monitoring tools
S.S7: Navigate and troubleshoot stateful distributed systems, in order to locate issues across the end-to-end service.
S.S8: Work in agile, multi-disciplinary delivery teams, taking a flexible, collaborative and pragmatic approach to delivering tasks.
S.S9: Application of a range of cloud security tools and techniques - e.g. threat modelling, vulnerability scanning, dependency checking, reducing attack surface area - incorporating these tools and techniques into the automated pipeline wherever possible.
S.S10: Assess identified and potential security threats and take appropriate action based on likelihood v impact.
S.S11: Employ a systematic approach to solving problems, using logic and hypotheses / experimentation to identify the source of issues.
S.S12: Automate tasks where it introduces improvements to the efficiency of business processes and reduces waste, considering the effort and cost of automation.
S.S13: Engage in productive pair/mob programming.
S.S14: Write tests and follow Test Driven Development discipline in various different contexts.
S.S15: Release automation and orchestration as part of a Continuous Integration workflow and Continuous Delivery pipeline, automating the delivery of code from source control to the end users.
S.S16: Invest in continuous learning, both your own development and others, ensuring learning activities dovetail with changing job requirements.  Keep up with cutting edge.
S.S17: Code in a general purpose programming language.
S.S18: Specify cloud infrastructure in an infrastructure-as-code domain-specific language.
S.S19: Interpret logs and metrics data within the appropriate context to identify issues and make informed decisions.
S.S20: Writing code in such a way that makes merging easier and facilitates branching by abstraction - i.e. feature toggling.
S.S21: Application of lightweight modelling techniques, such as whiteboarding, in order to gain consensus as a team on evolving architecture.
S.S22: Incremental refactoring by applying small behaviour-preserving code changes to evolve the architecture.

B.B1: Exhibits enthusiasm, openness and an aptitude for working as part of a collaborative community; e.g. sharing best practice, pairing with team members, learning from others and engaging in peer review practices.
B.B2: Invests time and effort in their own development, recognising that technology evolves at a rapid rate.
B.B3: Displays a commitment to the mantra 'You build it, you run it', taking ownership of deployed code and being accountable for its continual improvement, learning from experience and taking collective responsibility when things fail.
B.B4: Is inclusive, professional and maintains a blameless culture.

K.K1: Understand the Code: Professional standards of practice and behaviour for nurses, midwives and nursing associates (NMC, 2018), and how to fulfill all registration requirements
K.K2: Understand the demands of professional practice and demonstrate how to recognise signs of vulnerability in themselves or their colleagues and the action required to minimise risks to health
K.K3: Understand the professional responsibility to adopt a healthy lifestyle to maintain the level of personal fitness and well-being required to meet people’s needs for mental and physical care
K.K4: Understand the principles of research and how research findings are used to inform evidence-based practice
K.K5: Understand the meaning of resilience and emotional intelligence, and their influence on an individual’s ability to provide care
K.K6: Understand and apply relevant legal, regulatory and governance requirements, policies, and ethical frameworks, including any mandatory reporting duties, to all areas of practice
K.K7: Understand the importance of courage and transparency and apply the Duty of Candour
K.K8: Understand how discriminatory behaviour is exhibited
K.K9: Understand the aims and principles of health promotion, protection and improvement and the prevention of ill health when engaging with people
K.K10: Understand the principles of epidemiology, demography, and genomics and how these may influence health and well-being outcomes
K.K11: Understand the factors that may lead to inequalities in health outcomes
K.K12: Understand the importance of early years and childhood experiences and the possible impact on life choices, mental, physical and behavioural health and well-being
K.K13: Understand the contribution of social influences, health literacy, individual circumstances, behaviours and lifestyle choices to mental, physical and behavioural health outcomes
K.K14: Understand the importance of health screening
K.K15: Understand human development from conception to death, to enable delivery of person-centred safe and effective care
K.K16: Understand body systems and homeostasis, human anatomy and physiology, biology, genomics, pharmacology, social and behavioural sciences as applied to delivery of care
K.K17: Understand commonly encountered mental, physical, behavioural and cognitive health conditions as applied to delivery of care
K.K18: Understand and apply the principles and processes for making reasonable adjustments
K.K19: Know how and when to escalate to the appropriate professional for expert help and advice
K.K20: Know how people’s needs for safety, dignity, privacy, comfort and sleep can be met
K.K21: Understand co-morbidities and the demands of meeting people’s holistic needs when prioritising care
K.K22: Know how to meet people’s needs related to nutrition, hydration and bladder and bowel health
K.K23: Know how to meet people’s needs related to mobility, hygiene, oral care, wound care and skin integrity
K.K24: Know how to support people with commonly encountered symptoms including anxiety, confusion, discomfort and pain
K.K25: Know how to deliver sensitive and compassionate end of life care to support people to plan for their end of life
K.K26: Understand where and how to seek guidance and support from others to ensure that the best interests of those receiving care are upheld
K.K27: Understand the principles of safe and effective administration and optimisation of medicines in accordance with local and national policies
K.K28: Understand the effects of medicines, allergies, drug sensitivity, side effects, contraindications and adverse reactions
K.K29: Understand the different ways by which medicines can be prescribed
K.K30: Understand the principles of health and safety legislation and regulations and maintain safe work and care environments
K.K31: Understand how inadequate staffing levels impact on the ability to provide safe care and escalate concerns appropriately
K.K32: Understand what constitutes a near miss, a serious adverse event, a critical incident and a major incident
K.K33: Understand when to seek appropriate advice to manage a risk and avoid compromising quality of care and health outcomes
K.K34: Know and understand strategies to develop resilience in self and know how to seek support to help deal with uncertain situations
K.K35: Understand own role and the roles of all other staff at different levels of experience and seniority in the event of a major incident
K.K36: Understand the roles of the different providers of health and care
K.K37: Understand the challenges of providing safe nursing care for people with complex co-morbidities and complex care needs
K.K38: Understand the complexities of providing mental, cognitive, behavioural and physical care needs across a wide range of integrated care settings
K.K39: Understand the principles and processes involved in supporting people and families with a range of care needs to maintain optimal independence and avoid unnecessary interventions and disruptions to their lives
K.K40: Understand own role and contribution when involved in the care of a person who is undergoing discharge or a transition of care between professionals, settings or services
K.K41: Know the roles, responsibilities and scope of practice of different members of the nursing and interdisciplinary team, and own role within it
K.K42: Understand and apply the principles of human factors and environmental factors when working in teams
K.K43: Understand the influence of policy and political drivers that impact health and care provision

S.S1: Act in accordance with the Code: Professional standards of practice and behaviour for nurses, midwives and nursing associates (NMC, 2018), and fulfil all registration requirements
S.S2: Keep complete, clear, accurate and timely records
S.S3: Recognise and report any factors that may adversely impact safe and effective care provision
S.S4: Take responsibility for continuous self-reflection, seeking and responding to support and feedback to develop professional knowledge and skills
S.S5: Safely demonstrate evidence-based practice in all skills and procedures required for entry to the register: Standards of proficiency fornursing associates Annex A & B (NMC 2018)
S.S6: Act as an ambassador for their profession and promote public confidence in health and care services
S.S7: Communicate effectively using a range of skills and strategies with colleagues and people at all stages of life and with a rangeof mental, physical, cognitive and behavioural health challenges
S.S8: Recognise signs of vulnerability in self or colleagues and the action required to minimise risks to health
S.S9: Develop, manage and maintain appropriate relationships with people, their families, carers and colleagues
S.S10: Provide, promote, and where appropriate advocate for, non-discriminatory, person-centred and sensitive care at all times, reflecting on people’s values and beliefs, diverse backgrounds, cultural characteristics, language requirements, needs and preferences, taking account of any need for adjustments
S.S11: Report any situations, behaviours or errors that could result in poor care outcomes
S.S12: Challenge or report discriminatory behaviour
S.S13: Apply the aims and principles of health promotion, protection and improvement and the prevention of ill health when engaging with people
S.S14: Promote preventive health behaviours and provide information to support people to make informed choices to improve their mental, physical, behavioural health and wellbeing
S.S15: Identify people who are eligible for health screening
S.S16: Promote health and prevent ill health by understanding the evidence base for immunisation, vaccination and herd immunity
S.S17: Protect health through understanding and applying the principles of infection prevention and control, including communicable disease surveillance and antimicrobial stewardship and resistance
S.S18: Apply knowledge, communication and relationship management skills required to provide people, families and carers with accurate information that meets their needs before, during and after a range of interventions
S.S19: Recognise when capacity has changed recognise and how a person’s capacity affects their ability to make decisions about their own care and to give or withhold consent
S.S20: Recognise people at risk of abuse, self-harm and/or suicidal ideation and the situations that may put them and others at risk
S.S21: Monitor the effectiveness of care in partnership with people, families and carers, documenting progress and reporting outcomes
S.S22: Take personal responsibility to ensure that relevant information is shared according to local policy and appropriate immediate action is taken to provide adequate safeguarding and that concerns are escalated
S.S23: Work in partnership with people, to encourage shared decision making, in order to support individuals, their families and carers to manage their own care when appropriate
S.S24: Perform a range of nursing procedures and manage devices, to meet people’s need for safe, effective and person-centred care
S.S25: Meet people’s needs for safety, dignity, privacy, comfort and sleep
S.S26: Meet people’s needs related to nutrition, hydration and bladder and bowel health
S.S27: Meet people’s needs related to mobility, hygiene, oral care, wound care and skin integrity
S.S28: Support people with commonly encountered symptoms including anxiety, confusion, discomfort and pain
S.S29: Give information and support to people who are dying, their families and the bereaved and provide care to the deceased
S.S30: Recognise when a person’s condition has improved or deteriorated by undertaking health monitoring, interpreting, promptly responding, sharing findings and escalating as needed
S.S31: Act in line with any end of life decisions and orders, organ and tissue donation protocols, infection protocols, advanced planning decisions, living wills and lasting powers of attorney for health
S.S32: Work collaboratively and in partnership with professionals from different agencies in interdisciplinary teams
S.S33: Maintain safe work and care environments
S.S34: Act in line with local and national organisational frameworks, legislation and regulations to report risks, and implement actions as instructed, following up and escalating as required
S.S35: Accurately undertake risk assessments, using contemporary assessment tools
S.S36: Respond to and escalate potential hazards that may affect the safety of people
S.S37: Participate in data collection to support audit activity, and contribute to the implementation of quality improvement strategies
S.S38: Prioritise and manage own workload, and recognise where elements of care can safely be delegated to other colleagues, carers and family members
S.S39: Recognise when people need help to facilitate equitable access to care, support and escalate concerns appropriately
S.S40: Support and motivate other members of the care team and interact confidently with them
S.S41: Monitor and review the quality of care delivered, providing challenge and constructive feedback when an aspect of care has been delegated to others
S.S42: Support, supervise and act as a role model to nursing associate students, health care support workers and those new to care roles, review the quality of the care they provide, promoting reflection and providing constructive feedback
S.S43: Contribute to team reflection activities to promote improvements in practice and services
S.S44: Access, input, and apply information and data using a range of methods including digital technologies, and share appropriately within interdisciplinary teams

B.B1: Treat people with dignity, respecting individual's diversity, beliefs, culture, needs, values, privacy and preferences
B.B2: Show respect and empathy for those you work with, have the courage to challenge areas of concern and work to evidence based best practice
B.B3: Be adaptable, reliable and consistent, show discretion, resilience and self-awareness

K.K1: Standards of personal and professional conduct, possible conflicts of care and how to report breaches of professional standards.
K.K2: Limits of your practice.
K.K3: Lone working, personal and others’ safety, employer’s policies and the need to maintain the safety of service-users.
K.K4: Responsibilities to escalate and disclose information through the appropriate channels in regards to safeguarding and Prevent.
K.K5: Employers’ guidelines on reporting risks, incidents and escalation process.
K.K6: Registration requirements, continuous reflection and improvement to practice.
K.K7: National legislation, local policies, workplace procedures, advice, and guidance including relating to culture, equality, diversity, and inclusion.
K.K8: Professional duty of care and steps to reduce the risk of harm to service-users, carers, and colleagues.
K.K9: The importance of maintaining own health and wellbeing.
K.K10: Importance of ongoing professional development and training, professional registration and incorporating changes to own role.
K.K11: The nature and severity of a problem in professional situations.
K.K12: Developmental needs of the service-user and how to use normalising interventions, preparation, distraction, and post procedural play techniques by initiating, continuing, modifying and ceasing play.
K.K13: Play techniques and use of resources to improve the service users experience and wellbeing.
K.K14: Coping strategies in order to minimise service-user's distress and anxiety during clinical procedures.
K.K15: The importance of choice and control through service-user centred care that support positive outcomes.
K.K16: Partnership and team working approaches to ensure that clinical procedures are planned and managed.
K.K17: How sharing information, interventions and accurate records contributes to the management and the care provided for a service-user.
K.K18: Play interactions that enable a child and their family to understand their condition and learn the sensory and factual information they need to prepare for any treatment or procedure or to make any necessary lifestyle changes.
K.K19: The importance of gaining consent before providing care and support to service-users to maintain their own health and well-being.
K.K20: How to make and receive referrals in line with organisational policy.
K.K21: The importance of participation in training, mentorship, coaching and supervision in order to support service users, health professionals, colleagues, students, and apprentices.
K.K22: How to provide support and guidance to ensure that safe practice can be monitored and maintained when working with junior staff.
K.K23: How to supervise and delegate to others ensuring the knowledge, skills and experience required to work safely and effectively.
K.K24: The purpose of supporting the multidisciplinary team and other professionals to understand the needs and preferences of service-user's and how play and health services impact their care.
K.K25: Needs of different service-users and how to adapt practice or make reasonable adjustments to promote inclusive service provision.
K.K26: Ways to use, record and store data and information related to service-users securely and in line with General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) requirements and local and national policies, including the safe use of digital technology.
K.K27: Different communication skills and strategies to maximise understanding for service-users and to facilitate assessment and engagement of those with protected characteristics.
K.K28: Communication support systems which can assist the service-user and how interpersonal skills can encourage active participation.
K.K29: The importance and impact of team and partnership working to service delivery in and across different sectors and the value of sharing skills knowledge and expertise.
K.K30: The value of enabling and engaging service-users in planning and evaluating therapeutic play techniques to support treatments and interventions to meet their needs and goals.
K.K31: Evidence-based practice, audit procedures, systematic practice evaluation and continuous improvement.
K.K32: Information gathering to evaluate the effectiveness of interventions.
K.K33: Theoretical concepts underpinning play, therapeutic play and the development of service-user.
K.K34: Processes to devise, implement and review developmental and individual therapeutic play plans for the service-user.
K.K35: Normalising, developmental and therapeutic play activities within safe environments for service-users.
K.K36: Physical, psychological, social, cultural, and environmental needs, challenges and perspectives of service-users.
K.K37: The importance of continuous self-reflection, seeking and responding to support and feedback to develop professional knowledge and skills.
K.K38: Quality control, quality assurance and the role of audit and review in quality management and outcome measures.
K.K39: Socio-cultural diversity of the community and the specific local context of practice, and work-related policies and services that can impact on individual needs within a diverse society.
K.K40: The impact of economic inequality, poverty, and exclusion on and the ability to access services.
K.K41: Potential new and emerging areas of practice in play.
K.K42: Leadership theories and styles, and the impact these can have on service-users and the organisation.
K.K43: Current and developing sustainable principles and techniques.

S.S1: Maintain standards of personal and professional conduct, avoid possible conflicts of care and report breaches of professional standards.
S.S2: Work within the remit of your professional boundaries, inform appropriate practitioners if the care or treatment is not within the scope of your professional boundaries or expertise.
S.S3: Refer and delegate to appropriate practitioners wherever you find yourself unable to maintain objectivity and professional boundaries.
S.S4: Comply with lone working policies considering your own personal and others' safety.
S.S5: Adhere to safeguarding and prevent policies following procedures to escalate and disclosure of information, through the appropriate channels.
S.S6: Adhere to employers’ guidelines on reporting risks, incidents and escalation process.
S.S7: Reflection and improvement to practice to enable registration.
S.S8: Adhere to up to date national legislation, local policies, workplace procedures, advice, and guidance, supporting the rights of service users, colleagues and visitors, including relating to culture, equality, diversity, and inclusion.
S.S9: Exercise a professional duty of care ensuring no act or omission is detrimental to the condition or safety of service-users in your care or their families, carers and colleagues, to deal with concerns.
S.S10: Maintain own health and wellbeing, seeking advice and changing practise to reduce possible risks.
S.S11: Take personal responsibility for ongoing professional development and training opportunities and professional registration.
S.S12: Assess professional situations, determining the nature and severity of a problem acting within your professional scope of practice.
S.S13: Use normalising interventions, preparation, distraction, and post procedural play techniques by initiating, continuing, modifying, and ceasing play, based on the developmental needs of all service-users, to build on their abilities and enhance their experience.
S.S14: Apply play techniques selecting appropriate play resources to improve the service user's needs, experience and wellbeing.
S.S15: Apply coping strategies in order to minimise service-user's distress and anxiety during clinical procedures.
S.S16: Facilitate choice and control through service-user centred care, using play-based techniques that support positive outcomes.
S.S17: Work in partnership and with other professions as part of a team to ensure that clinical procedures are planned and managed.
S.S18: Share information, interventions and accurate records agreeing goals and priorities with other identified professionals to contribute to the management and the care provided for a service user.
S.S19: Guide a child and their family to understand their condition and learn the sensory and factual information they need to prepare for any treatment or procedure or to make any necessary lifestyle changes through play interactions.
S.S20: Encourage and help service-users to maintain their own health and well-being, and support them so they can make informed decisions ensuring patient consent is gained.
S.S21: Make and receive referrals in line with organisation’s referral policy.
S.S22: Participate in training, mentorship, coaching and supervision in order to support service users, health professionals, colleagues, students, and apprentices.
S.S23: Provide support and guidance to ensure that safe practice can be monitored and maintained when working with junior staff.
S.S24: Supervise and delegate to others ensuring the knowledge, skills and experience required to work safely and effectively.
S.S25: Raise awareness within the multidisciplinary team and other professionals of the needs, preferences and the impact of service-user's care through play and health services.
S.S26: Adapt practice or make reasonable adjustments to meet the needs of different service-users to take account of new developments, changing contexts and promote inclusive service provision.
S.S27: Use, record and store data and information related to service-users securely and in line with General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) requirements and local and national policies, including the safe use of digital technology.
S.S28: Use and adapt communication skills and strategies to maximise understanding for service-users and to facilitate assessment and engagement of those with protected characteristics.
S.S29: Undertake work collaboratively with others as part of a professional team in and across different sectors sharing skills knowledge and expertise.
S.S30: Engage service-users in planning and evaluating therapeutic play techniques to support treatments and interventions to meet their needs and goals.
S.S31: Engage in evidence-based practice and participate in audit procedures evaluating systematic practice, working towards continual improvement.
S.S32: Gather information to assess service-users, use information to evaluate the effectiveness of interventions and revise plans as necessary.
S.S33: Apply the theoretical concepts underpinning play, therapeutic play and the development of service-user.
S.S34: Devise and implement developmental and individual therapeutic play plans for the service-user including specific and timely reviews.
S.S35: Conduct normalising, developmental and therapeutic play activities, support procedures, treatments, therapy, or other actions within safe environments for service-users.
S.S36: Identify and assess physical, psychological, social, cultural, and environmental needs and challenges of service-users.
S.S37: Identify the importance of self reflection, using research, apply reasoning, problem-solving skills and feedback, to inform own practice and improve areas of personal performance.
S.S38: Participate in quality assurance programs.

B.B1: Show respect, compassion, and uphold the rights, dignity, values, and autonomy of all individuals whilst maintaining high standards of care.
B.B2: Recognise that you are personally responsible for own actions and decisions.
B.B3: Adopt an empathetic approach and demonstrate discretion.
B.B4: Promotes equality, diversity and inclusion within the team, the wider organisation and service users.
B.B5: Be adaptable, reliable and consistent, resilient and self-aware.

K.K1: The application of advanced level applied statistics, epidemiology and analysis.
K.K2: The statistical, epidemiological, social and scientific concepts underpinning the interpretation of health data to produce intelligence and the impact of these on both people and organisations.
K.K3: The strengths and weaknesses of statistical and epidemiological methods, analytical tools and approaches.
K.K4: The principles of developing data-driven models to understand activity, financial impact or outcomes.
K.K5: Economic forecasting, economic evaluation and scenario modelling of population health needs, resource and the rationale for longer-term investment in health and care delivery.
K.K6: A wide range of health and care data sources (e.g. demographic, health, social care, economic, finance, local and national NHS and other relevant data sources) and their relative strengths, weaknesses and uses.
K.K7: Clinical terminologies in common use across the health and care sector.
K.K8: Health and care data standards, the Data Dictionary, and data flows across the NHS and social care system; including the latest developments in these areas.
K.K9: The design principles, and technical aspects of, systems for the input, storage and dissemination of health and care statistical and epidemiological information.
K.K10: Health and care systems and processes, operational structures and patient pathways (e.g. hospital activity systems).
K.K11: The context of NHS, central and local government intelligence, including organisational structures and responsibilities, planning cycles, common policy issues, purpose of different types of quantitative assessments, audits and plans.
K.K12: The major factors influencing health needs and inequalities, including health behaviours and the wider determinants of health.
K.K13: The principles of using effective written and oral communications to influence others.
K.K14: The information needs of decision-makers (e.g. in the NHS, central and local government) and how they use information to support decision-making and how the publication of health information may impact the wider population.
K.K15: The key principles of partnership working, the basics of negotiation and different organisational cultures.
K.K16: Sources of evidence and examples of best practice and how to access them.
K.K17: Key techniques for assessing evidence and examples of best practice (e.g. critical appraisal, systematic reviews, meta-analysis and economic evaluation).
K.K18: The requirements for responsible, legal and ethical access and use of health and care data, including data protection considerations, protection of identifiable personal healthcare data, and understanding and consideration of the risks of statistical disclosure issues especially when presenting and publishing health information.
K.K19: The legal implications of sharing data and data linkage across organisations and the associated requirements, such as data sharing agreements and data mapping audits.
K.K20: The role of effective leadership in an organisation and how leadership differs from management.
K.K21: The role of the analytical function within an organisational structure and the strengths and weaknesses of different models for organising analytical teams.
K.K22: Organisational theory and behaviour and theories of organisational change.
K.K23: The processes by which resources are effectively managed, including how to analyse and interpret budget reports, how costs for services are calculated, and how to prepare simple financial statements.
K.K24: The principles of effective project management and commonly used project management approaches.
K.K25: How adults learn and how to design effective training in areas of healthcare analysis and intelligence.
K.K26: How to identify gaps in the knowledge and skills required to fulfil analytical and intelligence roles in the health and care system.
K.K27: Coaching and mentoring techniques to support the effective learning and development of others.
K.K28: The importance of continuing professional development and how to maintain specialist knowledge and practice in an ever transforming environment.
K.K29: The key issues to be considered when balancing patient’s rights versus the public interest, including the acceptability of access to NHS data by different public and private agencies, and how this is discussed in the public domain.
K.K30: Local child and adult protection procedures and who to contact for further advice.

S.S1: Collect data by utilising a variety of tools (e.g. databases, software systems, APIs, digital devices).
S.S2: Extract, import, clean, and manipulate a wide range of quantitative and qualitative data.
S.S3: Undertake advanced statistical and epidemiological analysis.
S.S4: Interpret and present advanced statistical and epidemiological analyses being mindful of the risks associated with presenting and publishing health information.
S.S5: Undertake linkage of health and care data accurately and in accordance with the relevant information governance requirements.
S.S6: Design and specify data flows, collection, storage and collation mechanisms for both qualitative and quantitative data.
S.S7: Develop data visualisations appropriate for a range of audiences and contexts using a variety of analytical tools including statistical programming software and industry standard packages (e.g. R, Tableau, PowerBI).
S.S8: Professionally interpret and present health and care intelligence analyses and recommendations in a well structured report.
S.S9: Formulate analysis questions and hypotheses which are answerable given the data available.
S.S10: Investigate patterns and variations in determinants, diseases, and other factors affecting health and care outcomes.
S.S11: Design and undertake surveys, audits or research (e.g. to investigate patterns of disease or support service development).
S.S12: Review, and critically appraise evidence and research including survey design and analysis. Summarise and disseminate relevant literature.
S.S13: Work with users of information to clarify their information needs, focusing on understanding the problem to solve or decision to inform, and tailor reports and presentations accordingly.
S.S14: Provide advice and guidance to internal and external stakeholders at all organisational levels, on data collection, analysis and interpretation.
S.S15: Develop and deliver management/Board level presentations which influence senior decision makers, both non-technical and technical.
S.S16: Communicate sensitively, accurately and appropriately to audiences from technical and non-technical backgrounds.
S.S17: Comply with, implement and advise on local and national data protection and confidentiality legislation, policies, procedures and any other relevant legal frameworks.
S.S18: Demonstrate effective leadership and change management skills to influence the use of analysis within an organisation, managing analytical teams and adhering to the principles of continuous improvement.
S.S19: Evaluate the significance of human factors in the effective development and implementation of organisational strategies for collection, analysis and use of data.
S.S20: Develop and communicate a vision of how data, intelligence and evidence can be used to influence and improve decision making in an organisation.
S.S21: Develop effective partnership working arrangements with a range of colleagues across departments and organisations, in particular the public sector and voluntary sectors and academia.
S.S22: Manage the resources of an analytical team, including staff and budget management.
S.S23: Support the professional development of others in the health and care system.
S.S24: Design and deliver effective training and evaluate its impact.
S.S25: Apply a range of coaching interventions and techniques relevant to the health and care system, selecting the most appropriate method to meet the needs of an individual or group.
S.S26: Determine when mentoring is appropriate for others in the health and care system and either act in a mentoring capacity or identify the appropriate individual(s) to act as mentors.
S.S27: Develop, maintain and enhance activity and financial and outcomes models, sense checking and refining the model based on various scenarios and advising on the best option for planning future activity and income or expenditure.
S.S28: Apply a range of project management techniques to lead and / or manage complex health and care intelligence projects.
S.S29: Protect and safeguard vulnerable people and promote the welfare of children, young people and vulnerable adults.
S.S30: Apply economic principles and tools to calculate value for money by determining costs, benefits and return on investment of interventions and services.

B.B1: Treat people with dignity, and respect diversity, beliefs and culture.
B.B2: Act with integrity with respect to ethical, legal and regulatory frameworks ensuring the protection of personal data, safety and security.
B.B3: Be customer focused both within own organisation and with external stakeholders.
B.B4: Be self-directed in learning and reflection to constantly improve and work towards evidence-based best practice.
B.B5: Be adaptable, reliable and consistent, demonstrating discretion, resilience, self-awareness and team working.
B.B6: Act as a role model to peers and demonstrate leadership.
B.B7: Constructively challenge inappropriate behaviour and use of information when necessary.

K.K1: The importance of maintaining a  watch when in harbour and when at sea.
K.K2: The ways in which watch-keeping  contributes to the safety of the vessel and all of those onboard.
K.K3: The principles of teamwork.
K.K4: Different vessel types and structure
K.K5: Techniques for determining a vessel’s capability. This includes, for example, ship stresses, the impact of external forces on a vessel such as sea state, swell and wind.
K.K6: Principles and regulations for manoeuvring a vessel in near coastal waters (An area of operation restricted to 150 miles from a safe haven on the coast of the UK and 30 miles from Ireland)
K.K7: The different types of cargo operations
K.K8: The range of internationally used cargo codes, their meaning and impact on operations. For example, codes indicating dangerous goods.
K.K9: The methods for calculating tide conditions and obtaining meteorological forecasts.
K.K10: The purpose of a passage plan and how it is created.
K.K11: Methods for finding vessel position or other location.
K.K12: The different types and purposes of chart work, electronic navigational aids, maritime publications and navigational tools.
K.K13: Methods for plotting, executing and monitoring vessel progress.
K.K14: Relevant maritime legislation and regulation, the bodies responsible and the legal consequences for transgression. This covers COSWP (Code of Safe Working Practices), IRPCS/COLREGS (International Rules for the Prevention of Collisions at Sea), class and flag state requirements, health and safety and environmental protection such as SOLAS (International Convention for the Safety of Life at Sea), MARPOL (the International Convention for the Prevention of Pollution from Ships) and LOLER (the Lifting Operations and Lifting Equipment Regulations).
K.K15: Procedures to be followed in the event of an emergency (for example fire, flooding), and crew roles. This includes contingency plans such as the use of distress signals and evacuation procedures.
K.K16: The international code of signals, its purpose and usage.
K.K17: A range of verbal and written communication methods and techniques for choosing a method to fit the audience.
K.K18: Methods for using digital platforms to access and complete reports.
K.K19: The range of OOW stakeholders, their roles and responsibilities and how the OOW interacts with them.
K.K20: The importance of their own organisations commercial or operational priorities.
K.K21: How the OOW occupation fits in the wider digital landscape including the impact of automation and artificial intelligence.
K.K22: Types of propulsion system(s), their features and uses.
K.K23: the procedures and techniques for applying emergency first aid to others.
K.K24: The principles for completing a ships log that accurately describes key internal/external factors that occur during a Watch.
K.K25: Recognise signs of fatigue
K.K26: Procedures for maintaining the security of a vessel and its contents

S.S1: Keep watch over the vessel and crew.
S.S2: Co-ordinate your team in order to provide constant watch cover.
S.S3: Manoeuvre a vessel in near-coastal waters, taking account of compliance requirements, vessel type, capability, loading and external factors like tide and weather.
S.S4: Select a mooring or anchoring solution that is relevant to the circumstances, including compliance, vessel type, capability, loading and external factors like tide and weather.
S.S5: Avoid collisions by using chart work and situational awareness, anticipating and responding to all potential threats to a safe navigation. This includes (but is not limited to) both the physical surroundings and other vessel traffic.
S.S6: Perform calculations and give necessary direction to your team to ensure the vessel has suitable load stability. This applies both when the vessel is moored and also when at sea.
S.S7: Create a passage plan that meets operational objectives, is safe and complaint and has due regard to the external environment and other vessels.
S.S8: Execute and adapt a passage plan taking account of the prevailing conditions at sea.
S.S9: Handle a range of vessel types in near coastal waters. This may include as examples single screw, twin screw, variable pitch, Schottel and other propulsion configurations.
S.S10: Monitor and respond to data provided by navigation and other equipment available on-board
S.S11: Cross-compare different data sources to reach an accurate conclusion. (For example, VHF reports).
S.S12: Support the Master in meeting the legal, regulatory, environmental, and organisational requirements of the vessel and crew.
S.S13: Coordinate the response in the event of an emergency. Operate emergency equipment and deploy contingency plans appropriate to the situation. (This will range, for example, from recovery of a man overboard through to evacuation of the vessel).
S.S14: Administer emergency first aid and coordinate with the coastguard in the event of a medevac if required.
S.S15: Communicate externally deploying the international code of signals when required. This includes for example using the flag system and Morse code as appropriate to the circumstances.
S.S16: Communicate effectively at different seniority levels of the organisation, selecting an appropriate communication method for the audience.
S.S17: Develop working relationships with crew, officers and other impacted maritime stakeholders to support the delivery of required performance standards.
S.S18: Recognise your own limitations and escalate decisions where appropriate to another member of the bridge team, including the Master
S.S19: Take charge in the event of the Master's incapacitation and take suitable contingency action such as getting the vessel to shore and alerting relevant authorities.
S.S20: Complete an accurate ship log
S.S21: Identify skills gaps across the team and deliver coaching and mentoring to less experienced staff.
S.S22: Identify and respond to all internal/external factors that provide for a consistent watch following a handover.
S.S23: Check that life-saving appliances and other safety equipment is functioning and assess any remediation needed.
S.S24: Identify the signs of crew, and one’s own, fatigue.
S.S25: Implement own organisations security procedures, instructing the crew accordingly.

B.B1: Follows safe working practices and promotes a safety culture
B.B2: Seeks to continuously improve and develop
B.B3: Takes ownership of work
B.B4: Calm and effective under pressure.
B.B5: Actively protects the marine environment from pollution.
B.B6: Role-models own organisations equality and diversity requirements

K.K1: Manage risk and identify when there is a need for change from routine to enhanced cleaning protocols.
K.K2: Select and use the most appropriate cleaning agent for different surface types.
K.K3: Handle, use and dispose of cleaning chemicals following Control of Substances Hazardous to Health Regulations (COSHH).
K.K4: Disposal of routine and non-routine waste, including segregation of waste into appropriate containers for storage, and disposal in line with environmental disposal legislation.
K.K5: Selection and safe use of cleaning equipment and machinery for routine and specialist cleaning tasks, including importance of Portable Appliance Testing (PAT).
K.K6: Clean cleaning equipment and machinery after use.
K.K7: Principles of infection prevention and control.
K.K8: Recognise and report the signs of pest infestation.
K.K9: The principle of rapid response cleans.
K.K10: The principles of scheduled work priorities and periodic cleaning.
K.K11: The difference between disinfection and cleaning.
K.K12: Correct storage of equipment, chemicals, and consumables.
K.K13: The reasons for security of premises, cleaning equipment and materials, and the implications of a security breach.
K.K14: Soil classification, soil contaminant removal methods appropriate to levels of soiling.
K.K15: The importance of accurate data reporting.
K.K16: The importance gaining guidance and support.
K.K17: The principles of colour coding.
K.K18: Sustainable practices in the workplace.
K.K19: The principle of reporting shortages, incidents, accidents, and issues with delivering cleaning services.
K.K20: The principles of using supplied data to inform the demand for cleaning.
K.K21: Audit and quality assurance processes.
K.K22: The principles of personal hygiene and PPE requirements in the workplace.
K.K23: Data protection legislation.
K.K24: The use of Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs) in line with national healthcare standards.
K.K25: Disassembling and assembling a range of service user and patient equipment for cleaning.
K.K26: Different ways to appropriately interact with a range of service users who may have complex needs or illnesses.
K.K27: The importance of preparing areas of work to be cleaned in line with contract requirements.
K.K28: The principles of cleaning according to specialist commercial requirements.
K.K29: Routine cleans according to a contract.

S.S1: Conduct tasks according to documented safe ways of working.
S.S2: Select and use the most appropriate cleaning agent appropriate to different surface types.
S.S3: Use and dispose of chemicals following COSHH regulations and apply safety data sheets and dilution rates.
S.S4: Deal with routine and non-routine waste in line with organisation policies and environmental disposal legislation.
S.S5: Prepare cleaning materials and machinery and check that all electrical equipment complies with PAT testing requirements.
S.S6: Clean cleaning equipment and machinery after use.
S.S7: Identify potential sources of infection and put controls in place.
S.S8: Recognise the signs of infestation and report and monitor the problem according to local procedures.
S.S9: Recognise and respond to rapid response clean requests.
S.S10: Conduct scheduled and periodic cleaning, prioritising work where required.
S.S11: Conduct cleaning and disinfection tasks.
S.S12: Deliver housekeeping services including maintaining a clean storage environment, maintenance of equipment, stock control and rotation.
S.S13: Take measures to ensure the security of cleaning equipment and materials, and premises.
S.S14: Communicate with colleagues, clients, and other stakeholders professionally.
S.S15: Use visual checks and objective auditing techniques to ensure that cleaning has been completed to  contractual standards.
S.S16: Identify and use correct methods for removal of contaminating material.
S.S17: Comply with data protection legislation.
S.S18: Use procedures for obtaining support.
S.S19: Apply colour coding correctly to conduct cleaning safely and effectively.
S.S20: Apply sustainable practices when carrying out duties.
S.S21: Apply procedures for reporting shortages, incidents, accidents, and issues with delivering cleaning services.
S.S22: Carry out and record different types of cleaning methods.
S.S23: Use supplied data to plan cleaning tasks.
S.S24: Engage with the audit and quality assurance process.
S.S25: Use PPE and comply with the organisational uniform policy to maintain required levels of hygiene.
S.S26: Conduct cleaning tasks to the appropriate hygiene standard to ensure infection prevention and control of the healthcare environment.
S.S27: Disassemble and reassemble healthcare service user equipment in line with manufacturers’ instructions and National Standards of Healthcare Cleanliness.
S.S28: Communicate with service users considering their possible emotional, mental health and physical issues.
S.S29: Safely prepare an area for commercial cleaning.
S.S30: Conduct cleaning tasks according to specialist commercial requirements.
S.S31: Carry out routine cleans in line with client contract requirements.

B.B1: Work with respect and professionalism.
B.B2: Work independently and as part of a team; escalating areas of concern and working to evidence based best practice.
B.B3: Work in a consistent manner, taking responsibility for your own actions and completed work.
B.B4: A flexible approach to your work when required.
B.B5: Work in a sustainable way, considering the environment and making best use of products and materials.

K.K1: What hazards are likely to be found at the scene of an incident and how to mitigate them to establish a safe working environment.
K.K2: How to preserve, record and analyse the scene of an incident. This may include examining, measuring, photographing, videoing and mapping the locus of the incident, the vehicles and other physical material present.
K.K3: How to accurately obtain the deceleration and acceleration rates of a vehicle using an accelerometer.  How to establish deceleration and acceleration rates of vehicles and other objects by drag testing, from manufacturers’ data or other documents.
K.K4: How to recover, critically appraise and interpret data from various data recorders such as digital tachographs, electronic control units, insurance data boxes.
K.K5: How to measure crush damage to a motor vehicle following a collision.  Understand how crush damage, equivalent energy speed (EES) and equivalent barrier speed (EBS) can be used to calculate velocity change and the actual speed of vehicles.
K.K6: How to use Newtonian mechanics in collision investigation.
K.K7: Know how to use and obtain results from spreadsheets and other math calculation software.
K.K8: Be conversant with published literature around human factors for use in investigations. Know how environmental factors may affect persons and vehicles involved in the collision.
K.K9: What constitutes a critical speed state and understand what criteria to apply to a vehicle in critical speed
K.K10: The kinematics involved when a pedestrian is struck by a vehicle.  Know which throw model to apply in the particular circumstances to find speed.
K.K11: The handling characteristics of a two wheeled motorcycle and the various ways in which control can be lost.  Know how to calculate speed from wheelbase shortening.
K.K12: Know how to evaluate video footage to calculate the speed of relevant objects.
K.K13: Following a collision, how to design and run experiments to obtain additional information to aid in a reconstruction ensuring that the results are valid , unambiguous and statistically sound.
K.K14: How to perform statistical calculations to find the uncertainty in calculations.
K.K15: How use software to produce plans, simulations and 3D models to assist in the reconstruction of an incident.
K.K16: How to design and execute a research project to reach balanced and valid conclusions.
K.K17: Following a collision, how to undertake a vehicle examination.  Identify the mechanical component of the vehicle including tyres, brakes, suspension and steering.  Identify both active and passive safety systems fitted to the vehicle.  Identify the components of a vehicles electrical system.  Know how to investigate/interrogate those components to detect defects and faults that may affect the handling of the vehicle (vehicles up to 7500kg and motorcycles).
K.K18: The law in relation to the writing of expert witness reports and what an expert may or may not say in Court.
K.K19: How to present evidence to a Court, tribunal or other arena.
K.K20: How emergency services work both individually and collaboratively at collision scenes and during the post-collision process.

S.S1: Determine the hazards and implement a safe working environment appropriate to the activity.
S.S2: Identify, preserve and mark the evidence found at the scene of an incident and/or on vehicles and objects relevant to the investigation.
S.S3: Document the scene and scene evidence including vehicles and other relevant features by notes (electronic, written, or audio or a combination) taking measuring either electronically or using manual measuring techniques.
S.S4: Photograph and video the incident scene including vehicles and other relevant features and objects.
S.S5: Use software to create visual products including 2D plans, 3D models and simulations using software.
S.S6: During a vehicle examination identify, document and record the condition of vehicle systems and component parts. Identify defects and faults
