S.S7: Following a vehicle examination, describe how any vehicle defects found during the examination process would affect the roadworthiness of a vehicle and how it would or would not have contributed to the collision.
S.S8: Determine the deceleration and acceleration rates of vehicles and other objects using an accelerometer or by drag testing.  Adjust the data to take account of slope or partial braking.
S.S9: Recover data from data recorders including digital tachographs, vehicle ECU’s and vehicle telematics.
S.S10: Use the appropriate technique to measure the crush damage caused to a vehicle(s) involved in a collision, assess the principle direction of force and any other adjusting factors to calculate speed.
S.S11: Using Newtonian mechanics calculate speed, time, distance and acceleration
S.S12: Use spreadsheets and other math calculation software to assist in the investigation process.
S.S13: Calculate the range of uncertainty in any calculations.
S.S14: Analyse and interpret the data from data recorders, including ECU’s, digital tachographs, performance computers and vehicle telematics.
S.S15: Use an appropriate method to calculate the driver response time in a variety of situations such as night time recognition, limited visibility, low contrast and looming situations.
S.S16: Calculate the critical speed of vehicles and/or bends.
S.S17: Calculate vehicle speed from pedestrian throw.
S.S18: Calculate speed from CCTV footage.
S.S19: Following a collision, use the appropriate technique to measure the damage to a motorcycle and use the data to calculate its immediate approach speed.
S.S20: Demonstrate how EES, EBS and momentum can be used to assess the speed of a vehicle involved in a collision.
S.S21: Maintain the integrity and security of digital data.
S.S22: Describe the behaviour of a vehicle in the immediate lead up to and during an incident.
S.S23: Carry out experiments as part of an investigation.
S.S24: Interpret and incorporate the results from published literature into the investigation.
S.S25: Identify, record and comment upon the condition of the vehicle, systems and component parts to identify defects, roadworthiness, incident causation, contributory factors
S.S26: Analyse the scene data, witness evidence any experiment’s, calculations and examinations to prepare a written report into the circumstances of a collision.
S.S27: Prepare written reports and in accordance the rules, regulations and guidance in force in relation to expert witnesses.
S.S28: Present evidence to a court or other arena e.g. criminal, civil, Coroners’ courts, tribunal or hearing.
S.S29: Critically review a completed investigation carried out by another investigator.  Give written and oral feedback as part of the review.
S.S30: Liaise with other emergency services personnel to ensure that scene evidence is not lost as a result of poor communication between different services.
S.S31: Critically appraise current subject literature and analyse shortcomings
S.S32: Identify a research need and develop a research question.
S.S33: Using the scientific method conduct experiments or analysis to test a hypothesis.
S.S34: From research develop valid and robust conclusions based on observations.

B.B1: Accountability - For self and others to ensure that actions are in the best interest of affected parties
B.B2: Professional integrity - Maintain the highest standards of professionalism and trustworthiness, making sure that values, moral codes and ethical standards are always upheld
B.B3: Emotionally astute - Understand and effectively manage own emotions in stressful situations and treat people with sensitivity
B.B4: Team working - Collaboration, influence, and respect for others. Value diversity and difference in approaches to work, thinking and background
B.B5: Supportive & inspirational leading - Role model the organisational values providing inspiration and clarity to colleagues and stakeholders. Consider how the wider organisation and others are impacted, and help others to deliver their objectives effectively
B.B6: Continuing Professional Development (CPD) - Critically review published research. Accountability of own and others development needs, undertaking CPD. Curiosity of science, mathematics and engineering, proactively developing knowledge to ensure that decisions are based on strong evidence

K.K1: Understand the organisations operating environment and the factors that may influence its direction and performance, including the markets it operates in, roles and responsibilities, who its stakeholders are and what they require from the organisation. This may include sustainability criteria.
K.K2: Understand the environment in which the organisation’s products/services are produced or supplied, and the factors that may influence performance, including legislation, customer requirements and regulatory requirements. This may include sustainability or climate change criteria.
K.K3: How the organisation’s strategy is sensitive to stakeholder perceptions and how this knowledge informs priorities at a tactical level. This may include sustainability, ethical, or climate change related criteria.
K.K4: How applicable contractual and commercial requirements for quality affect the organisation’s performance objectives for their specific products / services.
K.K5: The methods and tools for identifying customers/stakeholders and gathering information about their requirements including the tools for analysing and prioritising customer/stakeholder quality requirements using tools such as Kano model.
K.K6: How to convert quality requirements into performance measures objectives using tools such as Critical to Quality Trees (CTQ Trees), requirements matrices and operational definition.
K.K7: Risk and opportunity management, including the risk and opportunity management principles, framework and processes, types of risk/opportunity associated with new product/service development and improvement, process and supply chain management and methods and tools for identifying, assessing, and mitigating risks/realising opportunities, such as risk and opportunity register, risk and opportunity matrix, Fault Tree Analysis (FTA), Failure Mode and Effects Analysis.
K.K8: Products/services life cycle stages (such as Capture, Design and Development, Integration, Production, Support and Closure) and the implication for quality
K.K9: Concept of process design and how this supports specific organisational objectives using tools such as process flowchart, value stream mapping and SIPOC (Supplier, Input, Process, Output, and Customer).
K.K10: Tools and techniques for managing the organisation’s specific products / services to meet customer requirements such as Quality Function Deployment, Lean Product Development and Design for Manufacturing.
K.K11: How to plan, measure, manage and monitor organisation’s quality objectives.
K.K12: Understand the purposes for auditing and how to plan, conduct, report and follow up an audit.
K.K13: When to apply a range of business improvement approaches tools and techniques such as Problem definition, measurement systems analysis, Basic data analysis, graphical data analysis, use of software tools for data analysis, root cause analysis, identification and assessment of improvement options, process control tools.
K.K14: The key considerations (such as political, economical, social, technological, legal and environmental) and approaches necessary (such as Tuckman’s Storming, Norming, Forming and Performing) to enable change in organisations, products or services.
K.K15: The company’s key drivers for change (internal and external) may influence priorities and objectives.
K.K16: How to promote the right behaviours to create a quality culture in the organisation and how this leads to organisational performance improvements.
K.K17: The techniques used for improving awareness and performance in relation to quality objectives and requirements.
K.K18: Learn how different sources and methods will aid in maintaining own development in the quality profession.
K.K19: Principles of the foundation of Quality and Quality Management System.

S.S1: Identify, interpret and apply relevant legal, governmental or industry regulations affecting the organisation.
S.S2: Communicate using appropriate methods (verbal, written, visual) to influence internal and external stakeholders, using appropriate questioning techniques such as open questions, leading questions.
S.S3: Identify, collect and analyse relevant quality data using appropriate tools and techniques such as Pareto analysis, statistical methods and trending analysis.
S.S4: Apply methods and tools to improve the quality performance of processes, products and services such as production control plans, standardised work, use of failure modes and effects.
S.S5: Identify, analyse and prioritise quality specific risks and opportunities.  Support the development, implementation and effectiveness of resulting actions.
S.S6: Plan and conduct system, product or process audits.
S.S7: Assess the effectiveness of the measurement systems using tool such as Measurement Systems Analysis.
S.S8: Identify requirements from technical documents, commercial input or stakeholder statements and converting to definitions that can drive the organisations processes
S.S9: Identify gaps in process performance and develop improvement plans to close gaps.
S.S10: Apply structured problem solving including identification, definition, measurement, analysis, improvement and control methods and tools.
S.S11: Communicate organisational quality strategy to all levels of the organisation.
S.S12: Identify who the internal and external stakeholders are and their current and optimal positions (such as hostile, help it work, opposed, uncooperative, indifferent, hesitant, enthusiastic support) required to support quality related activities.

B.B1: Promote actively best practices and continuous improvement.
B.B2: Operates diligently with professionalism considering a wider picture.
B.B3: Act with integrity by being open and honest.
B.B4: Always put customers at the heart of every task.
B.B5: Seek continuous professional development opportunities such as self-reflection, gathering information, producing personal development plans and keeping up to date on sector/organisation regulation.

K.K1: Local and national legislation, policies and procedures within their scope of practice, professional and regulatory codes of conduct; the importance of working within boundaries of practice; the range of physical, psychological and diagnostic interventions within their scope of practice.
K.K2: Professional Standards Authority (PSA) and Academy for Healthcare Science (AHCS) standards and adheres to the Institute of Medical Illustrators (IMI) Code of Professional Conduct.
K.K3: Relevant anatomy, physiology and pathology, anatomical positioning and medical terminology.
K.K4: Key patient signs and symptoms in order to recognise and document their condition.
K.K5: Different patient needs and rights in relation to dignity, equality, diversity, inclusion and privacy, communication styles and clinical conditions e.g. dementia or learning disabilities.
K.K6: Protocols in relation to the appropriate use of Personal Protective Equipment (PPE).
K.K7: The underpinning knowledge of consent, how to obtain consent and the procedures to follow when consent cannot be obtained.
K.K8: Human anatomy and physiology to image the area of interest, using anatomical landmarks and the importance of patient positioning to obtain the photograph required.
K.K9: Safe patient moving and handling techniques.
K.K10: The process for escalation of unexpected findings identified on images to ensure optimum patient care.
K.K11: The procedures relating to major incident and their role within the Major Incident Response Team.
K.K12: Different methods of communication including verbal and non-verbal communication; the effect of own body language and attitude on others and the need for active listening skills.
K.K13: Legal, ethical and professional principles associated with equality, diversity, inclusion and safeguarding, including the use of consent chaperones when direct physical contact is needed.
K.K14: Patient confidentiality and awareness of responsibility to maintain it in line with ethical and legislative frameworks.
K.K15: Data Protection Regulations and consequences of good and poor data quality on the patient experience/pathway including the need for accurate record keeping (data capture and data processing).
K.K16: Different roles and scopes of practice for those under own supervision.
K.K17: Both good and bad practice, with the ability to deliver constructive feedback.
K.K18: Photographic, video and specialist imaging equipment and techniques.
K.K19: Post production imaging software techniques.
K.K20: How images are captured and appropriately shared and stored.
K.K21: Service management and the principles of service improvement.
K.K22: Current trends in relevant legislation, the profession and the wider healthcare service and how to use evidence-based practice in daily work.
K.K23: Different research methodologies and how to critically analyse research.
K.K24: Cyber security relevant to the clinical photography service.
K.K25: The application of different photographic, video and imaging procedures and techniques including standard representational photography relevant to a broad range of clinical conditions and settings. This includes clinical work such as ophthalmology, dermatology, dental, surgical and non-clinical such as public relations /corporate.
K.K26: Specific legislation relating to safeguarding policies and practices, (adults and paediatrics).
K.K27: Multi-disciplinary team structures and multi-agency working e.g. non-accidental injuries (NAI), police, coroner, social workers and solicitors.
K.K28: Photographic and imaging processes, procedure and adherence to protocols in relation to clinical trials.
K.K29: The theory of the audit cycle, including quality control methods, analysis of results and how to take appropriate action.

S.S1: Manage time and resources and prioritise workload according to clinical needs.
S.S2: Build and sustain professional relationships and work independently, as part of the clinical photography team, as part of a multi-disciplinary team, providing supervision as appropriate.
S.S3: Communicate appropriately with patients, their families, carers and clinical staff at all levels when exercising the duties.
S.S4: Collate and record information from different sources and critically evaluate that information to make a logical, informed decision, and communicate decisions appropriately and in a timely way.
S.S5: Analyse and critically assess a clinical or professional situation and respond accordingly, raising concerns as appropriate.
S.S6: Analyse results of audits and quality control, present findings and take appropriate action.
S.S7: Use professional photographic and video techniques and adapting as required to each clinical situation. For example selects and operates a range of equipment, maintains patient dignity and comfort, communicates with patients and colleagues to ensure the safe movement of patients into imaging position.
S.S8: Recognise normal and abnormal image appearances and use critical judgement when to act upon them.
S.S9: Assess image standards technically against national guidelines and local protocols, in terms of both image capture and patient positioning.
S.S10: Respect and maintain patient privacy and dignity at all times in all environments, including emergency situations.
S.S11: Maintain accurate and confidential patient records, paper and/or electronic as appropriate.
S.S12: Maintain data protection and patient confidentiality in clinical photography practice and complete relevant documentation.
S.S13: Use appropriate Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) for staff, patients and members of the public, e.g. correct selection of PPE when on wards, in theatre or any other clinical situation.
S.S14: Work in accordance with local and national legislation, policies and procedures, the professional and regulatory codes of conduct and within scope of practice.
S.S15: Supervise, facilitate learning, motivate and share good practice with others.
S.S16: Use Quality Assurance methods to analyse and interpret results and act upon them in a safe manner.
S.S17: Read and critically analyse research articles and relevant legislation and apply research findings to practice.
S.S18: Select and use post production software techniques in order to deliver high quality clinical photographic and video records and manage workflow. E.g. make adjustments to the exposure or contrast of the image to improve clinical value while maintaining the integrity of the image.
S.S19: Use image storing and delivery systems to ensure images are appropriately and securely shared and stored e.g. various access levels for image database in accordance with consent levels and data protection. Analyse and critically assess different image storage and delivery systems and contribute to the procurement of a solution, ensuring that it adheres to Data Protection, GDPR, and IT security.
S.S20: Contribute to continuous improvement of service and personal practice through reflection and adaptation.
S.S21: Critically evaluate and employ current trends in the profession and wider healthcare environment.
S.S22: Apply different photographic, video and imaging procedures and techniques relevant to a broad range of clinical conditions and settings, (including standard representational photography, dermoscopy, UV/IR as appropriate).
S.S23: Provide photographic/video evidence to standards appropriate for safeguarding purposes to support the multi-agency and multi-disciplinary work.
S.S24: Adapt techniques in order to adhere to clinical trials protocols.
S.S25: Demonstrate emotional intelligence, critical thinking, independent decision-making skills, problem solving skills and professional judgement.

B.B1: Empathy, compassion and underpinning emotional resilience.
B.B2: Confident, flexible and adaptable within own scope of practice.
B.B3: Be a reflective practitioner.
B.B4: Act with their code of conduct, honesty, integrity and respect in all interactions, including when direct physical contact is needed, obtaining the appropriate consent.
B.B5: Reflect on own impact on others, take responsibility and be accountable for own actions. Sensitively challenge others and raise issues when appropriate.

ST0862.name: Domestic and sexual abuse support worker

K.K1: Organisational policies and procedures for responding to crisis situations.
K.K2: Organisational policies and procedures for risk assessment, safeguarding and safety planning and the tools which can be used.
K.K3: Gendered, historical, social, and intersectional contexts of domestic abuse and or sexual violence.
K.K4: Legislation and organisational policies and procedures relevant to the sector including those relating to anti-discrimination and equality.
K.K5: Case management systems used by own organisation, how they operate and monitor outcomes, and how they should be used within own role and in line with organisational policies and procedures.
K.K6: Organisational policies and procedures for supporting survivors of domestic abuse and or sexual violence, through police and court processes.
K.K7: Civil and criminal sanctions, remedies and orders available to hold perpetrators accountable and promote the safety of victims.
K.K8: Types of information available from external organisations and how to obtain it.
K.K9: Different communication aids that can be used when working remotely or face to face.
K.K10: The importance of communicating with survivors of domestic abuse and or sexual violence, considering their individual backgrounds and experiences of domestic abuse and or sexual violence.
K.K11: Professional boundaries and responsibilities of the role and the importance of maintaining independence.
K.K12: Models  of reflective practice and how reflection can be used to improve own practice.
K.K13: Programmes that are available to aid recovery and promote the empowerment of survivors of domestic abuse and or sexual violence and or how to deliver the programmes.
K.K14: Ways in which survivors of domestic abuse and or sexual violence can input into own organisation’s and national processes and governance.
K.K15: Sources of secondary and institutional abuse and ways of addressing these including through complaints procedures to promote the empowerment of survivors of domestic abuse and or sexual violence.
K.K16: The importance of raising awareness of accountability of perpetrators of domestic abuse and or sexual violence.
K.K17: Options available to survivors of domestic abuse and or sexual violence to access safe accommodation and secure a permanent address.
K.K18: Ways that survivors of domestic abuse and or sexual violence can access legal, financial, voluntary and statutory support services.
K.K19: How statutory homelessness duties apply, and local and regional policies on obtaining social housing.
K.K20: How to support survivors of domestic abuse and or sexual violence with child maintenance services applications.
K.K21: How immigration status can affect statutory access to healthcare and other services and support.
K.K22: Ways to support survivors of domestic abuse and or sexual violence who are not British citizens with accessing help and support.
K.K23: The availability and priorities of other agencies and support services for survivors of domestic abuse and or sexual violence.
K.K24: What constitutes a needs-led approach to safety.
K.K25: The impacts of domestic abuse on children and young people and how to support them.
K.K26: Ways to communicate with children and young people using technologies adapted to individual needs.
K.K27: How to design appropriate activities for children and young people.
K.K28: Ways to support and involve survivors who are parents when working with their children.
K.K29: Techniques to support and promote the empowerment of survivors of domestic abuse and or sexual violence when dealing with contact from children and young people and family court processes.
K.K30: The importance of carrying out preventative work and activities for children and young people, and the different programmes available.
K.K31: The rights, entitlements and support options available to children and young people, and how to access services.
K.K32: Agencies that support children and young people, and their referral and information requirements.
K.K33: Core values and empowerment models to adopt when supporting and working with survivors of sexual violence.
K.K34: Agencies that support survivors of child sexual abuse (CSA) and child sexual exploitation (CSE), and how to make referrals to these.
K.K35: When and how to challenge myths and stereotypes about survivors of sexual abuse.
K.K36: Impacts of sexual assault and sexual exploitation on children, young people, and adult survivors.
K.K37: Different ways that survivors of sexual abuse and violence may be impacted by their experiences, including cognitive, practical, emotional and behavioural responses and how to adapt provision of  support to meet their needs.
K.K38: Barriers to disclosure and how to identify and overcome them.
K.K39: The rights, entitlements and support options available to survivors of sexual violence, and how to access services and specialist advice.
K.K40: The agencies and support services in place for survivors of  sexual violence and how priorities may affect their availability.
K.K41: The range of impacts domestic abuse and or sexual violence can have on survivors and how to adapt service provision to support them.
K.K42: The importance of self-care and available support structures.

S.S1: Recognise crisis situations  and respond within the required timescales in line with organisational policies and procedures.
S.S2: Carry out risk assessments, safeguarding and safety planning using risk assessment tools.
S.S3: Assist and promote the empowerment of survivors of domestic abuse and or sexual violence who may have experienced inequality, discrimination and or forms of injustice.
S.S4: Engage with survivors of domestic abuse and or sexual violence from marginalised and disadvantaged groups and diverse social backgrounds and identities in ways that respond to their individual identities.
S.S5: Challenge intersectional gaps in service provision and or redress them.
S.S6: Raise awareness about the intersections of multiple forms of disadvantage and their relevance to domestic abuse and or sexual violence.
S.S7: Use own organisation’s case management systems in line with organisational policies and procedures.
S.S8: Use information sources to advise survivors of domestic abuse and or sexual violence on protective sanctions, orders and remedies they can access through court processes and or refer them to appropriate specialist advice.
S.S9: Support survivors of domestic abuse and or sexual violence through police investigations and or court processes within the context of own role.
S.S10: Act as an independent advocate for survivors of domestic abuse and or sexual violence.
S.S11: Adapt your communication method and style to meet the needs of the survivors of domestic abuse and or sexual violence.
S.S12: Use communication aids to meet survivors of domestic abuse and or sexual violence’ specific needs.
S.S13: Facilitate or assist in running programmes to aid recovery and promote the empowerment of survivors of domestic abuse and or sexual violence.
S.S14: Use trauma-informed and needs-led approaches when assisting survivors of domestic abuse and or sexual violence to participate in service provision activities.
S.S15: Raise awareness of the importance of accountability of perpetrators’ actions.
S.S16: Refer and signpost survivors of domestic abuse and or sexual violence to other relevant voluntary or statutory multi-agency partners.
S.S17: Reflect on own practice and access available supervision and case management support.
S.S18: Maintain own continuous  professional development and revise practice based on feedback received.
S.S19: Support survivors of domestic abuse and or sexual violence to access safe and permanent accommodation and advocate for their rights.
S.S20: Secure specialist legal and financial advice for survivors of domestic abuse and or sexual violence.
S.S21: Support survivors of domestic abuse and or sexual violence to be more aware of their rights and options and support with applications through child maintenance services when required.
S.S22: Work with survivors of domestic abuse and or sexual violence who are not British citizens to access relevant help and support for themselves and or their families including those with no recourse to public funds.
S.S23: Advocate for healthcare provision for all survivors of domestic abuse and or sexual violence, including those with no recourse to public funds.
S.S24: Use a range of interventions to support and promote the safety and wellbeing of survivors of domestic abuse and or sexual violence.
S.S25: Explore the emotional and practical needs of children and young people to support and help them to recover.
S.S26: Communicate with children and young people using technology to meet their needs and situations.
S.S27: Enable children and young people to co-design and adapt activities in a safe environment.
S.S28: Support and involve survivors of domestic abuse and or sexual violence in  their role as parents when  undertaking work with their children and young people.
S.S29: Develop and carry out preventative work and activities to support other professionals who work with children and young people in different contexts.
S.S30: Support and empower survivors of domestic abuse and or sexual violence and their children and or young people to ensure their voices are included within local and national responses and or processes related to domestic violence and or sexual abuse.
S.S31: Collaborate with different agencies when dealing with safeguarding children and young people.
S.S32: Work within the boundaries of legislation and organisational policies and procedures and apply these to the responsibilities of own role.
S.S33: Establish, develop and manage professional relationships with survivors of sexual violence, setting out the boundaries of the relationship within own area of competence.
S.S34: Work within the empowerment model to ensure that the service is client-led and recognises the impact of the victims and or survivors’ experiences of sexual abuse.
S.S35: Provide client-led practical and emotional support to survivors of domestic abuse and or sexual violence.
S.S36: Act as an institutional advocate for survivors of different forms of sexual abuse.
S.S37: Recognise the impacts of child sexual abuse and or child sexual exploitation on children and or young people and the longer-term impacts of child sexual abuse and or child sexual exploitation on adult survivors.
S.S38: Provide emotional support to survivors of domestic abuse and or sexual violence experiencing impacts of child sexual abuse and or child sexual exploitation.
S.S39: Promote the empowerment of survivors of sexual abuse to identify barriers to disclosure and work with them to over-come the barriers.
S.S40: Recognise the importance of self-care, taking appropriate action when required.
S.S41: Empower survivors to contribute to the co-design of services and provide them with a safe environment to do so.
S.S42: Provide services that reflect the specific needs of both children and young people and adult survivors of sexual violence.
S.S43: Assist survivors of sexual violence with the referral processes of external support agencies.
S.S44: Support survivors to access specialist information to raise their awareness of their rights and options.

B.B1: Treat people with dignity and respect.
B.B2: Be caring and compassionate.
B.B3: Speak up and challenge.
B.B4: Be competent, reliable and committed.
B.B5: Be honest and accountable.
B.B6: Be non-discriminating anti-oppressive and anti-racist.
B.B7: Be survivor-centred.

K.K1: The characteristics and categories of disabilities (including temporary, situational and permanent disabilities) and how these affect individuals.
K.K2: Commonly used accessibility guidelines (for example the Web Content Accessibility Guidelines, EN301549 and Section508), methods to implement and achieve conformance and how these assist users with disabilities.
K.K3: How accessibility is managed and integrated within an enterprise environment including policies, and reasonable adjustments.
K.K4: How to utilise productivity suites ensuring output is accessible and advising on the implementation of accessibility features and content.
K.K5: The implications and effect on users with disabilities, and UK businesses of international disability legislation (including Equality Act 2010, Public Sector Bodies (Websites and Mobile Applications) No. 2 Accessibility Regulations 2018 and Accessible Canada Act C81 2018).
K.K6: How to present using relevant technologies and aids in a manner that communicates the information (including a business case) to the chosen target audience, and understand the key facts and figures relating to accessibility.
K.K7:
The process to set-up, moderate and conduct activities relating to hosting workshops
including moderation usability testing, user research testing and end-user testing.
K.K8: The basics of Hyper Text Mark-up Language, Cascading Style Sheets, JavaScript and Accessibility Rich Internet Applications, and how these should be used to enhance the accessibility of services (for example alt text, dynamic content, colour contrast).
K.K9: How mobile applications are developed using appropriate development applications and the basics of the languages used.
K.K10: The fundamentals of the Web Content Accessibility Guidelines (WCAG), including the purpose, application and utilisation of WCAG in a variety of situations (for example web, mobile, desktop applications).
K.K11: The basic principles of project management (including project life cycle methodologies), and how accessibility considerations are integrated.
K.K12: The accessibility considerations when designing and developing an application's user experience using related standards and guidelines including ISO9241 Ergonomics of human-system interaction, The Principles of Universal Design by the Centre for Universal Design and the Web Content Accessibility Guidelines to create an inclusive design.
K.K13: How to use assistive technologies (both digital and physical), their use-cases and functionality, degrees of complexity (hi-tech, low-tech and  no-tech).
K.K14: How assistive technologies interact with other digital technologies.
K.K15: The principles and application of testing methodologies to accessibility criteria and assistive technologies, and the impact of accessibility findings (including test documentation).
K.K16: How to construct a business report (for example document layout and contents).
K.K17: The purpose and importance of reasonable adjustments, and how these are managed.
K.K18: Best practices regarding digital security including General Data Protection Regulations 2018, how these are applied to an organisation and the factors which can result in these being effective and how these can be mitigated.
K.K19: The security considerations relating to the installation and utilisation of assistive technologies, how these tools may be misidentified by common security practices and the methods to mitigate such security constraints.
K.K20: How to communicate through different mediums, including tailoring communications to different user groups.
K.K21: How to use incident management tools, triage incidents and appropriately communicate with assistive technology users.
K.K22: How to audit digital applications (including web and mobile applications) against digital assistive technologies including screen readers, screen magnifiers, speech to text and literacy aids.
K.K23: The preparation and processes required to conduct training with a variety of user groups for example project managers, web designers, developers, end users and line management.

K.K1: Statistical knowledge of methods that are commonly used in medical research and enable effective analysis of data in research studies.
K.K2: Project management techniques and strategies (meeting timelines, managing budgets and contingency planning).
K.K3: Statistical knowledge that enables effective research study design (for example: the drug development process;  Study design - parallel group, cross-over, adaptive, placebo controlled, active comparator, open label; Methods allied to different trial objectives – superiority, non-inferiority and equivalence; Randomisation and blinding; Methods for data presentation; Estimands; Missing data strategies; Multiple testing and alpha control methods; Simulation; Sample size and power calculations; Complex innovative designs (CID)).
K.K4: Strategic approaches to risk and compliance in relation to study design and data collection and interpretation.
K.K5: Communication and influencing techniques and strategies, both written and oral (including presenting).
K.K6: Leadership and management techniques and strategies, including coaching and mentoring techniques.
K.K7: The structure and function of a multidisciplinary team and the role of the Medical Statistician within it, and how to achieve effective partnership working.
K.K8: Learning and development strategies, to enable personal and professional development, including giving and receiving feedback and critical reflection.
K.K9: The importance of continuing personal and professional development and the role of critical reflection in maintaining fitness to practice.
K.K10: Awareness of key regulatory authorities and documentation relevant to the study they are working on  (International Council for Harmonisation (ICH) of Technical Requirements for Pharmaceuticals for Human Use Guidelines; European Medicines Evaluation Agency (EMEA); Pharmaceutical and Medical Devices Agency (PMDA); Food and Drug Administration (FDA); The National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE); Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency (MHRA); Therapeutic area specific guidance; Good Clinical Practice (GCP); Good Laboratory Practice (GLP);Good Manufacturing Practice (GMP).
K.K11: Ethics in clinical and non-clinical research.
K.K12: Methods to safely store and handle data in line with national and international data protection and cyber security regulations.

S.S1: Interpret, apply and comply with legislation, statutory frameworks, professional codes of practice and guidance, including quality control.
S.S2: Select and perform the appropriate statistical technique that are commonly used in medical research relevant to the given data set and objective.
S.S3: Use statistical software (SAS® and R or other appropriate software) to perform the required statistical methods that are commonly used in medical research.
S.S4: Use statistical software (SAS® and R or other appropriate software) to create appropriate graphical and tabular representations of the data to aid interpretation that are commonly used in medical research (such as summary tables, individual data listings, histograms, boxplots, scatter plots, line charts, bar charts, frequency tables).
S.S5: Assess and interpret the results of data analysis and communicate these to peers in written and verbal discussion (such as written medical statistical reports and oral presentations).
S.S6: Adapt communication technique when communicating statistical concepts to different audiences including people from a non-scientific background.

S.S10: Lead projects to completion within agreed and defined timescales and project parameters.

S.S13: Evaluate new statistical methodologies relevant to medical statistics.
S.S14: Facilitate learning and provide feedback to others as appropriate.
S.S15: Critically review own practice and identify areas for personal and professional development.
S.S16: Collaborate with other professionals to deliver mutually agreed outcomes.
S.S17: Contribute to the wider statistical community (including their own organisation), through sharing knowledge, such as peer review, authorship and co-authorship of papers for publication or presentation at conference.
