S.S11: Provide remote/F2F support to resolve customer requirements
S.S12: Maintain a safe working environment for own personal safety and others in line with Health & Safety appropriate to the task
S.S13: Identify and scope the best solution informed by the system data associated with the task
S.S14: Test and evaluate the system's performance and compliance with customer requirements.
S.S15: Escalate non routine problems in line with procedures
S.S16: Use basic scripting to execute the relevant tasks for example PowerShell, Linux
S.S17: Carry out routine maintenance across systems, (such as IT, Communications), ensuring organisational compliance at all times
S.S18: Apply the necessary security, in line with access and/or encryption requirements
S.S19: Use a range of Cabling or Connectors equipment in line with technical requirements for example physically or remotely
S.S20: Test and evaluate network environments
S.S21: Monitor performance and usage of a network
S.S22: Deploy applications on a network
S.S23: Set up storage and data access for staff
S.S24: Apply necessary security measures, in line with access requirements to a network
S.S25: Carry out routine maintenance across network systems, ensuring organisational compliance
S.S26: Monitor network-related workloads including DNS and firewalls
S.S27: Install or undertake basic upgrades, either physically or remotely
S.S28: Establish digital communication or telecommunications systems through, for example cabling and connecting equipment.
S.S29: Identify a range of tools and or diagnostic equipment, for example, Hardware or Software components, to resolve Communications or Telecommunications requirements.

S.S31: Use information necessary to identify operational issues and rectify or escalate accordingly in line with policy

B.B1: Works professionally, taking initiative as appropriate and acting with an ethical approach
B.B2: Communicates technical and non-technical information in a variety of situations to support effective working with internal or external stakeholders
B.B3: Demonstrates a productive and organised approach to their work
B.B4: Self-motivated, for example takes responsibility to complete the job.

K.K1: Role of the digital learning designer, types of activities and projects they may engage with, and how they relate to other roles within their organisation, and as part of a rapidly evolving professional community.
K.K2: Operational, technical, regulatory, organisational, quality requirements, and evolving requisites such as carbon consciousness, that need to be gathered to inform a digital learning design project or initiative.
K.K3: Project management approaches and how they may be combined with digital learning design and development methodologies to achieve objectives.
K.K4: Information needed to establish learning needs, project requirements, and inform digital learning design processes.
K.K5: Diverse needs, abilities, and motivations of learners and the challenges and opportunities they may encounter in different learning contexts.
K.K6: Concepts and principles that underpin a range of learning theories, such as the differences between pedagogy and andragogy, and how to interpret them to guide the design of learning experiences and the selection and application of technologies for learning.
K.K7: Tools and approaches that can be used to facilitate the application of learning theories and methodologies within collaborative settings, such as design workshops and course templates.
K.K8: Diversity of delivery modes and formats used in different contexts, including self-directed, user generated synchronous, and asynchronous learning, and also formats that combine digital and non-digital features, such as hybrid and blended learning.
K.K9: Digital content and platform types that may be used to facilitate or enhance learning, including learning management systems, interactive modules, videos, podcasts, immersive formats, user generated content and games.
K.K10: Approaches and techniques for articulating aims and objectives in order to generate a learning journey and curriculum conducive to, and in alignment with, measurable outcomes and assessment strategies.
K.K11: Principles of high-quality digital learning design, including accessibility, user interface (UI), user experience (UX), visual communication, and use of branding and style guides.
K.K12: Constraints and benefits of different types of technologies in order to determine their suitability for facilitating the design, development, or implementation of digital learning experiences and achieving learning objectives.
K.K13: Features, functionality and technical standards associated with different platforms and software used in the design, development, and curation of digital learning experiences, and how these can be combined and configured to optimise user experiences.
K.K14: Measurements and methodologies that can be applied to assure and evaluate the quality and effectiveness of learning products and experiences.
K.K15: Sources of professional guidance, support, frameworks, and communities of practice available to stay up to date and continually develop skills in digital learning design practice.

S.S1: Develop communication strategies to manage and engage with project stakeholders and use appropriate methods and technologies to facilitate and document communications.
S.S2: Plan and facilitate discussions and activities to initiate and progress work, analyse and interpret information, gather requirements, and engage effectively with stakeholders.
S.S3: Gather, analyse, and interpret information about learners and learning environments, such as learner feedback, learning analytics, needs analysis, and profile mapping, to inform the learning design approach and technical requirements.
S.S4: Select and apply appropriate learning theories and instructional design models and methodologies to inform digital learning design approaches, outputs, and implementation strategies.
S.S5: Use instructional design tools, taxonomies, and frameworks to articulate meaningful learning objectives and learning content, through scripts or storyboards.
S.S6: Use learning objectives to map a learning journey to facilitate and measure their achievement through formative and summative activities.
S.S7: Interpret and synthesise information sources and concepts to organise content and re-present information to align with learning objectives and meet learner needs.
S.S8: Communicate concepts, designs, and strategies to suit different stakeholder audiences and facilitate Collaborative processes, using appropriate formats and technologies, such as face to face and virtual presentations, storyboards, and project documentation.
S.S9: Select and use distinct software, hardware, platforms, and tools to design, develop, and implement digital learning products and experiences.
S.S10: Use professional techniques to script, edit, create, and produce a range of multimedia formats, including text, imagery, audio, and video.
S.S11: Configure and apply regulatory, professional, organisational and technical standards and techniques including accessibility standards and data security to the sustainable design and formatting of documents, multimedia, user interface, digital products and platforms.
S.S12: Conduct the quality checking and testing of digital outputs whilst ensuring there is ongoing improvement of quality assurance processes with internally and or with target users, prior to implementation, including proofreading, updating, renewing and revising existing content, application of branding, accessibility and functionality.
S.S13: Evaluate the effectiveness of digital learning products and experiences in achieving project requirements and intended learning objectives, using appropriate tools and methodologies.
S.S14: Plan and manage your own design and development activities and collaborate with others to achieve shared objectives and outputs.
S.S15: Organise and manage digital assets and outputs on a platform in accordance with organisational or professional standards, to maintain regulatory compliance, version control, efficient collaborative processes, and quality assurance.
S.S16: Assimilate and use evaluative information to contribute to the review of organisational policies, processes and systems.

B.B1: Champions the diverse needs, interests, and wellbeing of colleagues and learners, to create inclusive solutions.
B.B2: Takes responsibility and uses own initiative to solve problems, finding opportunities for improvement and innovation.
B.B3: Driven to keep up to date with the latest digital learning design trends, tools, techniques, and practices through relevant community networks to support the ongoing development of their own skills and knowledge and the sharing of that knowledge to develop the skills of others.
B.B4: Comfortable interacting with and learning from people from different backgrounds, demographics, and specialist areas.
B.B5: Reliable, objective, and capable of both independent and team working.
B.B6: Explore and reflect on how people learn and the interplay between learning and technology, sharing their knowledge to inspire others.
B.B7: Collaborate with other team members and wider stakeholders to continuously improve policies, processes, and systems to meet organisational needs.
B.B8: Welcomes feedback to build constructive relationships and improve practice.
B.B9: Acts with integrity with respect to ethical, legal, and regulatory frameworks ensuring the protection of personal data, safety, and security.

K.K1: The current ethical dilemmas in medical science and healthcare practice; the ethical issues that can arise in everyday clinical decision-making; and apply ethical reasoning to situations which may be encountered in the first years after graduation
K.K2: The potential impact of their attitudes, values, beliefs, perceptions and personal biases (which may be unconscious) on individuals and groups and know how to identify personal strategies to address this
K.K3: The principles of quality assurance, quality improvement, quality planning and quality control, and in which contexts these approaches should be used to maintain and improve quality and safety
K.K4: How errors can happen in practice and that errors should be shared openly to be able to learn from own and others’ errors and promote a culture of safety
K.K5: Basic human factors principles and practice at individual, team, organisational and system levels and the importance of recognising and responding to opportunities for improvement to manage or mitigate risks
K.K6: The principles and methods of quality improvement to improve practice (for example, plan, do, study, act or action research)
K.K7: The value of national surveys and audits for measuring the quality of care.
K.K8: The complex medical needs, goals and priorities of patients, the factors that can affect a patient’s health and wellbeing and how these interact. These include psychological and sociological considerations that can also affect patients’ health
K.K9: Signs and symptoms of abuse or neglect and systems for sharing information, recording and raising concerns, obtaining advice, making referrals and taking action
K.K10: Legislation that may result in the deprivation of liberty to protect the safety of individuals and society
K.K11: How addiction (to drugs, alcohol, smoking or other substances), poor nutrition, self neglect, environmental exposure, or financial or social deprivation contribute to ill health.
K.K12: The principles of equality legislation in the context of patient care.
K.K13: The role of doctors in contributing to the management and leadership of the health service
K.K14: The principles of how to build teams and maintain effective team work and interpersonal relationships with a clear shared purpose
K.K15: The impact of own behaviour on others
K.K16: Theoretical models of leadership and management that may be applied to practice
K.K17: The communication techniques and strategies that can be used with the patient, their relatives, carers or other advocates
K.K18: How normal human structure and function and physiological processes applies, including at the extremes of age, in children and young people and during pregnancy and childbirth
K.K19: The relevant scientific processes underlying common and important disease processes
K.K20: Justify, through an explanation of the underlying fundamental principles and clinical reasoning, the selection of appropriate investigations for common clinical conditions and diseases
K.K21: The principles of holding a fitness for work conversation with patients, including how to assess social, physical, psychological and biological factors supporting the functional capacity of the patient, and how to make referrals to colleagues and other agencies
K.K22: Principles and knowledge relating to anatomy, biochemistry, cell biology, genetics, genomics and personalised medicine, immunology, microbiology, molecular biology, nutrition, pathology, pharmacology and clinical pharmacology, and physiology
K.K23: Clinical phenomena and the clinical reasoning in how to formulate a differential diagnosis and management plan
K.K24: Describe and illustrate from examples of normal human behaviour at an individual level
K.K25: Integrate psychological concepts of health, illness and disease into patient care and apply theoretical frameworks of psychology to explain the varied responses of individuals, groups and societies to disease
K.K26: The relationship between psychological and medical conditions and how psychological factors impact on risk and treatment outcomes
K.K27: The impact of patients’ behaviours on treatment and care and how these are influenced by psychological factors
K.K28: How patients adapt to major life changes, such as bereavement, and the adjustments that might occur in these situations
K.K29: Appropriate strategies for managing patients with substance misuse or risk of self-harm or suicide
K.K30: How psychological aspects of behaviour, such as response to error, can influence behaviour in the workplace in a way that can affect health and safety and know how to apply this understanding to their personal behaviours and those of colleagues
K.K31: The range of settings in which patients receive care, including in the community, in patients’ homes and in primary and secondary care provider settings
K.K32: Explain and illustrate from their own professional experience the importance of integrating patients’ care across different settings to ensure person-centred care
K.K33: Emerging trends in settings where care is provided, for example the shift for more care to be delivered in the community rather than in secondary care settings
K.K34: The relationship between healthcare and social care and how they interact
K.K35: That there are differences in health and social care systems across the four nations of the UK
K.K36: How to access information about the different systems, including the role of private medical services in the UK
K.K37: The processes by which doctors make and test a differential diagnosis and how to prepare to explain own clinical reasoning to others
K.K38: The potential consequences of over-diagnosis and over-treatment
K.K39: The concept of wellness or wellbeing as well as illness, and be able to help and empower people to achieve the best health possible, including promoting lifestyle changes such as smoking cessation, avoiding substance misuse and maintaining a healthy weight through physical activity and diet
K.K40: The health of a population using basic epidemiological techniques and measurements
K.K41: Evaluate the environmental, social, behavioural and cultural factors which influence health and disease in different populations
K.K42: The principles underlying the development of health, health service policy, and clinical guidelines, including principles of health economics, equity, and sustainable healthcare
K.K43: The role of ecological, environmental and occupational hazards in ill-health and the ways to mitigate their effects
K.K44: The role and impact of nutrition to the health of individual patients and societies
K.K45: The determinants of health and disease and variations in healthcare delivery and medical practice from a global perspective and explain the impact that global changes may have on local health and wellbeing
K.K46: How society influences and determines the behaviour of individuals and groups and apply this to the care of patients
K.K47: The sociological concepts of health, illness and disease and apply these to the care of patients
K.K48: Apply theoretical frameworks of sociology to explain the varied responses of individuals, groups and societies to disease
K.K49: The sociological factors that contribute to illness, the course of the disease and the success of treatment and apply these to the care of patients − including issues relating to health inequalities and the social determinants of health, the links between occupation and health, and the effects of poverty and affluence
K.K50: The sociological aspects of behavioural change and treatment concordance and compliance, and apply these models to the care of patients as part of person-centred decision making
K.K51: Describe and illustrate from examples the spectrum of normal human behaviour at an individual level
K.K52: Integrate psychological concepts of health, illness and disease into patient care and apply theoretical frameworks of psychology to explain the varied responses of individuals, groups and societies to disease
K.K53: The relationship between psychological and medical conditions and how psychological factors impact on risk and treatment outcomes
K.K54: The impact of patients’ behaviours on treatment and care and how these are influenced by psychological factors
K.K55: How patients adapt to major life changes, such as bereavement, and the adjustments that might occur in these situations
K.K56: Appropriate strategies for managing patients with substance misuse or risk of self-harm or suicide
K.K57: How psychological aspects of behaviour, such as response to error, can influence behaviour in the workplace in a way that can affect health and safety and apply this understanding to their personal behaviours and those of colleagues
K.K58: Medications and medication actions:•	therapeutics and pharmacokinetics •	medication side effects and interactions, including for multiple treatments, long term physical and mental conditions and non-prescribed drugs•	the role of pharmacogenomics and antimicrobial stewardship
K.K59: The role of clinical pharmacologists and pharmacists in making decisions about medications
K.K60: The challenges of safe prescribing for patients with long term physical and mental conditions or multiple morbidities and medications, in pregnancy, at extremes of age and at the end of life
K.K61: The existence and range of complementary therapies, why patients use them, and how this might affect the safety of other types of treatment that patients receive
K.K62: The challenges of delivering the required standards of care when prescribing and providing treatment and advice remotely, for example via online services
K.K63: The risks of over-prescribing and excessive use of medications and apply these principles to prescribing practice
K.K64: Professional and legal responsibilities when accessing information sources in relation to patient care, health promotion, giving advice and information to patients, and research and education
K.K65: The role of doctors in contributing to the collection and analysis of patient data at a population level to identify trends in wellbeing, disease and treatment, and to improve healthcare and healthcare system
K.K66: The role and hierarchy of evidence in clinical practice and decision making with patients
K.K67: The role and value of qualitative and quantitative methodological approaches to scientific enquiry
K.K68: Basic principles and ethical implications of research governance including recruitment into trials and research programmes
K.K70: The concept of personalised medicine to deliver care tailored to the needs of individual patients

S.S1: Demonstrate clinical responsibilities and role of the doctor
S.S2: Maintain confidentiality and respect patients’ dignity and privacy
S.S3: Manage their time and prioritise effectively
S.S4: Recognise and acknowledge their own personal and professional limits and seek help from colleagues and supervisors when necessary, including when they feel that patient safety may be compromised
S.S5: Protect patients from any risk posed by their own health including: •	the risks to their health and to patient safety posed by self-prescribing medication and substance misuse•	the risks to their health and to patient safety posed by fatigue – they must apply strategies to limit •	the impact of fatigue on their health.
S.S6: Demonstrate person-centred care and include patients and, where appropriate, their relatives, carers or other advocates in decisions about their healthcare needs
S.S7: Seek patient consent, or the consent of the person who has parental responsibility in the case of children and young people, or seek the views of those with lasting power of attorney or independent mental capacity advocates
S.S8: Provide information about options for investigations, treatment and care in a way that enables patients to make decisions about their own care
S.S9: Assess the mental capacity of a patient to make a particular decision, including when the lack of capacity is temporary, knowing when and how to take action.
S.S10: Act appropriately, with an inclusive approach, towards patients and colleagues
S.S11: Raise and escalate concerns through informal communication with colleagues and through formal clinical governance and monitoring systems about:•	patient safety and quality of care •	bullying, harassment and undermining
S.S12: Demonstrate commitment to professional development and lifelong learning
S.S13: Mentor and teach other learners in the multi-professional team
S.S14: Access and analyse reliable sources of current clinical evidence and guidance and have established methods for making sure their practice is consistent with these
S.S15: Engage with revalidation, maintaining a professional development portfolio which includes evidence of reflection, achievements, learning needs and feedback from patients and colleagues
S.S16: Engage in induction and orientation activities, learn from experience and feedback, and respond constructively to the outcomes of appraisals, performance reviews and assessments.
S.S17: Adhere to the principles of the legal framework in which medicine is practised in the jurisdiction in which they are practising, and is aware of where further information on relevant legislation can be found
S.S18: Self-monitor, self-care and seek appropriate advice and support, including by being registered with a GP and engaging with them to maintain their own physical and mental health
S.S19: Manage the personal and emotional challenges of coping with work and workload, uncertainty and change
S.S20: Develop a range of coping strategies, such as reflection, debriefing, handing over to another colleague, peer support and asking for help, to recover from challenges and set-backs.
S.S21: Place patients’ needs and safety at the centre of the care process
S.S22: Promote and maintain health and safety in all care settings and escalate concerns to colleagues where appropriate, including when providing treatment and advice remotely
S.S23: Learn from their own and others’ errors to promote a culture of safety
S.S24: Apply measures to prevent the spread of infection, and apply the principles of infection prevention and control
S.S25: Apply quality improvement to improve practice and seek ways to continually improve the use and prioritisation of resources
S.S26: Adapt management proposals and strategies for dealing with health problems to take into consideration patients’ preferences, social needs, multiple morbidities, frailty and long term physical and mental conditions
S.S27: Work collaboratively with patients, their relatives, carers or other advocates, in planning their care, negotiating and sharing information appropriately and supporting patient self-care
S.S28: Work collaboratively with other health and care professionals and organisations when working with patients, particularly those with multiple morbidities, frailty and long term physical and mental conditions
S.S29: Recognise how treatment and care can place an additional burden on patients and make decisions to reduce this burden where appropriate, particularly where patients have multiple conditions or are approaching the end of life
S.S30: Manage the uncertainty of diagnosis and treatment success or failure and communicate this openly and sensitively with patients, their relatives, carers or other advocates
S.S31: Evaluate the clinical complexities, uncertainties and emotional challenges involved in caring for patients who are approaching the end of their lives and demonstrate the relevant communication techniques and strategies that can be used with the patient, their relatives, carers or other advocates.
S.S32: Safeguard children, young people, adults and older people, using appropriate systems for sharing information, recording and raising concerns, obtaining advice, making referrals and taking action
S.S33: Take a history that includes consideration of the patient’s autonomy, views and any associated vulnerability, and reflect this in the care plan and referrals
S.S34: Assess the needs of, and support required, for children, young people and adults and older people who are the victims of domestic, sexual or other abuse
S.S35: Assess the needs of, and support required, for people with a learning disability
S.S36: Assess the needs of, and support required, for people with mental health conditions
S.S37: Adhere to the professional responsibilities in relation to procedures performed for non-medical reasons, such as female genital mutilation and cosmetic interventions
S.S38: Take action by seeking advice from colleagues and making appropriate referrals where addiction (to drugs, alcohol, smoking or other substances), poor nutrition, self neglect, environmental exposure, or financial or social deprivation are contributing to ill health
S.S39: Maintain effective teamwork and interpersonal relationships with a clear shared purpose
S.S40: Demonstrate leadership and the ability to accept and support leadership by others
S.S41: Contribute to effective interdisciplinary team working with doctors from all care settings and specialties, and with other health and social care professionals for the provision of safe and high-quality care
S.S42: Work effectively with colleagues in ways that best serve the interests of patients.
S.S43: Safely pass on information using clear and appropriate spoken, written and electronic communication: •	at handover in a hospital setting and when handing over and maintaining continuity of care in primary, community and social care settings •	when referring to colleagues for investigations or advice •	when things go wrong, for example when errors happen questioning colleagues during handover where appropriate •	working collaboratively and supportively with colleagues to share experiences and challenges that encourage learning •	responding appropriately to requests from colleagues to attend patients •	applying flexibility, adaptability and a problem-solving approach to shared decision making with colleagues
S.S44: Recognise and show respect for the roles and expertise of other health and social care professionals and doctors from all specialties and care settings in the context of working and learning as a multi-professional team.
S.S45: Communicate clearly, sensitively and effectively with patients, their relatives, carers or other advocates, and colleagues from medical and other professions, by: •	listening, sharing and responding•	demonstrating empathy and compassion•	demonstrating effective verbal and non-verbal interpersonal skills•	making adjustments to their communication approach if needed, for example for people who communicate differently due to a disability or who speak a different first language•	seeking support from colleagues for assistance with communication if needed
S.S46: Communicate by spoken, written and electronic methods (including in medical records) clearly, sensitively and effectively with patients, their relatives, carers or other advocates, and colleagues from medical and other professions. This includes, but is not limited to, the following situations: •	where there is conflict or disagreement •	when sharing news about a patient’s condition that may be emotionally challenging for the patient and those close to them •	when sharing news about a patient’s death with relatives and carers or other advocates •	when discussing issues that may be sensitive for the patient, such as alcohol consumption, smoking, diet and weight management or sexual behaviour •	when communicating with people who lack insight into their illness or are ambivalent about treatment •	when communicating with children and young people •	when communicating with people who have impaired hearing, language, speech or sight •	when communicating with people who have cognitive impairment •	when communicating with people who have learning disabilities •	when English is not the patient’s first language - by using an interpreter, translation service or other online methods of translation •	when the patient lacks capacity to reach or communicate a decision on their care needs •	when advocating for patients’ needs •	when making referrals to colleagues from medical and other professions •	when providing care remotely, such as carrying out consultations using telecommunications.
S.S47: Use methods of communication used by patients and colleagues such as technology-enabled communication platforms, respecting confidentiality and maintaining professional standards of behaviour.
S.S48: Elicit and accurately record a patient’s medical history, including family and social history, working with parents and carers or other advocates when the patient is a child or young person or an adult who requires the support of a carer or other advocate
S.S49: Encourage patients’ questions, discuss their understanding of their condition and treatment options, and take into account their ideas concerns, expectations, values and preferences
S.S50: Acknowledge and discuss information patients have gathered about their conditions and symptoms, taking a collaborative approach
S.S51: Provide explanation, advice and support that matches patients’ level of understanding and needs, making reasonable adjustments to facilitate patients’ understanding if necessary
S.S52: Assess a patient’s capacity to understand and retain information and to make a particular decision, making reasonable adjustments to support their decision making if necessary, in accordance with legal requirements in the relevant jurisdiction and the GMC’s ethical guidance as appropriate
S.S53: Work with patients, or their legal advocates, to agree how they want to be involved in decision making about their care and treatment
S.S54: Apply scientific principles, methods and knowledge to medical practice and integrate these into patient care.
S.S55: Select appropriate forms of management for common diseases, and ways of preventing common diseases their modes of action and their risks from first principles
S.S56: Illustrate, by professional experience, the principles for the identification, safe management and referral of patients with mental health conditions
S.S57: Conduct appropriate critical appraisal and analysis of clinical data
S.S58: Interpret and communicate research evidence in a meaningful way for patients to support them in making informed decisions about treatment and management
S.S59: Apply epidemiological data to manage healthcare for the individual and the community and evaluate the clinical and cost effectiveness of interventions
S.S60: 60.	Apply the basic principles of communicable disease control in hospital and community settings, including disease surveillance
S.S61: Assess, by taking a history, the environmental, social, psychological, behavioural and cultural factors influencing a patient’s presentation, and identify options to address these, including advocacy for those who are disempowered
S.S62: Apply social science principles, methods and knowledge to medical practice and integrate these into patient care
S.S63: Apply the principles, methods and knowledge of population health and the improvement of health and sustainable healthcare to medical practice
S.S64: Assess, by taking a history, the environmental, social, psychological, behavioural and cultural factors influencing a patient’s presentation, and identify options to address these, including advocacy for those who are disempowered
S.S65: Apply the principles of primary, secondary and tertiary prevention of disease, including immunisation and screening
S.S66: Propose an assessment of a patient’s clinical presentation, integrating biological, psychological and social factors, agree this with colleagues and use it to direct and prioritise investigations and care
S.S67: Safely and sensitively undertake: •	an appropriate physical examination (with a chaperone present if appropriate) a mental and cognitive state examination, including establishing if the patient is a risk to themselves or others, seeking support and making referrals if necessary•	a developmental examination for children and young people
S.S68: Interpret findings from history, physical and mental state examinations
S.S69: Propose a holistic clinical summary, including a prioritised differential diagnosis/diagnoses and problem list
S.S70: Propose options for investigation, taking into account potential risks, benefits, cost effectiveness and possible side effects and agree in collaboration with colleagues if necessary, which investigations to select
S.S71: Interpret the results of investigations and diagnostic procedures, in collaboration with colleagues if necessary
S.S72: Synthesise findings from the history, physical and mental state examinations and investigations, in collaboration with colleagues if necessary, and make proposals about underlying causes or pathology
S.S73: Make clinical judgements and decisions with a patient, based on the available evidence, in collaboration with colleagues and as appropriate for their level of training and experience, and understand that this may include situations of uncertainty
S.S74: Take account of patients’ concerns, beliefs, choices and preferences, and respect the rights of patients to reach decisions with their doctor about their treatment and care and to refuse or limit treatment
S.S75: Seek informed consent for any recommended or preferred options for treatment and care
S.S76: Propose a plan of management including prevention, treatment, management and discharge or continuing community care, according to established principles and best evidence, in collaboration with other health professionals if necessary
S.S77: Support and motivate the patient’s self-care by helping them to recognise the benefits of a healthy lifestyle and motivating behaviour change to improve health and include prevention in the patient’s management plan
S.S78: Make appropriate clinical judgements when considering or providing compassionate interventions or support for patients who are nearing or at the end of life, understanding the need to involve patients, their relatives, carers or other advocates in management decisions, making referrals and seeking advice from colleagues as appropriate
S.S79: Provide immediate care to adults, children and young people in medical and psychiatric emergencies and seek support from colleagues if necessary
S.S80: Recognise when a patient is deteriorating and take appropriate action
S.S81: Assess and determine the severity of a clinical presentation and the need for immediate emergency care
S.S82: Diagnose and manage acute medical and psychiatric emergencies, escalating appropriately to colleagues for assistance and advice
S.S83: Perform the core set of practical skills and procedures (defined by the GMC) safely and effectively, and identify, according to own level of skill and experience, the procedures for which they need supervision to ensure patient safety
S.S84: Provide immediate life support (GMC Practical Skills and Procedures)
S.S85: Provide cardiopulmonary resuscitation (GMC Practical Skills and Procedures)
S.S86: Establish an accurate medication history, covering both prescribed medication and other drugs or supplements, and establish medication allergies and the types of medication interactions that patients experience
S.S87: Carry out an assessment of benefit and risk for the patient of starting a new medication taking into account the medication history and potential medication interactions in collaboration with the patient and, if appropriate, their relatives, carers or other advocates
S.S88: Provide patients, their relatives, carers or other advocates, with appropriate information about their medications in a way that enables patients to make decisions about the medications they take
S.S89: Agree a medication plan with the patient that they are willing and able to follow
S.S90: Access reliable information about medications and be able to use the different technologies used to support prescribing
S.S91: Calculate safe and appropriate medication doses and record the outcome accurately
S.S92: Write a safe and legal prescription, tailored to the specific needs of individual patients, using either paper or electronic systems and using decision support tools where necessary
S.S93: Prescribe in consultation with clinical pharmacologists and pharmacists and other colleagues as appropriate
S.S94: Communicate appropriate information to patients about what their medication is for, when and for how long to take it, what benefits to expect, any important adverse effects that may occur and what follow-up will be required
S.S95: Detect and report adverse medication reactions and therapeutic interactions and react appropriately by stopping or changing medication
S.S96: Monitor the efficacy and effects of medication and with appropriate advice from colleagues, reacting appropriately by adjusting medication, including stopping medication with due support, care and attention if it proves ineffective, is no longer needed or the patient wishes to stop taking it
S.S97: Respect patient choices about the use of complementary therapies
S.S98: Make effective use of decision making and diagnostic technologies
S.S99: Apply the requirements of confidentiality and data protection legislation and comply with local information governance and storage procedures when recording and coding patient information
S.S100: Apply the principles of health informatics applied to medical practice
S.S101: Interpret common statistical tests used in medical research publications
S.S102: Critically appraise research information, including study design, the results of relevant diagnostic, prognostic and treatment trials, and other qualitative and quantitative studies as reported in the medical and scientific literature
S.S103: Formulate simple relevant research questions in biomedical science, psychosocial science or population science, and design appropriate studies or experiments to address the questions
S.S104: Evidence from large scale public health reviews and other sources of public health data to inform decisions about the care of individual patients

B.B1: Compassionate professional behaviour and professional responsibilities making sure the fundamental needs of patients and carers are addressed
B.B2: Act with integrity, be polite, considerate, trustworthy, conscientious and honest
B.B3: Take personal and professional responsibility for their actions
B.B4: Manage time and prioritise effectively
B.B5: Be open and honest in their interactions with patients, carers, colleagues and employers when things go wrong – known as the professional duty of candour
B.B6: Respect  patients’ wishes about whether they wish to participate in the education of learners
B.B7: Meets the standards for Good medical practice (GMC)

K.K1: Regulatory and legislative framework and technical application of relevant Acts, Regulations and Guidance in the consumer protection environment.
K.K2: Powers or remit to carry out monitoring of activities to ensure consumer protection and the investigative processes to achieve compliance, including test purchasing and surveillance.
K.K3: Principles and rules of collecting evidence for monitoring, investigations and enforcement purposes including seizure and detention powers, formal sampling procedures and exhibit referencing.
K.K4: Risk assessment methodologies and approaches to evaluate harm and planned interventions.
K.K5: Software tools used to analyse, interpret, and evaluate intelligence to inform judgements and enable decision making.
K.K6: Communication techniques and approaches to interact with a range of key internal and external stakeholders, including using current and emerging technologies to support communication.
K.K7: Rules of evidence, disclosure, procedure, and professional standards for presenting or defending cases in both the Criminal and Civil Courts.
K.K8: Decision making techniques and approaches that protect against reputational harm of the organisation.
K.K9: Operational standards and processes to promote consumer protection.
K.K10: Social inclusion and ethical practices and their importance in the organisation.
K.K11: Principles of environmental sustainability and its impact on organisation behaviours.
K.K12: Project management principles and how they are applied to consumer protection.
K.K13: Partnership working and the benefits of collaboration in advice, enforcement, and compliance.
K.K14: Role of trading standards practitioners and how it adds value, ensuring consumers and legitimate businesses are protected and thrive.
K.K15: How consumer protection is impacted by external factors affecting businesses and regulators.
K.K16: Drivers that deliver support to the organisation, business community and consumers.
K.K17: How to support the continuous development requirements and training and learning needs of the team.
K.K18: Approaches to strategic planning for their organisations such as horizon scanning, intelligence, and governance arrangements.
K.K19: Techniques for the production and implementation of plans against targets, performance indicators and budgets.
K.K20: Current and emerging technologies to adopt processes that better protect consumers and businesses.
K.K21: Safeguarding vulnerable consumers.
K.K22: Resource management and allocation.
K.K23: Best practice and guidance as applied in the consumer protection sector.
K.K24: Organisation structure, governance and controls and roles of key stakeholders.

S.S1: Lead and manage investigations and interventions.
S.S2: Apply investigative techniques and evidence gathering to produce reports with recommendations.
S.S3: Analyse, interpret, challenge, and evaluate data and intelligence.
S.S4: Interpret and apply relevant Acts, Regulations, Codes of Practice and Guidance in the consumer protection environment.
S.S5: Influence, negotiate and challenge stakeholders in the delivery and decision-making process.
S.S6: Use research, analytical and problem-solving techniques to resolve and prevent consumer protection issues.
S.S7: Conduct and/or respond to formal interviews in line with legal controls and best practice collaborating with legal representatives.
S.S8: Gather evidence for use for potential legal proceedings and acting as a witness when required.
S.S9: Make legal and tactical decisions to improve the outcomes in consumer protection investigations.
S.S10: Promote the improvement of compliance standards in consumer protection.
S.S11: Promote social inclusion in the workplace with businesses, stakeholders, and consumers.
S.S12: Promote environmental sustainability and support change to improve the environment.
S.S13: Lead and manage operational projects to achieve time dependent outcomes.
S.S14: Communicate complex information, best practice, and guidance in consumer protection, adapting delivery for different audiences.
S.S15: Collaborate with stakeholders to achieve agreed collective outcomes.
S.S16: Identify and apply industry developments to achieve continual service improvement.
S.S17: Lead the development and monitoring of financial strategies and setting of organisational budgets based on Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) or similar, and challenge financial assumptions underpinning strategies.
S.S18: Build constructive working relationships across teams.
S.S19: Identify the training and development requirements of the team and stakeholders.
S.S20: Set strategic direction through the implementation of plans.
S.S21: Lead and respond to risks using risk management techniques.
S.S22: Provide support, specialist advice, and guidance for businesses, consumers, and stakeholders.
S.S23: Act as an advocate for projects and transformation of services across organisational boundaries such as those impacted by sustainability and the UK Net Carbon Zero.

B.B1: Role models ethical behaviour and practices.
B.B2: Works flexibly and adapts to circumstances.
B.B3: Seek learning and continuous professional development opportunities for self and wider team.
B.B4: Takes responsibility, shows initiative, and is organised.
B.B5: Take personal accountability aligned to clear values.

K.K1: Local and operational procedures for operations control.
K.K2: Sector specific documentation in operations control.
K.K3: The sector specific notifications and the impact to operational performance data. For example, Notices to Air Missions, Industrial action, Safety & Security bulletin.
K.K4: Civil Aviation Authority (CAA) or Military Aviation Authority (MAA) regulations, and legislation.
K.K5: The roles and responsibilities of different colleagues and stakeholder in operations and control.
K.K6: The organisational and sector digital toolkits and systems in operations control.
K.K7: Sector specific factors that will influence decision-making within operations control.
K.K8: Limits of authority, when to escalate tasks and issues, and to whom.
K.K9: Principles of risk-based decision making to support safe aircraft operations including safety, costs, and time factors.
K.K10: Environment and sustainability regulations, relevant to the occupation and co-ordinator’s responsibilities.
K.K11: Their organisation’s operational, ethical and safety priorities
K.K12: Principles and techniques of delivering an operational safety culture.
K.K13: Aircraft operational status indicators, and technical conditions of aircraft.
K.K14: The principles of using recording and analysing flight arrival information.
K.K15: The principles of using recording and analysing enroute flight information.
K.K16: The principles of using recording and analysing flight departure information.
K.K17: Techniques for verbal communication, giving and receiving information, matching style to audience, communication barriers and how to overcome them.
K.K18: Techniques for written communication, plain English principles, and industry terminology.
K.K19: Aircraft position trends and analysis techniques.
K.K20: Weather forecasts, trends, and the effect of changing weather conditions on the aircraft, flight crew, and operational delivery.
K.K21: Equity, diversity and inclusion legislation, and its impact on the aviation sector

S.S1: Comply with local operational and organisational procedures for operations control.
S.S2: Use sector specific documentation to support in operational control.
S.S3: Monitor and respond to sector specific data to support in operational control.
S.S4: Comply with CAA or MAA regulations and legislation.
S.S5: Operate organisational and sector digital toolkits and systems.
S.S6: Apply risk-based decision making to support safe aircraft operations.
S.S7: Escalate issues and tasks in line with organisational and sector procedures.
S.S8: Assess the risk and manage the impact of external factors on operational activities, for example scheduled disruptions, force majeure.
S.S9: Deliver an operational safety culture.
S.S10: Use aircraft operational status indicators to assess technical condition of aircraft
S.S11: Record and analyse flight arrival information.
S.S12: Record and analyse enroute flight information.
S.S13: Record and analyse flight departure information.
S.S14: Communicate data and information with others verbally, for example internal and external stakeholders, colleagues, and managers.
S.S15: Communicate data and information in writing with others, for example internal and external customers, colleagues, and managers.
S.S16: Analyse aircraft positioning trends.
S.S17: Use weather forecasts, trends, and conditions to support in operational delivery.
S.S18: Follows equity, diversity and inclusion legislative guidance

B.B1: Contributes to equity, diversity, and inclusivity in the workplace.
B.B2: Committed to maintaining and enhancing competence of self through Continued Professional Development (CPD).
B.B3: Respond and adapt to work demands and situations.
B.B4: Collaborate within teams, across disciplines and with internal and external stakeholders.
B.B5: Act professionally, considers their organisation's operational, ethical, safety and sustainability priorities.
B.B6: Prioritise aviation safety in all instances.

K.K1: Principles of networks: OSI and TCP/IP models, data, protocols and how they relate to each other; the main routing protocols; the main factors affecting network performance including typical failure modes in protocols and approaches to error control; virtual networking.
K.K2: the concepts, main functions and features of at least three Operating Systems (OS) and their security functions and associated security features.
K.K3: Cyber security concepts and why cyber security matters to business and society; Security assurance concepts and how assurance may be achieved in practice including penetration testing and extrinsic assurance methods.
K.K4: the main types of common attack techniques; also the role of human behaviour, including the significance of the 'insider threat'. Including: how attack techniques combine with motive and opportunity to become a threat. Techniques and strategies to defend against attack techniques and mitigate hazards.
K.K5: the significance of identified trends in cyber security threats and understand the value and risk of this analysis. How to deal with emerging attack techniques (including 'zero day'), hazards and vulnerabilities relevant to the digital systems and business environment.
K.K6: lifecycle and service management practices to an established standard to a foundation level for example Information Technology Infrastructure Library (ITIL) foundation level.
K.K7: cyber incident response processes, incident management processes and evidence collection/preservation requirements to support incident investigation.
K.K8: Understands the main features, applicability and how to apply the significant law, regulations and standards relevant specifically to cyber security. To include: laws, regulations & standards relating to personal data and privacy (e.g. Data Protection Act 2018 implementing General Data Protection Regulation); use of digital systems (e.g. Computer Misuse Act 1990 ); regulatory standards for cyber security, intelligence collection and law enforcement (e.g. Intelligence Services Act 1994, Regulation of Investigatory Powers Act 2000; standards for good practice in cyber security (e.g. ISO 27001, CyberEssentials, NIST) and any updates or additions.
K.K9: ethical principles and codes of good practice of at least one significant cyber security professional body and the ethical responsibilities of a cyber security professional.
K.K10: how to analyse employer or customer requirements to derive security objectives and taking account of the threats and overall context develop a security case which sets out the proposed security measures in the context with reasoned justification.
K.K11: horizon scanning including use of recognised sources of threat intelligence and vulnerabilities.
K.K12: common security architectures and methodologies; be aware of reputable security architectures that incorporates hardware and software components, and sources of architecture patterns and guidance. How cyber security technology components are typically deployed in digital systems to provide security functionality including: hardware and software to implement security controls.
K.K13: the basic terminology and concepts of cryptography; common cryptography techniques in use; the importance of effective key management and the main techniques used; legal, regulatory and export issues specific to the use of cryptography.
K.K14: risk assessment and audit methodologies and approaches to risk treatment; approaches to identifying  the vulnerabilities in organisations and security management systems; the threat intelligence lifecycle; the role of the risk owner in contrast with other stakeholders.
K.K15: principles of security management systems, including governance, organisational structure, roles, policies, standards, guidelines and how these all work together to deliver the identified security outcomes.
K.K16: function and features of significant digital system components; typical architectures; common vulnerabilities in digital systems; principles and common practice in digital system security.
K.K17: programming or scripting languages.

S.S1: Discover vulnerabilities in a system by using a mix of research and practical exploration.
S.S2: Analyse and evaluate security threats and hazards to a system or service or processes. Use relevant external source of threat intelligence or advice (e.g. National Cyber Security Centre) Combine different sources to create an enriched view of cyber threats and hazards.
S.S3: Research and investigate common attack techniques and relate these to normal and observed digital system behaviour and recommend how to defend against them. Interpret and demonstrate use of external source of vulnerabilities (e.g. OWASP, intelligence sharing initiatives, open source).
S.S4: Undertake security risk assessments for simple systems without direct supervision and propose basic remediation advice in the context of the employer.
S.S5: Source and analyse security cases and describe what threats, vulnerability or risks are mitigated and identify any residual areas of concern.
S.S6: Analyse employer or customer requirements to derive security objectives and taking account of the threats and overall context develop a security case which sets out the proposed security measures in the context with reasoned justification.
S.S7: Identify and follow organisational policies and standards for information and cyber security and operate according to service level agreements or other defined performance targets.
S.S8: Configure, deploy and use computer, digital network and cyber security technology.
S.S9: Recommend improvements to the cyber security posture of an employer or customer based on research into future potential cyber threats and considering threat trends.
S.S10: Design, build, test and troubleshoot a network incorporating more than one subnet with static and dynamic routes, to a given design requirement without supervision. Provide evidence that the system meets the design requirement.
S.S11: Analyse security requirements given (functional and non-functional security requirements that may be presented in a security case) against other design requirements (e.g. usability, cost, size, weight, power, heat, supportability etc.) for a given system or product. Identify conflicting requirements and propose, with reasoning, resolution through appropriate trade-offs.
S.S12: Design and build, systems in accordance with a security case within broad but generally well-defined parameters. This should include selection and configuration of typical security hardware and software components. Provide evidence that the system has properly implemented the security controls required by the security case.
S.S13: Write program code or scripts to meet a given design requirement in accordance with employers' coding standards.
S.S14: Design systems employing encryption to meet defined security objectives. Develop and implement a plan for managing the associated encryption keys for the given scenario or system.
S.S15: Use tools, techniques and processes to actively prevent breaches to digital system security.
S.S16: Conduct cyber-risk assessments against an externally (market) recognised cyber security standard using a recognised risk assessment methodology.
S.S17: Identify cyber security threats relevant to a defined context.
S.S18: Develop information security policies or processes to address a set of identified risks, for example from security audit recommendations.
S.S19: Develop information security policies within a defined scope to take account of legislation and regulation relevant to cyber security.
S.S20: Take an active part in a security audits against recognised cyber security standards, undertake gap analysis and make recommendations for remediation.
S.S21: Develop plans for incident response for approval within defined governance arrangements for incident response.
S.S22: Develop plans for local business continuity for approval within defined governance arrangements for business continuity.
S.S23: Assess security culture using a recognised approach.
S.S24: Design and implement a simple ‘security awareness’ campaign to address a specific aspect of a security culture.
S.S25: Integrate and correlate information from various sources (including log files from different sources, digital system monitoring tools, Secure Information and Event Management (SIEM) tools, access control systems, physical security systems) and compare to known threat and vulnerability data to form a judgement based on evidence with reasoning that the anomaly represents a digital system security breach.
S.S26: Recognise anomalies in observed digital system data structures (including by inspection of network packet data structures) and digital system behaviours (including by inspection of protocol behaviours) and by inspection of log files and by investigation of alerts raised by automated tools including SIEM tools.
S.S27: Accurately, objectively and concisely record and report the appropriate cyber security information, including in written reports within a structure or template provided.
S.S28: Configure digital system monitoring and analysis tools (e.g. SIEM tools), taking account of threat & vulnerability intelligence, indicators of compromise.
S.S29: Undertake root cause analysis of events and make recommendations to reduce false positives and false negatives.
S.S30: Manage local response to non-major incidents in accordance with a defined procedure.

B.B1: Logical - Applies logical thinking, for example, uses clear and valid reasoning when making decisions related to undertaking the work instructions.
B.B2: Analytical - working with data effectively to see patterns, trends and draw meaningful conclusions.
B.B3: Works independently and takes responsibility. For example works diligently regardless of how much they are being supervised, and stays motivated and committed when facing challenges.
B.B4: Shows initiative, being resourceful when faced with a problem and taking responsibility for solving problems within their own remit.
B.B5: Thorough & organised. For example uses their time effectively to complete work to schedule and takes responsibility for managing their own work load and time.
B.B6: Works effectively with a wide range of people in different roles, internally and externally, with a regard to inclusion & diversity policy.
B.B7: Communicates effectively in a wide variety of situations for example contributing effectively to meetings and presenting complex information to technical and non-technical audiences.
B.B8: Maintains a productive, professional and secure working environment.
B.B9: Creative - taking a variety of perspectives, taking account of unpredictable adversary and threat behaviours and approaches, bring novel and unexpected solutions  to address cyber security challenges.
B.B10: Problem Solving - Identifies issues quickly, solves complex problems and applies appropriate solutions. Dedicated to finding the true root cause of any problem and find solutions that  prevent recurrence.

K.K1: Urban vehicle preparation and maintenance requirements, within limits of own role.
K.K2: Different types of goods transported by fixed axle vehicles over 3500 kg in weight.

K.K13: Hazard perception techniques when driving, including the different approaches used in urban areas and other UK road environments.
K.K14: Accident reporting and incident management.
K.K15: Environmental and sustainability factors when driving in urban areas.
K.K16: Techniques for protecting goods in urban areas, including both when in transit and when the vehicle is unattended.
K.K17: Uses and limitations of urban vehicle in-cab technology  for safety, reporting and compliance. This includes driver aids, telematics, handheld terminals, and on-board weighing systems.
K.K18: The importance of brand identity in the urban delivery sector.
K.K19: The role of customer services standards in urban delivery, including the impact that own service provision can have on both the customer and on the wider organisation.
K.K20: Different forms of communication. For example, electronic, written and in-person.
K.K21: A range of dynamic risk assessment methods and associated reporting.
K.K22: The different regulations and legislation that apply when working on-site. For example, compliance and health and safety requirements in yards, businesses, and homes.
K.K23: Techniques for managing own well-being (physical and mental health) in an urban delivery environment.
K.K24: The different types of organisation that make up the urban delivery supply chain.
K.K25: The range and applications of own organisations products and on-site services.

S.S1: Prepare a fixed axle vehicle over 3500 kg in weight for the planned daily workload. This includes the cab, fluid levels, and general inspection.
S.S2: Monitor charge or fuel level of the vehicle to meet the daily requirements of the urban schedule
S.S3: Monitor the vehicle for defects.
S.S4: Co-ordinate own work with others to meet business priorities.
S.S5: Apply protections, manual handling and mechanical aids to the situation, when loading or off-loading goods.
S.S6: Prepare, position and secure goods appropriate for the goods type, the vehicle type and the urban conditions.
S.S7: Manage goods in transit. For example, security, and checking seals for signs of damage and leaks.
S.S8: Plan and/or adapt a driving route to meet contractual arrangements. For example single and multiple urban deliveries or collections.
S.S9: Drive fixed axle vehicles over 3500 kg in weight considering all relevant factors. This includes vehicle type, road surface, goods, environmental conditions, vulnerable road users and pedestrians
S.S10: Use on-board electronic systems in line with operating procedures.
S.S11: Respond and adapt to urban driving incidents, accidents roadworks and hazards.
S.S12: Manoeuvre  fixed axle vehicles over 3500 kg in weight on site. For example, a customer driveway, building site, or recycling centre.
S.S13: Perform dynamic risk assessment of a site and take remedial action. For example, adjusting or aborting a delivery or collection due to safety issues.
S.S14: Manage relationships that enable successful urban delivery, collection and on-site contracts.
S.S15: Adapt communication style to meet the needs of the audience.
S.S16: Utilise available IT systems to manage data relating to the delivery/collection contract.
S.S17: Complete contractual obligations on site. For example, installing white goods in a home, or removing waste, leaving the site to the expected standard.
S.S18: Brief the customer on the technical specifications of the delivery, collection or installation, answering questions. This could mean, for instance, demonstrating how a product works.
S.S19: Adjust the services provided in response to customer requirements, within the limits of own role.
S.S20: Comply with relevant legislation and regulation, both when driving and on site

B.B1: Work flexibly (for example, working alone and in a team as required).
B.B2: Puts safety first for themselves and others.
B.B3: Respectful of others.
B.B4: Takes ownership of own work.
B.B5: Sources solutions.
B.B6: Committed to keeping continuous professional development up to date with industry best practice.

K.K1: Principles of safeguarding  and how to respond to safeguarding concerns  within own scope of practice and organisational protocol.

K.K15: How to make a comprehensive and accurate referral to children or adult social care.
K.K16: Safeguarding legislation, local and national policies, procedures and codes of conduct that apply to own role.
K.K17: How to link safeguarding theory to practice.
K.K18: Ways to support the management of safeguarding caseloads working together within the wider team.

K.K23: Legislative safeguarding training requirements and how to deliver training content to stakeholders using different teaching and learning approaches.

K.K25: Methods of collating evidence to prepare for external safeguarding inspections, reviews and audits.
K.K26: How to interpret the results of inspections, reviews and audits to apply feedback to practice.
K.K27: The importance of continuous professional development and identifying and evaluating own learning and development needs.
K.K28: Models of safeguarding supervision and how to prepare and access supervision.
K.K29: How safeguarding can impact on own wellbeing and personal safety.
K.K30: Principles in supporting colleagues to follow safeguarding policy and procedures.
K.K31: Principles of safeguarding culture, safeguarding supervision and compassionate care.

S.S1: Identify and respond to safeguarding concerns within role and responsibilities.
S.S2: Use person-centred communication.
S.S3: Use safeguarding legislation and organisational policies and procedures to advise colleagues on safeguarding matters.
S.S4: Triage and prioritise safeguarding concerns.
S.S5: Recommend courses of action in relation to safeguarding concerns.
S.S6: Tailor safeguarding responses to suit the individuals views, feelings and cultural influences.
S.S7: Escalate cases to senior staff in accordance with local policy and procedures.
S.S8: Report allegations about staff in line with local policies and procedures, whilst maintaining confidentiality.
S.S9: Use, record and store paper based and digital data and information securely and in line with local and national policies and procedures.
S.S10: Share data and information in a safeguarding context in line with local and national policy and procedures.
S.S11: Collate and use data to create reports to inform safeguarding meetings, processes and audits.
S.S12: Maintain accurate and up-to-date safeguarding logs and registers through the use of digital or paper systems in line with local and national policy and procedures.
S.S13: Identify when decisions are not in an individuals best interest and escalate concerns within scope of own role using the local safeguarding escalation pathway.
S.S14: Recognise and report incidents to own organisation.
S.S15: Provide emotional and practical support to internal and external stakeholders during safeguarding work.
S.S16: Support and supervise internal and external stakeholders to make multi-agency referrals.
S.S17: Apply safeguarding legislation, local and national policies, procedures and codes of conduct to safeguarding practice within own role.
S.S18: Apply safeguarding theory to practice.
S.S19: Work together within the wider team to plan and manage safeguarding caseloads.
S.S20: Comply with the safeguarding process when a safeguarding concern is raised in line with organisational policy.
S.S21: Use communication styles and approaches to suit the needs of individuals.
S.S22: Reflect on own performance within a group environment with other colleagues to inform best safeguarding practice.
S.S23: Embed lessons learned from local and national safeguarding reviews to practice.
S.S24: Assist with the delivery of safeguarding training to stakeholders in accordance with safeguarding training frameworks to ensure stakeholders achieve desired learning outcomes.
S.S25: Collate evidence to prepare for external safeguarding inspections, reviews and audits.
S.S26: Apply feedback from safeguarding inspections, reviews and audits to improve safeguarding practice.
S.S27: Participate in training and continuous professional development activities and evaluate the impact of learning on own practice.
S.S28: To seek advice and support from senior staff to preserve own wellbeing and personal safety when required.

B.B1: Treat people fairly and with dignity and respect.
B.B2: Be caring and compassionate.
B.B3: Show discretion and empathy whilst maintaining confidentiality.
B.B4: Be adaptable and consistent.
B.B5: Be self-aware, resilient and objective.
B.B6: Show honesty and integrity.
B.B7: Be professional and courteous.

K.K1: Marketing theory, concepts and basic principles such as what marketing is, the marketing mix the promotional mix and the differences between each channel used.
K.K2: The business’ structure, vision, priorities, and objectives, and how their marketing role supports these.
K.K3: Business tools used to measure the impact of business objectives, the wider environment and sustainability on marketing activities.
K.K4: What a marketing plan is, how it is built and its purpose.
K.K5: The importance of competitor analysis and how to undertake it.
K.K6: Brand theory such as positioning, value, identity, guidelines, and tone of voice.
K.K7: Create content using principles of design and copywriting, and how to adapt for online and offline mediums e.g., writing digital content for the web compared to leaflets.
K.K8: Current and emerging technologies, software and systems which impact on marketing.
K.K9: Relevant regulatory and legislative requirements such as data protection, GDPR, cyber security, trading laws, and copyright law for the handling and processing of data and its application.
K.K10: Principles of conducting marketing communications in an ethical and diverse manner.
K.K11: How internal stakeholders work to support the delivery of all marketing campaigns.
K.K12: Common marketing channels, cross channel behaviour, and how to manage and operate an integrated campaign using online and offline channels.
K.K13: How to brief and manage external marketing suppliers.
K.K14: Adapt communications for appropriate stakeholders and internal audiences.
K.K15: The principles of content marketing, and content creation.
K.K16: Budget management and how to measure return on investment (ROI).
K.K17: The metrics for the delivery and evaluation of marketing activity
K.K18: The importance of reviewing campaigns regularly to ensure effectiveness and optimisation.
K.K19: The campaign management process including research, planning, budgeting, implementation, and delivery.
K.K20: Tools used to support campaign management such as social media, Gantt charts, data analytics, and project management software.
K.K21: The customer journey including customer offline and digital touchpoints, customer personas, how to engage customers at different stages of their journey, sales funnels and how to segment an audience for targeting.
K.K22: The impact marketing has on the level of customer service or the customer experience, including community management channels
K.K23: Quality management and the maintenance of online and offline assets.

S.S1: Develop or interpret briefs for external or internal stakeholders and measure delivery in-line with the specification and agreed timelines
S.S2: Plan and coordinate a marketing activity using marketing tactics to acquire and retain one or more customer segments using available resources.
S.S3: Contribute to the generation of innovative and creative approaches across video, images, and other formats, both online and offline, to support campaign development.
S.S4: Use research/survey software to gather audience insight and/or evaluation to support the project.
S.S5: Use copywriting techniques to write persuasive text/copy to meet a communications objective ensuring it is in-line with organisational brand guidelines.
S.S6: Build and implement multi-channel campaigns across a variety of platforms, either offline or digital media.
S.S7: Proofread marketing copy ensuring it is accurate, persuasive and is on brand.
S.S8: Use software to design and create marketing assets to meet the technical specification.
S.S9: Contribute to the research of external suppliers to support recommendations and procurement of marketing goods and services.
S.S10: Organise offline and digital assets ensuring they are co-ordinated and legally compliant.
S.S11: Use a content management system to publish text, images, and video/animated content.
S.S12: Create and maintain spreadsheets to support marketing activities such as project/budget planning and organisation of marketing assets.
S.S13: Use technology and software packages to support day to day activities, e.g., stakeholder communications, development of briefs, data analysis, report writing, presentations and project management.
S.S14: Identify and use data and technologies to achieve marketing objectives.
S.S15: Monitor and amend campaigns to meet budget requirements including time and monetary costs.
S.S16: Review campaigns regularly to ensure effectiveness, to optimise the results.
S.S17: Measure and evaluate campaign delivery to identify areas for improvement.
S.S18: Use data analysis tools to record, interpret and analyse customer or campaign data.

B.B1: Has accountability and ownership of their tasks and workload.
B.B2: Takes responsibility, shows initiative and is organised.
B.B3: Works flexibly and adapts to circumstances.
B.B4: Works collaboratively with others across the organisation and external stakeholders.
B.B5: Seeks learning opportunities and continuous professional development.
B.B6: Acts in a professional manner with integrity and confidentiality.

K.K1: The importance of continuing professional development throughout own career.
K.K2: The importance of safeguarding, recognising signs of abuse, and relevant safeguarding processes.
K.K3: What is required of them by the Health and Care Professions Council, including but not limited to the standards of conduct, performance and ethics.
K.K4: The importance of valid consent.
K.K5: The importance of capacity in the context of delivering care and treatment.
K.K6: The scope of a professional duty of care.
K.K7: Legislation, policies and guidance relevant to own profession and scope of practice.
K.K8: The importance of own mental and physical health, and wellbeing strategies in maintaining fitness to practise.
K.K9: How to take appropriate action if own health may affect own ability to practise safely and effectively, including seeking help and support when necessary.
K.K10: The need for active participation in training, supervision and mentoring in supporting high standards of practice, and personal and professional conduct, and the importance of demonstrating this in practice.
K.K11: Equality legislation and how to apply it to own practice.
K.K12: The duty to make reasonable adjustments in practice.
K.K13: The characteristics and consequences of barriers to inclusion, including for socially isolated groups.
K.K14: That regard to equality, diversity and inclusion must be embedded in the application of all HCPC standards and across all areas of practice.
K.K15: When disclosure of confidential information may be required.
K.K16: The principles of information and data governance and be aware of the safe and effective use of health, social care and other relevant information.
K.K17: The need to maintain confidentiality in all situations in which service users rely on additional communication support, such as interpreters or translators.
K.K18: That the concepts of confidentiality and informed consent extend to all mediums, including illustrative clinical records such as photography, video and audio recordings and digital platforms.
K.K19: The characteristics and consequences of verbal and non-verbal communication and how these can be affected by difference of any kind including, but not limited to, protected characteristics, intersectional experiences and cultural differences.
K.K20: The need to support service users’ and carers’ communication needs, such as through using an appropriate interpreter.
K.K21: The need to provide service users or people acting on own behalf with the information necessary in accessible formats to enable them to make informed decisions.
K.K22: The principles and practices of other health and care professionals and systems and how they interact with own profession.
K.K23: The need to build and sustain professional relationships as both an autonomous practitioner and collaboratively as a member of a team.
K.K24: The qualities, behaviours and benefits of leadership.
K.K25: That leadership is a skill all professionals can demonstrate.
K.K26: The need to engage service users and carers in planning and evaluating diagnostics, and therapeutic interventions to meet their own needs and goals.
K.K27: The value of reflective practice and the need to record the outcome of such reflection to support continuous improvement.
K.K28: The value of multi-disciplinary reviews, case conferences and other methods of review.
K.K29: The value of gathering and using data for quality assurance and improvement programmes.
K.K30: The structure and function of the human body, together with knowledge of physical and mental health, disease, disorder and dysfunction relevant to own profession.
K.K31: The roles of other professions in health and social care and how they may relate to the role of orthoptist.
K.K32: The structure and function of health and social care systems and services in the UK.
K.K33: Human growth, physical and mental, and human development across the lifespan, as it relates to the practice of orthoptics.
K.K34: The development of anatomical substrates and their relevance to the development of binocular single vision, visual function and visual perception.
K.K35: The detailed anatomical and physiological development of the visual system, and understand which components of the visual pathway and cortex relate to specific aspects of visual function and visual perception.
K.K36: Neuroanatomy and the effects of disruption of neural pathways on the visual system, cranial nerves and supranuclear control of eye movements.
K.K37: The factors which influence individual variations in human ability and development.
K.K38: How psychology and sociology can inform an understanding of health, illness and health care in the context of orthoptics and how to apply this in practice.
K.K39: The theoretical basis of, and the variety of approaches to, assessment and intervention.
K.K40: Ocular alignment and binocular single vision, and the sensory and motor elements required to attain and maintain these.
K.K41: The principles governing visual function and the development of vision.
