K.K42: The factors which can cause the disruption of binocular vision.
K.K43: The principles governing binocular vision, its investigation and the significance of its presence or absence.
K.K44: The principles of uniocular and binocular perception, and the anatomical substrate of these functions.
K.K45: Refractive error and its effect on ocular alignment, visual perception and visual development.
K.K46: How convergence, accommodation and pupillary response affect investigation, diagnosis and service user management.
K.K47: Ocular motility systems, their neural control and how typical and atypical anatomical structures influence them.
K.K48: The principles governing ocular motility and their relevance to diagnosis and management.
K.K49: The sensory and motor adaptive mechanisms that occur in order to compensate for strabismus and abnormalities of binocular vision.
K.K50: The effect of other acquired disorders of the body on the eye, the visual and ocular motor systems including paediatric, endocrine, autoimmune, oncological, trauma, psychological and neurological disease.
K.K51: The range of ophthalmic conditions which can disrupt vision, binocular vision and produce eye movement disorders.
K.K52: The principles and application of orthoptic and ophthalmological equipment used during the investigative process.
K.K53: Pharmacological principles which include pharmacodynamics and pharmacokinetics of medicines relevant to own practice and how these may be altered by certain characteristics.
K.K54: The legal context relevant to the use of exemptions in legislation for the sale, supply and administration of medicines, as well as current local and national policy and guidance concerning medicines use.
K.K55: The differences between the sale, supply and administration of medicines using exemptions, other supply or administration mechanisms and prescribing mechanisms.
K.K56: When to sell or supply medicines using exemptions, based on a relevant examination, assessment and history taking.
K.K57: The different non-pharmacological and pharmacological approaches to modifying disease management relevant to own practice and the risks and benefits of each option.
K.K58: The potential for medicines to have adverse effects and how to minimise them including effects caused by medicines given in error.
K.K59: Antimicrobial resistance and the roles of infection prevention and control.
K.K60: The importance of shared decision-making with service users to encourage self-care and adherence with medicines advice.
K.K61: The value of research to the critical evaluation of practice.
K.K62: Principles of measurement techniques used to assess binocular vision and other ocular conditions.
K.K63: Tests required to aid in differential diagnosis.
K.K64: The principles and techniques of an objective and subjective refraction.
K.K65: The principles and techniques used to perform an examination of the anterior and posterior segments of the eye.
K.K66: The principles and techniques used to perform visual fields assessments.
K.K67: The principles and techniques used in electrophysiological assessment of visual function and the visual pathway.
K.K68: How to apply orthoptic and ophthalmological intervention appropriately at different stages of visual development and ageing.
K.K69: How to use optical methods to influence vision and binocular vision.
K.K70: The role, pharmacological action, clinical indications and contraindications of ophthalmic drugs and how they may be selected and used in orthoptic practice.
K.K71: Research in the fields of ocular motility, strabismus, amblyopia and binocular disorders and how it could affect practice.
K.K72: The need to maintain own and others’ safety, including service users, carers and colleagues.
K.K73: Relevant health and safety legislation and local operational procedures and policies.
K.K74: How to position or immobilise service users correctly for safe and effective interventions.
K.K75: The role of the profession in health promotion, health education and preventing ill health.
K.K76: How wider determinants of health including social, economic and environmental factors can influence a person’s health and well-being.

S.S1: Identify the limits of own practice and when to seek advice or refer to another professional or service.
S.S2: Recognise the need to manage own workload and resources safely and effectively, including managing the emotional burden that comes with working in a pressured environment.
S.S3: Keep own skills and knowledge up to date.
S.S4: Maintain high standards of personal and professional conduct.
S.S5: Promote and protect the service user’s interests at all times.
S.S6: Engage in safeguarding processes where necessary.
S.S7: Respect and uphold the rights, dignity, values, and autonomy of service users, including own role in the assessment, diagnostic, treatment and or therapeutic process.
S.S8: Recognise that relationships with service users, carers and others should be based on mutual respect and trust, and maintain high standards of care in all circumstances.
S.S9: Obtain valid consent, which is voluntary and informed, has due regard to capacity, is proportionate to the circumstances and is appropriately documented.
S.S10: Exercise a duty of care.
S.S11: Apply legislation, policies and guidance relevant to own profession and scope of practice.
S.S12: Recognise the power imbalance which comes with being a health care professional, and ensure it is not for personal gain.
S.S13: Identify own anxiety and stress and recognise the potential impact on own practice.
S.S14: Develop and adopt clear strategies for physical and mental self-care and self-awareness, to maintain a high standard of professional effectiveness and a safe working environment.
S.S15: Recognise that they are personally responsible for, and must be able to, justify own decisions and actions.
S.S16: Use own skills, knowledge and experience, and the information available, to make informed decisions and or act where necessary.
S.S17: Make reasoned decisions to initiate, continue, modify or cease treatment or the use of techniques or procedures, and record the decisions and reasoning appropriately.
S.S18: Make and receive appropriate referrals, where necessary.
S.S19: Exercise personal initiative.
S.S20: Demonstrate a logical and systematic approach to problem solving.
S.S21: Use research, reasoning and problem-solving skills when determining appropriate actions.
S.S22: Coordinate a complete service user pathway, where appropriate, and in line with local guidelines.
S.S23: Respond appropriately to the needs of all different groups and individuals in practice, recognising this can be affected by difference of any kind including, but not limited to, protected characteristics, intersectional experiences and cultural differences.
S.S24: Recognise the potential impact of own values, beliefs and personal biases, which may be unconscious, on practice and take personal action to ensure all service users and carers are treated appropriately with respect and dignity.
S.S25: Make and support reasonable adjustments in own and others’ practice.
S.S26: Actively challenge barriers to inclusion, supporting the implementation of change wherever possible.
S.S27: Adhere to the professional duty of confidentiality.
S.S28: Recognise and respond in a timely manner to situations where it is necessary to share information to safeguard service users, carers and or the wider public.
S.S29: Use effective and appropriate verbal and non-verbal skills to communicate with service users, carers, colleagues and others.
S.S30: Communicate in English to the required standard for the profession.
S.S31: Work with service users and or own carers to facilitate the service user’s preferred role in decision-making, and provide service users and carers with the information they may need where appropriate
S.S32: Modify own means of communication to address the individual communication needs and preferences of service users and carers, and remove any barriers to communication where possible.
S.S33: Use information, communication and digital technologies appropriate to own practice.
S.S34: Recognise the need to modify interpersonal skills for the assessment and management of children.
S.S35: Keep full, clear and accurate records in accordance with applicable legislation, protocols and guidelines.
S.S36: Manage records and all other information in accordance with applicable legislation, protocols and guidelines.
S.S37: Use digital record keeping tools, where required.
S.S38: Work in partnership with service users, carers, colleagues and others.
S.S39: Contribute effectively to work undertaken as part of a multi-disciplinary team.
S.S40: Identify anxiety and stress in service users, carers and colleagues, adapting own practice and providing support where appropriate.
S.S41: Identify own leadership qualities, behaviours and approaches, taking into account the importance of equality, diversity and inclusion.
S.S42: Demonstrate leadership behaviours appropriate to own practice.
S.S43: Act as a role model for others.
S.S44: Promote and engage in the learning of others.
S.S45: Recognise the need to participate effectively in the planning, implementation and evaluation of multi-professional approaches to healthcare delivery by liaising with other health or social care professionals.
S.S46: Recognise the orthoptist’s role in the promotion of ocular health by other health professionals.
S.S47: Engage in evidence-based practice.
S.S48: Gather and use feedback and information, including qualitative and quantitative data, to evaluate the response of service users to own care.
S.S49: Monitor and systematically evaluate the quality of practice, and maintain an effective quality management and quality assurance process working towards continual improvement.
S.S50: Participate in quality management, including quality control, quality assurance, clinical governance and the use of appropriate outcome measures.
S.S51: Evaluate care plans or intervention plans using recognised and appropriate outcome measures, in conjunction with the service user where possible, and revise the plans as necessary.
S.S52: Demonstrate awareness of the principles and applications of scientific enquiry, including the evaluation of treatment efficacy and the research process.
S.S53: Demonstrate awareness of human behaviour and recognise the need for sensitivity to the psychosocial aspects of ocular conditions, including strabismus.
S.S54: Apply the principles governing visual function and the development of vision to clinical practice.
S.S55: Apply the principles governing binocular vision, its investigation and the significance of its presence or absence to clinical practice.
S.S56: Apply an understanding of how convergence, accommodation and pupillary response affect investigation, diagnosis and service user management in clinical practice.
S.S57: Apply the principles governing ocular motility and their relevance to diagnosis and management to clinical practice.
S.S58: Recognise the functional and perceptual difficulties that may arise as a result of defective visual, binocular or ocular motor functions.
S.S59: Use orthoptic and ophthalmological equipment during the investigative process.
S.S60: Plan, operate and evaluate appropriate vision screening programmes.
S.S61: Demonstrate safe use of medicines including being able to undertake drug calculations accurately.
S.S62: Monitor response to medicines and modify or cease treatment as appropriate within own professional scope of practice, including referral to another professional.
S.S63: Apply the principles of evidence-based practice, including clinical and cost- effectiveness, to the supply and administration of exemption listed medicines relevant to own practice.
S.S64: Change their practice as needed to take account of new developments, technologies and changing contexts.
S.S65: Gather appropriate information.
S.S66: Analyse and critically evaluate the information collected.
S.S67: Select and use appropriate assessment techniques and equipment.
S.S68: Undertake thorough, sensitive, and detailed patient medical history, including an appropriate medication history.
S.S69: Undertake or arrange investigations as appropriate.
S.S70: Conduct appropriate assessment or monitoring procedures, treatment, therapy or other actions safely and effectively.
S.S71: Recognise a range of research methodologies relevant to own role.
S.S72: Critically evaluate research and other evidence to inform own practice.
S.S73: Engage service users in research as appropriate
S.S74: Use diagnostic and therapeutic procedures to address anomalies of binocular vision, visual function and ocular motility defects resulting in a clinically defined outcome, which can be recorded and monitored in a manner appropriate to safe orthoptic practice.
S.S75: Apply measurement techniques used to assess binocular vision and other ocular conditions.
S.S76: Conduct a thorough investigation of ocular motility.
S.S78: Identify where there is a clinical need for medical, neurological, social or psychological investigations or interventions.
S.S79: Use investigative techniques to identify ocular defects within a specific population to form a diagnosis and devise an appropriate course of action.
S.S80: Diagnose conditions and select appropriate management.
S.S81: Diagnose a range of vision, binocular vision and ocular motility defects and all categories of strabismus.
S.S82: Identify pathological changes and related clinical features of conditions commonly encountered by orthoptists.
S.S83: Perform, an objective and subjective refraction.
S.S84: Perform an examination of the anterior and posterior segments of the eye.
S.S86: Formulate specific and appropriate management plans, and set timescales.
S.S87: Recognise and document any adverse reaction to treatment and take appropriate action in response to this.
S.S88: Demonstrate awareness of relevant health and safety legislation and comply with all local operational procedures and policies.
S.S89: Work safely, including being able to select appropriate hazard control and risk management, reduction or elimination techniques in a safe manner and in accordance with health and safety legislation.
S.S90: Select appropriate personal protective equipment and use it correctly.
S.S91: Establish safe environments for practice, which appropriately manages risk.
S.S92: Empower and enable individuals, including service users and colleagues to play a part in managing own health.
S.S93: Engage in occupational health, including being aware of immunisation requirements.

B.B1: Kind, caring, compassionate, empathetic and supportive.
B.B2: Promotes and protects the interest of service users and carers, treating people with dignity, respecting an individual’s diversity, beliefs, culture, needs, and preferences.
B.B3: Self-aware, self-regulated, open-minded, inquisitive and innovative.
B.B4: Honest, trustworthy and open when things go wrong.
B.B5: Professional, respectful, diplomatic and considerate in all interactions with service users and colleagues.
B.B6: Organised, efficient and values the time of service users and colleagues.

K.K1: The role of the financial planner, paraplanner and other supporting roles, for example administrator, compliance officer.
K.K2: The structure of the financial services market, including product providers, technology providers and support services.
K.K3: The ethics and key principles of the advice process, and different client propositions and advice models, including independent, restricted and non-advised.
K.K4: Responsible and sustainable investment solutions.
K.K5: The financial services regulatory framework and its application in the advice process.
K.K6: Systems and controls to protect the client and the firm, including internal licencing, and service and quality assurance standards.
K.K7: Regulated advice standards, methods for building and maintaining client relationships, and the paraplanner or financial planner’s responsibilities in terms of these.
K.K8: Methods of researching and analysing financial products and solutions in the UK market, including the intended target market, features and benefits, risks and disadvantages, and suitability for individual clients.
K.K9: The significance and importance of Knowing Your Client (KYC), including establishing personal and financial circumstances, level of financial knowledge and experience, and potential vulnerabilities.
K.K10: How to establish and prioritise a client’s financial needs and objectives.
K.K11: Methods and approaches to assess a client’s knowledge and understanding of investments, attitude to investment risk, capacity for loss, and understanding of their vulnerabilities in relation to their needs and objectives.
K.K12: Principles of data analysis, data security, and cyber security to support collation, analysis, and compliant handling of data, the implications of non-compliance, and the importance of working safely online in a financial planning or paraplanning context.
K.K13: Purpose, scope and limitations of any in-house or third-party tools used within the financial planning process, and how emerging digital tools could impact the advice process in the future, for example attitude to risk questionnaires, cash flow modelling tools, cost comparison tools, digital tools, AI tools and digital visualisation tools.
K.K14: Communication techniques, methods and strategies and the importance of adapting communication style to facilitate understanding for financial and non-financial stakeholders
K.K15: How to analyse performance data and metrics effectively using qualitative and quantitative methods.
K.K16: Principles and policies of equity, diversity and inclusion in the workplace and their impact on the organisation and their customers.
K.K17: Paraplanner – Methods and approaches used to create personalised client-facing documents tailored appropriately to the audience and in line with regulatory and firm standards.
K.K18: Paraplanner – How to review and validate the methodology and conclusions drawn from research and analysis.
K.K19: Financial Planner – The requirements and practical implications of the Statements of Principle for Approved Persons (APER) and the Fit and Proper Test for Approved Persons (FIT).
K.K20: Financial Planner – Approaches to structuring client meetings, handling objections, and providing the required regulatory disclosures.
K.K21: Financial Planner – Strategies to establish a client’s views on responsible and sustainable investing, and the impact this may have on available solutions and outcomes.
K.K22: Financial Planner – Methods for training, competence, and continued professional development activities for becoming and remaining a regulated adviser.

S.S1: Use software packages and digital tools to deliver financial advice or paraplanning services in line with cyber and data security requirements, using data securely and safely.
S.S2: Provide services to clients in line with the business proposition, adhering to Consumer Duty and Treating Customers Fairly (TCF) principles.
S.S3: Prepare financial recommendations in accordance with risk, regulatory, and governance requirements.
S.S4: Comply with service and quality assurance standards for the business, for example complaints procedures and productivity.
S.S5: Communicate with clients and colleagues using methods appropriate for the intended audience, including verbal, written, digital, and virtual.
S.S6: Interpret and communicate financial and business information in a way that facilitates understanding for the intended audience, for example finance and non-finance stakeholders.
S.S7: Build and maintain collaborative, professional relationships with internal and external stakeholders to deliver advisory tasks and achieve successful customer outcomes.
S.S8: Plan and prioritise work to support positive outcomes for both the customer and the firm.
S.S9: Provide individual advice relevant to clients in line with regulatory requirements.
S.S10: Use feedback to reflect on own practice and identify opportunities for continuous professional development (CPD).
S.S11: Paraplanner – Analyse clients’ current circumstances and research appropriate solutions to meet their needs, objectives, and attitudes to risk.
S.S12: Paraplanner – Provide financial planning support to colleagues using appropriate market knowledge and available software and research tools.
S.S13: Paraplanner – Conduct technical product and fund analysis.
S.S14: Paraplanner – Create personalised client-facing financial planning reports that are tailored appropriately to the audience and in line with regulatory and firm standards.
S.S15: Paraplanner – Securely maintain client files with evidence of any research and analysis undertaken.
S.S16: Financial Planner – Conduct KYC activities to fact-find relevant information and establish clients’ financial needs and objectives, including identifying when and where clients may be experiencing vulnerabilities.
S.S17: Financial Planner – Establish overall risk profiles for clients through an understanding of the clients’ financial knowledge and experience, attitude to investment risk, capacity for loss, and understanding of their vulnerabilities.
S.S18: Financial Planner – Identify, prioritise, and address clients’ needs in line with regulatory requirements.
S.S19: Financial Planner – Deliver advice solutions that support clients in achieving their financial objectives.
S.S20: Financial Planner – Collaborate with customers to create new opportunities for business growth, for example through referrals and networking.
S.S21: Financial Planner – Establish clients’ views on responsible and sustainable investing and the impact this may have on available solutions and outcomes.

B.B1: Acts professionally, ethically, and with integrity.
B.B2: Seeks learning opportunities and continuous professional development (CPD) in line with industry requirements.
B.B3: Works flexibly and adapts to changing circumstances, including overcoming challenges and responding to setbacks.
B.B4: Takes personal responsibility for sustainable outcomes in how they carry out the duties of their role, with reference to environmental good practice.
B.B5: Supports an inclusive culture and treats colleagues and external stakeholders fairly and with respect.

K.K1: The role of the company secretary or governance professional.
K.K2: Regulatory and legislative frameworks and requirements such as company law, charity law, and data protection.
K.K3: Organisational structures, governance roles and decision-making frameworks.
K.K4: The application of governance codes, constitutional requirements, and an organisation’s governance policies and processes.
K.K5: How the role of company secretary or governance officer fits into the organisation’s governance function, and how it adds value to it.
K.K6: The roles of the key stakeholders in their organisation and how they interact with their own role.
K.K7: Governance and compliance record-keeping requirements.
K.K8: Governance practices and associated administration for meetings.
K.K9: Financial accounting concepts and working knowledge of financial statements and reports.
K.K10: Governance reporting and disclosure obligations of the organisation such as legal and regulatory filings and announcements, and annual reports.
K.K11: Technology and software used in the performance of governance activities.
K.K12: The risks associated with data processing.
K.K13: Continuous development of organisational systems, processes, and procedures to ensure that regulatory, governance, and risk management requirements are met.
K.K14: Organisational changes and developments.
K.K15: The importance of horizon scanning for future changes to regulation and developments in governance practices that affect the organisation.
K.K16: Frameworks used to assess, capture, mitigate and report on risk, such as its link to climate change and sustainability for that organisation.
K.K17: The context and concepts for governance today.
K.K18: The purpose and application of measures requiring the disclosure and verification of individuals' and entities’ identity and connected relationships.
K.K19: Project management principles and techniques.

S.S1: Interpret regulation, legislation and governance practices and advise and train stakeholders on its practical application.
S.S2: Establish, maintain, and influence relationships with key stakeholders and team members.
S.S3: Use IT systems to manage, share and store information in accordance with regulatory requirements and organisational policies and procedures.
S.S4: Communicate complex information through a variety of media to enable key stakeholders to understand what is required.
S.S5: Review instructions, following them to a conclusion through the execution of tasks.
S.S6: Identify and use precedents adapted for specific circumstances such as resolutions, announcements, reports.
S.S7: Produce work which is factually accurate and well presented for submission to the members of a decision-making governing body and for publication by the organisation.
S.S8: Develop and maintain structure charts for the organisation.
S.S9: Plan and prioritise their workload to meet deadlines.
S.S10: Maintain and organise forward looking plans and calendars, keeping relevant stakeholders informed of upcoming events such as meetings, reporting timetables, compliance deadlines.
S.S11: Gather, analyse, use, and share data to inform the decision-making process and make judgements on actions to take.
S.S12: Identify problems and present potential solutions to stakeholders.
S.S13: Respond to stakeholder questions and requests, knowing when to consult with others or escalate on matters of a more complex or sensitive nature.
S.S14: Identify and implement improvements to support the governance of the organisation.

B.B1: Operates professionally with integrity and confidentiality.
B.B2: Works collaboratively and shares knowledge with colleagues and stakeholders across the organisation.
B.B3: Has accountability and ownership of their tasks and workload.
B.B4: Seeks learning opportunities and continuous professional development.
B.B5: Works flexibly and adapts to circumstances.

K.K1: The regulatory framework of financial accounting that apply to sole traders, partnerships, companies, charities, and public sector organisations.
K.K2: Accounting concepts and standards in UK GAAP and IFRS as applicable to sole traders, partnerships, companies, charities, and public sector organisations.
K.K3: The technology available for financial accounting, reporting, and internal controls. For example, iXBRL
K.K4: Approaches to analysing issues relating to competitors, stakeholders and market trends. For example ratio analysis, trend analysis, stakeholder analysis, competitor analysis.
K.K5: Approaches to data analytics using technology to visualise findings from the analysis of financial and non-financial data from internal and external sources.
K.K6: The requirements of the data commissioner under GDPR, and other stakeholders.
K.K7: Digital technology and protocols to protect data from cyber attacks and data corruption.
K.K8: The principles of legal, professional, and ethical frameworks including regulations regarding money laundering, criminal finance, data protection, health and safety, computer misuse and cybercrime.
K.K9: The principles of digital systems management to ensure they are compliant with all relevant legal, professional and ethical frameworks.
K.K10: The principles and significance of reconciling internal system records with external information sources such as banks and suppliers.
K.K11: The purpose and techniques of budgeting and forecasting required to manage revenue, expenditure and cash flow.
K.K12: The use of digital technology to compile, manage and report on budgets
K.K13: Approaches to creating cash flow forecasts.
K.K14: The use of digital technology to forecast, monitor and report on movements in an organisations' cash flows.
K.K15: Approaches used to analyse the financial and non-financial impact of management decisions.
K.K16: The use of digital technology to provide management reports to assist decision making.
K.K17: The importance of taxation in society and its impact on government policy.
K.K18: The relationship of UK taxation to the accounting and finance function within organisations.
K.K19: Implications for taxation reporting and obligations of organisational or client decision making.
K.K20: Principles of digital technology used to process data and fulfil organisational and HMRC digital taxation reporting requirements.
K.K21: The importance of the internal and external audit function and the significance of providing information requested from the audit teams to fulfil their duties.
K.K22: Strategic planning techniques that are used by organisations to analyse, develop, and implement strategy.
K.K23: Approaches to digital technology used to analyse, communicate, monitor and report on implementing strategy and meeting strategic objectives.
K.K24: Approaches to identify and evaluate quantitative and qualitative factors that influence business performance.
K.K25: The use of technology to collect, analyse and communicate quantitative and qualitative data.
K.K26: The sources of finance that are available to organisations.
K.K27: Approaches to using technology to identify and apply for finance and maintain credit relationships.
K.K28: The legal and organisational framework for managing receivables, credit control, and internal and external relationships.
K.K29: Approaches to using technology that manages credit control for internal and external stakeholders.
K.K30: The principles and practice of designing, implementing and maintaining internal controls that mitigate risk and prevent issues, such as with quality, accuracy, fraud, cyber-attacks, or other irregularity.
K.K31: Approaches to internal controls for both manual and digital systems and processes, with consideration for the control environment.
K.K32: Approaches to managing external relationships that affect the organisation.
K.K33: Approaches to digital technology to produce relevant information for stakeholder reports such as Integrated Reporting or Environmental Social and Governance (ESG) reports.
K.K34: Principles of effective staff recruitment, training, appraisal, mentoring and management.
K.K35: Approaches to use of digital technologies to appraise, monitor and manage staff.

S.S1: Prepare financial statements, or extracts thereof, in accordance with International Financial Reporting Standards (IFRS) and UK Generally Accepted Accounting Practice (GAAP).
S.S2: Identify and use appropriate digital technology to achieve desired outputs in the context of producing statutory of financial reports.
S.S3: Analyse financial and non-financial internal data and compare with benchmark data such as from competitors, stakeholders, and market trends.
S.S4: Use data analytics tools, visualise findings, make evidenced-based recommendations for future action and communicate them.
S.S5: Interpret and implement the latest guidance on secure data management to satisfy the requirements of the Data Commissioner and GDPR.
S.S6: Implement protocols and technology that minimise risks to data integrity and cyber security.
S.S7: Evaluate internal and external factors relevant to the operational compliance of the organisation with all necessary legal, professional and ethical frameworks.
S.S8: Apply the principles of digital systems management to ensure continued compliance with all relevant frameworks applicable to the organisation.
S.S9: Analyse and interpret the reasons for differences between internal and external source documentation and produce reconciliations that clearly identify those differences and required actions.
S.S10: Create budgets and forecasts using the most appropriate tools and techniques.
S.S11: Interpret budget reports to evaluate the reliability of forecasts and to identify variances which require further action.
S.S12: Calculate and interpret cash flow forecasts.
S.S13: Interpret cash flow reports and identify future action required to manage cash balances.
S.S14: Apply management accounting techniques to assist decision making within the organisation.
S.S15: Interpret management reports, and utilise a variety of media, including verbal communication, to deliver advice that impacts management decisions.
S.S16: Interpret and apply HMRC rules and guidance to produce tax calculations and statutory returns.
S.S17: Implement and manage systems and processes that gather the information required by HMRC.
S.S18: Analyse and summarise organisational  or client  decision making  and the impact on taxation reporting and obligations.
S.S19: Implement and manage the technology to satisfy HMRC reporting requirements for digital taxation.
S.S20: Produce the information required by internal and external auditors in a clear format.
S.S21: Evaluate strategic options, taking into consideration the impact of internal and external factors.
S.S22: Develop, communicate, and deliver operational plans that meet strategic objectives; being able to set targets and KPIs, manage resources, and monitor and measure outcomes to establish operations effectively, efficiently and economically.
S.S23: Ability to interpret quantitative and qualitative data in order to assess business performance, and utilise a variety of media, including verbal communication that demonstrates emotional intelligence, to make recommendations.
S.S24: Analyse and interpret the process of how data is collected, extracted, analysed and reported.
S.S25: Evaluate the impact of different sources of finance on an organisation, including risk and recommend appropriate finance options.
S.S26: Advise organisations and implement technology to raise finance and maintain credit relationships.
S.S27: Design and manage a compliant credit control system that meets strategic objectives regarding customers, insurers, and cash flow.
S.S28: Implement technology that enables delivery of a credit control system that meets strategic objectives.
S.S29: Analyse, design, implement, communicate, monitor and review internal control systems and processes that mitigate risk, including error, fraud, and cyber risks.
S.S30: Document organisational manual and digital internal controls, identify weaknesses, and make practical recommendations for improvement.
S.S31: Develop and maintain effective relationships with internal and external stakeholders.
S.S32: Identify relevant information to be included in reports used by stakeholders.
S.S33: Identify potential improvements to HR policies and activities such as recruitment, training, appraisal, mentoring or management.
S.S34: Evaluate the effectiveness of digital technology used to appraise, monitor and manage staff.

B.B1: Conscientious and diligent attention to detail that strives for completeness and accuracy.
B.B2: Rigorous and empirical approach to the analysis of data and research the facts and gathering expert opinion before recommending action.
B.B3: Committed to continuing professional development (CPD) by keeping up to date with the latest legislation, releases from standard setters,  the latest advances in, and applications of, digital technology as applicable to accounting and finance and guidance from HMRC.
B.B4: Objective and capable of independent and team working, committed to a reflective approach and therefore continuous improvement of internal and external relationships, including reviewing their own performance and performance of their team.
B.B5: Act with professional scepticism including a questioning mind, being alert to conditions which may indicate a possible misstatement due to error or fraud and a critical assessment of evidence.
B.B6: Act with professional judgment and values.  Apply ethical standards in making informed decisions about the courses of action that are appropriate to their business or their client including acting in the public interest.
B.B7: Creative, innovative and enterprising when seeking solutions to business needs. Positive and adaptable, responding well to feedback and need for change. Open to new ways of working and new management theories.

K.K1: The Association of Family Therapists (AFT) and UK Council for Counselling and Psychotherapy (UKCP) codes of ethics and practice.
K.K2: Statutory and legal frameworks that surround and inform practice contexts.
K.K3: National and local clinical governance guidelines and policies.
K.K4: The principles of confidentiality, consent its limits, and the specific conditions when it is appropriate to breach confidentiality.
K.K5: Models of risk assessment and the practice of ongoing risk management.
K.K6: Policies and practices needed in relation to safeguarding children and vulnerable adults and the role of the systemic psychotherapist in relation to this.
K.K7: Policies and processes for reporting and escalating risks, hazards or harms to self, the individuals, or others.
K.K8: Theories and research that informs systemic assessment.
K.K9: Current evidence based systemic models of assessment.
K.K10: Systemic models for determining patterns of distress.
K.K11: Societal factors that cause, maintain and exacerbate relational distress, physical and mental ill health.
K.K12: Diverse family values, structures and close interpersonal relationships.
K.K13: Information necessary to inform a systemic psychotherapeutic assessment.
K.K14: Mental health problems, their presentations, interventions, and the ways in which they may affect relationships.
K.K15: Models of human development and human relationships throughout the lifecycle of family and other relational systems.
K.K16: Biopsychosocial approaches to distress and mental health problems.
K.K17: Systemic model, theories and their limitations underpinning systemic psychotherapy and their applications to different situations and groups.
K.K18: Systems and processes for making referrals to internal or external teams or agencies.
K.K19: Systemic psychotherapeutic theories that enable change.
K.K20: How different systemic psychotherapeutic models and techniques can be used to meet the needs of diverse communities, relationships and individuals’ life choices.
K.K21: Techniques and interventions used in systemic psychotherapy.
K.K22: An approach to systemic psychotherapy that is a manualised evidenced based protocol.
K.K23: When to revise the therapeutic plan.
K.K24: Outcomes measures and approaches that inform and evaluate practice.
K.K25: The process of online systemic psychotherapy.
K.K26: Factors that underpin a therapeutic alliance and engagement.
K.K27: Models, approaches and actions to be followed for planned and unplanned therapeutic endings.
K.K28: The therapeutic and emotional processes of ending systemic psychotherapeutic work for individuals, systems and therapists.
K.K29: The use of self and consideration of power within the therapeutic relationship.
K.K30: Anti-discriminatory and anti-oppressive practice.
K.K31: The importance of partnership working with community groups and experts by experience in tackling racial, social and health inequalities.
K.K32: The influence of health and social inequalities on individual and family wellbeing.
K.K33: Culturally sensitive practices.
K.K34: Principles of systemic psychotherapy co-production.
K.K35: Theoretically informed approaches to supervision both as a supervisee and as a supervisor.
K.K36: Own role in developing an effective supervision relationship.
K.K37: Principles of reflective and reflexive practice.
K.K38: The process of personal development planning.
K.K39: Differences between clinical and management supervision.
K.K40: The limits of own personal expertise and skills.
K.K41: Quantitative and qualitative research methodologies relevant to the situation and service context.
K.K42: Critical evaluation techniques applied to evidence and practice based research findings.
K.K43: How research is conducted and implemented to inform effectiveness in systemic practice.
K.K44: Legal, ethical, professional, financial and organisational policies and procedures that apply to clinical research activities.
K.K45: A range of research approaches that draw on specialist systemic tools to collect data to evaluate own practice as well as to enhance service delivery.
K.K46: How knowledge of evidence-based practice is applied by supporting others in planning audit, evaluation and research of their work.
K.K47: Adult learning theories.
K.K48: The range of tools and techniques that can be used to support learning, set goals and evaluate learning.
K.K49: The impact of engaging with others to enhance reflective and reflexive practice in a range of learning and service settings.
K.K50: Principles of systemic leadership and how this can be applied to working in teams.
K.K51: Principles, roles and responsibilities of multidisciplinary working and their effect on service delivery.
K.K52: The use of self and consideration of power within the multidisciplinary or multi-agency team.
K.K53: Principles of handling clinical information and knowing how and when to share this information.
K.K54: The importance of providing and maintaining clear, concise, timely and factual clinical records.

S.S1: Act in accordance with the Association of Family Therapists (AFT) and UK Council for Counselling and Psychotherapy (UKCP) codes of ethics and practice.
S.S2: Adopt a rights-based approach consistent with national and service standards.
S.S3: Act in accordance with national and local clinical governance, standards and policies.
S.S4: Communicate the range of options to enable choice, decision-making and informed consent.
S.S5: Work collaboratively to manage the ongoing nature of risk.
S.S6: Report and escalate concerns around risk, hazards, or harms to self, individuals and others.
S.S7: Act within organisational and national safeguarding policies and procedures.
S.S8: Work with individuals to understand safeguarding concerns and effectively communicate safeguarding processes in line with national safeguarding confidentiality and sharing information guidance.
S.S9: Undertake a detailed and collaborative systemic psychotherapy assessment of the presenting concerns.
S.S10: Use theories and research findings to inform assessment and formulation.
S.S11: Collaboratively develop a systemic psychotherapy formulation.
S.S12: Adapt assessments to fit the needs of individuals across the life span.
S.S13: Communicate complex assessment material in both oral and written forms as appropriate.
S.S14: Recognise the limits of a systemic psychotherapy approach and make referrals to internal or external professionals based on need when required.
S.S15: Co-create a context in which change becomes possible.
S.S16: Use outcome measures to inform the therapeutic plan.
S.S17: Collaboratively agree the therapeutic plan.
S.S18: Adapt the therapeutic plan to take account of emotional distress and mental health difficulties.
S.S19: Adapt the therapeutic plan to take account of the needs of diverse communities, relationships and individuals life choices.
S.S20: Deliver systemic psychotherapy.
S.S21: Revise therapeutic plans as appropriate during the work.
S.S22: Apply systemic psychotherapeutic interventions with flexibility and creativity, adapting them to meet the individuals needs.
S.S23: Deliver systemic psychotherapy using a range of digital media.
S.S24: Use outcomes measures and qualitative approaches and adjust the therapy accordingly.
S.S25: Use outcome measures and qualitative approaches to assess the therapeutic alliance.
S.S26: Develop and maintain effective therapeutic relationships even when there are different views and goals.
S.S27: Communicate clinically sensitive information.
S.S28: Act in a self and relationally reflexive manner in relation to others.
S.S29: Attend to power and differences in respect to human identity, relationships, and experience.
S.S30: Gauge and manage emotions within sessions, including own, so that vulnerable members are protected in situations of discomfort and tension and important issues which may be contentious, or distressing can be explored safely.
S.S31: Manage planned and unplanned endings of the therapeutic relationship.
S.S32: Promote anti-discriminatory practices.
S.S33: Promote equality, diversity and inclusion in practice and organisations.
S.S34: Promote social justice by acknowledging and responding to individuals experience of marginalisation.
S.S35: Prepare and actively engage in systemic supervision and live supervised practice to sustain safe and effective practice.
S.S36: Manage personal connections with the work and reflect on changes that could be made.
S.S37: Actively participate in personal learning and identify areas of personal strength and learning needs, seeking and responding to support and feedback.
S.S38: Identify and act on own well-being needs when faced with challenging, distressing, or abusive behaviour.
S.S39: Recognise the limits of own personal expertise and skills and where required refer individuals to internal or external professionals based on the need.
S.S40: Evaluate research evidence and outcomes to take an evidence-based approach to systemic practice.
S.S41: Engage in research activity applying quantitative and qualitative methods within the context of own practice.
S.S42: Evaluate and audit systemic practice through conducting service evaluations to inform change.
S.S43: Disseminate clinically relevant research and service evaluation findings to individuals and or groups.
S.S44: Form and maintain the leadership-follower relationship in ways that are mutually enhancing.
S.S45: Provide a systemic psychotherapeutic perspective to colleagues.
S.S46: Work collaboratively to identify and meet the learning and development needs of professional colleagues.
S.S47: Provide constructive feedback to challenge and overcome barriers to implementation of best systemic psychotherapeutic practice to others.
S.S48: Present systemic psychotherapeutic principles and techniques to individuals and or groups.
S.S49: Develop and sustain collaborative relationships with colleagues from a range of disciplines and backgrounds surrounding the individual.
S.S50: Act in a self and relationally reflexive manner in relation to professional self.
S.S51: Produce and maintain clear, legible and contemporaneous records regarding direct and indirect contacts adhering to organisational, professional and ethical standards.
S.S52: Use clinical record systems in accordance with national and local governance requirements.

B.B1: Inclusive and culturally sensitive.
B.B2: Value lived experiences and partnership working.
B.B3: Treat individuals with dignity, empathy and compassion.
B.B4: Trustworthy, honest and act with integrity.
B.B5: Open and transparent putting best interests of the individual first.
B.B6: Committed to supporting individuals to become empowered.

ST1311.name: Licensed conveyancer or licensed probate practitioner

K.K1: Principles of legal and regulatory liabilities with regard to the client or clients, and organisation, to ensure compliance with all professional obligations.
K.K2: Requirements for acting in the best interest of the client to protect clients and limit liability for negligence, such as identifying and acting for vulnerable clients.
K.K3: Principles of Anti-Money Laundering regulations in relation to legal transactions, including personal and professional liability.
K.K4: Principles of managing client accounts.
K.K5: Approaches to communication with different stakeholders, and how to translate complex concepts into plain English to provide clarification and explanation of standard Legal terms.
K.K6: Approaches to client engagement and conducting Know Your Client activities to establish needs and risks.
K.K7: Principles of digital tools and software, such as case management systems, databases, and online drafting software.
K.K8: Fundamentals of the legal requirements relating to the use of data, including risks to data confidentiality posed by, and arising from, clients’ actions.
K.K9: Fundamentals of cybersecurity vulnerabilities in an organisation, and steps that can be taken to mitigate those risks.
K.K10: The impact of diversity and inclusion on client service delivery.
K.K11: The activities of the occupation which contribute to their ‘professional carbon footprint’, and steps that can be taken to reduce it.
K.K12: Principles of Law applicable to conveyancing in respect of residential property and land.
K.K13: Principles of regulatory arrangements for personal compliance with requirements as a licensed conveyancer.
K.K14: The impact of climate change on the legal landscape, such as the Green Deal, electric vehicle charge points, green buildings, EPC certification, planning implications, and emerging green technologies and regulations.
K.K15: Fundamentals of tax implications for conveyancing.
K.K16: Principles of regulatory arrangements for personal compliance with requirements as a licensed probate practitioner.
K.K17: Principles of law applicable to Will drafting, Lasting Powers of Attorney, and probate activities.
K.K18: Fundamentals of tax implications for probate activities.

S.S1: Manage caseload to prioritise client service and working in the clients’ best interests.
S.S2: Manage relationships with other lawyers and professional third parties using the most effective channels to progress caseloads.
S.S3: Manage clients and their expectations using the most effective channels to ensure clarity of understanding and facilitate clients in making informed choices.
S.S4: Handle points of contact with clients and key milestones in an organised and controlled way to lead the client experience.
S.S5: Use the principles of law and translate using plain English to provide clarification and explanation of the legal process and standard legal terms.
S.S6: Organise and manage workflow, such as legal files, case management, and accounting systems, to progress work within service level expectations.
S.S7: Protect interests of clients to ensure they receive an honest and lawful service.
S.S8: Manage risk in relation to legal and regulatory liabilities with regard to the client and the organisation, in order to satisfactorily discharge all professional obligations and limit exposure to complaints and reputational harm to the organisation.
S.S9: Manage legal processes, adhering to professional, supervisory and regulatory Codes of Conduct.
S.S10: Deliver a cost‑effective service by analysing the financial costs and fees generated by activities to inform good decision‑making through price transparency. For example, operating within the organisation’s cost model, or identifying when fees have exceeded the initial quote and taking mitigating action.
S.S11: Apply fraud protection measures and cyber security procedures in relation to transactions.
S.S12: Undertake Know Your Client activities to ensure a clear picture of needs and risks throughout legal transactions.
S.S13: Provide legal services in connection with sale and purchase and or re-mortgage or transfers of equity of residential property, including advice and guidance to clients relevant to conveyancing.
S.S14: Assess legal, regulatory and commercial risk related to sale and purchase, re-mortgage, or transfers of equity of residential property.
S.S15: Review and interpret legal documents related to sale and purchase, re-mortgage, or transfers of equity of residential property, including land registry file documents, statutory declarations, and completing documents as required.
S.S16: Provide legal services in connection with disposal of an estate, including advice and guidance to clients relevant to wills and probate.
S.S17: Assess legal, regulatory, and commercial risk related to disposal of estate.
S.S18: Review and interpret legal documents related to disposal of estate, including completing documentation.
S.S19: Draft legal documents related to probate transactions, such as Wills and Lasting Powers of Attorney.

B.B1: Accountable for a caseload of fee‑generating work and the costs incurred in delivering organisational outputs.
B.B2: Provides a timely, efficient, and legally correct service to generate a positive client experience. Manages professional relationships and resolves conveyancing or probate issues impartially and comprehensively, taking advice where needed.
B.B3: Generates solutions to conveyancing or probate problems, considering a range of options and identifying when to escalate problems that fall outside their remit.
B.B4: Takes an analytical approach to risk in legal transactions, ensuring that the client and organisation are protected from financial crimes and cybercrime.
B.B5: Adheres to professional codes of conduct in legal activities and relationships with others.

ST1312.name: Legal technician - conveyancing technician or probate technician

K.K1: Fundamentals of legal and regulatory liabilities with regards to the client and business to ensure compliance with all professional obligations.
K.K2: Fundamentals of the requirements for acting in the best interest of the client to protect clients and limit liability for negligence, such as identifying and acting for vulnerable clients.
K.K3: Fundamentals of anti-money laundering regulations in relation to legal transactions, including personal and professional liability.
K.K4: Fundamentals of the principles of client billing, including the principles of time-recording and fee charging structures.
K.K5: Various communication techniques to translate legal terms and concepts, using plain English to explain standard legal terms.
K.K6: Principles of digital tools and software, such as case management systems, databases, and online drafting software.
K.K7: Organisational data and information security standards, policies, and procedures relevant to data management activities.
K.K8: Fundamentals of the legal requirements relating to the use of data, including risks to data confidentiality posed by, and arising from, clients’ actions.
K.K9: Fundamentals of organisational vulnerabilities in digital systems, including cybersecurity, and steps that can be taken to mitigate those risks.
K.K10: Principles of best practice for diversity and inclusion when providing services to clients.
K.K11: The activities of the occupation which contribute to their ‘professional carbon footprint’, and steps that can be taken to reduce it.
K.K12: Fundamentals of professional regulatory arrangements for personal compliance with requirements.
K.K13: Principles of law applicable to conveyancing in respect of residential property.
K.K14: Fundamentals of taxes relevant for conveyancing.
K.K15: Approaches to stakeholder engagement and conducting Know Your Client activity to establish needs and risks within conveyancing activity.
K.K16: Principles of law applicable to Will drafting and Lasting Powers of Attorney.
K.K17: Principles of law applicable to probate.
K.K18: Fundamentals of taxes relevant for probate activities.
K.K19: Principles of establishing legal capacity and identifying potentially vulnerable clients, including during drafting of Wills, Lasting Powers of Attorney, and administration of estate.
K.K20: Principles of reviewing deceased client documents and records, including identification and significance of digital assets.
K.K21: Principles of drafting Wills and Lasting Powers of Attorney.

S.S1: Manage points of contact with clients and key milestones to lead the client experience.
S.S2: Administer legal procedures, under supervision, to work effectively with other lawyers and professional third parties.
S.S3: Assist clients and manage their expectations, under supervision, using the most effective channels and approaches.
S.S4: Protect client's interests, under supervision, to ensure they receive an honest and lawful service.
S.S5: Organise and administer workflow, such as legal files, case management, and accounting systems, to progress work within service level expectations.
S.S6: Use the principles of Law and translate using plain English to provide clarification and explanation of the legal process and standard Legal terms.
S.S7: Support service delivery with transparency and within the cost model of the organisation.
S.S8: Undertake Know Your Client activities throughout legal transactions.
S.S9: Undertake fraud protection measures and cyber security procedures in relation to legal transactions.
S.S10: Manage risk, under supervision, in relation to legal and regulatory liabilities with regards to the client and business.
S.S11: Undertake case load management, under supervision, to prioritise customer service and work in the client's best interests.
S.S12: Adhere to professional, supervisory, and regulatory requirements as required by organisational policies and procedures.
S.S13: Undertake the provision of legal services, under supervision, in connection with sale and purchase, re-mortgage, or transfers of equity of residential property, including advice and guidance to clients relevant to conveyancing.
S.S14: Review and administer legal documents related to sale and purchase, re-mortgage, or transfers of equity of residential property, including, land registry file documents, statutory declarations, and completing documents as required.
S.S15: Undertake the assessment of client risk, under supervision, in relation to Will drafting and Lasting Powers of Attorney, for example risks to vulnerable or elderly clients.
S.S16: Undertake the assessment of client risk, under supervision, in relation to probate.
S.S17: Undertake the provision of legal services, under supervision, in connection with administration of an estate, including advice and guidance to clients relevant to Will drafting and Lasting Powers of Attorney.
S.S18: Undertake the provision of legal services, under supervision, in connection with administration of an estate, including advice and guidance to clients relevant to probate.
S.S19: Undertake the review and administration of legal documents, under supervision, related to Will drafting and Lasting Powers of Attorney, including drafting and completing documents as required.
S.S20: Undertake the review and administration of legal documents, under supervision, related to probate, including drafting and completing documents as required.

B.B1: Accountable for own fee generating work and the costs incurred to deliver services in line with the organisation's approach.
B.B2: Provides a timely and efficient service.
B.B3: Helps generate solutions to legal problems, working with senior colleagues to consider a range of options and identify when to escalate problems outside of their remit.
B.B4: Works closely with senior colleagues to ensure that the client and organisation are protected from undue influence, financial crimes, and cybercrime.
B.B5: Approaches work with professionalism in legal service activities and relationships with others.

K.K1: The importance of continuing professional development throughout own career.
K.K2: The importance of safeguarding and relevant safeguarding processes.
K.K3: What is required by the Health and Care Professions Council, including but not limited to the Standards of conduct, performance and ethics.
K.K4: The importance of valid consent which is voluntary and informed, has due regard to capacity, is proportionate to the circumstances and is appropriately documented.
K.K5: The importance of capacity in the context of delivering care and treatment.
K.K6: The scope of a professional duty of care and how to exercise that duty.
K.K7: The legislation, policies and guidance relevant to own profession and scope of practice.
K.K8: The British, European and International Standards that govern and affect pathology laboratory practice.
K.K9: The importance of own mental and physical health and wellbeing strategies in maintaining fitness to practise.
K.K10: How to take appropriate action if own health may affect own ability to practise safely and effectively, including seeking help and support when necessary.
K.K11: The need for active participation in training, supervision and mentoring in supporting high standards of practice, and personal and professional conduct, and the importance of demonstrating this in practice.
K.K12: Equality legislation and how to apply it to own practice.
K.K13: The duty to make reasonable adjustments in practice and be able to make and support reasonable adjustments in own and others’ practice.
K.K14: The characteristics and consequences of barriers to inclusion, including for socially isolated groups.
K.K15: That equality, diversity and inclusion needs to be embedded in the application of all HCPC standards and across all areas of practice.
K.K16: When disclosure of confidential information may be required.
K.K17: The principles of information and data governance and the safe and effective use of health, social care and other relevant information.
K.K18: The need to ensure confidentiality is maintained in all situations in which service users rely on additional communication support, such as interpreters or translators.
K.K19: Recognise that the concepts of confidentiality and informed consent extend to all mediums, including illustrative clinical records such as photography, video and audio recordings and digital platforms.
K.K20: The characteristics and consequences of verbal and non-verbal communication and how these can be affected by difference of any kind including, but not limited to, protected characteristics, intersectional experiences and cultural differences.
K.K21: The need to support the communication needs of service users and carers, such as through the use of an appropriate interpreter.
K.K22: The need to provide service users or people acting on their behalf with the information necessary in accessible formats to enable them to make informed decisions.
K.K23: The risks and possible serious consequences of errors and omissions in both requests for, and results of, laboratory investigations.
K.K24: The need to adhere to protocols of specimen identification, including bar coding and electronic tag systems.
K.K25: The importance of backup storage of electronic data.
K.K26: The principles and practises of other health and care professionals and systems and how they interact with own profession.
K.K27: The need to build and sustain professional relationships as both an autonomous practitioner and collaboratively as a member of a team.
K.K28: The qualities, behaviours and benefits of leadership.
K.K29: Recognise that leadership is a skill all professionals can demonstrate.
K.K30: The need to engage service users and carers in planning and evaluating diagnostics and assessment outcomes to meet their needs and goals.
K.K31: The impact of pathology services on the service user care pathway.
K.K32: The value of reflective practice and the need to record the outcome of such reflection to support continuous improvement.
K.K33: The value of multi-disciplinary reviews, case conferences and other methods of review.
K.K34: The value of gathering and using data for quality assurance and improvement programmes.
K.K35: The structure and function of the human body, together with knowledge of physical and mental health, disease, disorder and dysfunction relevant to their profession.
K.K36: The principles and applications of scientific enquiry, including the evaluation of treatment efficacy and the research process.
K.K37: The roles of other professions in health and social care and understand how they may relate to the role of biomedical scientist.
K.K38: The structure and function of health and social care systems and services in the UK.
K.K39: The theoretical basis of, and the variety of approaches to, assessment and intervention.
K.K40: The underpinning scientific principles of investigations provided by clinical laboratory services.
K.K41: The role of the following specialisms in the diagnosis, treatment and management of disease: cellular science, blood science, infection science, molecular and genetic science and reproductive science.
K.K42: The techniques and associated instrumentation used in the practice of biomedical science.
K.K43: The biological hazards groups and associated containment levels.
K.K44: Recognise a range of research methodologies relevant to own role.
K.K45: Recognise the value of research to the critical evaluation of practice.
K.K46: The implications of non-analytical errors.
K.K47: The extent of the role and responsibility of the laboratory with respect to the quality management of hospital, primary care and community based laboratory services for near- service user testing and non-invasive techniques.
K.K48: The need to assess and evaluate new procedures prior to routine use.
K.K49: The need to maintain the safety of self and others, including service users, carers and colleagues.
K.K50: Relevant health and safety legislation, local operational procedures and policies.
K.K51: The principles of good laboratory practice.
K.K52: The role of own profession in health promotion, health education and preventing ill health.
K.K53: How social, economic and environmental factors, wider determinants of health, can influence a person’s health and well-being.

S.S1: Identify the limits of own practice and when to seek advice or refer to another professional or service.
S.S2: Recognise the need to manage own workload and resources safely and effectively, including managing the emotional burden that comes with working in a pressured environment.
S.S3: Keep own skills and knowledge up to date.
S.S4: Maintain high standards of personal and professional conduct.
S.S5: Promote and protect the service user’s interests at all times.
S.S6: Actively look for signs of abuse and engage in relevant safeguarding processes.
S.S7: Respect and uphold the rights, dignity, values, and autonomy of service users, including own role in the assessment, diagnostic, treatment and/or therapeutic process.
S.S8: Recognise that relationships with service users, carers and others should be based on mutual respect and trust, and maintain high standards of care in all circumstances.
S.S9: Obtain valid consent, which is voluntary and informed, has due regard to capacity, is proportionate to the circumstances and is appropriately documented.
S.S10: Exercise a professional duty of care.
S.S11: Apply legislation, policies and guidance relevant to own profession and scope of practice.
S.S12: Recognise the power imbalance which comes with being a health care professional, and ensure it is not for personal gain.
S.S13: Work in accordance with the British, European and International Standards that govern and affect pathology laboratory practice.
S.S14: Identify own anxiety and stress and recognise the potential impact on own practice.
S.S15: Develop and adopt clear strategies for physical and mental self-care and self-awareness, to maintain a high standard of professional effectiveness and a safe working environment.
S.S16: Recognise that they are personally responsible for and must be able to justify their decisions and actions.
S.S17: Use own skills, knowledge and experience, and the information available, to make informed decisions and / or take action where necessary.
S.S18: Make reasoned decisions to initiate, continue, modify or cease treatment or the use of techniques or procedures, and record the decisions and reasoning appropriately.
S.S19: Make and receive appropriate referrals, where necessary.
S.S20: Exercise personal initiative.
S.S21: Demonstrate a logical and systematic approach to problem solving.
S.S22: Use research, reasoning and problem solving skills when determining appropriate actions.
S.S23: Respond appropriately to the needs of all different groups and individuals in practice, recognising this can be affected by difference of any kind including, but not limited to, protected characteristics, intersectional experiences and cultural differences.
S.S24: Recognise the potential impact of own values, beliefs and personal biases, which may be unconscious, on practice and take personal action to ensure all service users and carers are treated appropriately with respect and dignity.
S.S25: Actively challenge barriers to inclusion, supporting the implementation of change  wherever possible.
S.S26: Adhere to the professional duty of confidentiality.
S.S27: Respond in a timely manner to situations where it is necessary to share information to safeguard service users, carers and/or the wider public and recognise situations where it is necessary to share information to safeguard service users, carers and/or the wider public.
S.S28: Use effective and appropriate verbal and non-verbal skills to communicate with service users, carers, colleagues and others.
S.S29: Communicate in English to the required standard for the profession.
S.S30: Work with service users and/or carers to facilitate the service user’s preferred role in decision-making, and provide service users and carers with the information they may need where appropriate.
S.S31: Modify own means of communication to address the individual communication needs and preferences of service users and carers, and remove any barriers to communication where possible.
S.S32: Use information, communication and digital technologies appropriate to own practice.
S.S33: Communicate the outcomes of biomedical procedures.
S.S34: Keep full, clear and accurate records in accordance with applicable legislation, protocols and guidelines.
S.S35: Manage records and all other information in accordance with applicable legislation, protocols and guidelines.
S.S36: Use digital record keeping tools, where required.
S.S37: Recognise and communicate the risks and possible serious consequences of errors and omissions in both requests for, and results of, laboratory investigations.
S.S38: Use systems for the accurate and correct identification of service users and laboratory specimens.
S.S39: Work in partnership with service users, carers, colleagues and others.
S.S40: Contribute effectively to work undertaken as part of a multi-disciplinary team.
S.S41: Identify anxiety and stress in service users, carers and colleagues, adapting own practice and providing support where appropriate.
S.S42: Identify own leadership qualities, behaviours and approaches, taking into account the importance of equality, diversity and inclusion.
S.S43: Demonstrate leadership behaviours appropriate to own practice.
S.S44: Act as a role model for others.
S.S45: Promote and engage in the learning of others.
S.S46: Engage in evidence-based practice.
S.S47: Gather and use feedback and information, including qualitative and quantitative data, to evaluate the responses of service users to own care.
S.S48: Monitor and systematically evaluate the quality of practice, and maintain an effective quality management and quality assurance process working towards continual improvement.
S.S49: Participate in quality management, including quality control, quality assurance, clinical governance and the use of appropriate outcome measures.
S.S50: Evaluate care plans or intervention plans using recognised and appropriate outcome measures, in conjunction with the service user where possible, and revise the plans as necessary.
S.S51: Select and apply quality and process control measures.
S.S52: Identify and respond appropriately to abnormal outcomes from quality indicators.
S.S53: Apply the principles and applications of scientific enquiry, including the evaluation of treatment efficacy and the research process.
S.S54: Evaluate analyses using qualitative and quantitative methods to aid the diagnosis, screening and monitoring of health and disorders.
S.S55: Change own practice as needed to take account of new developments, technologies and changing contexts.
S.S56: Gather appropriate information.
S.S57: Analyse and critically evaluate the information collected.
S.S58: Select and use appropriate assessment techniques and equipment.
S.S59: Undertake and record a thorough, sensitive, and detailed assessment.
S.S60: Undertake or arrange investigations as appropriate.
S.S61: Conduct appropriate assessment or monitoring procedures, treatment, therapy or other actions safely and effectively.
S.S62: Critically evaluate research and other evidence to inform own practice.
S.S63: Engage service users in research as appropriate.
S.S64: Perform and supervise procedures in clinical laboratory investigations to reproducible standards.
S.S65: Operate and utilise specialist equipment according to own discipline.
S.S66: Validate scientific and technical data and observations according to pre-determined quality standards.
S.S67: Demonstrate proficiency in practical skills in cellular science, blood science, infection science, molecular and genetic science and reproductive science, where appropriate to the discipline.
S.S68: Demonstrate practical skills in the processing and analysis of specimens including specimen identification, the effect of storage on specimens and the safe retrieval of specimens.
S.S69: Demonstrate practical skills in the investigation of disease processes.
S.S70: Work in conformance with standard operating procedures and conditions.
S.S71: Work with accuracy and precision.
S.S72: Perform calibration and quality control checks.
S.S73: Demonstrate operational management of laboratory equipment to check that equipment is functioning within its specifications and to respond appropriately to abnormalities.
S.S74: Formulate specific and appropriate management plans including the setting of timescales.
S.S75: Select suitable specimens and procedures relevant to service users’ clinical needs, including collection and preparation of specimens as and when appropriate.
S.S76: investigate and monitor disease processes and normal states.
S.S77: Use standard operating procedures for analyses including point of care in vitro diagnostic devices.
S.S78: Use statistical packages and present data in an appropriate format.
S.S79: Design experiments, report, interpret and present data using scientific convention, including application of SI units and other units used in biomedical science.
S.S80: Safely interpret and authorise service user results.
S.S81: Comply with all relevant health and safety legislation, local operational procedures and policies.
S.S82: Work safely, including being able to select appropriate hazard control and risk management, reduction or elimination techniques in a safe manner and in accordance with health and safety legislation.
S.S83: Select appropriate personal protective equipment and use it correctly.
S.S84: Establish safe environments for practice, which appropriately manages risk.
S.S85: Empower and enable individuals, including service users and colleagues, to play a part in managing their own health.
S.S86: Engage in occupational health, including being aware of immunisation requirements.

B.B1: Demonstrate a logical and systematic approach to problem solving.
B.B2: Treat people with dignity.
B.B3: Show respect and empathy for those you work with.
B.B4: Be adaptable, reliable and consistent.
B.B5: Uphold high quality and safe practice.

ST1333.name: Ship’s master – less than 500 gross tonnage near coastal

K.K1: Master’s over-riding authority as defined by the International Safety Management (ISM) code.
K.K2: Flag state and international regulations: International Convention for the Safety of life at sea (SOLAS), International convention for the Prevention of Pollution from Ships (MARPOL), Maritime Labour Convention (MLC), International Regulations Prevention of Collision at Sea (IRPCS), International Ship and Port Facilities Code (ISPS), International Aeronautical and Maritime Search and Rescue (IAMSAR), Life Saving Appliances (LSA) Code, Fire Safety Systems Code (FSS), Lifting Operations and Lifting Equipment Regulations (LOLER), Code of Safe working practices for merchant seafarers (UK only) (COSWP).
K.K3: Audit requirements, including, port state control, flag states and classification society.
K.K4: Company’s safety management procedures: Safety Management System (SMS), reporting procedures and internal audit requirements.
K.K5: Crew certification, composition and requirements by rank or position.
K.K6: The vessel particulars as outlined in the Officer of the Watch Training Record Book (TRB).
K.K7: Official documents and requirements for their maintenance: Official Log Book, Oil Record Book, Garbage Record Book, Radio Log, Crew list, Muster list.
K.K8: Crew welfare and wellbeing.
K.K9: Fatigue and Hours of Rest Regulations and how this relates to the working patterns and requirements of the small vessel sector.
K.K10: Emergency situation responses on board a vessel.
K.K11: Conflict management techniques.
K.K12: The commercial influences and priorities of the operation.
K.K13: Principles and techniques of watchkeeping: requirements at sea, requirements at anchor, requirements in port, limitations of watchkeeping responsibility and crew’s authority to call Master whenever deemed necessary, Competent Harbour Authority (CHA) regulations and requirements.
K.K14: Navigation considerations: operations in restricted visibility, weather routing, tidal and meteorological conditions, navigational features including restricted areas and hazards, buoyage,  situational awareness of other traffic: proximity, speed.
K.K15: Passage Planning including Emergency Contingencies: passage planning and navigational considerations for all conditions, their importance, and legal application, application and safe usage of autopilot systems, ports of refuge and safe havens, emergency steering and communications, emergency command and control.
K.K16: Under keel clearance and contributing factors: squat, heave, pitch, roll, list.
K.K17: The different types and purposes of navigational systems: paper charts, electronic navigational aids (ECS, ECDIS) and maritime publications.
K.K18: Marine notices and their purpose: Notices to Mariners, Temporary & Preliminary Notice to Mariners, ALRS and Navigational Aids.
K.K19: Routine and emergency communications equipment, including maintaining appropriate radio watch.
K.K20: Speed and manoeuvring including in restricted conditions.
K.K21: Mooring Plans: using shoreside assistance, self-mooring, buoys and anchoring, adjusting moorings and anchors as required and according to tide and prevailing weather conditions.
K.K22: Crew competence requirements.
K.K23: Requirements for safe means of access and egress: safety and monitoring of gangway, monitoring or tide and weather conditions, level access requirements.
K.K24: Methods, Systems and Tools to manage and mitigate risk: Master’s standing orders Code of Safe Working Practices (COSWP) Competent Harbour Authorities (CHAs) regulations, Safe systems of work, Task Risk Assessment / hierarchy of risk, Permit to work, Toolbox talks, Stop work authority, Control of Substances Hazardous to Health (COSHH), Lifting Appliance regulations (LOLER), Cargo movements, Enclosed space entry, Hot work, Isolation – electrical / liquid / gas, Working at height, Working overside, Emergency exercises, Emergency communications, Critical equipment and functions, Winches, windlass, towing hooks, cranes, lifting equipment, Defect reporting, Planned maintenance and recording.
K.K25: Different types of cargoes and cargo operations: processes for the safe handling, stowage and securing of cargoes, requirements of the International Maritime Dangerous Goods (IMDG) Code.
K.K26: Vessel stability: stages of loading, stability calculations.
K.K27: The operation, maintenance and servicing requirements of the safety equipment on board the vessel.
