K.K28: Handling, care and aftercare of casualties.
K.K29: Role and responsibilities of the Designated Person Ashore (DPA).
K.K30: Provisioning: company requirements, number of personnel and intended voyage, calculations and specific requirements for consumables, fresh produce, potable water, dry stores, cleaning products, personal hygiene.
K.K31: Principles of managing performance on board.
K.K32: Sector and organisational specific handover requirements: state of navigation equipment and machinery, vessel accounts, MLC compliance, disciplinary or grievance matters.
K.K33: Handling and propulsion characteristics of different vessel types, including hydrodynamic interaction.
K.K34: Accident and incident reporting processes, including accident investigation techniques.
K.K35: Information technology: Management Information Systems (MIS), spreadsheets, presentation, word processing, email, virtual communication and learning platforms. General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR). Cyber security.
K.K36: Planned and unplanned CPD and recording methods.
K.K37: Written communication techniques. Plain English principles. Industry terminology.
K.K38: Verbal communication techniques. Giving and receiving information. Matching style to audience. Industry terminology.
K.K39: Principles of effective leadership and management, including delegation and the prioritisation of resources.
K.K40: Environmental and sustainability requirements and guidance, for example, environmental management systems standard, environmental protection act, alternative fuel sources.
K.K41: Principles of equity, diversity, and inclusion in the workplace. Unconscious bias. The Equality act - requirements on organisations.
K.K42: Problem solving tools and techniques.

S.S1: Safely navigate vessel to meet operational and customer requirements.
S.S2: Manage the safe conduct of navigation of the passage, including use of autopilot system.
S.S3: Manage all aspects of navigational information.
S.S4: Select steering method according to prevailing environmental conditions.
S.S5: Implement performance checks and tests of navigation systems.
S.S6: Evaluate and respond to meteorological information.
S.S7: Manage compliance with legislation and guidance on lights, shapes and sound signals aboard the vessel.
S.S8: Follow principles and procedures of vessel traffic monitoring, including in response to environmental conditions.
S.S9: Act according to collision regulations to avoid hazardous close encounters and collisions with other vessels.
S.S10: Allocate tasks and responsibilities to crew.
S.S11: Manage crew competences.
S.S12: Manage and handle cargoes, stores, bunkers and waste.
S.S13: Monitor and manage vessel stability, using load-line calculations consistent with size of vessel.
S.S14: Manage cargo operations in accordance with cargo plan or other documents.
S.S15: Manage and supervise others in handling, hazardous and harmful cargoes in compliance with international regulations and safe working practices.
S.S16: Manoeuvre a vessel in near-coastal waters, taking account of compliance requirements, vessel type, capability, loading and external factors such as tide and weather.
S.S17: Manage and supervise others in operating, plant auxiliary machinery and equipment.
S.S18: Select mooring or anchoring solutions in accordance with compliance, vessel type, capability, loading and external factors such as tide and weather.
S.S19: Operate vessels with a range of propulsion types in near coastal waters, for example single screw, twin screw, variable pitch, directional propulsion.
S.S20: Maintain or replace life-saving appliances and other safety equipment.
S.S21: Communicate Master’s standing orders to internal and external stakeholders.
S.S22: Operate within limits of role and follow escalation procedures.
S.S23: Apply the Code of Safe Working Practices for Merchant Seafarers.
S.S24: Identify skills gaps within the crew and deliver coaching and mentoring.
S.S25: Identify and manage the signs of fatigue.
S.S26: Dynamically risk assess the type and scale of an emergency situation as it develops.
S.S27: Manage coordination of emergency response, including with the emergency services in the event of a medevac.
S.S28: Administer emergency first aid.
S.S29: Implement own organisation’s security procedures.
S.S30: Conducts vessel handover.
S.S31: Applies accident and incident reporting processes and accident investigation techniques.
S.S32: Use vessel technology, complying with GDPR and cyber security regulations and policies.
S.S33: Carry out and record planned and unplanned learning and development activities for self and others.
S.S34: Communicate in writing with others for example, internal and external customers, colleagues, and managers.
S.S35: Communicate with others verbally for example, colleagues and stakeholders.
S.S36: Manage human resources onboard.
S.S37: Satisfy environmental and sustainability requirements and guidance, for example, environmental management systems standard, environmental protection act, alternative fuel sources.
S.S38: Apply and promote policies and practices to support equity, diversity and inclusion.
S.S39: Diagnose, investigate and resolve problems in line with responsibilities.

B.B1: Establishes a safe working environment and practices that foster a positive safety culture.
B.B2: Committed to maintaining and enhancing competence of self and others through CPD.
B.B3: Takes ownership of the Master’s responsibilities and work of self and others.
B.B4: Being calm and effective under pressure.
B.B5: Actively seeks to minimise negative environmental impact and waste.
B.B6: Be a role model for the organisation and maritime sector with regards to diversity, equality, and inclusion.

ST1337.name: Library, information and knowledge management professional

K.K1: Specialist library, information, and knowledge systems and how to evaluate them within the context of organisational needs.
K.K2: Sources of up-to-date and reliable data and information from trusted bodies and how to assess their quality.
K.K3: Tools and information retrieval techniques used for discovering information and evaluating the quality of outputs to meet stakeholder requirements.
K.K4: Techniques and tools for research, analysis, and critical appraisal.
K.K5: Principles of information and knowledge architecture.
K.K6: Information and knowledge flows within their organisation.
K.K7: How to write and implement an information or knowledge strategy through influencing and negotiating with stakeholders.
K.K8: Principles of organising information and collection management, such as classification systems, taxonomies, and metadata.
K.K9: Procedures to manage the lifecycle of knowledge and information assets, from identification through to preservation.
K.K10: The principles and approaches of information literacy and related literacies, their frameworks, application, and promotion.
K.K11: Stakeholder needs and how they access and use information.
K.K12: User behaviour and the use of teaching methods to enhance user information literacy skills.
K.K13: Current and emerging trends in technology, and the impact on service provision and professional practice.
K.K14: Best practice and latest developments in the wider information and knowledge professions.
K.K15: Ethics and principles that impact on the role of the library, information, and knowledge management (LI&KM) professional, and implications for practice within the organisation.
K.K16: Marketing and promotion tools and techniques to promote services.
K.K17: The role of the library, information, and knowledge management (LI&KM) professional, and how it contributes to the delivery of organisation strategy and objectives.
K.K18: The key stakeholders within the organisation and how they interact to contribute to the wider organisational goals.
K.K19: The international legislative environment and relevant regulatory requirements, such as General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), Copyright, Intellectual Property (IP), and their impact on professional practice and service provision in the organisation.
K.K20: Equity, diversity, and inclusion (EDI), and its application in the workplace and service practice.
K.K21: The principles of environmental sustainability, legislation, and best practice, and its impact on the service.
K.K22: Project management tools and techniques.
K.K23: Risk identification, management, and mitigation for business continuity.
K.K24: Financial planning, budget management, procurement, and contract management.
K.K25: Leadership and management practice, staff development and ethical leadership.
K.K26: Coaching and mentoring methods.
K.K27: Negotiation and influencing models and techniques.

S.S1: Search, critically appraise, synthesise, and summarise data, information, or knowledge.
S.S2: Identify reliable and trusted information sources.
S.S3: Write reports and present information for stakeholders.
S.S4: Identify resources and information to meet stakeholder needs.
S.S5: Assist stakeholders with information enquiries and research needs.
S.S6: Design and develop training programmes and materials to facilitate the development of information and related literacies for stakeholders.
S.S7: Analyse user needs to design tools and services for stakeholders.
S.S8: Select, organise, and classify information resources to assist resource discovery.
S.S9: Manage and develop information, data, and reading resources.
S.S10: Use specialist library, information, and knowledge systems to manage information.
S.S11: Analyse and evaluate evidence and data to respond to stakeholder needs to improve service provision.
S.S12: Use evidence and data to demonstrate their service and profession within the organisation and to stakeholders.
S.S13: Apply an evidence-based approach to decision-making and development of policies and procedures.
S.S14: Contribute to projects and the transformation of library and information services across organisational boundaries, such as those impacted by targets.
S.S15: Manage and use relevant regulatory requirements, such as General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), Copyright, Intellectual Property (IP), for service provision.
S.S16: Apply equity, diversity, and inclusion (EDI) practices in line with organisation policies.
S.S17: Collaborate on the strategic planning for their organisation, such as objective setting and business planning.
S.S18: Use project management techniques to prioritise tasks.
S.S19: Analyse problems and provide solutions.
S.S20: Lead projects or elements of projects to implement change or continuous improvement of their service.
S.S21: Communicate with stakeholders using liaison and facilitation skills.
S.S22: Influence and negotiate with stakeholders to shape and agree service aims.
S.S23: Coach and mentor individuals within their organisation.
S.S24: Use horizon scanning to research and implement new developments in the profession within the organisational context.
S.S25: Present information and data to stakeholders.
S.S26: Generate, manipulate, and present digital information to assist and inform users and stakeholders of the service.
S.S27: Identify the impact of technology on service provision and professional practice.

B.B1: Role models ethical and inclusive behaviours and practices.
B.B2: Works flexibly and creatively adapts to circumstances.
B.B3: Seeks learning opportunities and continuous professional development for self.
B.B4: Takes responsibility, shows initiative, and is organised.
B.B5: Takes personal accountability aligned to clear professional values.
B.B6: Works collaboratively with others across the organisation and external stakeholders.

K.K1: The UK social and policy context and how this will inform and impact their research.
K.K2: The role social research plays in providing evidence for social or policy questions.
K.K3: The advantages and limitations of qualitative and quantitative research and evaluation theories and methods, and how to apply these in research design.
K.K4: Types of bias in research and how to identify and avoid them.
K.K5: Quantitative research approaches and techniques, such as sampling, data collection, data cleaning, descriptive and inferential statistics.
K.K6: Qualitative research approaches and techniques, such as recruiting participants, sampling and analysis.
K.K7: Social research evaluation approaches and techniques such as theory of change, impact evaluation and process evaluation.
K.K8: Critical approaches to secondary research, such as how to source information, assess research quality and use research from previously conducted studies.
K.K9: Commercial frameworks, and how to write, contribute or respond to proposals, such as tender responses or grant proposals.
K.K10: The organisation’s processes, procedures, and code of conduct.
K.K11: Data protection regulation and legislation requirements, such as how to store, process and transfer data in line with the Data Protection Act and GDPR.
K.K12: The key ethical issues and principles in research outlined in professional body guidance, such as informed consent and safeguarding.
K.K13: Social inclusion, accessibility and how to design research to enable this.
K.K14: Quality assurance principles and techniques for social research.
K.K15: Cost estimation techniques and budgetary requirements for research.
K.K16: Processes for procuring suppliers.
K.K17: Project management principles and techniques.
K.K18: Resource management and allocation.
K.K19: Digital tools and software for fieldwork, analysis, communication, and reporting, and the impact of emerging technologies such as AI on research.
K.K20: Reporting styles for social research.
K.K21: Communication, presenting, influencing, and negotiating techniques and strategies, to interact with a range of key stakeholders.
K.K22: Tools and principles for training and people development.
K.K23: Role of technical professionals within a research team and how they impact each stage of a research project.
K.K24: Taking account of the carbon footprint of each research project and considering how to reduce this.

S.S1: Interpret research objectives and recommend which research methods are the most appropriate to meet objectives.
S.S2: Lead on the design of smaller research or evaluation studies and collaborate with others to design larger studies.
S.S3: Lead and design data collection tools such as topic guides and questionnaires.
S.S4: Undertake data collection using qualitative and quantitative research techniques.
S.S5: Design research projects with consideration of sustainability best practice including reducing carbon emissions.
S.S6: Undertake quality assurance and checks to avoid data errors.
S.S7: Work with commercial teams and contractors to commission and deliver social research.
S.S8: Project manage smaller research projects and manage discrete workstreams within larger studies.
S.S9: Interpret and apply relevant legislation, Regulations, Codes of Practice, and ethical guidance where appropriate to their work.
S.S10: Clean, analyse, and manipulate qualitative and quantitative data, including descriptive and inferential statistical analysis.
S.S11: Evaluate and bring together evidence from a range of sources to inform decisions.
S.S12: Interpret and derive insights from quantitative, qualitative, and secondary analytical and research outputs.
S.S13: Collaborate with stakeholders and research customers to design research projects.
S.S14: Use influencing and negotiation skills and techniques to design and deliver research projects.
S.S15: Develop and deliver analytical or professional training teach-ins or documents for others.
S.S16: Report and present on research findings, adapting the approach for different audiences.
S.S17: Organise and lead meetings, using digital collaboration tools to work with colleagues.
S.S18: Use software to clean, manage, manipulate, and analyse qualitative and quantitative research data and identify solutions to research questions.

B.B1: Champions ethical behaviour and meets required standards of practice.
B.B2: Works flexibly and adapts to circumstances.
B.B3: Seek learning and continuous professional development opportunities for self and wider team.
B.B4: Takes responsibility, shows initiative, and is organised.
B.B5: Champions accessibility and diversity by designing inclusive research.
B.B6: Works collaboratively with others across the organisation and external stakeholders.

K.K1: The DFT role and how it contributes to investigations and the criminal justice process.
K.K2: What are the considerations for maximising evidential capture and sequencing.
K.K3: Forensic science regulators codes of practice and activities.
K.K4: The digital forensics terminology.
K.K5: Standard operating procedures to a dynamic operational environment.
K.K6: Make appropriate decisions of how to progress devices through the evidential process utilising local procedures.
K.K7: The national good practise guidance for the capture and preservation of digital evidence  (For example the ACPO principles and NPCC Good Practises Advice manual).
K.K8: The workings, principles and relevant use of a range of digital devices, peripherals and associated operating systems relevant to their role in digital forensics.
K.K9: Functions, features, and limitations of digital forensics tools, components and software.
K.K10: Evidence management, continuity and integrity of evidence and how it relates to investigations.
K.K11: Different forensic working environments and understand how to select and utilise forensic tools appropriate to the circumstances.
K.K12: What other forensic evidence types can be recovered from devices and the best methods and sequence for evidential capture and preservation.
K.K13: Different ways of recording and presenting relevant investigative information (reports, contemporaneous notes/statement writing) and how they should be appropriately used and presented.
K.K14: The principles of working in a quality controlled setting.  To include; knowledge of appropriate accreditation bodies, the quality management process/system and individual legal entities, individual expectations of working in a quality controlled environment.
K.K15: Quality management system information associated with reporting, corrective action, validation, verification, individual competency and peer review, and how to navigate this information.
K.K16: The health & safety procedures/legislation. Organisational sustainability procedures.
K.K17: The risks of processing sensitive or potentially distressing content while understanding the benefits of good wellbeing working practices.
K.K18: Awareness of the impact of diversity and inclusion such as on teamwork, client relationships and organisational strategies.
K.K19: Understand the requirements for continual professional development within the industry.
K.K20: Procedures for capturing and preserving evidence.

S.S1: Use appropriate terminology defined by the forensic science regulator and included in standard operating procedures.
S.S2: Standard operating procedures for a range of digital forensic capture and preservation processes.
S.S3: Apply standard methodology in a safe and effective way while working in operational environments.
S.S4: Demonstrate confidentiality, integrity, and availability limitations of secure devices and best practice methods for preserving evidence.
S.S5: Implement limitations of competency and authority and escalate when necessary.
S.S6: Considered decision making.
S.S7: Adhere to powers of seizure/examinations and legislation.  Demonstrate compliance with this through the appropriate use of standard forms.
S.S8: Adopt communication methods suitable for audience. Using technical language where appropriate.
S.S9: Identify, set up and use a range hardware components and devices.
S.S10: Demonstrate correct use of write blocking techniques.
S.S11: Manage applications software relevant to the role.
S.S12: Show an ability to troubleshoot real-world device and network issues effectively and diagnosing and correcting common software, hardware, or connectivity problems.
S.S13: Use features and functions of common specialist digital forensic tools and software. Selecting based on suitability.
S.S14: Understanding the responsibilities of data management.
S.S15: Provide preservation advice for physical evidence (DNA, fingerprints) for new digital exhibits, taking into account anticontamination and cross contamination.
S.S16: Apply continuity of evidence and appropriate recording of a chain of evidence or an exhibit for disclosure purposes.
S.S17: Produce notes documenting activity undertaken during the capture and preservation processes; including the appropriate use of digital evidence/case management systems.
S.S18: Produce evidential continuity witness statements and demonstrate how to present in a courtroom or professional enquiry
S.S19: Navigate and inform a quality management system information associated with reporting, corrective action, validation, verification, individual competency and peer review.
S.S20: Conducting dynamic risk assessments, taking into account the safety of all parties and using PPE.
S.S21: Apply strategies to develop personal resilience and maintain welling, know how to access organisational support mechanisms.
S.S22: Process technical information effectively and accurately to provide critical thinking to issues and problems.

B.B1: Acts in an ethical and professional manner in the workplace.
B.B2: Builds and maintains positive relationships with colleagues, customers and suppliers.
B.B3: Takes an organised and methodical approach to their work and scrutinises own objectivity.
B.B4: Takes personal responsibility for developing knowledge and skills and continually seeks to improve their performance.
B.B5: Commitment to develop personal resilience and maintain wellbeing.
B.B6: Sustainable practices and challenge unsustainable practices.
B.B7: Support an inclusive culture and treat colleagues and external stakeholders fairly and with respect without bias or discrimination.

K.K1: Technical understanding of cake making methods.
K.K2: Technical understanding of biscuit making methods.
K.K3: Technical understanding of sweet and savoury pastry making methods.
K.K4: Technical understanding of fermented and chemically leavened dough product making methods.
K.K5: The purpose of recipe specifications and consequences of non-compliance with recipe specifications.
K.K6: Bakery Equipment appropriate to different processes and products.
K.K7: Principles of Bakery production planning and scheduling.
K.K8: Key performance indicators and how they are used to manage production and bakery performance.
K.K9: Production methods and processes: monitoring, checks, equipment setting.
K.K10: Principles of continuous improvement.
K.K11: Importance of training a bakery team.
K.K12: Principles of team working.
K.K13: Principles of organoleptic testing to measure the quality of products.
K.K14: Principles for benchmarking products.
K.K15: Principles of route cause analysis and preventative action.
K.K16: Productive use, care and preventative maintenance of bakery equipment and technology.
K.K17: The principles of food safety supervision for bakery.
K.K18: Principles of stock control and inventory management.
K.K19: Health and safety legislation.
K.K20: Food safety legislation.
K.K21: Environmental legislation, waste management, and pest control principles in bakeries.
K.K22: Principles of handling feedback and complaints.
K.K23: Packaging types, functionality, sustainability and effects on shelf life and quality, for example; gas flushing, poly propylene, perforated film, paper, waxed paper.
K.K24: Scientific principles of the mechanical dough development method, including the Chorleywood Bread Process.
K.K25: Scientific principles of fermentation and sourdough microbiology.
K.K26: Importance of food labelling legislation, and the legal consequences of non-compliance.
K.K27: Bakery trends and implications of seasonality.
K.K28: Principles of bakery profitability.
K.K29: Sales and promotion models for different media including social and digital.
K.K30: Technical understanding of couverture chocolate.

S.S1: Produce refined cake products using complex techniques.
S.S2: Produce refined biscuit products using complex techniques.
S.S3: Produce refined sweet and savoury pastry products using complex techniques.
S.S4: Produce refined fermented and chemically leavened dough product using complex techniques.
S.S5: Coordinate ingredients, processing aids, equipment/ technology, and production techniques to achieve expected product results.
S.S6: Apply refined finishing techniques.
S.S7: Contribute to production planning to meet demand.
S.S8: Monitor the efficiency of productivity.
S.S9: Manage preparation, baking, and finishing techniques.
S.S10: Contribute to the development of operating procedures and continual improvement activity.
S.S11: Provide guidance or training to colleagues.
S.S12: Communicate with colleagues and stakeholders visually and verbally.
S.S13: Conduct a test baking protocol to compare bakery ingredients and processes applying techniques to measure quality; physical and organoleptic properties.
S.S14: Contribute to the development, improvement and adaptation of products.
S.S15: Interpret quality information and data to identify and rectify real time errors during production.
S.S16: Identify and resolve issues with bakery equipment and technology.
S.S17: Plan the ordering of stock and materials in line with production demand.
S.S18: Manage the rotation and safe storage of stock and materials in line with production demand.
S.S19: Monitor compliance with food safety, health and safety at work, and environmental legislation.
S.S20: Measure and monitor product yield efficiency and waste minimisation.
S.S21: Assist in the resolution of feedback and complaints and implement changes for improvement.

B.B1: Prioritises and promotes health and safety and food safety.
B.B2: Accountable for own actions and actions of team.
B.B3: Maintains a customer focus.
B.B4: Positive and adaptable in approach to new demands and situations.
B.B5: Leads a team effectively.
B.B6: Committed to maintaining knowledge of current industry best practices.

K.K1: Formation of the earth and the solar system and how this informs our understanding of planet earth.
K.K2: Earth layers, their movement, distribution of materials, composition and properties of materials and how this can be used to predict natural events.
K.K3: Petrography and petrology of rocks and minerals, including metamorphic processes, sedimentary processes and igneous processes.
K.K4: The role of modelling in earth system science.
K.K5: Earth surface processes, including superficial deposits, hydrology, geomorphology and the impact on landscape natural resources and natural hazards.
K.K6: Sub-surface processes, including hydrogeology, fluid flow, rock deformation and mechanics and how this informs 4D distribution of Earth materials, natural resources and natural hazards.
K.K7: Earth history, evolution and stratigraphy and how this can inform modelling and geoscientist predictions.
K.K8: Long term and anthropogenic climate change and how it can be influenced by the management and development of natural and renewable resources, and how this affects natural and human related geohazards.
K.K9: Applied geoscience, including engineering geology, geophysics, environmental geology, urban geology, mining geology.
K.K10: Socio-environmental global development frameworks (for example, UN Sustainable Development Goals) and the concepts of geoethics and environmental justice.
K.K11: Socio-economic, commercial and business principles relevant to the earth sciences.
K.K12: Global perspectives on the historical development of the geosciences and how these influence the approach of current practices and geopolitics.
K.K13: Coding to facilitate analysis, modelling and interpretation of Earth and other planetary systems across a range of scales.
K.K14: Creation, analysis and interpretation of geospatial data (including maps and cross-sections) in both digital and analogue formats, using appropriate professional software such as a Geographic Information System (GIS).
K.K15: Instrument-based investigation of the surface and sub-surface of the Earth, including drilling, remote sensing, geophysical and geochemical techniques.
K.K16: Handling of data and drawing conclusions, taking into account uncertainty and incomplete data.
K.K17: Health and safety risk, hazard and consequence definitions and how they apply to carrying out geoscientific tasks, including design.
K.K18: The importance of Equality, Diversity and Inclusion (EDI), how it is managed within the employer and geoscientific community.
K.K19: The impact of exploitation of other cultures and geopolitical landscape.
K.K20: The implications, limitations and consequences of applying different geoscientific theories, paradigms, concepts and principles.
K.K21: Methods used to cost geoscientific projects, and the principles of cost management and control.
K.K22: How to establish the client brief, the form of contract and terms of engagement.
K.K23: Company procedures and policies and organisational objectives.
K.K24: Numerical, statistical, geostatistical and qualitative techniques in the evaluation of data and information.
K.K25: The importance of keeping up to date with advances in working practices and emerging technologies and how this can positively impact the wider business.

S.S1: Integrate and critically evaluate information from different sources to test findings and hypotheses.
S.S2: Consider, appraise and inform ground related issues to multidisciplinary teams.
S.S3: Define complex geoscience related problems, a strategy to understand the problem and evaluate possible solutions. For example: resource scarcity, foundations, sustainability energy supply.
S.S4: Test geological models to inform and design investigations.
S.S5: Plan, conduct and present independent geoscientific projects with appropriate guidance.
S.S6: Consider inconsistencies and uncertainty and gaps in data when developing geological models.
S.S7: Apply geoscientific theories, paradigms, concepts and principles.
S.S8: Conduct and supervise fieldwork, geoscience mapping and laboratory investigations as appropriate and in accordance with safe working practices and legal requirements.
S.S9: Apply appropriate numerical, statistical, geostatistical and qualitative techniques in the evaluation of data and information.
S.S10: Utilise digital technologies, instrumentation and remote sensing for the collection, analysis and modelling of data.
S.S11: Communicate relevant geoscience insights to external stakeholders using different formats and media.
S.S12: Evaluate personal strengths and weaknesses and develop personal development goals.
S.S13: Plan and organise own workload and contribute to the completion of geoscientific projects.
S.S14: Collaborate with stakeholders  online, remotely and in person, using appropriate techniques.
S.S15: Carry out geoscientific site surveys independently across a range of environments.
S.S16: Keep up to date with advances in working practices and emerging technologies and disseminate learning across the organisation.
S.S17: Prepare geoscience risk assessments in accordance with associated legislation for health, safety and wellbeing.
S.S18: Apply equality, diversity and inclusivity good practice to inform decision making.
S.S19: Produce recommendations that align to client requirements and organisational objectives that demonstrate the application of ethical, environmental sustainability and compliance with relevant legislation.

B.B1: Act as a role model and advocate for ethical, social, environmental and sustainable professional practices.
B.B2: Collaborate and promote teamwork across disciplines.
B.B3: Adapt to and show resilience in challenging or changing situation.
B.B4: Commit to their own and supports others' professional development.
B.B5: Act as an advocate for equality, diversity, and inclusion.
B.B6: Act as a role model and advocate for health, safety and wellbeing.
B.B7: Reflect on the process of learning and evaluate personal strengths and weaknesses.
B.B8: Inspires, guides and motivates others.
B.B9: Takes responsibility for decisions, designs and procedures.
B.B10: Recognise and respect the views of others and acknowledges different perspectives.

K.K1: Soil characteristics including how soils differ, soil nutrients, the soil formation process (pedogenesis), geology, topography, types and structure considering the combination and interaction of soil chemical, physical and biological properties.
K.K2: How to carry out a soil survey (pedology) including classifying soils to a specified standard, considering health and safety, external soil data,  relevant legislation, social, economic and environmental factors.
K.K3: Nutrient management and neutrality, carbon accounting and greenhouse gas emission assessment and monitoring, pollution assessments and control, flood risk and natural capital accounting.
K.K4: Substrates and amendments used to benefit soils including composts, and remediation in contaminated soils.
K.K5: Soil-water-air-plant-animal interactions and interfaces including how soil health and resilience is built.
K.K6: Soil function and the value of soil ecosystem goods and services for both businesses and the wider environment.
K.K7: Soil health including soil degradation and considerations for construction, agricultural and horticultural management.
K.K8: Land management implications including contamination, compaction, soil sealing and remediation and considerations for construction, agricultural and horticultural management.
K.K9: The interactions between soil and climate change including organic matter depletion and soil carbon.
K.K10: Existing UK policy, guidance, legislation and strategies, basic protection measures for soils and its place within an international context and the UN Sustainable Development Goals.
K.K11: How different soils respond to different types of management practices.
K.K12: The formation and creation of artificial or engineered soils and how they can be used.
K.K13: Land management economics including the cost of getting it wrong: for horticulture, forestry, construction and soil use for habitat creation.
K.K14: The global political environment and its impact on soil management.
K.K15: The wider ecological and archaeological environment and its impact on soil management.
K.K16: Land drainage design and sustainable drainage systems (SuDS) for different environments and drainage system and catchment hydrological modelling.
K.K17: Secondary data and information including soil maps, Geographic Information System Mapping (GIS).
K.K18: Levels of authority and the circumstances in which escalation or referral to other colleagues or stakeholders is required.
K.K19: Working with clients including project management and understanding the brief.
K.K20: Developing and implementing soil resource and research plans, sustainable soil management plans, budgets (financial and non-financial), time management, commercial awareness, health and safety (including risk assessments and method statements) and quality standards.
K.K21: How to commission soil surveys, testing and analysis.
K.K22: Opportunities for introducing innovative techniques across projects or the wider business.
K.K23: Critical awareness of stakeholder engagement, mapping, drivers, engagement opportunities and building collaborative relationships.
K.K24: Approaches to soil science research questions and the methodologies and techniques required to deliver valid and reliable results.
K.K25: Methods used to review, analyse and draw conclusions from data (including how to apply statistically valid comparisons and understand the reliability of data) strategies to present conclusions to clients and other audiences and influence stakeholders.

S.S1: Interpret the relationships between soil and landscape, land use and climate.
S.S2: Initiate, evaluate, and select the most effective soil sampling and survey technique to meet the project objectives and landscape context.
S.S3: Critique Agricultural Land Classification reports.
S.S4: Select and apply relevant and effective data collection and interpretation, including before and after analysis, for the required purpose including Earth Observation (EO) data, Geographic Information System Mapping (GIS) and soil maps.
S.S5: Based on the client brief, develop project scope and identify outcomes, deliverables and key performance indicators, including sustainability.
S.S6: Select and justify cost effective soil testing methods that achieve project outcomes and ensure accuracy.
S.S7: Consider the interrelationship between the soil science project, the wider geo-political environment and current regulations and guidelines.
S.S8: Design and implement soil experiments, resource and research plans, analysis methodologies, and survey approaches including land access and statistical analysis.
S.S9: Undertake digital modelling of soil and use soil data to strengthen the applicability of models.
S.S10: Analyse and handle confidential information securely.
S.S11: Manage the delivery of the project plans including budget, tenders for services, obtaining relevant permits, timelines and address any risks and concerns.
S.S12: Develop, maintain and monitor compliance with health and safety requirements including Risk Assessment and Method Statements (RAMS).
S.S13: Take a systematic approach to identify interacting factors of soil investigations and the key stakeholders.
S.S14: Analyse soil information to draw robust conclusions and identify the limitations of the results and the context within which they apply.
S.S15: Produce verbal and written specialist technical reports, following standard policies and procedures, based on the evidence collected.
S.S16: Communicate soil information to meet the needs of various stakeholders.
S.S17: Apply a systematic approach to solving problems that involve interacting factors and provide sustainable solutions.
S.S18: Present project outcomes and justify the fit with objectives.
S.S19: Identify opportunities to implement best practice and innovative techniques and promote their application into the project or across the organisation.

B.B1: Work proactively and collaboratively with team members and stakeholders from all backgrounds.
B.B2: Act professionally, demonstrating integrity, empathy and working ethically.
B.B3: Committed to ethical reporting and when making recommendations, including trade-offs.
B.B4: Committed to using innovative approaches and new practices.
B.B5: Works within limits of responsibility and internal policies and procedures.
B.B6: Respect confidentiality on work related and personal matters.
B.B7: Be adaptable, reliable and objective.
B.B8: Take responsibility for personal development, demonstrating commitment to learning, self-improvement and to continual development of technical skills.

ST1368.name: Chartered legal executive litigator and advocate

K.K1: law and legal practice and procedures of specialist area of practice as set out in CILEx Regulation Chartered Legal Executive Handbooks.
K.K2: law and legal practice and procedures of advocacy for specialist area of practice, including the law of evidence as set out in CILEx Regulation Practice Rights Handbooks.
K.K3: approaches to research and information gathering including identification and selection of trusted sources and documentation.
K.K4: approaches to gathering, assessing and testing evidence to identify relevant issues.
K.K5: approaches to gathering, assessing and testing relevant factual, legal, procedural and evidential issues including identification and selection of trusted sources and documentation.
K.K6: approaches to problem solving, analytical techniques and synthesis, including the use of digital tools.
K.K7: approaches to assessment of strengths and weaknesses of a case.
K.K8: approaches to drafting a case theory and skeleton argument.
K.K9: the fundamental duties to the court, the administration of justice, to other advocates and appropriate ethics, etiquette and conventions of advocacy.
K.K10: Making an appropriate decision to appear and represent the client at court, including when it is inappropriate to act/cease to act for a client.
K.K11: Deal appropriately with client care and ethical issues, including good practice guidance when dealing with vulnerable witnesses and the available procedures relating to vulnerable witnesses.
K.K12: communication skills and techniques used by an advocate.
K.K13: preparation for advocacy and effective ways to undertake this.
K.K14: purpose, techniques, and tactics of all aspects of court advocacy, including opening speeches, examination in-chief, cross-examination, re-examination, and closing speeches to adduce, rebut and clarify evidence.
K.K15: approaches to drafting techniques.
K.K16: the principles of customer service including the impact of diversity and inclusion, be able to identify user need and manage customer expectations.
K.K17: the principles of ethical and professional frameworks and their relevance for legal service delivery including the purpose of regulatory arrangements for personal compliance.
K.K18: the ethical issues and obligations applying to the provision of legal services, including legal rules and regulations, organisational policies and the CILEx Regulation Code of Conduct.
K.K19: the principles of legal and regulatory liabilities with regards to clients and business to ensure compliance with all professional obligations, for example, the provision of ethical legal advice in the best interests of the customer and Anti-Money Laundering obligations.
K.K20: best practice in relation to emotional competence when dealing with customers from different backgrounds, including vulnerable clients and the principles of unconscious bias and its effects.
K.K21: approaches to formal and informal conflict resolution, advocacy, including the requirements for rights of audience for reserved areas of practice, and negotiation. Including, planning, preparation, strategy development, client expectations and risks to the client whilst behaving ethically and resisting pressure to condone, ignore or act unethically.
K.K22: approaches to communication, including use of clear language and the most appropriate media to communicate with different stakeholders and the principles of stakeholder management.
K.K23: emotional competence in communications with others, including respect for Equality Diversity Inclusion and vulnerability.
K.K24: approaches to building effective working relationships with third parties.
K.K25: financial methods and drivers operating within legal organisations including the purpose and principles of filing and recording systems and procedures. The principles of administering client accounts including the principles of time-recording and charging fee structures.
K.K26: principles of risk assessment, risk management and approaches to selecting the best actions for progressing matters.
K.K27: approaches to prioritising workloads, caseloads and time management, to ensure matters progress without delay and files are managed in accordance with office practices.
K.K28: approaches to digital technology, tools and software in the legal sector such as case management systems and drafting software for tasks including quality assurance of caseload outputs and storage, retrieval and analysis of data and information.
K.K29: legal requirements and ethical challenges relating to the use of legal technology in the legal sector, including risks to confidentiality of data posed by and to client’s actions, vulnerabilities to cyber threats and defensive measures.
K.K30: own organisation’s position in relation to different business models operating in the legal services sector, including an awareness of impact of commercial practices such as networking and marketing on organisations, developments in the legal sector and the wider economic and political environment and the principles of continuous improvement within organisations.
K.K31: commercial drivers of organisations including time recording and billing processes for legal services.
K.K32: approaches to stakeholder identification and stakeholder management including the significance of external and internal stakeholders to organisations.
K.K33: strategies for professional development to ensure ongoing competence and good practice, including identification of needs, reflection, evaluation, emotional competence, and recognition of emotions and vulnerabilities in others.
K.K34: fundamentals of teamworking, coaching and mentoring.
K.K35: how the activities of a Chartered Legal Executive litigator and advocate may contribute to their ‘professional carbon footprint’ and steps that can be taken to reduce it.

S.S1: establish the clients' existing position, needs and expectations and identify needs that they may not recognise, utilising specialist expertise as required.
S.S2: undertake independent research in the areas of specialism using standard legal information sources.
S.S3: conduct and collate legal and factual research around all aspects of the matter, including case law, legal procedure, legal changes and documentation in a thorough, pragmatic and organised manner, using the full range of available tools and digital technologies, including assessment of quality of research sources and apply it to the legal matter in hand, to produce research notes.
S.S4: identify the applicable law and procedures for legal matters.
S.S5: test and challenge information and data, establish evidence and evaluate its validity.
S.S6: analyse, synthesise, and evaluate information in different ways to determine how to act on client’s instructions and identify any real or potential conflicts of interest.
S.S7: undertake the analysis of factual, legal, procedural, and evidential information.
S.S8: integrate information and materials from a variety of different sources and identify appropriately the relevant chronology and issues.
S.S9: interpret results, present findings accurately and apply these to the legal matter using supporting evidence.
S.S10: summarise the strengths and weaknesses of each party’s case.
S.S11: provide clear and unambiguous legal advice to clients and service users, including advice on the available options, the risks, the costs and the benefits of alternative courses of action, next steps and providing supporting evidence.
S.S12: make critical judgments of the merits of particular arguments.
S.S13: present and make a reasoned choice between alternative solutions.
S.S14: develop an effective case presentation strategy.
S.S15: prepare a case theory that is both succinct and persuasive and that observes the rules of professional conduct.
S.S16: prepare a coherent submission to the court based upon relevant facts, general principles, and legal authorities in a structured, concise and persuasive manner in a practical setting in relation to an interim application.
S.S17: undertake an opening speech, examination-in-chief, cross-examination, re-examination, and a closing speech.
S.S18: deal effectively with uncooperative witnesses.
S.S19: call expert evidence when appropriate and use and challenge expert evidence effectively.
S.S20: deal with the court’s questions and concerns promptly.
S.S21: respond to an opponent’s points.
S.S22: prepare the necessary documentation on behalf of clients, draft and present legal documentation in line with professional standards including documents with bespoke content.
S.S23: select most appropriate method of communication and use accurate and suitable language in communication, both written and oral, to communicate to clients and stakeholders in a clear and impactful way.
S.S24: take accurate instructions relating to legal matters from colleagues and clients through use of interviewing skills, including effective listening.
S.S25: identify where information is missing or in the hands of third parties and take steps to obtain it.
S.S26: communicate clear, accurate and practical advice to the client, including where objectives may be unrealistic and respond appropriately to manage clients' legal needs and engage others in the team and externally with the necessary legal expertise.
S.S27: represent and advise clients through negotiation and advocacy.
S.S28: mitigate against conflict by managing underlying issues and mediate and manage conflict when it arises.
S.S29: negotiate outcomes on behalf of stakeholders to deliver the best possible outcome, taking steps to ensure agreement or compromise is reached which is clear, lawful and legally binding.
S.S30: build an internal and external network of contacts.
S.S31: take a customer-focused approach and build strong relationships with customer and third-party contacts at all levels.
S.S32: deliver legal services in line with customer service level agreements and commitments.
S.S33: identify steps to achieve customer's wishes or further their objectives and plan and implement progress steps.
S.S34: undertake ongoing communications with the customer to keep them informed and identify and communicate emerging risks.
S.S35: assist others in combining commercial and legal expertise to bring value to customers and the organisation.
S.S36: maintain filing and recording systems in compliance with organisational procedures.
S.S37: plan caseload and wider workload to meet deadlines and deliver outcomes for the client and the organisation.
S.S38: prioritise, plan and review progress and reschedule activities in line with changing priorities to meet changing client and business demands, in accordance with procedural requirements and legal time limits.
S.S39: contribute to the financial management of cases, including budgets, work in progress, timely billing and collection and variance recognition.
S.S40: meet quality assurance expectations within legal service provision.
S.S41: work collaboratively towards achieving customer and organisational objectives.
S.S42: manage own resource requirements, caseload and workflow, to objectives and budgets.
S.S43: support and advise colleagues on routine legal matters.
S.S44: contribute to a culture of continuous improvement including feedback mechanisms.
S.S45: using reflection and evaluation of own competence, recognise when the limit of technical competence and skills is reached and ask for support.
S.S46: agree and achieve own performance objectives and key performance indicators for personal delivery.
S.S47: apply the CILEx Regulation code of conduct and related professional and ethical frameworks, including, representing information and data honestly, accurately, completely and confidentially whilst maintaining its security, contributing to a culture of data and cyber security ensuring that digital technology is used safely and securely and upholding ethical risk management and professional standards and escalating issues as required.

B.B1: Ethical and Professional - Acts with integrity, impartiality and independence, demonstrates principled behaviour, follows rules, adheres to organisational and professional standards and manages risk. Knows limitations of abilities, refers technical work to others with the right expertise, admits mistakes and resists pressure to condone, ignore or act unethically.
B.B2: Analytical - Collects and understands information, analyses and challenges it, identifies issues, draws appropriate conclusions to solve problems and makes robust recommendations and decisions.
B.B3: Advocate - Takes instructions, holds appropriate advocacy rights, prepares for and acts for clients at trials and hearings and represents the interests of clients in the best way.
B.B4: Collaborative - Builds positive and trusted working relationships, respects the views and opinions of others, shares knowledge, deals with issues as they arise, works across boundaries, and makes contributions to the team.
B.B5: Accountable - takes responsibility for their actions and outcomes from their work.
B.B6: Innovative - seeks innovative solutions to problems and challenges.

K.K1: Employer or company values, beliefs and purpose
K.K2: Patient referral processes and procedures and the implications of not following procedures
K.K3: Policies, procedures, and regulated activities within the Optical working environment, such as GDPR, NHS, GOC health and safety and safeguarding
K.K4: Importance of personal presentation, time management and teamwork
K.K5: Different customer types and needs within the optical environment
K.K6: Communication principles and techniques to use to communicate at work to reduce barriers of communication
K.K7: Wider services that are available to support customers with specific needs such as non-English speaking, non-verbal, profoundly deaf, learning needs
K.K8: Health and safety at work legislation relevant to working in the optical environment, including hygiene and infection control measures and customer safety
K.K9: How to use and maintain optical equipment
K.K10: First aid procedures, accident and incident reporting and evacuation processes
K.K11: The services available to customers in the optical environment such as sight tests, contact lens and extended services including minor eye care services, low vision services and their benefits or limitations
K.K12: The non-prescribed products available to the customer within the optical environment such as contact lens solutions, common ophthalmic drops, ready readers, magnifiers, and other accessories
K.K13: Pre-appointment processes, record keeping (e.g. adhering to relevant legislation when recording and storing personal data) and the principles of gaining consent, including when and how information can be disclosed to a customer or service provider
K.K14: Appointment types, lengths, booking systems and clinic management in the optical environment including NHS exemptions and private appointments
K.K15: Business sight test and contact lens recall requirements
K.K16: Ocular emergencies and when to seek advice, including escalation and reporting procedures
K.K17: Clinic preparation processes such as preparing records, General Ophthalmic Services eligibility and entitlement, identifying customer needs, confirming appointment
K.K18: Product tolerances such as British standards, ISO, UKCA
K.K19: How to use lens measuring equipment
K.K20: How to recognise engravings such as progressive lenses safety spectacles and remark of lens
K.K21: The structure of the eye, spectacle prescriptions and vision correction options
K.K22: Lens form, types, materials, coatings and their features and benefits
K.K23: Specialised vision correction options such as safety spectacles, sports spectacles, contact lenses, magnifiers and their features and benefits
K.K24: Frame materials, shapes, components and their features and benefits.
K.K25: Frame fitting and suitability including facial, frame and lens measurements and the equipment used to measure these
K.K26: Pricing, promotions and offers, ordering and payment systems and collection options and procedures
K.K27: When to use visual acuity to check near vision and prescription adaption requirements
K.K28: Frame adjustments and the impact of poor fitting on both comfort and vision
K.K29: Adjustment tools and equipment used for spectacles
K.K30: Advice and guidance on frame fitting /and or lens care and after sales services for customers
K.K31: Guarantees, warranties available within an optical environment and the legal requirements for consumers
K.K32: Employer concern handling policies and procedures and when to escalate complaints such as to the NHS and/or the Optical Consumer Complaints Service (OCCS)
K.K33: Employer and manufacturer's remake and repair procedures for spectacles
K.K34: Implications of poor fitting on the customer
K.K35: Implications and impact of incorrect measurements, prescriptions and product recommendations for the customer and business
K.K36: Implications and impact of customer concerns and or complaints on the business brand and professional members of staff
K.K37: The principles of continuing professional development and the local arrangements for appraisal in the workplace, such as self- reflection, feedback, career opportunities and target setting
K.K38: Business operating models, targets, and key performance indicators in an optical environment
K.K39: Understand a customer's ability to make decisions on the products they are purchasing (Mental Capacity Act and Best Interest decisions and power of attorney)
K.K40: Understand ocular conditions such as glaucoma, cataracts, macular degeneration, diabetes
K.K41: The equipment used and purpose of different screening tests available within the optical environment, such as auto Refractor, Non-Contact Tonometer, Optical Coherence Tomographer, Visual Fields Screening, Fundus Camera
K.K42: How to set up and use screening equipment such as pressure tests, visual field tests
K.K43: Conduct screening test with customer and pass results to optometrist/dispensing optician, and  know who to refer to for support or guidance during screening checks
K.K44: Medical conditions which are screened for, such as glaucoma, cataracts, macular degeneration, diabetes
K.K45: Contact lens handling, insertion, and removal procedures
K.K46: Advice and guidance given to customers such as cleaning regime and solution types including wearing schedules and lens care
K.K47: What to do in an emergency (out of hours)
K.K48: How to independently plan and manage own workload and assess and mitigate risk
K.K49: How to manage own wellbeing and resources
K.K50: The importance of team communications when working remotely
K.K51: Environments they will work in,  including patient/ customer types

S.S1: Deliver customer service  which aligns with company values, beliefs and purposes
S.S2: Identify when and who to refer to when supervision is required such as ocular emergencies, screening, dispensing and collections of restricted categories
S.S3: Work within the limits of policies, procedures, and regulated activity such as GDPR, NHS, GOC, Health and Safety at Work and safeguarding
S.S4: Follow employer’s guidelines and expectations for presentation and team working
S.S5: Identify and meet customer needs within the optical environment
S.S6: Communicate with customers and the optical team to maximise understanding
S.S7: Refer customers needing communication support such as language, hearing, visual or learning difficulties to appropriate services
S.S8: Follow health and safety legislation in the optical environment including customer safety, hygiene, and infection control
S.S9: Follow safe use instructions when using optical equipment such as adjustment tools, screening equipment and measuring equipment
S.S10: Follow procedures and processes for first aid, accident and incident reporting or evacuations
S.S11: Provide the benefits and limitations of the different services (e.g. sight tests, contact lenses) and extended services (e.g. minor eye condition services, low vision services) to the customers within the optical environment
S.S12: Offer non-prescribed products such as contact lens solutions, common ophthalmic drops, ready readers, magnifiers, and other accessories to customers
S.S13: Select appointments, recall information, appointment types and exemptions on the employer’s system, and maintain accurate records, e.g. customer details
S.S14: Report and record ocular emergencies following correct employer procedures
S.S15: Accurately prepare clinical records for use following employer procedures
S.S16: Accurately complete customer pre-appointment procedures
S.S17: Use product tolerances to validate product accuracy
S.S18: Use lens measuring equipment to identify prescriptions and lens measurements
S.S19: Identify lens types using engravings such as progressive lenses and safety spectacles and remark lenses where required
S.S20: Interpret the spectacle prescription to identify a range of vision correction options suitable for the customer
S.S21: Offer suitable lens products to the customer based on customer needs and preferences, using features and benefits to highlight suitability
S.S22: Offer specialised products to the customer based on their needs and preferences, using features and benefits to highlight suitability
S.S23: Offer suitable frames to the customer based on their needs and preferences, using features and benefits to highlight suitability
S.S24: Identify frame fitting suitability including facial, frame and spectacle lens measurements required to process spectacle orders for the customer
S.S25: Process spectacle orders, payments and arrange collections, offering relevant promotions to the customer where appropriate
S.S26: Identify and check visual acuity for near vision spectacle, explaining adaption as required
S.S27: Fit spectacle frames through the use of questioning, measurements, and tools
S.S28: Use tools correctly to adjust frame without causing damage
S.S29: Provide advice and guidance on frame fitting, lens care and after sales service
S.S30: Identify guarantees and warranties of optical products and adhere to the legal requirements for consumers
S.S31: Manage customer concerns and or complaints in line with employer procedures
S.S32: Use problem solving techniques to identify concern and or complaint causes
S.S33: Communicate with customers to resolve concerns or complaints within the limits of their own authority
S.S34: Follow remake procedures
S.S35: Follow repair procedures
S.S36: Recognise and resolve customer complaints e.g. poor fitting, incorrect measurements, or offer alternative options in line with business requirements
S.S37: Participate in training and development activities to maintain own practice
S.S38: Participate in appraisal, obtain feedback, and use self-reflection to plan further development opportunities and identify available career opportunities to support progression
S.S39: Adapt approach for customers with ocular conditions such as glaucoma, cataracts, macular degeneration, diabetes
S.S40: Conduct screening using screening equipment and record accurate results
S.S41: Inform the patient about the equipment, processes and procedures used for screening
S.S42: Gain support or guidance from colleagues during screening when required
S.S43: Inform the patient that the tests check for medical conditions such as glaucoma, cataracts, macular degeneration, diabetes
S.S44: Demonstrate contact lens handling and insertion and removal to a patient
S.S45: Show the patient how to care for their lens
S.S46: Provide advice and guidance on cleaning of lenses and solution types
S.S47: Follow out of hours emergency procedures
S.S48: Recognise a customer’s ability to make an informed decision
S.S49: Tell the customer what the sight test includes and how it is carried out
S.S50: Work independently to plan and manage workload
S.S51: Assess and mitigate risk in patient’s home to ensure an appropriate eye test can be delivered
S.S52: Mange own wellbeing and resources
S.S53: Maintain team communications when working remotely

B.B1: Treat people with dignity and respect.
B.B2: Show discretion and empathy for those you work with
B.B3: Be adaptable, reliable and committed
B.B4: Be caring and compassionate
B.B5: Show resilience and self-awareness
B.B6: Show openness and integrity at all times

K.K1: The component parts of the railway system including different railway organisations, industry bodies and business systems such as planning, performance management and asset management.
K.K2: Events that have shaped the evolution of the railway in Great Britain and how these have influenced change in the industry, including significant train accidents, significant timetable and network disruptions, the role of the unions and modernisation programmes.
K.K3: The relevant safety legislation and procedures, where applicable, to an operating railway including risk assessments, safety management systems, and safety culture.
K.K4: The operating procedures required to support the operation of a railway system, and manage planned, unplanned, and emergency situations that can occur on the network.
K.K5: The operational planning and timetabling principles and processes that underpin an effective train plan, resource plan, and engineering works programme.
K.K6: Awareness of environment and sustainability regulations, relevance to the occupation and the operator's responsibilities.
K.K7: Principles and policies of equality, diversion and inclusion in the workplace.
K.K8: The requirements for testing operational equipment, operational work locations and systems including pre-work and in-work testing.
K.K9: Handover requirements at the beginning, during and end of a shift.
K.K10: The roles and responsibilities of different colleagues and stakeholders that combine to provide an effective network.
K.K11: Limits of authority, when to escalate tasks and issues, and to whom.
K.K12: Common complaints, their causes and mitigators, for passengers and/or freight and other users of the network, such as staffing issues, onboard train incidents, passenger incidents at stations.
K.K13: Communication techniques: verbal and written, including specific protocols for communicating safety critical information.
K.K14: Their organisations commercial or operational priorities.
K.K15: The type of incidents and events which can occur on a network, the impact they have on passengers, freight, staff, and the associated safety mitigation and recovery procedures.
K.K16: Different types of investigations that apply to train running incidents and infrastructure related events and staff accidents, and the associated reporting procedures.
K.K17: The internal and external stakeholder roles and responsibilities of those involved in the different stages of an incident including response, recovery and lessons learnt.
K.K18: Strategies for collaborative working to manage conflicting aims and objectives, resolve conflict and reach effective consensus.
K.K19: Principles of risk-based decision making to support safe network operation including knowledge of different sources of information that inform those decisions, such as drawings, plans, schedules, safe systems of work, permits, relevant stakeholders and experts.
K.K20: The principles of personal responsibility in a safety critical work environment.
K.K21: When and how to challenge others to ensure compliance with safety regulations and procedures.
K.K22: Factors that affect decision making when working under pressure in planned, unplanned and emergency situations
K.K23: Strategies for effective decision making, such as informed prioritisation, compliance with procedures, and training.

S.S1: Apply risk-based safety principles to ways of working and decision-making.
S.S2: Operate and manage train control and information systems to support the operation of a railway system.
S.S3: Apply statutory and company environmental safe working practices during planned, unplanned and emergency situations.
S.S4: Operate and manage the train service in accordance with the train plan. Respond and adapt when disruptions occur to the planned service.
S.S5: Identify, report and record information, using correct terms, standards, templates, and protocols.
S.S6: Produces and manages a train plan, resource plan or engineering works programme.
S.S7: Maintain safety and security of own work environment.
S.S8: Identify and manage personal mental and physical fitness for duty, and role model mental and physical fitness for duty for others in the context of maintaining safety.
S.S9: Negotiate with colleagues or stakeholders, for example to access equipment or arrange system outage.
S.S10: Respond to and resolve common complaints.
S.S11: Communicate with others, for example internal and external stakeholders, colleagues, and managers.
S.S12: Identify, carry out and record role and industry related CPD activities to improve own practice.
S.S13: Respond to and act upon information received regarding incidents and delays to manage the disruption to train services and return them to the scheduled timetable.
S.S14: Implement initial response and incident management plans to match the scale of the situation and minimise the impact to the wider network, stakeholders and experts.
S.S15: Initiate and facilitate investigations into incidents and events. Adhere to reporting procedures for different types of investigations including unplanned and emergency incidents or events.
S.S16: Work collaboratively with internal and external parties as appropriate to resolve problems and deliver an efficient train service.
S.S17: Explore and gather information from a range of sources needed to support operating activities and decisions.
S.S18: Takes responsibility for the safety of themselves and others in a safety critical work environment.
S.S19: Challenge others and escalate safety issues to comply with safety regulations and procedures.
S.S20: Prioritise workload, for example balancing incident and event recovery with maintaining other services.

B.B1: Puts safety first for themselves and others.
B.B2: Takes responsibility for the quality of work and enables others to work to high standards.
B.B3: Supports an inclusive workplace, for example respectful of different views.
B.B4: Embeds their organisations commercial, operational, ethical, and safety priorities into their own practice over time.
B.B5: Willingness and ability to learn.
B.B6: Adapt to, and resilient in, challenging or changing situations.

K.K1: The aetiology, pathogenesis and epidemiological trends of oral and dental disease and their application to patient management.
K.K2: Clinical presentations of oral and dental diseases relevant to that of a dental hygienist and the principles underpinning their diagnosis, prevention and treatment.
K.K3: Variance in disease presentation across diverse cultural and social groups, and those with protected characteristics, and how this impacts diagnosis, prevention and treatment.
K.K4: General and systemic diseases and psychological conditions, and their relevance to oral health and impact on clinical treatment, patient compliance, self-care and outcomes.
K.K5: Relevant and appropriate dental, oral, craniofacial and general anatomy including the diversity of anatomy across the patient population and their relevance to patient management.
K.K6: Relevant physiology and its application to patient management.
K.K7: Psychological and sociological concepts and theoretical frameworks of health, illness, behavioural change and disease, and how these can be applied in clinical practice.
K.K8: Potential routes of transmission of infectious agents in dental practice, mechanisms for the prevention of infection, the scientific principles of decontamination and disinfection and their relevance to health and safety.
K.K9: The need for effective recorded maintenance and testing of equipment and requirements for appropriate storage, handling and use of materials.
K.K10: The properties of relevant medicines and therapeutic agents and their application to patient management.
K.K11: Medicines that may cause adverse effects in patients and the appropriate actions to manage and report.
K.K12: Commonly used complementary and alternative therapies that may impact on patient management.
K.K13: The health risks of prescribed, non-prescribed and recreational drug use and misuse on oral and general health and how to provide appropriate advice and support including signposting or referral.
K.K14: The scientific principles underpinning the use of materials and biomaterials used in dentistry, their limitations and selection.
K.K15: The scientific principles of medical ionizing radiation and statutory regulations, and their application to clinical practice.
K.K16: The principles of obtaining valid patient consent.
K.K17: The importance of each component of the patient assessment process.
K.K18: The meaning of a prognostic indicator and application of prognostic indicators to a range of clinical situations.
K.K19: The risks and benefits of treatment under general anaesthesia including patient selection criteria and making referrals.
K.K20: The risks and benefits of treatment under conscious sedation including patient selection criteria and making referrals.
K.K21: The components of patient management including risks, benefits, contra-indications and indications.
K.K22: The risks, benefits, complications of and contra-indications to interventions, non-surgical and surgical.
K.K23: The signs of abuse, neglect or emotional trauma, local and national systems that safeguard welfare and how to raise concerns and act accordingly.
K.K24: The principles of preventive care and how to apply as part of a comprehensive personalised treatment plan.
K.K25: The importance of achieving a healthy oral environment prior to restoration and, or replacement of teeth.
K.K26: The influence of diet and nutritional status on oral and general health and how to provide appropriate advice and support.
K.K27: The need for adjunctive chemotherapeutic agents for the management of periodontal conditions in individual patients.
K.K28: The aetiology and pathogenesis of diseases of the oral and maxillofacial complex.
K.K29: The importance of early referral for individuals identified as having potential malignancy and tumours.
K.K30: The significant of normal and abnormal facial growth, physical, mental and dental development milestones.
K.K31: The roles and organisation of various referral networks, clinical guidelines and policies and local variation.
K.K32: The need to take responsibility for establishing personal networks with local dental and medical colleagues.
K.K33: The responsibilities of the dental team as an access point to and from wider healthcare.
K.K34: The impact of medical and psychological conditions, cultural identity and values in a range of patients and how this can influence patient compliance, self-care and outcomes.
K.K35: The risks around the clinical environment.
K.K36: The role of surgical management of periodontal disease.
K.K37: The conditions or complications that may arise following dental implant therapy.
K.K38: Communication methods and technologies and their appropriate application in support of clinical practice.
K.K39: The importance of non-verbal communication, including listening skills, and the barriers to effective communication.
K.K40: Professional expectations, potential impact, and consequence of using social media as a communication tool.
K.K41: Responsibilities and limitations of referring to other members of the dental team.
K.K42: The role and professional responsibilities associated with appraisal, training and review of colleagues, provision of and receipt of effective feedback in the context of developing members of the dental team.
K.K43: The roles of dental and other healthcare professionals in the context of learning and working in a dental and wider healthcare team.
K.K44: The contribution that team members and effective team working makes to the delivery of safe and effective high-quality care, including the benefits of working in culturally diverse teams.
K.K45: The team working guidance provided by the GDC and other relevant bodies.
K.K46: The impact of Direct Access on own and others’ scope of practice.
K.K47: The scope of practice of each member of the dental team and how the roles interact for effective teamwork and patient care.
K.K48: The need to ensure that those who raise concerns are protected from discrimination or other detrimental effects.
K.K49: The differences between management and leadership.
K.K50: Own management and leadership role and the range of skills and knowledge required to do this effectively.
K.K51: How to take responsibility for the quality of services and devices provided to the patient as relevant to your scope of practice.
K.K52: The importance of contemporaneous, complete and accurate patient records in accordance with legal requirements and best practice.
K.K53: The legal responsibilities of maintaining and protecting patients’ information.
K.K54: GDC’s expectations and requirements as set out in regulations and guidance and other relevant laws, ethical guidance and systems in addition to the legal frameworks.
K.K55: The importance of having appropriate indemnity arrangements in place for both the professional and patient.
K.K56: The importance of candour and effective communication with patients when things go wrong or when dealing with a complaint.
K.K57: How and where to report any patient safety issues which arise.
K.K58: The attributes of professional attitudes and behaviour in all environments and media, including interaction with social media.
K.K59: The responsibilities of, and mechanisms for raising concerns about your own or others’ health, behaviour or professional performance as described in GDC guidance.
K.K60: The principles and procedures for good complaints handling.
K.K61: The responsibility that dental practices and individual practitioners have in compliance with legal and regulatory frameworks.
K.K62: The meaning of cultural competence and its relevance in assessing the needs and planning care for patients from diverse backgrounds.
K.K63: Diversity, equality, inclusion and discrimination and the underpinning legislation, and how to apply these principles to manage patients with protected characteristics and work within the dental team noting that this legislation may differ in England, Scotland, Wales and Northern Ireland.
K.K64: The basic principles of a population health approach including demographic and social trends, UK and international oral health trends, determinants of health and inequalities in health, and the ways in which these are measured and current patterns.
K.K65: The dental and wider healthcare systems dental professionals work within including local and national health policy and organisation, delivery of healthcare and equity.
K.K66: The principles of planning oral health care for communities to meet needs and demands.
K.K67: The principles and limitations of the currently available options for funding of dental healthcare provision for individual patients.
K.K68: The ethical challenges associated with providing patient care within the current dental healthcare systems.
K.K69: Considerations for the management of resources in provision of care decisions including appropriate use of primary and secondary care networks.
K.K70: The importance of collaboration across the health and social care sector for the benefit of communities and individual patients.
K.K71: The barriers and challenges which prevent sections of the population accessing oral healthcare, including patients from marginalised populations and patients with protected characteristics.
K.K72: The main principles relating to sustainable oral health care, and the challenges and barriers to implementing a sustainable approach.
K.K73: The meaning of insight in the context of professional practice.
K.K74: The importance of insight in ensuring safe and effective patient care, and to personal development.
K.K75: The principles of an evidence-based approach.
K.K76: Models for self-reflection and how this process can be used to inform personal development, viewpoint, preconceptions, bias and behaviour.
K.K77: The importance of assessment, feedback, critical reflection, identification of learning needs and appraisal in personal development planning.
K.K78: The importance and requirement for commitment to lifelong learning.
K.K79: The principles of personal development planning, recording of evidence, and reflective practice.
