K.K20: The impact of networks and how they enhance and support the mental and physical needs of people, families and communities, and any potential deficiencies in support.
K.K21: The role and application of genomics and epigenetics and how to inform and advise people about the implications for personalised health care.
K.K22: The range of advanced communication skills needed to develop public health information that is accessible and enables people to make informed decisions about their health and wellbeing.
K.K23: How to share information regarding communicable diseases and the approaches necessary for communicable disease surveillance, infection prevention and control, including immunisation and vaccination programmes.
K.K24: How to mitigate risks of environmental factors and other pollutants that have the potential to affect the health and wellbeing of people now and in the future.
K.K25: Person-centred approaches to care, including working in partnership to support shared decision making within the assessment and care planning processes when working with people, their families or carers, and communities.
K.K26: Advanced communication strategies and relationship management skills when interacting with people, including families and carers, who may have a range of mental, physical, cognitive, behavioural and social health challenges.
K.K27: The principle of the presumption of capacity, and the requirement to seek informed consent throughout the assessment and planning process.
K.K28: How to make reasonable adjustments to maximise opportunities for people to understand the outcome of their abilities and needs assessment, and the implications for their treatment and care.
K.K29: How to make professional judgements, within the required legislative framework, about a person’s lack of capacity to make a decision or give consent at that time.
K.K30: The required level of support needed to ensure that vulnerable people receive the maximum levels of independence throughout the continuum of care.
K.K31: How to escalate, report, plan and coordinate immediate and continuing care for people in need of safeguarding.
K.K32: How to proactively obtain and distil information from formal and informal sources to inform individual assessments, involving others as required.
K.K33: How to critically analyse complex assessment information and data, distinguishing between normal and abnormal findings, recognising when prompt action is required, including requesting additional investigations, and involving others when appropriate.
K.K34: New and emerging science and technology, including genomics, and how it can be used to inform assessment and treatment options, when agreeing personalised care plans with people and their families, carers or nominated persons.
K.K35: Problem solving, influencing and negotiation skills needed to maximise opportunities for shared decision making when co-producing care plans.
K.K36: How to assess individual abilities and needs when co-producing plans of care, and agree opportunities for supported self-care and treatment interventions.
K.K37: The impact and influence of people’s preferences, close relationships, support systems, home environment, social, environmental, and spiritual factors when agreeing the plan of care.
K.K38: The opportunities for people, and where needed their families, carers or nominated person, to remain independent and to facilitate self-care.
K.K39: How to effectively communicate the benefits and risks of different care and treatment options, and how the person and their family or carers will be supported in the choices they make.
K.K40: The impact that unexpected events and changes may have on the plan of care.
K.K41: The management and evaluation of complex episodes of care from referral to service and admission, to discharge from caseload, or referral to other appropriate services or agencies.
K.K42: How to assess and manage transition of people to other services or agencies, and collaborate with colleagues of other disciplines and agencies to find solutions to mitigate any risks.
K.K43: The implications of delegation of any aspect of the individual’s care to an alternative person.
K.K44: What factors may lead to reduced concordance, changes in motivation or dissatisfaction with the care and treatment plan.
K.K45: How to proactively engage with, and effectively advocate for, people using services provided by other professionals or agencies to identify and find solutions where there is inconsistency, disagreement or conflict.
K.K46: Evidence-based care and treatment, including care, therapeutic interventions and social prescribing, that may be supportive, curative, symptom relieving or palliative.
K.K47: Complex medicines administration, optimisation and medicines reconciliation, and how to continually evaluate to ensure optimum effectiveness.
K.K48: How to ensure adequate safeguards for people when they are vulnerable.
K.K49: How to maintain therapeutic relationships with people, their families and/or carers throughout the episode of care and treatment.
K.K50: Techniques to educate people, their families, carers or nominated persons about their condition, treatment and care, to promote independence and confidence in supported self-care and self-management.
K.K51: The importance of including people and their families or carers in making decisions about their care and how to mitigate any risks as a result of changes in a person’s mental and physical health, their living environment, or social arrangements.
K.K52: The preferences, beliefs, cultural requirements and wishes of the deceased and people who are bereaved.
K.K53: Why it’s important to clearly explain and accurately record the rationale for decisions, actions taken and resulting outcomes either in writing, or using digital technology, which can be shared with the person, their family, carers, nominated person and interdisciplinary and interagency teams.
K.K54: The role of leadership in applying human rights, equality, diversity and inclusion, to improve the health and wellbeing of people, families and communities.
K.K55: The importance of compassionate leadership.
K.K56: How to influence and improve the care of communities through partnership working.
K.K57: The range of available local community assets and how to engage them.
K.K58: Indicators used to determine the skill mix and appropriate characteristics of the workforce required to meet the needs of specific caseloads.
K.K59: How to effectively communicate complex information to justify decisions about resource allocation.
K.K60: How to delegate responsibility for the management of budget, people and other resources to team members, while retaining overall accountability.
K.K61: Personal workload requirements and that of the wider team and how to lead and prioritise activities in order to manage demand and capacity.
K.K62: How to safely and effectively delegate responsibilities to team members based on an assessment of their level of knowledge, skill and confidence.
K.K63: How digital technology can maximise the use of resources across interdisciplinary and interagency teams.
K.K64: Procurement policies, value for money considerations and health and safety requirements.
K.K65: The need to continually reflect on own leadership approach and adapt own leadership style to different situations, including but not limited to when working with diverse teams who may be geographically dispersed.
K.K66: How to effectively use systems to measure the impact, quality, productivity and cost efficacy of interdisciplinary and interagency teams to allow effective leadership and performance management.
K.K67: How and when to conduct conversations with team members to provide opportunities for positive reinforcement and challenge.
K.K68: The importance of developing a positive learning culture for interdisciplinary and interagency teams.
K.K69: The approaches and resources available to educate, support and motivate people, manage talent and succession plan.
K.K70: Leadership strategies that are effective in supporting positive team development and cohesion across disciplines and agencies.
K.K71: Strategies which are appropriate to the composition of the team, to enable supervision, reflection and peer review.
K.K72: The importance of understanding the individual’s abilities and learning needs when applying the standards of education and training for pre- and post-registration nursing, midwifery and nursing associate students, in order to educate, supervise and assess effectively.
K.K73: How health and safety legislation and regulations inform policy and guidance to support staff working across the range of home and community environments.
K.K74: The importance of evaluating the outputs and recommendations of internal and external risk reporting to enable prioritisation, decision making and the development of action plans to mitigate risk.
K.K75: The judgements required to balance competing risks and priorities.
K.K76: The use of innovative and emerging technology to collect and store data which allows analysis and forecasting to inform service improvement and safety plans.
K.K77: The importance of systematically and effectively capturing and evaluating people’s lived experiences of care to lead improvements in the quality of service delivery.
K.K78: Different research designs and methodologies used to generate evidence for service improvement.
K.K80: How service evaluation, research findings, data and audit can inform service improvement.
K.K81: How to present relevant research, quality and audit findings and proposals for care improvement to a range of audiences.
K.K82: How political and economic policies and drivers that may have an impact on the health, care and wellbeing of local communities.
K.K83: The economic principles that drive health and social care, and their impact on resource allocation in integrated primary and community care services.
K.K84: The impact and influence of epidemiological, demographic, social, political and economic trends on current and prospective community nursing services.
K.K85: How to build relationships between teams within different systems in health and care, appreciating the value of different approaches, skill sets and expertise.
K.K86: How different services within the system can collaborate and co-design to ensure that services work seamlessly together to meet the needs of people and communities.
K.K87: Methodologies that drive continuous service improvement within the variety of different organisations and agencies that deliver services.
K.K88: How the creation and development of effective system networks enhance communication and decision making across organisations and agencies.
K.K89: The importance of cultural competence and leadership when challenging discriminatory, oppressive cultures and behaviours at a system level.
K.K90: How to influence the health and social care strategies and policies at a local, regional and national level.
K.K91: How effective partnership working can promote and influence change and improve health outcomes for the people and communities served.

S.S1: Practise autonomously, proactively and innovatively, demonstrating self-awareness, emotional intelligence and openness.
S.S2: Lead and manage a service, with the ability to effectively admit, discharge and refer people to other professionals, services and agencies as appropriate.
S.S3: Deliver specialist person-centred care in complex, challenging and unpredictable circumstances.
S.S4: Account for their decisions, actions and omissions when working with complexity, risk, unpredictability and when all of the information required might not be available.
S.S5: Apply relevant legal, regulatory and governance requirements, policies, and professional and ethical frameworks, differentiating where appropriate between the devolved legislatures of the United Kingdom.
S.S6: Lead and promote care provision that is person-centred, anti-discriminatory, culturally competent and inclusive.
S.S7: Demonstrate critical awareness of stigma and the potential for bias, taking action to resolve any inequity arising from either, and educate others where necessary.
S.S8: Recognise the need for, and lead on action to provide, reasonable adjustments for people, groups and communities, influencing health policy and promoting best practice.
S.S9: Demonstrate the principles of courage, transparency and the professional duty of candour, taking responsibility to address poor practice wherever it is encountered.
S.S10: Critically reflect and recognise when own personal values and beliefs might impact on own behaviour and practice.
S.S11: Assess the opportunities, risk and demands of specialist community nursing practice, and take action to maintain own mental and physical health and wellbeing.
S.S12: Apply the numeracy, literacy, digital and technological skills required to deliver safe and effective specialist practice that meets the needs of people, their families and carers.
S.S13: Be an effective ambassador and role model, and a positive influence on the profession.
S.S14: Apply specialist knowledge of epidemiology, demography and the social determinants of health and illness, taking action to influence policy, service design and delivery.
S.S15: Critically analyse the factors that may lead to inequalities in health outcomes, and their associated ethical dilemmas, to plan care in partnership with people, families and communities to improve them.
S.S16: Recognise health as a fundamental human right and evaluate the effects of social influences, health literacy, individual circumstances, behaviours and choices on people’s current and future mental and physical health.
S.S17: Critically assess health needs in partnership with people, families, communities and populations, to support them to take decisions and actions that improve their own mental, physical, and behavioural health and wellbeing.
S.S18: Maximise opportunities for people, families, communities and populations to use their personal strengths and assets to make informed choices about their own health and wellbeing.
S.S19: Conduct, interpret and evaluate health and social care assessments, screening and profiling activity for people and communities, to take appropriate action to improve health outcomes.
S.S20: Apply specialist knowledge of social prescribing to support individual and community health outcomes.
S.S21: Critically analyse and assess the characteristics of communities, their assets and any areas for development in order to build networks and alliances that can enhance health outcomes for people and families.
S.S22: Promote and support people, communities and populations to connect effectively with local initiatives, support networks, programmes and third sector organisations that support their health and wellbeing.
S.S23: Utilise and evaluate the impact of networks to enhance and support the mental and physical needs of people, families and communities, and identify and address any deficiencies in support.
S.S24: Inform and advise people about the implications of genomics and epigenetics for personalised health care.
S.S25: Apply a range of advanced communication skills to develop public health information that is accessible and enables people to make informed decisions about their health and wellbeing.
S.S26: Share information regarding communicable diseases and approaches necessary for communicable disease surveillance, infection prevention and control, including immunisation and vaccination programmes.
S.S27: Mitigate risks of environmental factors and other pollutants that have the potential to affect the health and wellbeing of people now and in the future.
S.S28: Create and apply a person-centred approach to care, working in partnership to support shared decision making within the assessment and care planning process when working with people, their families or carers, and communities.
S.S29: Use advanced communication strategies and relationship management skills when interacting with people, including families and carers, who may have a range of mental, physical, cognitive, behavioural and social health challenges.
S.S30: Recognise and apply the principle of the presumption of capacity, and the requirement to seek informed consent throughout the assessment and planning process.
S.S31: Make reasonable adjustments to maximise opportunities for people to understand the outcome of their abilities and needs assessment, and the implications for their treatment and care.
S.S32: Make best interests decisions within the required legislative framework if, after seeking informed consent and making reasonable adjustments, their professional judgement is that a person lacks capacity to make a decision or give consent at that time.
S.S33: Assess and plan the care of people when they are vulnerable, agreeing on the required level of support needed to ensure maximum levels of independence throughout the continuum of care.
S.S34: Escalate, report, plan and coordinate immediate and continuing care for people in need of safeguarding.
S.S35: Proactively obtain and distil information from formal and informal sources to inform individual assessments, involving others as required.
S.S36: Critically analyse complex assessment information and data, distinguishing between normal and abnormal findings, recognising when prompt action is required, including requesting additional investigations, and involving others when appropriate.
S.S37: Critically apply clinical reasoning to decision making, taking into account differential diagnosis and the potential for diagnostic overshadowing.
S.S38: Maximise the potential use of technology and informatics to assist with assessment and diagnosis.
S.S39: Apply knowledge and understanding of new and emerging science and technology, including genomics, to inform assessment and treatment options, when agreeing personalised care plans with people and their families, carers or nominated persons.
S.S40: Apply a range of problem solving, influencing and negotiation skills to maximise opportunities for shared decision making when co-producing care plans.
S.S41: Assess individual abilities and needs when co-producing plans of care, agreeing opportunities for supported self-care and treatment interventions.
S.S42: Take into account the impact of people’s preferences, their close relationships and support systems, their home environment, and the influence of social, environmental and spiritual factors when agreeing the plan of care.
S.S43: Create and maximise opportunities for people, and where needed their families, carers or nominated person, to remain independent and to facilitate self-care.
S.S44: Effectively communicate the benefits and risks of different care and treatment options, explaining how the person and their family or carers will be supported in the choices they make.
S.S45: Anticipate and explain the impact that unexpected events and changes may have on the plan of care.
S.S46: Autonomously manage and evaluate complex episodes of care from referral to service and admission, to discharge from caseload, or referral to other appropriate services or agencies.
S.S47: Assess and manage transition of people to other services or agencies, proactively collaborating with colleagues of other disciplines and agencies to find solutions to mitigate any risks.
S.S48: Agree and negotiate with the person and where necessary their family, carer or nominated person, the implications of delegation of any aspect of their care to an alternative person.
S.S49: Recognise reduced concordance, changes in motivation or dissatisfaction with the care and treatment plan, and work in partnership with people to influence and negotiate any revisions to the plan.
S.S50: Proactively engage with, and effectively advocate for, people using services provided by other professionals or agencies to identify and find solutions where there is inconsistency, disagreement or conflict.
S.S51: Initiate a range of evidence-based care and treatment, including care, therapeutic interventions and social prescribing, that may be supportive, curative, symptom relieving or palliative.
S.S52: Safely and effectively manage complex medicines administration, optimisation and medicines reconciliation, and continually evaluate to ensure optimum effectiveness.
S.S53: Evaluate and adjust plans to ensure adequate safeguards for people when they are vulnerable.
S.S54: Maintain therapeutic relationships with people, their families and/or carers throughout the episode of care and treatment, and actively address any differing views.
S.S55: Apply a range of techniques to educate people, their families, carers or nominated persons about their condition, treatment and care, to promote independence and confidence in supported self-care and self-management.
S.S56: Work in partnership with people, their families, carers and other members of the team to continuously monitor and evaluate the care and treatment provided.
S.S57: Include people and their families or carers in making decisions about their care and mitigate any risks as a result of changes in a person’s mental and physical health, their living environment, or social arrangements.
S.S58: Make autonomous decisions in challenging and unpredictable situations, and be able to take appropriate action to assess and manage risk.
S.S59: Work with people and where appropriate their families, carers or nominated person to agree and provide evidence-based person-centred nursing care for those who are dying or near to the end of life.
S.S60: Sensitively accommodate the preferences, beliefs, cultural requirements and wishes of the deceased and people who are bereaved.
S.S61: Clearly explain and accurately record the rationale for decisions, actions taken and resulting outcomes either in writing, or using digital technology, which can be shared with the person, their family, carers, nominated person and interdisciplinary and interagency teams.
S.S62: Demonstrate leadership in applying human rights, equality, diversity and inclusion, to improve the health and wellbeing of people, families and communities.
S.S63: Demonstrate compassionate leadership when managing community nursing, interdisciplinary and interagency teams, to promote equality, diversity and inclusion, support individual professionals’ wellbeing, motivate, and encourage team cohesion and productivity.
S.S64: Lead, promote and influence the nursing profession in wider health and social care contexts.
S.S65: Identify available local community assets and engage with a range of providers, including third sector and faith-based support organisations and networks, to enhance the support and care of people.
S.S66: Evaluate a range of indicators to determine the skill mix and appropriate characteristics of the workforce required to meet the needs of specific caseloads.
S.S67: Review, lead and manage the people, financial and other resources required to safely meet caseload requirements, making professional risk-based decisions when necessary to resolve resource issues.
S.S68: Construct cogent arguments and effectively communicate complex information to justify decisions about resource allocation.
S.S69: Delegate responsibility for the management of budget, people and other resources to team members, while retaining overall accountability.
S.S70: Critically analyse own personal workload requirements and that of the wider team to lead and prioritise activities in order to manage demand and capacity.
S.S71: Safely and effectively delegate responsibilities to team members based on an assessment of their level of knowledge, skill and confidence.
S.S72: Use digital technology to maximise the use of resources across interdisciplinary and interagency teams.
S.S73: Procure equipment and other items in line with relevant procurement policies, value for money considerations and health and safety requirements.
S.S74: Articulate a clear and evidence-based rationale for complex decision making and professional judgement when leading teams in challenging situations.
S.S75: Continually reflect on own leadership approach and take action to adapt own leadership style to different situations, including but not limited to when working with diverse teams who may be geographically dispersed.
S.S76: Effectively use systems to measure the impact, quality, productivity and cost efficacy of interdisciplinary and interagency teams to allow effective leadership and performance management.
S.S77: Conduct conversations with team members to provide opportunities for positive reinforcement and challenge, and agree any development plans or remedial actions in line with appraisal processes.
S.S78: Lead the development of a positive learning culture for interdisciplinary and interagency teams.
S.S79: Use a range of approaches and resources available to educate, support and motivate people, manage talent and succession plan.
S.S80: Apply a range of leadership strategies that are effective in supporting positive team development and cohesion across disciplines and agencies.
S.S81: Select, implement and evaluate strategies which are appropriate to the composition of the team, to enable supervision, reflection and peer review.
S.S82: Recognise individual abilities and learning needs when applying the standards of education and training for pre- and post-registration nursing, midwifery and nursing associate students, in order to educate, supervise and assess effectively.
S.S83: Interpret health and safety legislation and regulations in order to develop local policy and guidance to support staff working across the range of home and community environments.
S.S84: Evaluate the outputs and recommendations of internal and external risk reporting to enable prioritisation, decision making and the development of action plans to mitigate risk.
S.S85: Exercise the knowledge, skills and professional judgement required to balance competing risks and priorities, undertaking quality impact assessments that reflect the balance between safety, quality and least restrictive practices.
S.S86: Co-produce strategies and plans for service design with people, families and communities to improve care outcomes.
S.S87: Use innovative and emerging technology effectively to ensure collection and storage of data to allow analysis and forecasting to inform service improvement and safety plans.
S.S88: Devise methods of systematically and effectively capturing and evaluating people’s lived experiences of care to lead improvements in the quality of service delivery.
S.S89: Evaluate different research designs and methodologies and their application to develop and address research questions and generate evidence for service improvement.
S.S90: Initiate and lead a continuous quality improvement programme, selecting an appropriate improvement methodology, collating and presenting results and proposing improvement actions.
S.S91: Critically appraise published results of service evaluation, research findings, improvement data and audit, and distil relevant learning that can be applied in practice to bring about service improvement.
S.S92: Present relevant research, quality and audit findings and proposals for care improvement to a range of audiences.
S.S93: Critically analyse political and economic policies and drivers that may have an impact on the health, care and wellbeing of local communities.
S.S94: Synthesise epidemiological, demographic, social, political and economic trends to forecast their impact and influence on current and prospective community nursing services.
S.S95: Build relationships between teams within different systems in health and care, appreciating the value of different approaches, skill sets and expertise.
S.S96: Maximise the effectiveness of different services within the system through collaboration and co-design, ensuring that services work seamlessly together to meet the needs of people and communities.
S.S97: Apply a range of methodologies to drive continuous service improvement within the variety of different organisations and agencies that deliver services.
S.S98: Proactively lead on the creation and development of effective system networks that enhance communication and decision making across organisations and agencies.
S.S99: Demonstrate cultural competence and leadership when challenging discriminatory, oppressive cultures and behaviours at a system level.
S.S100: Influence health and social care strategies and policies at a local, regional and national level.
S.S101: Effectively work in partnership with peers at a strategic level to promote and influence change and improve health outcomes for the people and communities served.

B.B1: Treat people with dignity at all times.
B.B2: Respect people’s diversity, beliefs, culture and individual needs.
B.B3: Show respect and empathy for individuals, their families and carer’s and those you work with at all times.
B.B4: Be adaptable, reliable and consistent and have the courage to challenge areas of concern.
B.B5: Show leadership, flexibility, self-awareness and emotional intelligence when dealing with individuals, carers, colleagues, other teams and self.
B.B6: Act as a positive role model for all those you work with, including wider teams and students.

K.K1: Techniques and information for facilitating the guest journey during a stay, particularly when guests move between teams e.g. bar to restaurant or reception to room.
K.K2: Techniques to maximise sales and improve guest experience; cross selling, upselling. supporting guest loyalty.
K.K3: Methods of communication with guests, how to make a personal connection,  and how to tailor communication to different needs and situations.
K.K4: Principles of customer service, and service mentality, and how individuals impact guest satisfaction.
K.K5: Principles of handling feedback complaints, and issues, including dispute de-escalation techniques.
K.K6: Legislation, guidelines, and local policies on guest privacy and safety e.g. data protection, child protection, modern slavery.
K.K7: Procedures for handling room keys and guest property, including lost property.
K.K8: Process for handling transactions and payments securely.
K.K9: Different packages, allowances and process for implementing those packages and allowances, including payment if required.
K.K10: Central role of communication within and between teams in ensuring operational effectiveness and efficiency.
K.K11: Process for reporting or recording faults, issues or damage, or escalating guest feedback.
K.K12: Steps of food service eg set up, reset, touch points, sequence of service in line with business processes and standards.
K.K13: Food safety and allergen legislation and procedures including handling, labelling and temperature monitoring.
K.K14: Responsibilities of a server under the licensing act and legislation related to weights and measures when serving alcohol.
K.K15: Safe and efficient use of  on site specialist equipment and technology eg cleaning equipment, computer systems relevant to business.
K.K16: Sequence for cleaning guest accommodation and public areas in line with business processes and standards.
K.K17: Hygiene management techniques to maintain a safe, clean environment, including pest and virus control,  following COSHH guidelines.
K.K18: Methods for the safe and environmentally appropriate handling and disposal of waste including; food, broken dish or glassware, biohazards, controlled substances, chemicals, general waste.
K.K19: Stock management procedures across departments relevant to own role.
K.K20: Health and safety legislation and local policies relevant to own role, including manual handling, fire safety, emergency evacuation, and lone working.
K.K21: Key performance indicators and own responsibility for contributing to them in terms of efficiency, performance and profitability.
K.K22: How to use feedback from managers and team to improve own performance.
K.K23: Professional standards for uniform, personal hygiene and appearance in line with business expectations.
K.K24: Procedures for staying up to date with business information and new procedures and discussing implementation in your team.
K.K25: Methods of planning own workload and prioritising tasks.
K.K26: Methods to sustainably reduce the waste of resources.
K.K27: Procedures for starting and finishing a shift, including handover.
K.K28: Legislation and principles relating to equity, diversity and inclusion in the workplace.

S.S1: Asks questions to support and direct the guest journey, and responds to, redirects, or escalates guest requests, taking opportunities to signpost other services eg dining, bar.
S.S2: Identifies and acts on opportunities to increase sales and guest loyalty.
S.S3: Tailors communication to meet guest needs and build rapport.
S.S4: Delivers customer service to business standards, checks that guests are satisfied with products or service, and acts on feedback.
S.S5: Assists in the resolution of feedback, complaints, and issues.
S.S6: Handles room keys and guest property, including lost property, in line with business procedures
S.S7: Handles transactions and payments securely.
S.S8: Applies packages and allowances to guest purchases, explains packages,  allowances, and processes to guests, and takes payments if required.
S.S9: Communicates within and between teams to ensure operational effectiveness and efficiency.
S.S10: Reports or records faults, issues or damage to e.g. equipment, rooms, and escalates guest feedback as appropriate.
S.S11: Follows steps of food service for relevant business e.g. set up, reset,  touch points, sequence of service.
S.S12: Follows food safety and allergen legislation eg handling, labelling, and temperature monitoring.
S.S13: Prepares and serves alcoholic and non-alcoholic beverages to business standards.
S.S14: Uses on site specialist equipment and technology relevant to business correctly and efficiently.
S.S15: Follows sequence for cleaning guest accommodation and public areas in line with business processes and standards for relevant business e.g. touch points, sequence of service.
S.S16: Selects and applies hygiene management techniques to maintain a safe, clean  environment, including pest and virus control, following COSHH guidelines.
S.S17: Handles and disposes of waste safely.
S.S18: Manage stock across departments in line with local procedures relevant to own role.
S.S19: Complies with health and safety legislation, regulations, security and safety guidelines and procedures, including manual handling, fire safety, emergency evacuation, and lone working.
S.S20: Delivers to key performance indicators to support efficiency, performance and profitability within own area of responsibility.
S.S21: Uses feedback from managers and team to improve own performance and meet personal goals.
S.S22: Attends team briefings and implements instructions, offering input or feedback where relevant within team.
S.S23: Manages own time to ensure allocated tasks are completed.
S.S24: Reduces the waste of resources, taking sustainability into account, in line with business expectations.
S.S25: Follows equity, diversity and inclusion legislation and principles.

B.B1: Takes responsibility for own health, wellbeing and professional development, seeking support when appropriate.
B.B2: Commercially aware.
B.B3: Customer focused.
B.B4: Be diligent in safe and hygienic working practices.
B.B5: Be team focused, working collaboratively with colleagues and other professionals.
B.B6: Observe professional standards in own role e.g. time keeping and appearance.

K.K1: Types of stakeholders, including candidates, clients, internal teams and colleagues.
K.K2: Different types of recruitment organisation, including their own organisation’s brand and service offering.
K.K3: Stakeholder requirements, for example consultation, salary benchmarking, market trends analysis, competitor analysis, sourcing candidates and or job roles.
K.K4: Recruitment processes, techniques, and stages of the recruitment lifecycle.
K.K5: Recruitment models, including permanent, temporary, fixed term, managed service provider (MSP) contracts, and recruitment process outsourcing (RPO) contracts.
K.K6: Regulations, legislation, and codes of practice that impact their role and the organisation, and the implications of non-compliance, including data protection, the Employment Agencies Act and the Equality Act.
K.K7: External influences on the recruitment market, including social, economic, legislative, political, and technology.
K.K8: Principles of assessing labour market conditions, including identifying shortages for specific roles and demand for candidates with transferrable skills to move from the legacy carbon economy into green economy jobs.
K.K9: Methods used for assessing candidates, for example planning and facilitating assessment centres, interview panels, informal telephone conversations, and how to support the candidate experience, including those requiring reasonable adjustments.
K.K10: Candidate sourcing techniques, including how to research, identify and attract candidates using methods to satisfy job requirements.
K.K11: Principles and techniques of candidate assessment, including assessing transferable skills to fulfil stakeholder requirements, for example to identify candidates with skills that could transfer into new green economy jobs.
K.K12: How to create and manage recruitment campaigns to meet stakeholder requirements.
K.K13: The use of networking and marketing tools in recruitment activities, for example meetings, social media, job fairs, exhibitions, events, advertisements, job boards and online.
K.K14: How to develop, maintain and improve relationships with stakeholders.
K.K15: Sales and marketing activities that support stakeholder requirements.
K.K16: Negotiating and influencing techniques.
K.K17: Methods for communicating information and interacting with candidates and other stakeholders to facilitate understanding, for example face-to-face or online meetings, emails, reports, and presentations.
K.K18: Managing and supporting candidates through the recruitment lifecycle, including onboarding, providing advice and aftercare.
K.K19: Technology and software tools used to support recruitment management activities, for example Artificial Intelligence (AI).
K.K20: The organisation's resource strategy and goals, and how this impacts their role.
K.K21: Recruitment budgets and how these fit into the organisation’s business strategy.
K.K22: How to plan and prioritise activities to meet the organisation’s recruitment strategy and processes, including allocating and managing resources throughout the recruitment life cycle and methods for increasing talent and client pipelines.
K.K23: Ethical and sustainable recruitment strategies, processes and working practices.
K.K24: Principles and policies of equity, diversity and inclusion in the workplace, and their impact on the organisation and recruitment activities.
K.K25: The complaint handling process for their organisation.

S.S1: Identify, progress, and convert leads into new candidates, placements, or clients.
S.S2: Source vacancies in line with stakeholder requirements and organisational policies and procedures.
S.S3: Manage and maintain stakeholder relationships and their role within the recruitment process.
S.S4: Interpret and apply regulation and legislation, share best practice, and advise stakeholders on their application.
S.S5: Plan and manage recruitment campaigns to attract candidates, including agreeing objectives with stakeholders and monitoring performance.
S.S6: Research, identify and attract candidates using methods to satisfy job requirements, including those with transferrable skills to move from the legacy carbon economy to green economy jobs.
S.S7: Process, review, and progress candidate applications.
S.S8: Create and present diverse short lists of candidates to stakeholders.
S.S9: Inform and advise candidates on the outcome of their application at the individual stages of the recruitment process, including those that have been unsuccessful.
S.S10: Manage the recruitment and selection process for candidates, including those who require reasonable adjustments.
S.S11: Communicate information through different media, for example face-to-face or online meetings, emails, reports, and presentations.
S.S12: Engage with stakeholders to review recruitment processes and identify opportunities for continuous improvement and improving own performance.
S.S13: Review services provided and implemented improvements based on stakeholder feedback.
S.S14: Place candidates into roles that match their skills and stakeholder requirements.
S.S15: Use technology and software tools to manage information, ensuring compliance with organisation and legislation requirements.
S.S16: Challenge poor practice and non-compliance with the recruitment process and escalate where appropriate.
S.S17: Identify future changes in the sector that may impact the organisation, for example technology advances.
S.S18: Interpret policies to support and promote the delivery of equity, diversity, and inclusion in the workplace, and monitor their impact on recruitment activities.
S.S19: Identify and apply sustainable and greener methods of working, for example reducing energy and paper consumption, managing diaries to reduce carbon emissions (hold virtual meetings or attend multiple external meetings on the same day), considering greener options when booking venues for assessment centres or planning recruitment campaigns.
S.S20: Identify and maximise opportunities to support the organisation’s business strategy, for example growing client or candidate pipelines.
S.S21: Manage resources within budget requirements.
S.S22: Respond to stakeholder complaints and escalate where appropriate.

B.B1: Acts professionally, ethically and with integrity.
B.B2: Supports an inclusive culture, treating colleagues, candidates, and external stakeholders fairly and with respect.
B.B3: Takes accountability and ownership of their tasks and workload.
B.B4: Seeks learning opportunities and continuous professional development.
B.B5: Works flexibly and adapts to changing circumstances.

K.K1: The aetiology, pathogenesis and epidemiological trends of oral and dental disease and their application to patient management.
K.K2: Clinical presentations of oral and dental diseases relevant to the role of a dental nurse and the principles underpinning their diagnosis, prevention, and treatment.
K.K3: The variance in disease presentation across diverse cultural and social groups, and those with protected characteristics, and how this impacts diagnosis, prevention, and treatment.
K.K4: General and systemic diseases and psychological conditions, and their relevance to oral health and impact on clinical treatment, patient compliance, self-care, and outcomes.
K.K5: The relevance to patient management of dental, oral, craniofacial, and generalanatomy, recognising the diversity of anatomy across the patient.
K.K6: Physiology and its application to patient management.
K.K7: Psychological and sociological concepts and theoretical frameworks of health, illness, behavioural change and disease, and how these can be applied in clinical practice.
K.K8: The potential routes of transmission of infectious agents in dental practice, mechanisms for the prevention of infection, the scientific principles of decontamination and disinfection.
K.K9: The need for effective recorded maintenance and testing of equipment and requirements for appropriate storage, handling, and use of materials.
K.K10: Health risks of prescribed, non-prescribed and recreational drug use and misuse on oral and general health.
K.K11: The scientific principles underpinning the use of materials and biomaterials used in dentistry.
K.K12: The scientific principles of medical ionizing radiation and statutory regulations, and how these are applied to clinical practice.
K.K13: The principles of obtaining valid patient consent.
K.K14: The importance of each component of the patient assessment process.
K.K15: How to raise safeguarding concerns for children and adults and act accordingly.
K.K16: Principles of preventive care.
K.K17: How diet and nutritional status can influence oral and general health and how to provide appropriate advice and support.
K.K18: Common signs and symptoms of oral cancer and the importance of raising a concern and early referral.
K.K19: The responsibilities of the dental team as an access point to and from wider healthcare.
K.K20: The roles and organisation of various referral networks, clinical guidelines and policies and local variation.
K.K21: Common conditions and complications that may arise following dental implant therapy.
K.K22: The importance and components of a comprehensive and contemporaneous patient history.
K.K23: The risks within and around the clinical environment.
K.K24: The use of a range of communication methods and technologies and their appropriate application in support of clinical practice.
K.K25: The importance of non-verbal communication, including listening skills, and the barriers to effective communication.
K.K26: The professional expectations, potential impact, and consequence of using social media as a communication tool.
K.K27: The importance of contemporaneous, complete and accurate patient records in accordance with legal requirements and best practice.
K.K28: The legal responsibilities of maintaining and protecting patients’ information.
K.K29: The responsibilities and limitations of delegating to other members of the dental team.
K.K30: The role and professional responsibilities associated with appraisal; training and review of colleagues; provision of and receipt of effective feedback in the context of developing members of the dental team
K.K31: The roles of dental and other healthcare professionals in the context of learning and working in a dental and wider healthcare team.
K.K32: The contribution that team members and effective team working makes to the delivery of safe and effective high-quality care, including the benefits of working in culturally diverse teams.
K.K33: Team working guidance provided by the GDC and other relevant bodies.
K.K34: The impact of Direct Access on each registrant group and the impact on the application of each group’s scope of practice.
K.K35: The scope of practice of each member of the dental team and how the roles interact for effective teamwork and patient care.
K.K36: The need to ensure that those who raise concerns are protected from discrimination or other detrimental effects.
K.K37: The differences between management and leadership.
K.K38: Own management and leadership role and the range of skills and knowledge required to do this effectively.
K.K39: How to take responsibility for the quality of services and devices provided to the patient as relevant to own scope of practice.
K.K40: The GDC’s expectations and requirements as set out in regulations and guidance and other relevant laws, ethical guidance and systems, in addition to the above legal frameworks.
K.K41: The importance of having appropriate indemnity arrangements in place for both the professional and patient.
K.K42: The importance of candour and effective communication with patients when things go wrong or when dealing with a complaint.
K.K43: How and where to report any patient safety issues which arise.
K.K44: The responsibilities of, and mechanisms for raising concerns about your own or others’ health, behaviour or professional performance as described in GDC guidance.
K.K45: Diversity, equality, inclusion and discrimination and the underpinning legislation, and explain how to apply these principles to manage patients with protected characteristics and work within the dental team, noting that this legislation may differ in England, Scotland, Wales and Northern Ireland.
K.K46: Attributes of professional attitudes and behaviour in all environments and media, including interaction with social media.
K.K47: The principles and procedures for good complaints handling.
K.K48: The responsibility that dental practices and individual practitioners have in compliance with legal and regulatory frameworks.
K.K49: Cultural competence and its relevance in assessing the needs and planning care for patients from diverse backgrounds.
K.K50: The dental and wider healthcare systems dental professionals work within including local and national health policy and organisations, delivery of healthcare and equity.
K.K51: The basic principles of a population health approach including demographic and social trends, UK and international oral health trends, determinants of health and inequalities in health, and the ways in which these are measured and current patterns.
K.K52: The role of health promotion in terms of the changing environment, community and individual behaviours to deliver health gain.
K.K53: Methods of evidence-based prevention at a community and or population level.
K.K54: The principles of planning oral health care for communities to meet needs and demands.
K.K55: The ethical challenges associated with providing patient care within the current dental healthcare systems.
K.K56: The principles and limitations of the currently available options for funding of dental healthcare provision for individual patients.
K.K57: The management of resources in provision of care decisions including appropriate use of primary and secondary care networks.
K.K58: The importance of collaboration across the health and social care sector for the benefit of communities and individual patients.
K.K59: The barriers and challenges which prevent sections of the population accessing oral healthcare, including patients from marginalised populations and patients with protected characteristics.

K.K63: The principles of an evidence-based approach.

K.K65: The importance of assessment, feedback, critical reflection, identification of learning needs and appraisal in personal development planning.
K.K66: The importance of and requirement for commitment to lifelong learning.
K.K67: The principles of personal development planning, recording of evidence, and reflective practice.
K.K68: Strategies to identify and manage the personal and emotional challenges of work, teamwork and workload.
K.K69: Opportunities for improvement of a clinical service or to manage and or mitigate risks.
K.K70: Ways to self-monitor, self-care and routes of seeking appropriate advice in terms of personal wellbeing.
K.K71: Strategies to identify and manage the personal and emotional challenges of uncertainty and change.
K.K72: The role of coping strategies for practice, such as reflection, self-acceptance, debriefing, handing over to another colleague, peer support and asking for help in responding to challenges and setbacks.

S.S1: Identify relevant and appropriate dental, oral, craniofacial, and general anatomy, recognising the diversity of anatomy across the patient population.
S.S2: Evaluate the health risks of prescribed, non-prescribed and recreational drug use and misuse on oral and general health.
S.S3: Evaluate the scientific principles underpinning the use of materials and Biomaterials, their limitations and selection, with emphasis on those used in dentistry.

S.S8: Undertake orthodontic assessment.
S.S9: Obtain valid consent from patients explaining all the relevant factors and taking into account the legal requirements where appropriate within scope of practice.
S.S10: Record an accurate and contemporaneous patient history.
S.S11: Accurately record dental charting as carried out by other appropriate registrants.
S.S12: Accurately record an oral health assessment.
S.S13: Prepare records, images, equipment and materials for clinical assessment.
S.S14: Process and manage dental radiographs and images.
S.S15: Manage patient anxiety appropriately, effectively, and safely.
S.S16: Monitor, support and reassure patients through effective communication and behavioural techniques.
S.S17: Identify changes in the patient’s reported oral health status and take appropriate action.
S.S18: Make arrangements for follow-up care as prescribed by the operator.
S.S19: Provide chairside support to the operator during treatment.
S.S20: Prepare, mix and handle dental materials.
S.S21: Identify and explain the risks within and around the clinical environment and manage these in a safe and effective manner.
S.S22: Implement, perform, and manage effective decontamination and infection control procedures according to current guidelines.
S.S23: Prepare and maintain the clinical environment including the instruments and equipment.
S.S24: Identify, assess, and manage medical emergencies.
S.S25: Provide patients/carers with comprehensive, personalised preventive advice, instruction, and intervention in a manner which is accessible, promotes self-care and motivates patients/carers to comply with advice and take responsibility to maintain and improve oral health.
S.S26: Support the management of patients with acute oral conditions ensuring involvement of appropriate dental team members.
S.S27: Adopt an evidence-based approach to clinical practice.
S.S28: Communicate effectively and sensitively, tailoring to context, by spoken, written and or electronic means with all patients, including patients whose first language is not English, using representatives or interpreters where necessary, in relation to patients with anxious or challenging behaviour or special considerations such as emotional trauma and difficult circumstances, such as breaking bad news, or discussing issues such as alcohol consumption, smoking, or diet.
S.S29: Communicate effectively and sensitively by spoken, written and electronic means with the public.
S.S30: Communicate effectively by spoken, written and electronic means with colleagues from dental and other healthcare professions in relation to the direct care of individual patients, oral health promotion and raising concerns when problems arise, including where patients cause distress to staff.
S.S31: Maintain contemporaneous, complete and accurate patient records in accordance with legal requirements and best practice.
S.S32: Communicate with care, compassion, empathy and respect in all professional interactions with patients, their representatives, the public and colleagues.
S.S33: Communicate appropriately and effectively in professional discussions and transactions.
S.S34: Give feedback effectively to other members of the team.
S.S35: Respect the roles of dental and other healthcare professionals in the context of learning and working in a dental and wider healthcare team.
S.S36: Demonstrate effective team working.
S.S37: Contribute to your team in providing dental care for patients.
S.S38: Where appropriate manage, refer or delegate work according to the scope of practice of members of the dental team, in line with competence and professional practice.
S.S39: Take a patient-centred approach to working with the dental and wider healthcare team.
S.S40: Raise concerns where appropriate about your own or others’ health, behaviour or professional performance.
S.S41: Comply with systems and processes to support safe patient care.
S.S42: Act in accordance with current best practice guidelines.
S.S43: Act in accordance with national and local clinical governance and health and safety requirements.
S.S44: Act within the legal frameworks which inform personal behaviour, the delivery of healthcare and the protection and promotion of the health of individual patients.
S.S45: Act with integrity and ensure your actions maintain the trust of colleagues, patients, and the public in you, your team, and the profession across all environments and media.
S.S46: Demonstrate personal accountability to patients, the regulator, the team and wider community.
S.S47: Work in partnership with colleagues to develop and maintain an effective and supportive environment which promotes the safety and wellbeing of the patient and dental team.
S.S48: Where appropriate lead, manage and take professional responsibility for the actions of colleagues and other members of the team involved in patient care.
S.S49: Where appropriate support patients to negotiate the barriers and challenges which prevent sections of the population accessing oral healthcare, including patients from marginalised populations and patients with protected characteristics.
S.S50: Treat your patients, members of the public and your colleagues with dignity and respect and without discrimination.
S.S51: Support patients to make informed decisions about their care, making their interests your first concern.
S.S52: Demonstrate cultural competence, accepting and respecting the diversity of patients and colleagues.
S.S53: Provide the best possible outcome for your patients by using your knowledge and skills, acting as an advocate for their needs where appropriate.
S.S54: Speak up to protect others from harm.
S.S55: Evaluate the effectiveness of evidence-based prevention at a community and or population level.
S.S56: Proactively address discriminatory language, behaviour and microaggressions from colleagues, patients and other professionals.
S.S57: Evaluate the role of health promotion in terms of the changing environment, community and individual behaviours to deliver health gain.
S.S58: Evaluate and apply the evidence base in relation to the environmental impacts of common treatment methods and approaches to the delivery of oral healthcare.
S.S59: Contribute positively to the healthcare communities of which you are a part.
S.S60: Evaluate an evidence base.
S.S61: Utilise the receipt of effective feedback in the professional development of self.
S.S62: Demonstrate personal development planning, recording of evidence, and reflective practice.
S.S63: Evaluate the impact of new techniques and technologies as they relate to dental nurse practice.
S.S64: Accurately assess your own capabilities and limitations in the interest of high-quality patient care and seek advice from supervisors or colleagues where appropriate.
S.S65: Recognise personal assumptions, biases and prejudices and manage the impact of these on patient care and professional behaviour with colleagues, patients and wider society.
S.S66: Recognise the impact of contextual factors on the health care environment and patient safety and manage this professionally.
S.S67: Demonstrate own professional responsibility in the development of self.
S.S68: Develop and maintain professional knowledge and competence.
S.S69: Demonstrate engagement with systems and personal strategies which promote and maintain physical and mental wellbeing.
S.S70: Demonstrate appropriate continuous improvement activities.
S.S71: Recognise when and how to take action if wellbeing is compromised to a point of affecting an individual’s role or professional relationships.
S.S72: Effectively manage your own time and resources.
S.S73: Underpin all patient care with a preventive approach, that takes account of patient compliance and self-care, to contribute to the patient’s long-term oral and general health.

B.B1: Compassionate professional behaviour and professional responsibilities making sure the fundamental needs of patients and carers are addressed.
B.B2: Act with integrity, be polite, considerate, trustworthy, conscientious and honest.
B.B3: Take personal and professional responsibility for their actions.
B.B4: Manage time and prioritise effectively.
B.B5: Be open and honest in their interactions with patients, carers, colleagues and employers when things go wrong, known as the professional duty of candour.
B.B6: Respect patients’ wishes about whether they wish to participate in the education of learners.
B.B7: Uphold the General Dental Council Standards for The Dental Team (2013).
B.B8: Willingness to learn, and be proactive in progressing in your own development.
