S.S53: Evaluate an evidence base.
S.S54: Utilise the receipt of effective feedback in the professional development of self.
S.S55: Demonstrate personal development planning, recording of evidence, and reflective practice.
S.S56: Evaluate the impact of new techniques and technologies as they relate to dental technology practice.
S.S57: Accurately assess your own capabilities and limitations in the interest of high-quality patient care and seek advice from supervisors or colleagues where appropriate.
S.S58: Recognise personal assumptions, biases and prejudices and manage the impact of these on patient care and professional behaviour with colleagues, patients and wider society.
S.S59: Recognise the impact of contextual factors on the health care environment and patient safety and manage this professionally.
S.S60: Demonstrate own professional responsibility in the development of self.
S.S61: Develop and maintain professional knowledge and competence.
S.S62: Demonstrate appropriate continuous improvement activities.
S.S63: Demonstrate engagement with systems and personal strategies which promote and maintain physical and mental wellbeing.
S.S64: Recognise when and how to take action if wellbeing is compromised to a point of affecting an individual’s role or professional relationships.
S.S65: Effectively manage your own time and resources.

B.B1: Compassionate professional behaviour and professional responsibilities making sure the fundamental needs of patients and carers are addressed.
B.B2: Act with integrity, be polite, considerate, trustworthy, conscientious and honest.
B.B3: Take personal and professional responsibility for their actions.
B.B4: Manage time and prioritise effectively.
B.B5: Be open and honest in their interactions with patients, carers, colleagues and employers when things go wrong, known as the professional duty of candour.
B.B6: Respect patients’ wishes about whether they wish to participate in the education of learners.
B.B7: Uphold the General Dental Council Standards for The Dental Team (2013).
B.B8: Willingness to learn, and be proactive in progressing in your own development.

K.K1: Aetiology, pathogenesis and epidemiological trends of oral and dental disease and their application to patient management.
K.K2: Clinical presentations of oral and dental diseases relevant to the role of a clinical dental technician and the principles underpinning their diagnosis, prevention, and treatment.
K.K3: Variance in disease presentation across diverse cultural and social groups, and those with protected characteristics, and how this impacts diagnosis, prevention, and treatment.
K.K4: General and systemic diseases and psychological conditions, and their relevance to oral health and impact on clinical treatment, patient compliance, self-care, and outcomes.
K.K5: Relevance to patient management of dental, oral, craniofacial, and general anatomy, recognising the diversity of anatomy across the patient.
K.K6: Relevant physiology and its application to patient management.
K.K7: Psychological and sociological concepts and theoretical frameworks of health, illness, behavioural change and disease.
K.K8: Potential routes of transmission of infectious agents in dental practice, mechanisms for the prevention of infection, the scientific principles of decontamination and disinfection and their relevance to health and safety.
K.K9: The need for effective recorded maintenance and testing of equipment and requirements for appropriate storage, handling, and use of materials.
K.K10: Scientific principles of medical ionizing radiation and statutory regulations, and how these are applied to clinical practice.
K.K11: Principles of obtaining valid patient consent.
K.K12: Importance of each component of the patient assessment process.
K.K13: Signs of abuse, neglect or emotional trauma, local and national systems that safeguard the welfare of children and adults and how to raise concerns and act accordingly.
K.K14: How to provide appropriate advice and support including signposting or referral for health risks from the use of prescribed, non-prescribed and recreational drug use and misuse on oral and general health.
K.K15: Scientific principles underpinning the use of materials and biomaterials and their limitations and selection, with emphasis on those used in dentistry.
K.K16: Principles of preventive care.
K.K17: Importance of achieving a healthy oral environment prior to introduction of a prosthesis.
K.K18: How diet and nutritional status can influence oral and general health and how to provide appropriate advice and support.
K.K19: Roles and organisation of various referral networks, clinical guidelines and policies and local variation.
K.K20: Responsibilities of the dental team as an access point to and from wider healthcare.
K.K21: How the design and manufacture of custom-made dental devices can contribute to the prevention of oral disease and the interests of the patient’s long-term oral, health, safety and well-being.
K.K22: Impact of medical and psychological conditions in a range of patients and how this can influence patient compliance, self-care, and outcomes, taking into account the patient’s cultural identity and values.
K.K23: Risks within and around the clinical environment.
K.K24: Principles of evidence based and appropriate design in the manufacture and provision of custom-made dental devices.
K.K25: Use of a range of communication methods and technologies and their appropriate application in support of clinical practice.
K.K26: Importance of non-verbal communication, including listening skills, and the barriers to effective communication.
K.K27: Professional expectations, potential impact, and consequence of using social media as a communication tool.
K.K28: Importance of contemporaneous, complete and accurate patient records in accordance with legal requirements and best practice.
K.K29: The legal responsibilities of maintaining and protecting patients’ information.
K.K30: Responsibilities and limitations of delegating to other members of the dental team.
K.K31: Role and professional responsibilities associated with appraisal training and review of colleagues provision of and receipt of effective feedback in the context of developing members of the dental team.
K.K32: Roles of dental and other healthcare professionals in the context of learning and working in a dental and wider healthcare team.
K.K33: Contribution that team members and effective team working makes to the delivery of safe and effective high-quality care, including the benefits of working in culturally diverse teams.
K.K34: Team working guidance provided by the GDC and other relevant bodies.
K.K35: Impact of Direct Access on each registrant group and the impact on the application of each group’s scope of practice.
K.K36: Scope of practice of each member of the dental team and how the roles interact for effective teamwork and patient care.
K.K37: Need to ensure that those who raise concerns are protected from discrimination or other detrimental effects.
K.K38: Differences between management and leadership.
K.K39: Own management and leadership role and the range of skills and knowledge required to do this effectively.
K.K40: How to take responsibility for the quality of services and devices provided to the patient as relevant to your scope of practice.
K.K41: Importance of having appropriate indemnity arrangements in place for both the professional and patient.
K.K42: Importance of candour and effective communication with patients when things go wrong or when dealing with a complaint.
K.K43: How and where to report any patient safety issues which arise.
K.K44: Personal responsibility and the mechanisms for raising concerns about your own or others’ health, behaviour or professional performance as described in GDC guidance.
K.K45: Explain the attributes of professional attitudes and behaviour in all environments and media, including interaction with social media.
K.K46: Principles and procedures for good complaints handling.
K.K47: Responsibility that dental practices and individual practitioners have in compliance with legal and regulatory frameworks
K.K48: Diversity, equality, inclusion and discrimination and the underpinning legislation, and explain how to apply these principles to manage patients with protected characteristics and work within the dental team, noting that this legislation may differ in England, Scotland, Wales and Northern Ireland.
K.K49: Cultural competence and its relevance in assessing the needs and planning care for patients from diverse backgrounds.
K.K50: The GDC’s expectations and requirements as set out in regulations and guidance and other relevant laws, ethical guidance and systems, in addition to the above legal frameworks.
K.K51: The basic principles of a population health approach including demographic and social trends, UK and international oral health trends, determinants of health and inequalities in health, and the ways in which these are measured and current patterns.
K.K52: Dental and wider healthcare systems dental professionals work within including local and national health policy and organisations, delivery of healthcare and equity.
K.K53: Role of health promotion in terms of the changing environment, community and individual behaviours to deliver health gain.
K.K54: Principles of planning oral health care for communities to meet needs and demands.
K.K55: Principles and limitations of the currently available options for funding of dental healthcare provision for individual patients.
K.K56: The ethical challenges associated with providing patient care within the current dental healthcare systems.
K.K57: The considerations of the management of resources in provision of care decisions including appropriate use of primary and secondary care networks.
K.K58: Importance of collaboration across the health and social care sector for the benefit of communities and individual patients.
K.K59: Barriers and challenges which prevent sections of the population accessing oral healthcare, including patients from marginalised populations and patients with protected characteristics.
K.K60: Main principles relating to sustainable oral health care, both environmentally and in terms of patient compliance, and the factors that might affect implementing a sustainable approach.
K.K61: The term insight in the context of professional practice.
K.K62: Why insight is important in ensuring safe and effective patient care, and to personal development.
K.K64: Model for self-reflection and how this process can be used to inform personal development, viewpoint, preconceptions, bias and behaviour.
K.K65: Importance of assessment, feedback, critical reflection, identification of learning needs and appraisal in personal development planning.
K.K66: Importance of and requirement for commitment to lifelong learning.
K.K67: Principles of personal development planning, recording of evidence, and reflective practice.
K.K68: Opportunities for improvement of a clinical service or to manage and or mitigate risks.
K.K69: Ways of self-monitoring, self-care and routes of seeking appropriate advice in terms of personal wellbeing.
K.K70: Strategies to identify and manage the personal and emotional challenges of work, teamwork and workload.
K.K71: Strategies to identify and manage the personal and emotional challenges of uncertainty and change.
K.K72: The role of coping strategies for practice, such as reflection, self-acceptance, debriefing, handing over to another colleague, peer support and asking for help in responding to challenges and setbacks.

S.S1: Identify the clinical presentations of oral and dental diseases relevant to the role of a clinical dental technician and the principles underpinning their diagnosis, prevention, and treatment.
S.S2: Identify relevant and appropriate dental, oral, craniofacial, and general anatomy, recognising the diversity of anatomy across the patient population.
S.S3: Apply psychological and sociological concepts and theoretical frameworks of health, illness, behavioural change and disease to clinical practice.
S.S4: Critically evaluate all components of patient management including risks, benefits, contra-indications and indications.
S.S5: Adopt an evidence-based approach to clinical practice.
S.S6: Obtain, record, and interpret a comprehensive and contemporaneous patient history, taking into account social and or cultural sensitivities.
S.S7: Undertake an appropriate systematic intra- and extra-oral clinical examination.
S.S8: Assess patients’ levels of anxiety, experience, and expectations in respect of dental care and oral health.
S.S9: Undertake relevant special investigations and diagnostic procedures, including radiography.
S.S10: Formulate a differential diagnosis or diagnoses and from there a definitive diagnosis for the edentulous patient.
S.S11: Obtain valid consent from patients explaining all the relevant factors and taking into account the legal requirements where appropriate within scope of practice.
S.S12: Evaluate the health risks of prescribed, non-prescribed and recreational drug use and misuse on oral and general health.
S.S13: Underpin all patient care with a preventive approach, that takes account of patient compliance and self-care, to contribute to the patient’s long-term oral and general health.
S.S14: Describe, take account of and explain to the patient the impact of their periodontal health, including compliance with oral healthcare advice, potential effect on general health and the need for self-care in the overall treatment plan and how this influences their treatment outcome.
S.S15: Identify potential malignancy and tumours and explain the importance of early referral.
S.S16: Synthesise the full results of the patient’s assessment and make clinical judgements as appropriate for the edentulous patient taking into account patient compliance, values, cultural identity self-care.
S.S17: Formulate a personalised treatment plan, synthesising patient assessment, diagnostic data, prognosis, and shared decision making.
S.S18: Assess own capabilities and limitations and refer patients for treatment or advice when and where appropriate.
S.S19: Manage appropriate laboratory investigations.
S.S20: Identify and manage the impact of medical and psychological conditions in a range of patients and determine how this can influence patient compliance, self-care, and outcomes, taking into account the patient’s cultural identity and values.
S.S21: Prevent, diagnose, and manage patient anxiety appropriately, effectively, and safely.
S.S22: Prevent, diagnose, and manage patient pain appropriately, effectively, and safely.
S.S23: Monitor and review treatment outcomes and patient response to advice, providing aftercare, follow-up and ongoing preventive advice and intervention.
S.S24: Identify the risks within and around the clinical environment and manage these in a safe and effective manner
S.S25: Implement, perform, and manage effective decontamination and infection control procedures according to current guidelines.
S.S26: Identify, assess, and manage medical emergencies.
S.S27: Manage patients’ acute oral conditions ensuring involvement of appropriate dental team members.
S.S28: Provide patients and or carers with comprehensive, personalised preventive advice, instruction and intervention in a manner which is accessible, promotes self-care and motivates patients and or carers to comply with advice and take responsibility to maintain and improve oral health.
S.S29: Evaluate for individual patients the need for more complex treatment and seek advice.
S.S30: Apply the principles of evidence based and appropriate design in the manufacture and provision of custom-made dental devices.
S.S31: Design, manufacture, assess and provide biomechanically sound removable devices.
S.S32: Design, manufacture, assess and provide biomechanically sound fixed prostheses.
S.S33: Design, manufacture, assess and provide biomechanically sound orthodontic appliances.
S.S34: Repair and modify removable custom-made dental devices to meet the needs of the patient.
S.S35: Assess and ensure fitness for purpose of custom-made dental devices and propose alternative solutions where required.
S.S36: Carry out procedures to meet the prescription.
S.S37: Fit devices and appliances for the oral cavity according to prescription.
S.S38: Fit biomechanically sound complete dentures.
S.S39: Communicate effectively and sensitively, tailoring to context, by spoken, written and or electronic means with all patients, including patients whose first language is not English, using representatives or interpreters where necessary, in relation to patients with anxious or challenging behaviour or special considerations such as emotional trauma, difficult circumstances, such as breaking bad news, or discussing issues such as alcohol consumption, smoking, or diet.
S.S40: Communicate effectively and sensitively by spoken, written and electronic means with the public.
S.S41: Communicate effectively by spoken, written and electronic means with colleagues from dental and other healthcare professions in relation to: the direct care of individual patients, oral health promotion and raising concerns when problems arise, including where patients cause distress to staff.
S.S42: Use appropriate methods to provide accurate, clear and comprehensive information when referring patients to other dental and healthcare professionals.
S.S43: Communicate appropriately and effectively in professional discussions and transactions.
S.S44: Communicate with care, compassion, empathy and respect in all professional interactions with patients, their representatives, the public and colleagues.
S.S45: Maintain contemporaneous, complete and accurate patient records in accordance with legal requirements and best practice.
S.S46: Give feedback effectively to other members of the team.
S.S47: Respect the roles of dental and other healthcare professionals in the context of learning and working in a dental and wider healthcare team.
S.S48: Demonstrate effective team working.
S.S49: Contribute to your team in providing dental care for patients.
S.S50: Take a patient-centred approach to working with the dental and wider healthcare team.
S.S51: Where appropriate manage and refer or delegate work according to the scope of practice of members of the dental team, in line with competence and professional practice.
S.S52: Provide the best possible outcome for your patients by using your knowledge and skills, acting as an advocate for their needs where appropriate.
S.S53: Speak up to protect others from harm.
S.S54: Raise concerns where appropriate about your own or others’ health, behaviour or professional performance.
S.S55: Comply with systems and processes to support safe patient care.
S.S56: Act in accordance with current best practice guidelines.
S.S57: Act in accordance with national and local clinical governance and health and safety requirements.
S.S58: Act within the legal frameworks which inform personal behaviour, the delivery of healthcare and the protection and promotion of the health of individual patients.
S.S59: Act with integrity and ensure your actions maintain the trust of colleagues, patients, and the public in you, your team, and the profession across all environments and media.
S.S60: Proactively address discriminatory language, behaviour and microaggressions from colleagues, patients and other professionals.
S.S61: Demonstrate personal accountability to patients, the regulator, the team and wider community.
S.S62: Work in partnership with colleagues to develop and maintain an effective and supportive environment which promotes the safety and wellbeing of the patient and dental team.
S.S63: Where appropriate lead, manage and take professional responsibility for the actions of colleagues and other members of the team involved in patient care.
S.S64: Treat your patients, members of the public and your colleagues with dignity and respect and without discrimination.
S.S65: Support patients to make informed decisions about their care, making their interests your first concern.
S.S66: Demonstrate cultural competence, accepting and respecting the diversity of patients and colleagues.
S.S67: Evaluate the role of health promotion in terms of the changing environment, community and individual behaviours to deliver health gain.
S.S68: Evaluate evidence-based prevention at a community and or population level.
S.S69: Where appropriate support patients to negotiate the barriers and challenges which prevent sections of the population from accessing oral healthcare, including patients from marginalised populations and patients with protected characteristics.
S.S70: Evaluate and apply the evidence base in relation to the environmental impacts of common treatment methods and approaches to the delivery of oral healthcare.
S.S71: Contribute positively to the healthcare communities of which you are a part.
S.S72: Evaluate an evidence base.
S.S73: Critically appraise approaches to dental research and explain how to integrate the outcomes of research with patient care.
S.S74: Utilise the receipt of effective feedback in the professional development of self.
S.S75: Demonstrate personal development planning, recording of evidence, and reflective practice.
S.S76: Evaluate the impact of new techniques and technologies in clinical practice.
S.S77: Accurately assess your own capabilities and limitations in the interest of high-quality patient care and seek advice from supervisors or colleagues where appropriate.
S.S78: Recognise personal assumptions, biases and prejudices and manage the impact of these on patient care and professional behaviour with colleagues, patients and wider society.
S.S79: Recognise the impact of contextual factors on the health care environment and patient safety and manage this professionally.
S.S80: Demonstrate own professional responsibility in the development of self.
S.S81: Develop and maintain professional knowledge and competence.
S.S83: Demonstrate engagement with systems and personal strategies which promote and maintain physical and mental wellbeing.
S.S84: Recognise when and how to take action if wellbeing is compromised to a point of affecting an individual’s role or professional relationships.
S.S85: Effectively manage your own time and resources.

S.S37: Where appropriate manage and refer and or delegate work according to the scope of practice of members of the dental team, in line with competence and professional practice.
S.S38: Provide the best possible outcome for your patients by using your knowledge and skills, acting as an advocate for their needs where appropriate.
S.S39: Speak up to protect others from harm.
S.S40: Raise concerns where appropriate about your own or others’ health, behaviour or professional performance.
S.S41: Comply with systems and processes to support safe patient care.
S.S42: Act in accordance with current best practice guidelines.
S.S43: Act in accordance with national and local clinical governance and health and safety requirements.
S.S44: Act within the legal frameworks which inform personal behaviour, the delivery of healthcare and the protection and promotion of the health of individual patients.
S.S45: Act with integrity and ensure your actions maintain the trust of colleagues, patients, and the public in you, your team, and the profession across all environments and media.
S.S46: Proactively address discriminatory language, behaviour and microaggressions from colleagues, patients and other professionals.
S.S47: Demonstrate personal accountability to patients, the regulator, the team and wider community.
S.S48: Work in partnership with colleagues to develop and maintain an effective and supportive environment which promotes the safety and wellbeing of the patient and dental team.
S.S49: Where appropriate lead, manage and take professional responsibility for the actions of colleagues and other members of the team involved in patient care.
S.S50: Treat your patients, members of the public and your colleagues with dignity and respect and without discrimination.
S.S51: Support patients to make informed decisions about their care, making their interests your first concern.
S.S52: Demonstrate cultural competence, accepting and respecting the diversity of patients and colleagues.
S.S53: Evaluate the role of health promotion in terms of the changing environment, community and individual behaviours to deliver health gain.
S.S54: Evaluate evidence-based prevention at a community and or population level.
S.S55: Where appropriate support patients to negotiate the barriers and challenges which prevent sections of the population accessing oral healthcare, including patients from marginalised populations and patients with protected characteristics.
S.S56: Evaluate and apply the evidence base in relation to the environmental impacts of common treatment methods and approaches to the delivery of oral healthcare.
S.S57: Contribute positively to the healthcare communities of which you are a part.
S.S58: Evaluate an evidence base.
S.S59: Utilise the receipt of effective feedback in the professional development of self.
S.S60: Demonstrate personal development planning, recording of evidence, and reflective practice.
S.S61: Evaluate the impact of new techniques and technologies in clinical practice.
S.S62: Accurately assess your own capabilities and limitations in the interest of high-quality patient care and seek advice from supervisors or colleagues where appropriate.
S.S63: Recognise personal assumptions, biases and prejudices and manage the impact of these on patient care and professional behaviour with colleagues, patients and wider society.
S.S64: Recognise the impact of contextual factors on the health care environment and patient safety and manage this professionally.
S.S65: Demonstrate own professional responsibility in the development of self.
S.S66: Develop and maintain professional knowledge and competence.
S.S67: Demonstrate appropriate continuous improvement activities.
S.S68: Demonstrate engagement with systems and personal strategies which promote and maintain physical and mental wellbeing.
S.S69: Recognise when and how to take action if wellbeing is compromised to a point of affecting an individual’s role or professional relationships.
S.S70: Effectively manage your own time and resources.

B.B1: Compassionate professional behaviour and professional responsibilities making sure the fundamental needs of patients and carers are addressed.
B.B2: Act with integrity, be polite, considerate, trustworthy, conscientious and honest.
B.B3: Take personal and professional responsibility for their actions.
B.B4: Manage time and prioritise effectively.
B.B5: Be open and honest in their interactions with patients, carers, colleagues and employers when things go wrong, known as the professional duty of candour.
B.B6: Respect patients’ wishes about whether they wish to participate in the education of learners.
B.B7: Uphold the General Dental Council Standards for The Dental Team (2013).
B.B8: Willingness to learn, and be proactive in progressing in your own development.

K.K1: Sector and organisational specific handover information, from internal and external stakeholders, for example, crew numbers, crew replacement plans, train plans, planned maintenance activities
K.K2: Sector regulations that need to be considered when creating timetables, schedules, rosters or diagrams.
K.K3: Organisational procedures to create timetables, schedules, rosters or diagrams: transport services, contractual, passenger expectation, commercial.
K.K4: Sector regulations that need to be considered when revising timetables, schedules, rosters or diagrams in the case of special events, or unforeseen circumstances.
K.K5: Organisational procedures to revise timetables, schedules, rosters or diagrams including sporting events, national and local agreements, weather.
K.K6: Organisational digital platforms that are used to create and revise timetables, schedules, rosters or diagrams. General data protection regulation (GDPR). Cyber security requirements.
K.K7: Methods of presenting timetables, schedules, rosters or diagrams, for example, using software platforms, the actual communication to wider external stakeholders.
K.K8: Access requirements that are required for travel routes, including access plans, visa and qualification requirements, power types, registration of timetable, schedule, roster or diagram.
K.K9: Sector specific legislation and other requirements that must be complied with when creating or revising timetables, schedules, rosters or diagrams, for example, union agreements, commercial contracts
K.K10: Organisational procedures for monitoring and assessing the performance of transport services.
K.K11: Organisational procedures for escalating transport services performance issues identified from monitoring and assessment activities within their limits of authority.
K.K12: Organisational procedures and industry specific practices for responding to, and dealing with prolonged or persistent disruption, for example, weather conditions, mechanical issues, public events.
K.K13: Organisational procedures and industry specific practices for supporting situations caused by unforeseen circumstances.
K.K14: Compliance requirements of the organisation and wider industry in relation to transport services.
K.K15: Workload planning tools and techniques.
K.K16: Organisational and industry specific objectives and  key performance indicators (KPIs).
K.K17: Verbal communication techniques. Giving and receiving data and information. Matching style to audience. Industry terminology.
K.K18: Techniques for written communication, plain English principles.
K.K19: Impact of the sector on the environment. Efficient use of resources. Recycling, reuse, and safe disposal of waste.
K.K20: Principles of equity, diversity, and inclusion in the workplace.
K.K21: Documentation: methods and requirements - electronic and paper.
K.K22: CPD (planned and unplanned) and recording methods.

S.S1: Respond to sector and organisational specific handover data.
S.S2: Carry out and record CPD.
S.S3: Create timetables, schedules, rosters or diagrams.
S.S4: Revise timetables, schedules, rosters or diagrams.
S.S5: Use organisational digital platforms, for example, ATTUne, AIMs, Omnibus. Comply with GDPR and cybersecurity requirements.
S.S6: Undertake pre-planning activities to identify considerations to be made in relation to transport services, for example, research country specific medical and visa requirements, review local, national and international events calendars, consider transport service capacity
S.S7: Liaise with internal and external stakeholders to ensure access to travel routes and where required, register timetables, schedules, rosters or diagrams.
S.S8: Comply with sector legislation for example union agreements, commercial contracts.
S.S9: Monitor and assess the performance of transport services, intervening and providing support when required.
S.S10: Respond to prolonged or persistent disruption to transport services, ensuring solutions take into consideration the requirements of internal and external stakeholders.
S.S11: Support situations that occur due unforeseen circumstances, contributing where required in line with organisational procedures and industry specific practices, for example, emergency situations, extreme weather conditions, industrial action.
S.S12: Liaise with internal and external stakeholders to ensure full support is offered during situations that occur due to unforeseen circumstances, for example, emergency situations, extreme weather conditions, industrial action.
S.S13: Identify non-compliance in transport services through monitoring activities.
S.S14: Escalate situations to the appropriate person or authority.
S.S15: Manage own workload to ensure that contributions to organisational objectives meet all requirements.
S.S16: Make operational performance improvements.
S.S17: Communicate with others verbally for example, colleagues and stakeholders.
S.S18: Communicate in writing with others for example, internal and external customers, colleagues, and managers.
S.S19: Comply with regulations, standards, and guidance, including environmental and sustainability regulations.
S.S20: Follow equity, diversity, and inclusion principles.
S.S21: Record or enter information - paper based or electronic. For example, risk assessments, handover documents, work sheets, checklists,  and any legal reporting requirements

B.B1: Committed to maintaining and enhancing competence of self through Continuing Professional Development (CPD).
B.B2: Respond and adapt to work demands and situations.
B.B3: Collaborate within teams, across disciplines and with internal and external stakeholders.
B.B4: Act ethically.

K.K1: Statutory and rail specific legislation and regulations: The Railways and Transport Safety Act, Railways (Accident Investigation and Reporting) Regulations, Railway and Other Guided Transport Systems (Safety) Regulations (ROGS) and Health and Safety at work act (HASAWA)
K.K2: National and local operating procedures.
K.K3: The Rail Safety and Standards Board (RSSB) Rule Book instructions.
K.K4: The rail accident investigation branch (RAIB): their purpose, role and objectives within railway operations.
K.K5: Office of rail and road (ORR): their role within railway operations.
K.K6: Appendices definitions and descriptions, such as general and sectional.
K.K7: The principles of risk management including identification, assessment, mitigation and monitoring.
K.K8: Organisational rail policies, plans, procedures and systems including audits.
K.K9: Principles of safety investigations, such as, the different levels of investigations, designated competent person (DCP), recommendations and local actions. Initiate and facilitate safety investigations.
K.K10: Different types of rail incidents and significant events.
K.K11: The principles of reviewing rail incidents and significant events, including lessons learned reviews.
K.K12: Role and responsibilities during rail incidents and significant events, including emergency plans, escalation procedures and engagement with cross sector contacts, such as emergency services.
K.K13: Roles and responsibilities of Tactical Incident Commander (TIC), Rail Incident Commander (RIC) and Train Operation Liaison Officer (TOLO) Station Incident Officer (SIO).
K.K14: National security level and how it is applied locally.
K.K15: Local security issues: crime, trespass and vandalism.
K.K16: Environmental and sustainability requirements and guidance: wildlife regulations, animal encroachment, animal welfare and disposal, movement of goods, energy utilisation, vegetation management and waste streams.
K.K17: Organisational Competency Management Systems (CMS).
K.K18: Principles of checking, assessing and verification of competencies  relating to employees and contractors. Differences between responsibility and accountability.
K.K19: Workplace training and development techniques: coaching and mentoring.
K.K20: Workplace management techniques: conducting appraisals and providing feedback.
K.K21: The principles of workforce planning, such as, analysing, forecasting, planning workforce supply and demand, gap analysis and recruitment.
K.K22: Continuous professional development (CPD) requirements and learning needs of self and their team.
K.K23: The principles of equity, diversity, and inclusion in the workplace.
K.K24: The principles of strategic management, such as Porters 5 Forces Analysis, Stakeholder Mapping, PESTEL Analysis, SWOT Analysis.
K.K25: Organisational strategic goals and objectives and functional constraints.
K.K26: The principles of financial management, including risk and liability within railway operations.
K.K27: Revenue generation streams and costs within railway operations.
K.K28: Operational requirements and the concept of operational resilience.
K.K29: The principles of contingency planning and approaches to their development and revision.
K.K30: Degraded conditions and working methods in railway operations, such as failure of signals, severe weather.
K.K31: Key performance indicators (KPIs) and metrics relating to train service performance.
K.K32: Continuous improvement principles and techniques such as plan-do-check-act (PDCA), Lean, 6 Sigma, process mapping.
K.K33: Problem solving principles and techniques such as Root Cause Analysis, Fishbone Diagram, Brainstorming.
K.K34: Service disruption in railway operations.
K.K35: Preventative and reactive maintenance requirements of the railway operations environment, such as rolling stock, tracks, infrastructure, stations.
K.K36: Procurement of products and services: organisational approach and subsequent maintenance.
K.K37: Trade unions and employee representatives: structure and the role they play within their organisation and wider railway operations environment.
K.K38: Written communication techniques. digital and paper. Plain English principles. Industry terminology.
K.K39: Verbal communication techniques. Giving and receiving information. Matching style to audience. Industry terminology.
K.K40: Customer portfolio and service level agreements (SLAs) including existing and new customers.

S.S1: Comply with statutory and rail specific regulations and legislation.
S.S2: Manage and monitor workforce compliance with statutory and rail specific regulations and legislation, including health and safety.
S.S3: Comply with national and local operating procedures.
S.S4: Manage risks through risk identification, assessment, mitigation and monitoring.
S.S5: Implement organisational rail policies, plans procedures and systems monitoring compliance.
S.S6: Conduct audits and identify follow up actions.
S.S7: Manage safety investigations.
S.S8: Manage information received regarding incidents and significant events.
S.S9: Review incidents and significant events and identify lessons learnt
S.S10: Create, review and manage emergency plans to respond to rail incidents and significant events.
S.S11: Identify, lead, respond and implement solutions to rail security issues.
S.S12: Apply environmental and sustainability requirements and guidance, ensuring team compliance.
S.S13: Manage performance of employees and contractors including managing competency and appraisals
S.S14: Coach and mentor colleagues.
S.S15: Apply principles of workforce planning for example forecasting and recruitment.
S.S16: Manage and facilitate learning and CPD for self and their team.
S.S17: Apply and promote policies and practices to support equity, diversity and inclusion.
S.S18: Apply the principles of strategic management which contributes and supports the organisations own strategic objectives
S.S19: Apply financial management principles.
S.S20: Prioritise and deploy resources
S.S21: Create and revise contingency plans to ensure the achievement of operational requirements.
S.S22: Apply degraded conditions working methods.
S.S23: Collect and analyse data and information and identify rail service performance issues.
S.S24: Manage rail service performance.
S.S25: Apply continuous improvement principles and techniques.
S.S26: Identify and implement solutions for rail service issues.
S.S27: Manage the impact of  rail service disruptions and mitigate the impact
S.S28: Manage preventative and reactive maintenance schedules of railway infrastructure, buildings and equipment.
S.S29: Manage procurement activities, for example initial purchases and subsequent maintenance.
S.S30: Communicate and manage relationships with Trade Union and employer representatives to achieve collective objectives.
S.S31: Communicate in writing, digital and paper including technical reports
S.S32: Communicate with others verbally for example, colleagues and stakeholders.
S.S33: Manage customer portfolio and service level agreements (SLAs).

B.B1: Prioritises health & safety for themselves and others.
B.B2: Respond and adapt to work demands and situations.
B.B3: Act as a role model and advocate for environmental and sustainable practices.
B.B4: Committed to maintaining and enhancing competence of self and others through CPD.
B.B5: Encourage a diverse and inclusive culture.
B.B6: Acts in a professional manner.

K.K1: Principles of professional responsibilities and ethics in a tax environment, including approaches to interpreting ethics in complex situations.
K.K2: Principles of legal regulations relevant to an organisation’s tax affairs, such as tax legislation, and law affecting the tax affairs of stakeholders.
K.K3: Principles of accounting relevant to an organisation’s tax affairs, such as accounting for taxation and double entry that may impact on business tax calculations.
K.K4: The wider tax professional landscape, including the role of a tax technician, the requirement to stay abreast of changes in tax technology and practice, and awareness of the role of His Majesty's Revenue and Customs (HMRC) and professional bodies.
K.K5: Principles of personal and business taxation to prepare tax calculations.
K.K6: Principles of personal and business taxation to prepare HMRC forms, including understanding and complying with filing deadlines.
K.K7: Principles of a specialist area of taxation to be able to prepare specialist tax calculations.
K.K8: Situations that may lead to additional tax, penalties, and interest arising, and the rules and procedures used by HMRC to calculate penalties and interest.
K.K9: Systems of risk management and financial control, and identification of potential vulnerabilities, negligence, and deliberate actions that could lead to issues, such as internal fraud or money laundering or tax inaccuracies and errors.
K.K10: The importance of applying a transparent, objective, and sustainable approach to meet the ethical requirements of the profession, such as how to apply Professional Rules and Practice Guidelines (PRPG), Professional Conduct in Relation to Taxation (PRCT), and Standards for Tax Planning, and ensuring that HMRC is provided with all relevant information necessary to be assured that the stakeholder’s tax affairs are correctly stated.
K.K11: Principles of data analysis, data security, and cyber security to support collation, analysis, and compliant handling of data, and working safely online.
K.K12: Workload management principles to plan, organise and prioritise own tasks, and manage time effectively.
K.K13: Approaches used for building and maintaining collaborative, professional relationships with internal and external stakeholders to deliver taxation tasks.
K.K14: The nature and importance of key climate, environmental, and emerging sustainability challenges, and the impact on sustainable business practices, risk, and government regulation, leading to the potential tax benefits of economic sustainability for stakeholders, such as energy efficiency related to capital allowances and sustainability requirements for different sizes of businesses.
K.K15: The impact of equality, diversity and inclusion on service delivery within taxation, for example improving the tax position of individuals and businesses by improving accessibility with the move to making tax digital.
K.K16: The importance of Continuing Professional Development (CPD) and keeping up to date with relevant statutory obligations and procedural best practice to help improve own professional practice.

S.S1: Collate, analyse, and use financial information to support evidence-based, sustainable tax decisions for stakeholders, such as individuals and businesses.
S.S2: Undertake taxation activities within the scope and limits of their role and tax specialism, such as the preparation of tax information for individuals, sole traders, partnerships or limited companies, escalating issues to more senior colleagues where appropriate.
S.S3: Apply relevant principles and legislation when undertaking taxation activities to ensure records are compliant.
S.S4: Apply professional scepticism when undertaking taxation activities, being alert to conditions which may indicate possible misstatement of information due to error or fraud, establishing the facts sensitively and being aware of unconscious bias, and using those facts to inform and evaluate decision making in the context of providing accurate information to HMRC.
S.S5: Interpret information for tax purposes, such as using judgement to determine the correct way to report transactions or items of income or expenditure.
S.S6: Participate in or support others with quality improvement activities, for example the improvement of personal working practices.
S.S7: Use software packages to assist with taxation tasks in line with cyber and data security requirements, using data securely and safely, including backing up data.
S.S8: Deliver taxation tasks with a critical eye to trends, demonstrating a ‘right first-time approach’.
S.S9: Use workload management principles to plan, organise, and prioritise own tasks and manage time effectively, such as systems for managing key tax filing dates, penalty dates, and enquiry deadlines.
S.S10: Use taxation information to add value to stakeholders through use of data visualisations, analysis, and interpretation, and communicate tax information in a way that non-tax specialist stakeholders can understand.
S.S11: Undertake file management activities, such as preparing and collating information, making accurate file notes and managing version control.
S.S12: Build and maintain collaborative, professional relationships with internal and external stakeholders to deliver taxation tasks, recognising the importance of equality, diversity and inclusion.
S.S13: Communicate taxation information through different media to facilitate stakeholder understanding, considering the risks and benefits to the organisation of social media and other digital applications.

B.B1: Actively engages in the wider organisation to provide information that contributes to influencing stakeholders, such as peers, colleagues and clients.
B.B2: Applies a transparent, objective, and sustainable approach to meet the ethical requirements of the profession and actively encourages this behaviour with colleagues within their team.
B.B3: Committed to personal wellbeing and has an awareness of support and resources available.
B.B4: Adaptable and accepts changing priorities and working requirements to enable self and colleagues the flexibility to maintain high standards in a changing environment.
B.B5: Reflects on own practice and seeks learning opportunities for Continuing Professional Development (CPD).
B.B6: Works collaboratively with others, contributing to cultivating an open, honest, and empathetic work environment, and actively encourages this behaviour with colleagues in their team.

K.K1: Financial and management accounting principles used in the planning, decision making, implementation and control of strategic taxation options.
K.K2: Principles of risk assessment, risk management and approaches to selecting the best actions for progressing tax matters. Including identification of potential vulnerabilities that could lead to issues such as internal fraud or money laundering.
K.K3: Principles of strategic tax planning, this may include sustainable decision making and change management.
K.K4: Financial, taxation and business reporting to relevant stakeholders. This may include reports dealing with tax liabilities, tax planning, or compliance issues including how the tax specific issues fit within the wider client context.
K.K5: Legislation, standards and principles that apply to taxation in line with the specialism, role and the operating environment. This may include accounting standards, taxation legislation, legal requirements, and organisational principles.
K.K6: Approaches to research and information gathering including identification and selection of trusted sources and documentation.
K.K7: Approaches to problem solving, analytical techniques and synthesis, including the use of digital tools.
K.K8: Approaches to stakeholder identification and stakeholder management including the significance of external and internal stakeholders to organisations.
K.K9: Approaches to communication, including use of clear language and the most appropriate media to communicate with different stakeholders.
K.K10: Emotional intelligence in communications with others, including respect for Equality, Diversity, Inclusion and vulnerability.
K.K11: Digital technologies within a taxation environment.
K.K12: Principles of data analytics and data visualisations within a taxation environment.
K.K13: The principles of ethical and professional frameworks and their relevance for tax service delivery.
K.K14: The ethical issues and obligations applying to the provision of tax services, including legal rules and regulations, organisational policies and the Chartered Institute of Taxation's (CIOT) Member Rules.
K.K15: The principles of legal and regulatory liabilities with regards to clients to ensure compliance with all professional obligations, for example, the provision of ethical advice in the best interests of the client and Anti-Money Laundering obligations.
K.K16: Legal requirements and ethical challenges relating to the use of technology in the tax sector, including risks to confidentiality of data, vulnerabilities to cyber threats and defensive measures.
K.K17: The wider tax professional landscape including the role of a tax advisor; the requirement to be aware of changes in the political and legal landscape, tax technology, and tax practice; and the role of tax authorities and professional bodies.
K.K18: Approaches to identifying how changes in the tax landscape might be implemented in practice.
K.K19: Strategies for professional development to ensure ongoing competence and good practice, including identification of needs, reflection, evaluation, emotional intelligence, and recognition of emotions and vulnerabilities in others.

S.S1: Prepare, analyse and interpret a stakeholder’s tax information for compliance and advisory purposes by establishing the stakeholder’s existing position, needs and expectations and identifying needs that they may not recognise, utilising specialist expertise as required.
S.S2: Identify tax risks and approaches to risk management and control in providing assurance to stakeholders.
S.S3: Set key strategic and operational objectives and measure performance against those objectives.
S.S4: Interpret and apply appropriate legislation, standards and principles. This may include accounting standards, taxation legislation, legal requirements, ethical codes and internal principles.
S.S5: Gather and evaluate information and data from a range of sources, considering multiple stakeholder perspectives and operational environments, in order to make clear and justifiable recommendations that support sustainable decision-making.
S.S6: Undertake independent research in the areas of tax specialism using standard tax resources and use professional scepticism to identify and select the most appropriate source.
S.S7: Collate research including, where appropriate, case law, legal procedure, legal changes and documentation in a thorough, pragmatic and organised manner, using the full range of available tools and digital technologies, including assessment of quality of research sources and apply it to the matter in hand, to produce research notes.
S.S8: Test and challenge information and data by establishing what is relevant information, evaluating its validity, and identifying missing information to be provided by a stakeholder and ensure complete information and data.
S.S9: Analyse, synthesise, and evaluate information in different ways to determine how to act on a stakeholder's instructions and identify any real or potential conflicts of interest.
S.S10: Give clear and unambiguous tax advice to stakeholders including advice on the available options, the risks, the costs and the benefits of alternative courses of action, next steps and provide supporting evidence as appropriate.
S.S11: Present and make a reasoned choice between alternative solutions.
S.S12: Make strategic and sustainable decisions based on tax and non-tax based analysis. Present balanced conclusions, with supporting evidence, which includes internal and external factors.
S.S13: Take accurate instructions from colleagues and other stakeholders through use of interviewing skills, including effective listening.
S.S14: Communicate in a clear, articulate and appropriate manner to clients and other professionals, including tax authorities. Adapt communication techniques and styles to suit different situations, individuals or teams.
S.S15: Communicate clear, accurate and practical advice to stakeholders, including where stakeholder objectives may be unrealistic and respond appropriately to manage stakeholders' needs and engage others in the team or externally with the necessary specialist tax knowledge when appropriate.
S.S16: Mitigate against conflict by managing underlying issues and mediate and manage conflict when it arises.
S.S17: Negotiate outcomes on behalf of stakeholders to deliver the best possible outcome, taking steps to ensure agreement or compromise is reached which is clear, ethical and lawful.
S.S18: Plan and deliver work, including projects, within a predetermined timeframe, within budget, and to a predefined standard, to deliver outcomes for stakeholders based on a ‘right first time’ approach.
S.S19: Prioritise, plan and review progress and reschedule activities in line with changing priorities to meet changing stakeholder demands.
S.S20: Manage own resource requirements and workflow, to objectives and budgets.
S.S21: Use digital technologies and techniques, undertaking data management in compliance with legislation and employer policies.
S.S22: Use data analytics tools to visualise findings, make recommendations, and communicate them to stakeholders.
S.S23: Apply professional scepticism when undertaking taxation activities to inform and evaluate decision making, and in the context of providing accurate information to tax authorities.
S.S24: Be alert to conditions which may indicate possible misstatement of information due to error or fraud, establish the facts sensitively and be aware of unconscious bias.
S.S25: Contribute to the financial management of stakeholders, including budgets, work in progress, and, where appropriate, timely billing and collection and variance recognition.
S.S26: Work collaboratively towards achieving stakeholder and organisational objectives.
S.S27: Build and maintain trusted and sustainable relationships with internal and external stakeholders.
S.S28: Provide support to individuals and collaborate to achieve results as part of a team.
S.S29: Support and advise colleagues on routine tax matters.
S.S30: Contribute to a culture of continuous improvement including feedback mechanisms.
S.S31: Using reflection and evaluation of own competence, recognise when the limit of technical competence and skills is reached and ask for support.

B.B1: With an enquiring mind, exercises professional scepticism when evaluating information.
B.B2: Open minded and curious, seeks out information on external developments to enhance own performance and that of the organisation.
B.B3: Demonstrates professional judgement, making informed decisions about appropriate courses of action.
B.B4: Acts appropriately when faced with potential ethical issues. Demonstrates principled behaviour, follows rules, adheres to organisational and professional standards and manages risk. Recognises limitations of own abilities, refers technical work to others with the right expertise, admits mistakes and resists pressure to condone, ignore or act unethically.
B.B5: Demonstrates effective leadership, acting as a role model and providing leadership, guidance and challenge where necessary to their business area. Takes responsibility for own actions and outcomes from own work.
B.B6: Takes responsibility for personal professional development by seeking out opportunities that enhance knowledge, skills and experience.
B.B7: Flexible and willing to adapt approaches to assist organisations and individuals to manage conflicting priorities as circumstances change.
B.B8: Builds and maintains strong positive working relationships with stakeholders such as clients, colleagues, suppliers and regulators as appropriate. Actively engages in the wider business or practice and provides information that contributes to influencing decisions.
B.B9: Applies a transparent and appropriate manner in dealings with tax authorities in relation to the tax affairs of stakeholders.

K.K1: Osteopaths’ regulatory, ethical and professional responsibilities.
K.K2: The principles and standards set out in the Osteopathic Practice Standards and associated regulatory guidance.
K.K3: The importance of understanding personal scope of practice and operating within the limits of personal competence at any one time.
K.K4: The clinical sciences, biopsychosocial model, and osteopathic concepts, principles and guidelines that underpin the profession’s practice.
K.K5: Osteopathy’s contribution to delivering patient care across the lifespan in the context of wider healthcare systems and models of care.
K.K6: Approaches to delivering osteopathic care to meet individuals’ health needs, beliefs and preferences.
K.K7: The role and importance of touch, palpation and the use of developed sensory motor skills within osteopathic care.
K.K8: Structured approaches to critical thinking and problem solving within clinical assessment and decision making.
K.K9: How to deal with unfamiliar, complex and unpredictable situations within professional contexts.
K.K10: How to gain valid consent from individuals before assessing them and delivering care to them and what to do if consent is not given or is withdrawn.
K.K11: How to apply clinical knowledge and assessment skills to identify normal and abnormal clinical findings and manage specific conditions and clinical scenarios within personal scope of practice.
K.K12: How to identify and assess symptoms and conditions commonly presented within osteopathic care, focused on those relating to the musculoskeletal system, acute and chronic pain and biomechanical function and with an understanding of psychosocial factors impacting on individuals’ health and wellbeing.
K.K13: How to undertake a holistic osteopathic assessment of individual patients’ needs through history taking and clinical observation and examination to obtain relevant clinical data.
K.K14: How to obtain and record subjective and objective assessment findings within a full patient case history to inform clinical assessment and to articulate clinical decision-making.
K.K15: When and how to request appropriate clinical investigations, including diagnostic imaging and blood tests, and how to interpret the reported results to inform treatment planning.
K.K16: When and how to refer individuals to other healthcare professionals when this is in individuals’ best interests
K.K17: How to communicate verbally and in writing with peers and other healthcare professionals to engage in collaborative, interprofessional working and to deliver safe and effective patient care.
K.K18: The importance of providing clear, succinct and accurate information in making patient referrals.
K.K19: Different communication and interpersonal styles and strategies, including active listening, and how to use these to build and maintain effective therapeutic relationships based on understanding, empathy, respect and trust.
K.K20: How to devise and implement osteopathic treatment plans in line with clinical assessment, diagnosis and shared decision-making and to keep their effectiveness under review.
K.K21: Approaches to working in partnership with individuals to optimise their function, mobility and wellbeing, informed by their priorities and goals.
K.K22: How to select, explain, apply and adapt specific osteopathic treatment approaches in line with individuals’ needs, preferences and goals and by recognising relative and absolute contraindications.
K.K23: How to recognise, act and advise on patients’ adverse reactions to their receipt of osteopathic treatment.
K.K24: Ways to support individuals to manage their own health and wellbeing, including through health promotion, illness prevention advice, and health coaching.
K.K25: The role and value of social networks, communities and activities to support individuals’ health and wellbeing.
K.K26: Safeguarding responsibilities and how to fulfil these by acting on concerns about individuals’ safety.
K.K27: The importance of maintaining professional boundaries with patients and colleagues, including to uphold trust in therapeutic relationships and the osteopathic profession.
K.K28: Approaches to critically appraising research and evidence-based guidelines relating to osteopathic practice and applying relevant findings to osteopathic practice.
K.K29: Processes to collect qualitative and quantitative data within osteopathic practice, including to measure and optimise patient outcomes and as part of engaging with clinical audit, service evaluation and quality improvement initiatives.
K.K30: How to undertake risk assessments and report and act on these in line with appropriate guidelines.
K.K31: The importance of upholding patient confidentiality in professional activity in line with legislation, policies and local processes and procedures.

K.K33: First aid and basic life support principles and procedures.
K.K34: Health and safety legislation, policies and procedures to uphold the interests of patients, colleagues, the public and self.
K.K35: How to use population and public health data to inform the delivery of osteopathic care to meet health and wellbeing needs.

K.K38: The professional responsibilities attached to duty of candour within healthcare.
K.K39: Legislation, policies and guidance relating to equity, diversity and inclusion.
K.K40: The professional responsibility to engage in career-long learning to maintain professional knowledge and skills and to respond to changing population health, service delivery and practice needs.
K.K41: The importance of engaging with research methodologies and osteopathy’s evolving evidence base, including as part of demonstrating the value of the profession’s contribution to meeting health and wellbeing needs.

K.K43: Leadership theories and styles and the impact of their implementation on teams, service delivery and patient care.
K.K44: Approaches to supporting others’ learning and development and their relevance and value for supporting personal professional development.
K.K45: Theories and models of business development, financial management and entrepreneurialism that are relevant to setting up, delivering and developing osteopathic services.
K.K46: The importance of adhering to ethical business practices to uphold safe, effective, high-quality care and the reputation of osteopathy.
K.K47: How to manage patient concerns and complaints.

S.S1: Engage with and adhere to the regulatory, ethical and professional responsibilities of being an osteopath.
S.S2: Uphold and adhere to the principles and standards set out in the Osteopathic Practice Standards and associated regulatory guidance.
S.S3: Demonstrate self awareness of personal scope of practice and competence and how to work within these parameters.
S.S4: Apply the underpinning knowledge base and philosophy of osteopathy to deliver safe, effective, person-centred osteopathic care.
S.S5: Deliver osteopathy services within broader healthcare systems and models of care and service delivery.
S.S6: Deliver person-centred care in response to individuals’ health needs, beliefs, preferences and goals.
S.S7: Critically apply touch, palpation and sensory motor skills to inform and deliver osteopathic care in response to individuals’ needs.
S.S8: Apply critical thinking and problem-solving approaches within clinical assessments and decision-making.
S.S9: Manage unfamiliar, complex and unpredictable situations by exercising professional judgement, assessing and managing risk, acting within personal scope of practice, and seeking guidance from others.
S.S10: Gain individuals’ consent before assessing them and delivering episodes of care and manage situations in which a patient either does not give their consent or withdraws their consent.
S.S11: Exercise professional judgement in applying osteopathic principles and approaches, including touch and palpation, to assess and diagnose individuals’ needs.
S.S12: Assess patients’ presenting symptoms and condition and appraise and discuss the appropriateness of delivering osteopathic care to meet their individual needs.
S.S13: Assess individual patients’ needs through history-taking, clinical examination and obtaining and drawing on clinical data to understand their needs, preferences and goals.
S.S14: Use subjective questioning and objective examination to formulate a working diagnosis and inform decision-making on appropriate treatment approaches to meet individual patients’ needs.
S.S15: Develop, implement and evaluate care plans in partnership with patients to meet their individual needs, preferences and goals.
S.S16: Select and modify osteopathic treatment approaches to meet individuals’ needs, acting on relative and absolute contra-indications to uphold patient safety.
S.S17: Communicate verbally and in writing with peers and other healthcare professionals as part of collaborative interprofessional working and to advocate for individuals’ receipt of safe and effective care.
S.S18: Provide clear, accurate and succinct information on individuals’ specific needs in making appropriate patient referrals.
S.S19: Select, deploy and integrate different communication approaches to build and maintain individuals’ trust and confidence in the therapeutic relationship, keeping approaches’ effectiveness under review and adapting them where appropriate.
S.S20: Develop, agree, implement and evaluate care plans in partnership with individuals to meet their specific needs, preferences and goals and keep plans’ effectiveness under review as an integral part of partnership-working.
S.S21: Work in partnership with individuals to develop, regain or maintain their function, mobility and wellbeing, informed by their priorities and goals.
S.S22: Select, explain, apply and adapt specific osteopathic treatment approaches in line with individuals’ needs, preferences and goals, recognising and acting on relative and absolute contraindications to uphold patient safety.
S.S23: Identify individuals’ adverse reactions to their osteopathic treatment and take appropriate action to uphold patient safety, manage clinical risk and provide safety-netting advice.
S.S24: Guide individuals on ways to improve, manage and maintain their health and prevent illness, including by providing advice, using health-coaching approaches, and supporting behaviour change.
S.S25: Encourage individuals to use social networks and communities to support their own health and wellbeing, including through appropriate signposting to relevant resources and social prescribing initiatives.
S.S26: Uphold individuals’ safety, including by adhering to safeguarding procedures and using formal processes to raise concerns.
S.S27: Maintain appropriate professional boundaries with patients and colleagues.
S.S28: Critically appraise, synthesise and apply relevant research literature, evidence-based guidelines and other information sources to inform clinical decision-making, treatment approaches and advice.
S.S29: Collect data and apply appropriate outcome measures as part of engaging with clinical audit, service evaluation and quality improvement activities.
S.S30: Undertake risk assessments and manage and report on their outcome to uphold others’ and own safety in line with appropriate guidelines.
S.S31: Uphold patient confidentiality in all aspects of professional practice and be able to respond when fulfilling the public interest and safeguarding concerns override maintaining confidentiality.
S.S32: Create and store patient care records in line with all legal, regulatory and professional requirements and local protocols and procedures and as evidence of clinical decisions and actions.
S.S33: Provide first aid and basic life support and take appropriate actions to uphold individuals’ safety in urgent and emergency situations.
S.S34: Adhere to health and safety legislation, policies and procedures to uphold all parties’ safety and maintain a safe practice environment.
S.S35: Access, interpret and apply population and public health data to inform the planning and delivery of osteopathic care at a local level.
S.S36: Manage a clinical caseload, prioritising individual patients in line with need and adhering to local waiting list and triaging procedures and protocols, where relevant.
S.S37: Act as a first point of contact for patients within the limits of personal scope of practice.
S.S38: Engage with the professional responsibilities attached to duty of candour, including when things go wrong.
S.S39: Actively engage with equity, diversity and inclusion legislation, policies and good practice and their implications for delivering person-centred osteopathic care and working with others.
S.S40: Engage in ongoing professional learning and development through critical reflection on learning needs, gaining others’ feedback, and learning with others.
S.S41: Engage with research methods, practices and developments relevant to the profession, demonstrating awareness of different approaches' value and limitations and the importance of demonstrating the profession's value and impact.
S.S42: Work with others to support personal and team wellbeing, contribute to effective service delivery and uphold safe and effective patient care.
S.S43: Contribute to shared models of leadership in line with role, level of practice, practice context, and professional experience.
S.S44: Support others’ learning and development, including by providing constructive feedback and mentoring peers.
S.S45: Apply practical approaches to business development, financial management and entrepreneurialism within osteopathic service delivery.
S.S46: Engage in ethical business activity within personal scope of practice, including when developing, advertising, promoting and delivering osteopathic services.
S.S47: Manage patients’ concerns and complaints when their expectations have not been met and learn from service user feedback.
S.S48: Develop, apply and reflect on personal strategies to manage personal wellbeing and resilience.
S.S49: Apply relevant digital communication tools and technologies safely and appropriately within osteopathic care and record-keeping, including to enhance patient access and service delivery, and maintain patient confidentiality and cyber-security vigilance.
S.S50: Apply sustainability principles and practices within personal scope of practice and contributions to service delivery, review and development.

B.B1: Demonstrate kindness, empathy and compassion in delivering person-centred care.
B.B2: Promote and protect the interests of service users, treating all people with respect and dignity.
B.B3: Exercise self-awareness and self-management in practice, including in interactions with others and making independent decisions.
B.B4: Act with professional integrity, honesty and openness, including when things go wrong.
B.B5: Demonstrate engagement, curiosity and reflection within ongoing professional learning and development.
B.B6: Demonstrate adaptability, resilience and responsiveness to changing needs.
B.B7: Engage with and uphold the regulatory requirements of being an osteopath.

S.S15: Support the development or implementation of business development or customer contact strategies.
S.S16: Apply financial analysis techniques relevant to financial services tasks, for example providing guidance on credit appetite, developing practical and appropriate credit solutions or lending structures, or maximising portfolio return.
S.S17: Identify and provide relevant client market and product information to deliver client-focused, accessible, and ethical information that meets client needs.
S.S18: Undertake due diligence and risk assessment activities based on a detailed understanding of individual client needs, risk appetite, and exposure to risk.
S.S19: Support stakeholder account planning and growth activities.
S.S20: Undertake sales and marketing activities to generate positive commercial outcomes.
S.S21: Apply compliance processes and procedures to financial operations activities to meet regulatory requirements.
S.S22: Plan and support the implementation of live changes in financial services operation processes and procedures, including participation in stress tests and health checks to ensure compliance and viability.
S.S23: Support the provision of feedback to internal stakeholders regarding identified areas for improvement in compliance and operational efficiencies.

B.B1: Shows integrity by doing the right thing in adherence to the code of conduct and by demonstrating the organisation’s values.
B.B2: Maintains confidentiality and acts with due care and diligence.

S.S15: Identify opportunities to drive more effective performance for own role and improve services and or processes.

S.S26: Undertake team coaching to improve the performance of financial investment operations.
